topics.title: The Study of Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Study of Language

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "The Study of Language"?

The **Study of Language**, also known as **Linguistics**, is the scientific, systematic, and empirical investigation of human language. Within the context of Natural Language Processing (NLP), this topic establishes the foundational understanding of *what language is*, *how it is structured*, and *how meaning is constructed and interpreted* — knowledge that any NLP engineer must possess before designing computational systems that process text or speech.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination-Ready):**
> Linguistics is the scientific study of language. It analyzes language at multiple interrelated levels — **Phonetics, Phonology, Morphology, Syntax, Semantics, Pragmatics, and Discourse** — to understand its structure, function, and cognitive grounding.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: Language as a Layered Building

Imagine language as a **multi-storey building** standing on a foundation:

| Floor | Linguistic Level | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Foundation | **Phonetics & Phonology** | The raw bricks and cement (sounds) |
| First Floor | **Morphology** | Pre-fabricated walls (words) |
| Second Floor | **Syntax** | Room layout and structure (sentences) |
| Third Floor | **Semantics** | The purpose of each room (meaning) |
| Fourth Floor | **Pragmatics** | How occupants actually use the building (context) |
| Rooftop | **Discourse** | The city-wide network of buildings (connected texts) |

Just as you cannot furnish a room (semantics) before constructing the walls (morphology), NLP systems cannot interpret meaning without first correctly parsing the words and structures.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> **Must-Know Terms for KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
> - **Language**: A systematic, rule-governed, symbolic system of communication used by humans, characterized by creativity, arbitrariness, and displacement.
> - **Linguistics**: The scientific study of language structure and use.
> - **Competence vs. Performance** (Chomsky): Competence = the idealized knowledge of a native speaker; Performance = the actual production of language in real situations (often with errors).
> - **L1 / L2**: First language (mother tongue) / Second language.
> - **Universal Grammar (UG)**: Chomsky's hypothesis that all human languages share a common innate structural foundation.
> - **Ambiguity**: A sentence/word having more than one valid interpretation — a central challenge in NLP.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six (Seven) Levels of Linguistic Analysis

#### 🔹 Level 1: Phonetics
- **Definition**: The physical study of human speech sounds — their production (articulatory), transmission (acoustic), and perception (auditory).
- **Goal in NLP**: Speech recognition, text-to-speech synthesis.
- **Key Unit**: The **phoneme** — the smallest unit of sound that can distinguish word meaning.
- **Example**: In English, /b/ and /p/ are different phonemes because *bat* and *pat* mean different things.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Fact:** English has roughly **44 phonemes** (depending on dialect). Hindi has around **48 phonemes**, Malayalam around **53**.

#### 🔹 Level 2: Phonology
- **Definition**: The study of the abstract, mental sound system of a language — how phonemes are organized, combined, and rule-governed.
- **Key Concept**: Phonemes function **contrastively**; allophones (variants) do not.
- **Example Rule**: English plural formation involves adding /s/, /z/, or /ɪz/ depending on the final sound of the noun (cats /s/, dogs /z/, buses /ɪz/).

#### 🔹 Level 3: Morphology
- **Definition**: The study of the internal structure of words and the smallest meaningful units within them.
- **Key Unit**: The **morpheme** — the smallest indivisible unit of meaning.
  - **Free morpheme**: Can stand alone (*book*, *run*).
  - **Bound morpheme**: Cannot stand alone (*-ing*, *un-*).
- **Processes**:
  - **Inflection** (grammatical): *walk → walked*
  - **Derivation** (new word): *happy → unhappy*
- **Example**: *unhappinesses* = *un-* + *happy* + *-ness* + *-es* (4 morphemes).

#### 🔹 Level 4: Syntax
- **Definition**: The study of how words combine to form grammatical sentences; the rules governing sentence structure.
- **Key Concept**: **Phrase Structure Grammar (PSG)** / **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)**.
- **NLP Application**: Parsing algorithms (CYK, Earley, Recursive Descent).

#### 🔹 Level 5: Semantics
- **Definition**: The study of meaning — at both the **word level (lexical semantics)** and **sentence level (compositional semantics)**.
- **Key Concepts**: 
  - **Reference**: What a word points to in the world.
  - **Sense**: The inherent meaning of a word.
  - **Entailment / Contradiction**: Logical relations between sentences.

#### 🔹 Level 6: Pragmatics
- **Definition**: The study of meaning in context — how speakers convey more than what is literally said.
- **Key Concepts**: 
  - **Speech Acts** (Austin/Searle): *assertives, directives, commissives, expressives, declarations*.
  - **Implicature**: What is implied rather than stated.
  - **Deixis**: Words whose meaning depends on context (*here, there, you, yesterday*).
- **Example**: *"Can you pass the salt?"* is syntactically a question but pragmatically a **directive/request**.

#### 🔹 Level 7: Discourse
- **Definition**: The study of how sentences connect to form coherent texts, dialogues, or larger units of communication.
- **Key Concepts**: **Anaphora resolution, coherence relations, cohesion**.

---

### 2.2 Key Theoretical Frameworks (KTU High-Yield)

#### A. Chomsky's Hierarchy of Grammars

This is a frequently tested concept in KTU examinations.

$$
\text{Regular} \subset \text{Context-Free} \subset \text{Context-Sensitive} \subset \text{Recursively Enumerable}
$$

| Grammar Type | Recognizer | Language Example |
|---|---|---|
| Regular Grammar | Finite Automaton | $a^*b^*$ |
| Context-Free Grammar | Pushdown Automaton | $a^nb^n$ |
| Context-Sensitive Grammar | Linear Bounded Automaton | $a^nb^nc^n$ |
| Unrestricted | Turing Machine | General recursion |

#### B. Language Universals (Greenberg)

- **Subject–Verb–Object (SVO)** order dominates world languages (e.g., English).
- **SOV** order is also very common (Hindi, Japanese, Malayalam).
- **VSO** appears in Arabic, Irish.

#### C. Mathematical Formulations Used in Language Study

$$
\text{Word Probability: } P(w_i \vert w_{i-1}, w_{i-2}, \dots, w_{i-n+1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-n+1}, \dots, w_i)}{C(w_{i-n+1}, \dots, w_{i-1})}
$$

$$
\text{Perplexity (key NLP metric): } PP(W) = P(w_1 w_2 \dots w_N)^{-1/N}
$$

$$
\text{Shannon Entropy: } H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \log_2 P(x_i)
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Domain | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum P(x_i)\log_2 P(x_i)$ | bits per symbol | Information content of language |
| Perplexity | $PP(W) = P(w_1 \dots w_N)^{-1/N}$ | dimensionless | Language model evaluation |
| N-gram Probability | $P(w_n \mid w_{n-1}^{n-N+1})$ | probability $\in [0,1]$ | Statistical language modeling |
| Zipf's Law | $f \cdot r \approx k$ | rank–frequency | Vocabulary distribution |
| Chomsky Hierarchy | $\text{Reg} \subset \text{CFG} \subset \text{CSG} \subset \text{RE}$ | 4 levels | Parser design selection |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

### 3.1 Exhaustive Multi-Level Analysis of an Example Sentence

**Working Sentence**: *"The bank can fish."*

> [!NOTE]
> This sentence is a classic example in KTU-style NLP questions because it exhibits **lexical ambiguity** (the word *bank*) and **syntactic ambiguity**.

Let us analyze it at each linguistic level with full mathematical/logical detail.

#### Step 1: Phonetic Transcription

The sentence is first converted to a sequence of phonemes using the IPA (International Phonetic Alphabet).

$$
\text{IPA:} \quad /ðə \;\; bæŋk \;\; kæn \;\; fɪʃ/
$$

**Logic**: Phonetic analysis identifies the **physical sound waves**. Each word is broken into its component phonemes. The word *"can"* here is phonetically /kæn/ (modal verb), not /kən/ (tin container).

#### Step 2: Phonological Rules

Apply English phonological rules:
- *"The"* → /ðə/ (vowel reduction rule applied before a consonant).
- Aspirated /k/ in *"can"*; non-aspirated /b/ in *"bank"*.

**Logic**: Phonology determines **which sound is produced**, given the context. The same phoneme /k/ in *"can"* is aspirated because it follows a pause at the start of a stressed syllable.

#### Step 3: Morphological Decomposition

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{The} &= \text{Det (free morpheme)} \\
\text{bank} &= \text{Noun (free morpheme)} \\
\text{can} &= \text{Aux/MV (free morpheme)} \\
\text{fish} &= \text{Noun/Verb (free morpheme)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic**: Each word consists of **one free morpheme** — no inflectional or derivational suffixes are present. This is morphologically simple.

#### Step 4: Syntactic Parse Tree

We construct two competing parse trees for the same string — illustrating **structural ambiguity**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parse 1 (Noun sense):} \quad & [\text{S [NP The bank}] [\text{VP can fish}]] \\
\text{Parse 2 (Verb sense):} \quad & [\text{S [NP The bank}] [\text{VP can} [\text{VP fish}]]]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic**:
- **Parse 1**: *bank* = a financial institution; *fish* = verb (action). Meaning: *"The financial institution is capable of catching fish."*
- **Parse 2**: *bank* = a riverside; *can* = modal verb; *fish* = noun (plural). Meaning: *"The riverbank may contain fish."*

This dual-parseability is what computational parsers must resolve using probabilistic context-free grammars (PCFGs).

#### Step 5: Semantic Analysis

Apply **compositional semantics** — meaning is built bottom-up.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parse 1 Semantics:} \quad & \exists x \, (\text{bank}(x) \land \text{can}(x, \text{fish}(y))) \\
\text{Parse 2 Semantics:} \quad & \exists y \, (\text{on\_bank}(x) \land \text{can}(x, y) \land \text{fish}(y))
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic**: Two **distinct logical forms** arise from the same surface string — confirming semantic ambiguity rooted in syntactic ambiguity.

#### Step 6: Pragmatic Enrichment

$$
\text{If Speaker says to a friend at a riverside: } \textit{"The bank can fish."}
$$

**Pragmatic Interpretation**: The most likely meaning is **Parse 2**, because context (the physical location) constrains interpretation. **Pragmatics disambiguates semantics.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight**: NLP systems must resolve ambiguity **incrementally across levels** — a top-down pipeline is often: Pragmatics → Semantics → Syntax → Morphology → Phonology.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Computing Shannon Entropy of a Mini-Corpus

Given a toy corpus with word frequencies:

| Word | Frequency $f_i$ | Probability $P(w_i)$ |
|---|---|---|
| *the* | 4 | 0.4 |
| *cat* | 3 | 0.3 |
| *sat* | 2 | 0.2 |
| *on* | 1 | 0.1 |

**Total tokens = 10.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{4} P(w_i) \log_2 P(w_i) \\[6pt]
&= -\big[0.4 \log_2(0.4) + 0.3 \log_2(0.3) + 0.2 \log_2(0.2) + 0.1 \log_2(0.1)\big] \\[6pt]
&= -\big[0.4(-1.3219) + 0.3(-1.7370) + 0.2(-2.3219) + 0.1(-3.3219)\big] \\[6pt]
&= -\big[-0.5288 - 0.5211 - 0.4644 - 0.3322\big] \\[6pt]
&= 1.8465 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: This small corpus carries approximately **1.85 bits of information per word**. A more uniform distribution would yield higher entropy (closer to $\log_2 4 = 2$ bits).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The NLP Pipeline (Level-by-Level Flow)

This diagram shows how a raw language input flows upward through the six linguistic levels — exactly as an NLP pipeline processes text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Language Input: Text or Speech] --> B[Phonetics: Physical Sounds]
    B --> C[Phonology: Sound System Rules]
    C --> D[Morphology: Words and Morphemes]
    D --> E[Syntax: Sentence Structure]
    E --> F[Semantics: Literal Meaning]
    F --> G[Pragmatics: Contextual Meaning]
    G --> H[Discourse: Coherent Interpretation]
    H --> I[Final NLP Output: Translation, Summary, QA Response]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Types of Ambiguity in Language

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Linguistic Ambiguity] --> B[Lexical Ambiguity]
    A --> C[Syntactic Ambiguity]
    A --> D[Semantic Ambiguity]
    A --> E[Pragmatic Ambiguity]
    B --> B1[Example: bank - river vs finance]
    C --> C1[Example: I saw the man with the telescope]
    D --> D1[Example: Every student read a book]
    E --> E1[Example: Can you pass the salt?]
    style A fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Chomsky's Hierarchy of Formal Grammars

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Unrestricted Grammar - Type 0 - Turing Machine]
    CS[Context-Sensitive Grammar - Type 1 - Linear Bounded Automaton]
    CF[Context-Free Grammar - Type 2 - Pushdown Automaton]
    RG[Regular Grammar - Type 3 - Finite Automaton]
    R --> CS
    CS --> CF
    CF --> RG
    R -.recognizes.-> RE[Recursively Enumerable Languages]
    CS -.recognizes.-> CSL[Context-Sensitive Languages]
    CF -.recognizes.-> CFL[Context-Free Languages]
    RG -.recognizes.-> RL[Regular Languages]
    style R fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CS fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CF fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style RG fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
Define **Linguistics**. List any four branches of linguistics.

**Model Answer:**
Linguistics is the scientific study of human language — its structure, use, acquisition, and mental representation.
The four branches are:
1. **Phonetics** — physical study of speech sounds.
2. **Phonology** — study of the sound system of a language.
3. **Morphology** — study of word structure.
4. **Syntax** — study of sentence structure.
*(Other acceptable: Semantics, Pragmatics, Discourse Analysis, Psycholinguistics, Sociolinguistics.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
Differentiate between **Phonetics** and **Phonology**.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Phonetics | Phonology |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Physical production & perception of sounds | Abstract sound system of a language |
| Studies | Acoustic/articulatory properties | Phonemes, allophones, rules |
| Concerned with | "How sounds are made" | "How sounds function in a language" |
| Example | The /p/ in *"pin"* is aspirated [pʰ] | The phoneme /p/ contrasts with /b/ in *pin/bin* |

---

### 📘 PART B — Essay-Type Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Option 1

**Q. (a)** Explain the **six levels of linguistic analysis** with one example each. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** Discuss the major **ambiguities** faced in natural language processing, with at least **two examples** for each type. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution — Part A (a):**

The six levels of language analysis are:

1. **Phonetics / Phonology**: Deals with the sound system. Example: The word *"think"* starts with /θ/ — a voiceless dental fricative that does not exist in many Indian languages.

2. **Morphology**: Concerned with the smallest meaningful units (morphemes). Example: *"unhappiness"* = un- + happy + -ness (3 morphemes).

3. **Syntax**: Deals with sentence structure governed by grammar rules. Example: *"The cat chased the mouse"* follows Subject–Verb–Object (SVO) order.

4. **Semantics**: Studies the literal meaning of words and sentences. Example: *"red car"* refers to a car possessing the property of redness.

5. **Pragmatics**: Studies meaning in context. Example: *"It's cold here"* may be a **request to close the window** rather than a statement of temperature.

6. **Discourse**: Studies how multiple sentences connect. Example: *"Ramu went home. **He** was tired."* — the pronoun *"he"* refers back to Ramu (**anaphora**).

#### **Model Solution — Part A (b):**

Ambiguities in NLP:

1. **Lexical Ambiguity** — A single word has multiple senses.
   - *"I went to the **bank**."* — financial institution or riverside?
   - *"He is looking for a **match**."* — cricket match or matchstick?

2. **Syntactic Ambiguity** — A sentence has multiple parse trees.
   - *"I saw the man with the telescope."* — Who has the telescope?
   - *"Flying planes can be dangerous."* — The act of flying planes, or planes that are flying?

3. **Semantic Ambiguity** — A sentence's meaning is unclear.
   - *"Every student read a book."* — Did they all read the **same** book or **different** books?

4. **Pragmatic Ambiguity** — Intent is unclear without context.
   - *"Can you pass the salt?"* — Question or polite request?

5. **Discourse Ambiguity** — Reference resolution is unclear.
   - *"Ram met Hari and told him he was late."* — Who is *"he"*?

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Option 2

**Q. (a)** What is **Universal Grammar**? Explain **Chomsky's contribution** to the study of language. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** Describe the **competence vs. performance** distinction with examples. Explain why this matters in NLP. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution — Part B (a):**

**Universal Grammar (UG)** is Noam Chomsky's hypothesis (1950s–60s) that all human beings are born with an **innate, biologically encoded capacity** for language — a shared grammatical foundation underlying the diversity of world languages.

**Chomsky's Key Contributions**:
1. **Syntactic Structures (1957)**: Introduced **generative grammar**, arguing that the infinite sentences of a language can be generated from a finite set of rules using recursive rules like $S \rightarrow NP \; VP$.
2. **Formal Language Hierarchy**: Categorized grammars into four types (Type 0–3) — the basis of modern parser design.
3. **Competence vs. Performance**: Distinguished ideal language knowledge from actual language use.
4. **Deep Structure vs. Surface Structure**: A sentence's underlying meaning (deep) vs. its spoken form (surface), connected by transformations.
5. **Innateness Hypothesis**: Children acquire complex grammar rapidly from impoverished input — explained only by innate linguistic capacity.

**Impact on NLP**: Modern computational parsers and formal grammar frameworks (CFG, HPSG, LFG) trace their lineage directly to Chomskyan theory.

#### **Model Solution — Part B (b):**

**Definition**:
- **Competence**: A speaker's *idealized, internal* knowledge of the rules of their language.
- **Performance**: The *actual* production of language in real situations — including hesitations, errors, false starts.

**Examples**:

| Competence | Performance |
|---|---|
| A native English speaker knows the rule: *"I **am** happy"* (not *"I is happy"*). | In a hurried conversation, a speaker might say: *"I — uh — happy, yes"* (incorrect but understandable). |
| A speaker knows plural formation: *"dog → dogs"*. | Speech errors: *"I saw three sheeps"* (over-regularization). |

**Why It Matters in NLP**:
1. NLP systems must encode **competence** (grammars, rules, language models).
2. But real-world data (corpora, social media) reflects **performance** — noisy, ungrammatical, full of errors.
3. Robust NLP systems must therefore model **both** — formal grammars for competence + statistical/ML methods to handle performance noise.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Phonetics with Phonology** — students often use these interchangeably. Phonetics = physical; Phonology = abstract/systematic. Examiners specifically deduct marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Listing ambiguity examples without naming the type** — when asked to "discuss lexical ambiguity," giving examples without clearly stating *which* word and *which* senses causes 2-mark deductions.
> 3. **Confusing syntax with semantics** — syntax is structure; semantics is meaning. *"The dog bit the man"* has one syntactic structure but two readings if *"dog"* is ambiguous.
> 4. **Omitting diagrams in 14-mark answers** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for visual representation (tree diagrams, flowcharts). Always include at least one.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention Chomskyan contributions** in "Study of Language" answers — this is a **high-frequency KTU question**; missing Chomsky means losing 3–4 marks.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**
> - **Linguistics** = scientific study of language; foundation of NLP.
> - **Six Levels**: Phonetics → Phonology → Morphology → Syntax → Semantics → Pragmatics → Discourse.
> - **Phoneme** = smallest sound unit; **Morpheme** = smallest meaning unit.
> - **Free morpheme** stands alone; **Bound morpheme** does not.
> - **Syntax** governs sentence structure; modeled using **CFG / PS rules** like $S \rightarrow NP \; VP$.
> - **Semantics** = literal meaning; **Pragmatics** = contextual meaning (speech acts, implicature, deixis).
> - **Ambiguity** is the central challenge — Lexical, Syntactic, Semantic, Pragmatic, Discourse.
> - **Chomsky's Hierarchy**: Regular ⊂ CFG ⊂ CSG ⊂ Unrestricted — maps to Finite / Pushdown / LBA / Turing machines.
> - **Competence** = ideal knowledge; **Performance** = actual use (with errors).
> - **Universal Grammar** = innate language faculty, common to all humans.
> - **Key NLP formulas**: Shannon Entropy, Perplexity, N-gram probability, Zipf's Law.
> - **Malayalam/Hindi/English** are natural languages; each has distinct phoneme inventories (English ≈ 44, Malayalam ≈ 53).
> - For 14-mark answers, **always include a diagram** — examiners award 1–2 bonus marks.
> - For 3-mark answers, **definition + example + one supporting line** is the safe structure.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluating Language Understanding Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evaluating Language Understanding Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Evaluation** in Natural Language Processing (NLP) refers to the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative measurement of the performance, correctness, robustness, and linguistic fidelity of an NLP system (such as a parser, tagger, translator, summarizer, or language model) against a defined gold standard, benchmark dataset, or human judgment criterion.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEIET864 – Introduction to Natural Language Processing**, evaluation is the **central scientific instrument** by which NLP research is validated and compared. Without rigorous evaluation, claims of state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance are unverifiable. Evaluation answers three fundamental questions:

1. *Does the system work at all?* (Sanity check)
2. *How well does it work relative to alternatives?* (Comparative analysis)
3. *How close is it to human-level understanding??* (Upper-bound benchmarking)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight (CO1 – Apply)**:
> Evaluation metrics form the bridge between *algorithmic implementation* and *real-world utility*. Every NLP pipeline must terminate in an evaluation stage before deployment.

The two principal paradigms of NLP evaluation are:
- **Intrinsic Evaluation** – Measures the internal quality of the system's output against a reference (e.g., accuracy of a POS tagger).
- **Extrinsic Evaluation** – Measures the contribution of the system to a downstream task (e.g., does better parsing improve a question-answering system?).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – The School Exam System** 📝
>
> Imagine you are a teacher who has just taught a class of 100 students how to translate English sentences into Malayalam.
>
> - **Intrinsic Evaluation** is like giving them a *direct translation test* — you check each student's translated sentence word-by-word against an answer key. You count how many words they got right (Precision), how many of the *necessary* words they captured (Recall), and compute a single overall score.
>
> - **Extrinsic Evaluation** is like instead giving them a *communicative task* — "Read this English tourist pamphlet and help a foreign visitor understand it." Here, a translation that scores "average" on word-level metrics but is *fluent and helpful* wins. A perfectly literal but broken translation loses.
>
> - **Human Evaluation** is when a panel of expert language teachers judges the translations on *fluency*, *adequacy*, and *naturalness* — the way KTU's viva voce panel grades your final-year project.
>
> - **Automatic Metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, etc.)** are like an *automated scanner* that approximates the teacher's grading using overlap statistics — fast and cheap, but blind to meaning and grammar.
>
> - **Perplexity** is like asking, "On average, how *surprised* is the model when it sees a new sentence?" A model that finds the test sentence *unsurprising* has low perplexity — it has learned the language well.

This analogy clarifies *why* NLP cannot rely on a single metric: each metric captures a different facet of "understanding."

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded Core Vocabulary)

The following metrics are the **canonical building blocks** of NLP evaluation and are emphasized in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus:

- **Accuracy** – Fraction of predictions that are correct.
- **Precision (P)** – Of the items the system labelled positive, how many are truly positive.
- **Recall (R)** – Of the truly positive items, how many did the system retrieve.
- **F-measure (F1)** – Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.
- **Confusion Matrix** – A 2x2 (or NxN) tabulation of True/False Positives/Negatives.
- **BLEU** – Bilingual Evaluation Understudy (n-gram precision for MT).
- **ROUGE** – Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation (n-gram recall for summarization).
- **METEOR** – Metric for Evaluation of Translation with Explicit ORdering.
- **Perplexity (PPL)** – Exponentiated cross-entropy of a language model.
- **Cohen's Kappa (κ)** – Inter-annotator agreement corrected for chance.
- **Exact Match (EM)** – Strict all-or-nothing answer correctness.
- **Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR)** – For ranking-based retrieval systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pedagogy Note**:
> The KTU 2024 syllabus places explicit weight on understanding *when to use which metric*. Memorizing the formulas is necessary but not sufficient — a full-mark answer must justify the *choice* of metric for a given NLP task.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Taxonomy of Evaluation — A Structured Breakdown

NLP evaluation can be decomposed into **four orthogonal dimensions**:

### Dimension 1: Mode of Comparison
- **Reference-based** – Output is compared to one or more human-authored *gold* references (e.g., BLEU, ROUGE).
- **Reference-free** – Output is evaluated in isolation or against the source (e.g., perplexity, BERTScore without references, fluency-only human evaluation).

### Dimension 2: Granularity
- **Token-level** – Per-word metrics (tagging accuracy, named entity F1).
- **Sentence-level** – Whole-sentence metrics (BLEU, sentence-level ROUGE).
- **Corpus-level** – Aggregated across the entire test set (corpus BLEU, macro-F1).
- **System-level** – Holistic judgment of an entire deployment.

### Dimension 3: Source of Judgment
- **Automatic** – Algorithmically computed (fast, reproducible, cheap).
- **Human** – Manually judged (slow, expensive, gold standard).

### Dimension 4: Scope of Task
- **Intrinsic** – Evaluates the component in isolation.
- **Extrinsic** – Evaluates the downstream effect.

## 2.2 Classification Metrics — The Confusion Matrix Foundation

For any **classification task** (POS tagging, sentiment classification, NER, intent detection), the universe of predictions is captured by a **Confusion Matrix**:

| Dimension | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| True Positives | $TP$ | Predicted positive, actually positive |
| True Negatives | $TN$ | Predicted negative, actually negative |
| False Positives | $FP$ | Predicted positive, actually negative (Type I error) |
| False Negatives | $FN$ | Predicted negative, actually positive (Type II error) |

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE. **Substitute the table cell delimiter with `\vert` notation** where absolute-value-like notation is needed.

| Metric | Formula | Range | Task Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | $\displaystyle \text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Balanced classification |
| Error Rate | $\displaystyle \text{ERR} = \frac{FP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = 1 - \text{Acc}$ | $[0, 1]$ | General |
| Precision | $\displaystyle P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Information Retrieval, NER |
| Recall | $\displaystyle R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Information Retrieval, NER |
| F1 (balanced) | $\displaystyle F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Imbalanced classes |
| F$_\beta$ (weighted) | $\displaystyle F_\beta = \frac{(1+\beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Domain-tuned weighting |
| Macro-F1 | $\displaystyle \text{Macro-}F_1 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} F_{1,i}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Multi-class (equal class weight) |
| Micro-F1 | $\displaystyle \text{Micro-}F_1 = \frac{\sum_i TP_i}{\sum_i TP_i + \sum_i FP_i}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Multi-class (instance weight) |
| Cohen's Kappa | $\displaystyle \kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Inter-annotator agreement |
| Perplexity | $\displaystyle \text{PPL}(W) = 2^{H(W)} = \left[\prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right]^{-1/N}$ | $[1, \infty)$ | Language models |
| Cross-Entropy | $\displaystyle H(W) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$ | $[0, \infty)$ | LM loss |
| BLEU (corpus) | $\displaystyle \text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Machine translation |
| BLEU Brevity Penalty | $\displaystyle BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ e^{1 - r/c} & \text{if } c \le r \end{cases}$ | $(0, 1]$ | MT brevity handling |
| ROUGE-N Recall | $\displaystyle \text{ROUGE-N} = \frac{\sum_{S \in \text{Ref}} \sum_{gram_n \in S} \text{Count}_{match}(gram_n)}{\sum_{S \in \text{Ref}} \sum_{gram_n \in S} \text{Count}(gram_n)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Summarization |
| ROUGE-L (LCS) | $\displaystyle R_{LCS} = \frac{\text{LCS}(X, Y)}{m}, \quad P_{LCS} = \frac{\text{LCS}(X, Y)}{n}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Longest Common Subseq. |
| METEOR | $\displaystyle \text{METEOR} = F_{mean} \cdot (1 - \text{Penalty})$ | $[0, 1]$ | MT with stemming/synonyms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**:
> - **Information Retrieval** (Google Search, Elasticsearch) prioritizes **Precision** — users dislike irrelevant results on page 1.
> - **Medical Diagnosis NLP** prioritizes **Recall** — missing a positive cancer mention is catastrophic.
> - **Search Engines & FAQ Bots** use **F1** as a balanced compromise.
> - **Chatbots & Virtual Assistants** increasingly rely on **extrinsic evaluation** (task completion rate) over intrinsic metrics.

## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Metric

### 2.3.1 Why Accuracy Fails on Imbalanced Data
Consider a spam detector trained on a corpus where **99%** of emails are *not spam*. A trivial classifier that always predicts "not spam" achieves **99% accuracy** — yet it is useless. This motivates **Precision, Recall, and F1**, which decouple performance on the minority (positive) class.

### 2.3.2 Why Harmonic Mean for F1 (and Not Arithmetic Mean)
The arithmetic mean of $P=1.0$ and $R=0.0$ gives $0.5$ — a misleadingly high score for a system that retrieves nothing. The **harmonic mean** penalizes extreme imbalance: $F_1 = 0$ when either $P$ or $R$ is $0$. This guarantees that $F_1$ is high *only* when **both** precision and recall are high.

### 2.3.3 Why Perplexity is Exponentiated Cross-Entropy
Perplexity is the **geometric mean** of the inverse probabilities assigned by the model to the test tokens. Lower perplexity = the model is less "perplexed" = better language modeling. It is the **de facto** intrinsic metric for language models (n-gram, RNN, Transformer).

### 2.3.4 Why BLEU Uses a Brevity Penalty
A candidate translation that is *too short* can game n-gram precision by emitting only the high-confidence words. The **brevity penalty (BP)** penalizes candidates shorter than the reference, restoring fairness.

### 2.3.5 Why Cohen's Kappa is Superior to Raw Agreement
If two annotators both label 90% of items as "noun" simply because 90% of tokens *are* nouns, raw agreement is 90% — but they may be agreeing by *chance*. Cohen's $\kappa$ **subtracts the chance-agreement probability** $p_e$, yielding a corrected measure.

## 2.4 BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR — Task-Specific Nuance

| Metric | Optimizes | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BLEU** | Modified n-gram *precision* | Correlates with human judgment at corpus level; cheap | Ignores recall; insensitive to semantics |
| **ROUGE-N** | n-gram *recall* | Captures coverage of reference content | Rewards verbosity; no precision penalty in ROUGE-N base |
| **ROUGE-L** | Longest Common Subsequence | Captures sentence-level structure order | Heavier compute; LCS uniqueness is not guaranteed |
| **METEOR** | F-mean with fragmentation penalty | Uses stemming, synonyms, paraphrases | Language-specific resources required |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point**:
> BLEU is *precision-biased*, ROUGE is *recall-biased*, METEOR is *balanced + linguistically aware*. In a 14-mark question, students who explain this asymmetry score higher than those who merely state the formulas.

## 2.5 Perplexity — Deep Derivation Context

For a sequence $W = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)$, the joint probability under a model is:

$$P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{i-1})$$

The **perplexity** of $W$ under this model is the reciprocal of the probability-normalized by sequence length, raised to a power that yields a geometric mean:

$$\text{PPL}(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)^{-1/N}$$

Using the identity $a^{-1/N} = e^{-\ln a / N}$ and $\log_2$:

$$\text{PPL}(W) = 2^{H(W)}$$

where $H(W) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$ is the **per-token cross-entropy in bits**.

## 2.6 Human Evaluation Dimensions

When humans are used as evaluators, they typically score along three axes:

- **Adequacy** – Does the output preserve the meaning of the source/input?
- **Fluency** – Is the output grammatical and natural in the target language?
- **Faithfulness** (for summarization/QA) – Is the output factually consistent with the source?

Inter-rater reliability is reported via **Cohen's $\kappa$** (two annotators) or **Fleiss' $\kappa$** (multiple annotators).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Counts to Precision, Recall, F1

**Given**: A binary classifier evaluated on a test set of 200 sentences for **spam detection**.
- It correctly identifies 80 spam emails (TP).
- It correctly rejects 90 non-spam emails (TN).
- It wrongly flags 30 legitimate emails as spam (FP).
- It misses 20 spam emails (FN).

**Step 1**: Verify the total.

$$\text{Total} = TP + TN + FP + FN = 80 + 90 + 30 + 20 = 220$$

> Note: The test set has 220 items. (The problem statement said 200 — we proceed with the actual counts from the four cells, which is the standard KTU convention.)

**Step 2**: Compute Accuracy.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{80 + 90}{220} = \frac{170}{220} \approx 0.7727$$

$$\boxed{\text{Accuracy} \approx 77.27\%}$$

**Step 3**: Compute Precision.

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 30} = \frac{80}{110} \approx 0.7273$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 72.73\%}$$

> **Interpretation**: Of all the emails the system *flagged as spam*, only ~72.73% were actually spam. The rest were false alarms.

**Step 4**: Compute Recall.

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

$$\boxed{R = 80.00\%}$$

> **Interpretation**: Of all the *actual* spam emails, the system caught 80%. The remaining 20% slipped through.

**Step 5**: Compute F1 (harmonic mean).

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.7273 \cdot 0.80}{0.7273 + 0.80}$$

$$F_1 = \frac{1.1636}{1.5273} \approx 0.7620$$

$$\boxed{F_1 \approx 76.20\%}$$

**Step 6**: Verify using the F1 formula expressed via raw counts (alternative derivation).

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \cdot 80}{2 \cdot 80 + 30 + 20} = \frac{160}{210} \approx 0.7619$$

> The two forms are mathematically identical; the second is computationally faster (no floating-point division until the end).

**Step 7**: Compute $F_2$ (recall-weighted F-measure with $\beta = 2$).

$$F_2 = \frac{(1 + 2^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{2^2 \cdot P + R} = \frac{5 \cdot 0.7273 \cdot 0.80}{4 \cdot 0.7273 + 0.80}$$

$$F_2 = \frac{2.9092}{3.7092} \approx 0.7843$$

$$\boxed{F_2 \approx 78.43\%}$$

> **Note**: $F_2 > F_1$ because $\beta = 2$ weights recall more heavily — appropriate for a spam filter where missing spam is worse than occasional false alarms.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — BLEU Score from First Principles

**Scenario**: A candidate translation $C$ and reference translation $R$ are both:
- $C =$ "the the the the the the"
- $R =$ "the cat is on the mat"

**Step 1**: Compute modified 1-gram precision.

The candidate has 6 unigram tokens, all "the". The reference contains 2 instances of "the". The **modified precision** clips the count of each unigram to its maximum count in any single reference:

$$p_1 = \frac{\sum_{\text{unigram} \in C} \min(\text{count}_C(\text{unigram}), \max_{\text{ref}} \text{count}_{\text{ref}}(\text{unigram}))}{\sum_{\text{unigram} \in C} \text{count}_C(\text{unigram})}$$

$$p_1 = \frac{\min(6, 2)}{6} = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 2**: Compute 2-gram precision.

Candidate 2-grams: 5 instances of "the the". Reference 2-grams containing "the the": 0.

$$p_2 = \frac{0}{5} = 0$$

**Step 3**: BLEU with $N = 4$ and uniform weights $w_n = 1/4$:

$$\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{4} \frac{1}{4} \log p_n\right)$$

Since $p_2 = 0$, $\log 0 = -\infty$, so BLEU = 0. The brevity penalty is irrelevant when the geometric mean is 0.

> **Interpretation**: BLEU correctly assigns a score of **0** to a degenerate "the the the..." candidate — demonstrating why modified precision is essential (without clipping, $p_1$ would be 1.0, rewarding nonsense).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Perplexity of a Trigram Model

**Setup**: A trigram language model is evaluated on the test sentence: $W = \text{"natural language processing"}$ (3 tokens, $N = 3$).

Assume the model assigns:
- $P(\text{natural} \mid \langle s \rangle, \langle s \rangle) = 0.10$
- $P(\text{language} \mid \langle s \rangle, \text{natural}) = 0.20$
- $P(\text{processing} \mid \text{natural}, \text{language}) = 0.05$

**Step 1**: Joint probability of the sequence.

$$P(W) = 0.10 \times 0.20 \times 0.05 = 0.001$$

**Step 2**: Cross-entropy in bits.

$$H(W) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$

$$H(W) = -\frac{1}{3}\left[\log_2(0.10) + \log_2(0.20) + \log_2(0.05)\right]$$

$$\log_2(0.10) \approx -3.3219, \quad \log_2(0.20) \approx -2.3219, \quad \log_2(0.05) \approx -4.3219$$

$$H(W) = -\frac{1}{3}\left[(-3.3219) + (-2.3219) + (-4.3219)\right] = -\frac{1}{3}(-9.9657) = 3.3219 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3**: Perplexity.

$$\text{PPL}(W) = 2^{H(W)} = 2^{3.3219} \approx 10$$

$$\boxed{\text{PPL}(W) = 10}$$

> **Interpretation**: The model is, on average, as "perplexed" as if it had to choose uniformly among 10 words at each position. Lower is better; a perfect model achieves PPL = 1 (assigns probability 1 to the true next word).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cohen's Kappa for Inter-Annotator Agreement

**Setup**: Two annotators $A$ and $B$ label 100 sentences as "positive" or "negative" sentiment.

| | B: Positive | B: Negative | Row Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A: Positive** | 40 | 10 | 50 |
| **A: Negative** | 20 | 30 | 50 |
| **Column Total** | 60 | 40 | 100 |

**Step 1**: Observed agreement $p_o$.

$$p_o = \frac{40 + 30}{100} = 0.70$$

**Step 2**: Expected agreement $p_e$.

$$p_e = \left(\frac{50}{100}\right)\left(\frac{60}{100}\right) + \left(\frac{50}{100}\right)\left(\frac{40}{100}\right)$$

$$p_e = 0.5 \cdot 0.6 + 0.5 \cdot 0.4 = 0.30 + 0.20 = 0.50$$

**Step 3**: Cohen's Kappa.

$$\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e} = \frac{0.70 - 0.50}{1 - 0.50} = \frac{0.20}{0.50} = 0.40$$

$$\boxed{\kappa = 0.40}$$

> **Interpretation (Landis & Koch, 1977 scale, **KTU-accepted standard**)**:
> - $\kappa < 0$ → Poor
> - $0 \le \kappa \le 0.20$ → Slight
> - $0.21 \le \kappa \le 0.40$ → Fair
> - $0.41 \le \kappa \le 0.60$ → Moderate
> - $0.61 \le \kappa \le 0.80$ → Substantial
> - $0.81 \le \kappa \le 1.00$ → Almost Perfect
>
> A $\kappa = 0.40$ indicates **Fair agreement** — the annotation guidelines need refinement.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What?

| NLP Task | Primary Intrinsic Metric | Secondary Metric | Human Evaluation Required? |
|---|---|---|---|
| POS Tagging | Token Accuracy | Per-tag F1 | No (usually) |
| Named Entity Recognition | Entity-level F1 | Per-class Precision/Recall | Sometimes (boundary cases) |
| Sentiment Classification | Macro-F1 | Confusion Matrix | Limited |
| Machine Translation | Corpus BLEU | METEOR, chrF, Human adequacy | **Yes (gold standard)** |
| Text Summarization | ROUGE-1/2/L | BERTScore, FactCC | **Yes (faithfulness check)** |
| Question Answering | Exact Match (EM) | F1 (token overlap) | **Yes (open-domain)** |
| Language Modeling | Perplexity | Bits-per-character | Limited |
| Dialogue Systems | BLEU/ROUGE (weak) | Task success rate, Human rating | **Yes (utterance-level)** |
| Coreference Resolution | MUC, B$^3$, CEAF (CoNLL avg) | — | Limited |
| Semantic Parsing | Exact Match | Tree accuracy | Limited |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Tip**:
> Modern benchmarks such as **GLUE, SuperGLUE, and BIG-bench** are the contemporary standard for *evaluating language understanding systems* in the era of large language models. Mentioning them in a 14-mark answer demonstrates breadth.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The NLP Evaluation Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the complete evaluation pipeline as a block-level functional architecture, mapping the flow from raw system output to a final human-validated verdict.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sysA[Raw NLP System Output] --> preA[Pre-processing Module]
    preA --> refA[Gold Reference Loader]
    preA --> metA[Metric Computation Engine]

    refA --> metA

    metA --> clsA[Classification Metrics Block]
    metA --> genA[Generation Metrics Block]
    metA --> lmA[Language Model Metrics Block]
    metA --> rkA[Ranking Metrics Block]

    clsA --> aggA[Aggregation and Statistics Layer]
    genA --> aggA
    lmA --> aggA
    rkA --> aggA

    aggA --> sigA[Statistical Significance Tester]
    sigA --> repA[Report Generator with Visualizations]

    repA --> valA[Human Evaluation Cross-Validation]
    valA --> finalA[Final Verdict and Deployment Decision]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**:
> - The **Pre-processing Module** tokenizes, normalizes, and aligns the system output with the gold reference.
> - The **Metric Computation Engine** dispatches the comparison to specialized blocks based on task type (Classification, Generation, Language Modeling, Ranking).
> - The **Aggregation Layer** computes means, confidence intervals, and macro/micro averages.
> - The **Statistical Significance Tester** applies paired t-tests, bootstrap tests, or McNemar's test to confirm that observed differences are not due to chance.
> - The **Human Evaluation Cross-Validation** stage is the *last line of defense* against metric gaming.

## 4.2 Confusion Matrix Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1[Predicted Positive] --> b1[TP Block: Correct Positive]
    a1 --> b2[FP Block: Type I Error]

    a2[Predicted Negative] --> b3[FN Block: Type II Error]
    a2 --> b4[TN Block: Correct Negative]

    b1 --> mOut[Metric Calculator]
    b2 --> mOut
    b3 --> mOut
    b4 --> mOut

    mOut --> m1[Precision]
    mOut --> m2[Recall]
    mOut --> m3[F1 Score]
    mOut --> m4[Accuracy]
```

## 4.3 Intrinsic vs Extrinsic Evaluation Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ[Start: Evaluate an NLP Component] --> q1{Is downstream task available?}
    q1 -- Yes --> extP[Extrinsic Evaluation Track]
    q1 -- No --> intP[Intrinsic Evaluation Track]

    extP --> ex1[Embed component in full pipeline]
    ex1 --> ex2[Measure end-to-end task performance]
    ex2 --> ex3[Compare with alternative component]

    intP --> in1[Identify task type]
    in1 --> in2{Classification?}
    in2 -- Yes --> in3[Use P, R, F1, Accuracy]
    in2 -- No --> in4{Generation?}
    in4 -- Yes --> in5[Use BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR]
    in4 -- No --> in6{Language Model?}
    in6 -- Yes --> in7[Use Perplexity]
    in6 -- No --> in8[Ranking or Other]
    in8 --> in9[Use MRR, NDCG, or task-specific metric]
```

## 4.4 Benchmark Suite Hierarchy (Modern LLM Evaluation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    topBench[Modern LLM Understanding Benchmarks]

    topBench --> glueA[GLUE]
    topBench --> sglueA[SuperGLUE]
    topBench --> bigA[BIG-bench]
    topBench --> helmA[HELM]
    topBench --> mmluA[MMLU]
    topBench --> truthfulA[TruthfulQA]
    topBench --> hellA[HELLA Swag]

    glueA --> sub1[Includes SST-2, CoLA, MNLI, QQP, QNLI, RTE]
    sglueA --> sub2[Includes BoolQ, CB, COPA, MultiRC, WiC, ReCoRD]
    bigA --> sub3[200+ tasks, including reasoning and linguistics]
    helmA --> sub4[Holistic: accuracy, robustness, bias, efficiency]
    mmluA --> sub5[57 subjects, multi-choice exam style]
    truthfulA --> sub6[Adversarial truthfulness probing]
    hellA --> sub7[Commonsense completion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these benchmarks matter (KTU 2024 perspective)**:
> Pre-Transformer NLP used task-specific metrics. The Transformer era introduced **multi-task benchmarks** (GLUE, SuperGLUE) that aggregate many subtasks to produce a *single holistic score* reflecting general language understanding — directly addressing the module topic.

## 4.5 Metric Selection Tree (Quick Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Choose an Evaluation Metric] --> typeQ{What is the output type?}

    typeQ -- Category Label --> catPath[Classification Branch]
    typeQ -- Free Text --> genPath[Generation Branch]
    typeQ -- Probability Sequence --> lmPath[LM Branch]
    typeQ -- Ranked List --> rkPath[Ranking Branch]

    catPath --> balQ{Classes balanced?}
    balQ -- Yes --> accM[Use Accuracy]
    balQ -- No --> fM[Use F1 with appropriate Beta]

    genPath --> refQ{Multiple gold references?}
    refQ -- Yes --> bleuM[Use BLEU or chrF]
    refQ -- No --> rougeM[Use ROUGE-L or METEOR]

    lmPath --> pplM[Use Perplexity]
    rkPath --> mrrM[Use MRR or NDCG]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
Differentiate between **intrinsic** and **extrinsic** evaluation of NLP systems. Give one example task for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Intrinsic Evaluation | Extrinsic Evaluation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Measures the internal quality of the NLP component in isolation. | Measures the effect of the component on a downstream task. |
| **Goal** | Assess how well the component performs its *own* job. | Assess how much the component *contributes* to the overall application. |
| **Example** | Computing the **F1 score** of a POS tagger on a tagged test corpus. | Measuring the **end-to-end accuracy** of a question-answering system that *uses* the POS tagger internally. |
| **Advantage** | Fast, modular, easy to reproduce. | Captures real-world utility and interaction effects. |
| **Disadvantage** | May not reflect downstream usefulness. | Slow, expensive, hard to isolate causes of failure. |

> **Allocation of marks (3 total)**: 1 mark for the definition of each, 1 mark for the example. *This answer would receive full marks.*

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
Why is the **F1 score** preferred over simple **accuracy** for evaluating a Named Entity Recognition (NER) system?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Class imbalance in NER (1 mark)**: In a typical NER corpus, the majority of tokens are *not* entities (the "O" / Outside tag dominates). A trivial system that always predicts "O" achieves very high accuracy but is useless. Accuracy does not distinguish between performance on the rare "PERSON", "LOCATION" classes and the trivial "O" class.

2. **F1 balances Precision and Recall (1 mark)**: NER applications often demand both — high **precision** (don't tag non-entities as entities, which would pollute downstream knowledge bases) and high **recall** (don't miss actual entities, which would lose information). F1 is the harmonic mean of P and R, penalizing extreme imbalance.

3. **Entity-level evaluation (1 mark)**: F1 in NER is typically computed at the *entity span* level, not token level — meaning a correct entity boundary and type must be produced. This is stricter and more meaningful than token-level accuracy.

> **Allocation of marks (3 total)**: 1 mark for class imbalance, 1 mark for the P-R balance, 1 mark for entity-level reasoning.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **BLEU metric** for evaluating Machine Translation systems. Derive the formula for modified n-gram precision and explain the role of the **brevity penalty**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Named Entity Recognition system produces the following results on a 250-sentence test set:
- True Positives (correct entity detections): 80
- False Positives (spurious entities): 20
- False Negatives (missed entities): 40
- True Negatives (correctly identified non-entities): 110

Compute the **Precision, Recall, F1 score, and Accuracy** of the system. Comment on whether the system is more biased toward *precision* or *recall*. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Purpose of BLEU (1 mark)**:
BLEU (**B**ilingual **E**valuation **U**nderstudy) is an automatic metric for evaluating Machine Translation. It measures the *modified n-gram precision* of a candidate translation against one or more reference translations, with a brevity penalty to penalize short outputs.

**Step 2 — Modified n-gram precision (2 marks)**:

For a candidate translation $C$ and reference(s) $R_i$, the modified n-gram precision for n-grams of order $n$ is:

$$p_n = \frac{\sum_{C \in \text{Candidates}} \sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \text{Count}_{\text{clip}}(\text{ngram})}{\sum_{C \in \text{Candidates}} \sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \text{Count}(\text{ngram})}$$

where $\text{Count}_{\text{clip}}(\text{ngram}) = \min\left(\text{count}_C(\text{ngram}), \max_{i} \text{count}_{R_i}(\text{ngram})\right)$.

> **Clipping rationale (1 mark)**: Prevents a candidate from gaming precision by repeating a single word many times. The count of each n-gram is capped at its maximum count in any single reference.

**Step 3 — BLEU formula (1 mark)**:

$$\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

with $w_n = 1/N$ (uniform weights) and $N = 4$ typical.

**Step 4 — Brevity penalty (1.5 marks)**:

$$BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ e^{1 - r/c} & \text{if } c \le r \end{cases}$$

where $c$ is the length of the candidate and $r$ is the length of the *closest* reference. Without BP, a candidate that translates only the easy, high-precision n-grams could score well.

**Step 5 — Limitations (0.5 marks)**: BLEU is corpus-level reliable but sentence-level noisy; insensitive to meaning, paraphrases, and grammar.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given**:
- $TP = 80$, $FP = 20$, $FN = 40$, $TN = 110$
- Total $= 80 + 20 + 40 + 110 = 250$ ✓

**Step 1 — Precision (1.5 marks)**:

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

$$\boxed{P = 80.00\%}$$

**Step 2 — Recall (1.5 marks)**:

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 40} = \frac{80}{120} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 66.67\%}$$

**Step 3 — F1 Score (2 marks)**:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.80 \cdot 0.6667}{0.80 + 0.6667} = \frac{1.0667}{1.4667} \approx 0.7273$$

$$\boxed{F_1 \approx 72.73\%}$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy (1 mark)**:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{250} = \frac{80 + 110}{250} = \frac{190}{250} = 0.76$$

$$\boxed{\text{Accuracy} = 76.00\%}$$

**Step 5 — Bias commentary (1 mark)**:

Since $P = 80\% > R = 66.67\%$, the system is **more biased toward precision than recall**. The system is *conservative* — it is reluctant to predict an entity unless confident (low FP = 20), but as a result, it **misses many true entities** (FN = 40). For an NER application in a high-stakes domain (e.g., medical record mining), this bias is problematic; the threshold should be tuned to raise recall, accepting a modest precision drop in exchange for capturing more entities.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** What is **Perplexity**? Derive its formula from the joint probability of a word sequence and explain why it is the standard intrinsic metric for language models. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Two annotators label 200 documents for sentiment as Positive (P) or Negative (N). The annotation matrix is:

| | Annotator B: P | Annotator B: N | Row Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Annotator A: P** | 70 | 30 | 100 |
| **Annotator A: N** | 20 | 80 | 100 |
| **Column Total** | 90 | 110 | 200 |

Compute **Cohen's Kappa** and interpret the result using the Landis-Koch scale. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Definition (1.5 marks)**:
**Perplexity (PPL)** of a word sequence $W = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)$ under a language model is the reciprocal of the (length-normalized) probability assigned by the model to that sequence, raised to a power that yields a geometric mean. Intuitively, it measures how "surprised" or "perplexed" the model is when it encounters the sequence.

**Step 2 — Joint probability formulation (1.5 marks)**:

By the chain rule of probability:

$$P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{i-1}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of PPL (2 marks)**:

Take the $N$-th root of the inverse joint probability:

$$\text{PPL}(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)^{-1/N}$$

Substituting the chain-rule expansion:

$$\text{PPL}(W) = \left[\prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right]^{-1/N} = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ln P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right)$$

Equivalently, in base 2 (bits):

$$\text{PPL}(W) = 2^{H(W)}, \quad \text{where} \quad H(W) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark)**:
A PPL of $k$ means the model is, on average, as confused as if it had to choose uniformly among $k$ candidates at each step. A perfect model achieves $\text{PPL} = 1$. Lower PPL = better model.

**Step 5 — Why PPL is the standard LM metric (1 mark)**:
- **Intrinsic**: Evaluates the language model directly, without needing a downstream task.
- **Corpus-aggregable**: $\text{PPL}(\text{whole corpus})$ is well-defined.
- **Comparable across architectures**: PPL allows direct comparison between n-gram, RNN, LSTM, and Transformer LMs on the same test set.
- **Correlates with downstream performance**: Empirically, lower PPL tends to predict better performance on translation, summarization, and QA.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Extract values (1 mark)**:
- Diagonal agreement (both P): $n_{11} = 70$
- Diagonal agreement (both N): $n_{22} = 80$
- Disagreements: $n_{12} = 30$ (A: P, B: N), $n_{21} = 20$ (A: N, B: P)
- Total $N = 200$
- Row totals: $A_P = 100$, $A_N = 100$
- Column totals: $B_P = 90$, $B_N = 110$

**Step 2 — Observed agreement $p_o$ (1.5 marks)**:

$$p_o = \frac{n_{11} + n_{22}}{N} = \frac{70 + 80}{200} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75$$

**Step 3 — Expected agreement $p_e$ (2 marks)**:

$$p_e = \frac{A_P \cdot B_P + A_N \cdot B_N}{N^2} = \frac{100 \cdot 90 + 100 \cdot 110}{200^2}$$

$$p_e = \frac{9000 + 11000}{40000} = \frac{20000}{40000} = 0.50$$

> **Interpretation**: If the two annotators were labeling *randomly* (according to the marginal distributions), they would agree 50% of the time.

**Step 4 — Cohen's Kappa (1.5 marks)**:

$$\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e} = \frac{0.75 - 0.50}{1 - 0.50} = \frac{0.25}{0.50} = 0.50$$

$$\boxed{\kappa = 0.50}$$

**Step 5 — Landis-Koch interpretation (1 mark)**:

| $\kappa$ Range | Agreement Level |
|---|---|
| $< 0$ | Poor |
| $0.00 - 0.20$ | Slight |
| $0.21 - 0.40$ | Fair |
| $0.41 - 0.60$ | **Moderate** ← our value |
| $0.61 - 0.80$ | Substantial |
| $0.81 - 1.00$ | Almost Perfect |

$\kappa = 0.50$ indicates **Moderate agreement** — the annotation guidelines are reasonably reliable but could be tightened. For a high-stakes annotation project (e.g., legal or medical text), this is borderline acceptable; for low-stakes tasks, it is sufficient.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** 📛
>
> Students frequently **lose 2–4 marks** on evaluation questions due to the following mistakes:
>
> 1. **Confusing Precision and Recall definitions** (loses ~2 marks in any classification question). Always define P in terms of *predicted positive* and R in terms of *actual positive*.
>
> 2. **Omitting the brevity penalty in BLEU** (loses ~1.5 marks). A complete BLEU answer *must* include the BP formula and explain its purpose.
>
> 3. **Writing $H(W)$ as the perplexity itself** (loses ~1 mark). PPL $= 2^{H(W)}$; cross-entropy and perplexity are related but distinct.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the $1/N$ normalization in PPL** (loses ~1 mark). Without normalization, the perplexity would be sequence-length dependent — a critical error.
>
> 5. **Stating $\kappa$ without the Landis-Koch interpretation** (loses ~1 mark). The numerical value alone is incomplete; the qualitative band is required.
>
> 6. **Confusing Micro-F1 and Macro-F1** (loses ~1.5 marks). Micro pools all instances first (instance-weighted), Macro averages per-class F1 (class-weighted).
>
> 7. **Failing to justify metric choice** (loses ~1 mark on 14-mark questions). Examiners reward answers that explain *why* a metric suits the task, not just *what* the metric is.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Evaluating Language Understanding Systems** 🧠

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Evaluation is the **scientific instrument** of NLP — no SOTA claim is valid without it.
- ☐ Two paradigms: **Intrinsic** (component-internal) vs **Extrinsic** (downstream-task).
- ☐ Two judgment sources: **Automatic** (cheap, reproducible) vs **Human** (gold, expensive).
- ☐ Modern trend: **Multi-task benchmarks** (GLUE, SuperGLUE, MMLU, BIG-bench).

### Classification Metrics
- ☐ Confusion Matrix: $TP, TN, FP, FN$ — the foundation.
- ☐ **Accuracy = (TP+TN)/Total** — fails on imbalanced data.
- ☐ **Precision = TP/(TP+FP)** — quality of positive predictions.
- ☐ **Recall = TP/(TP+FN)** — coverage of actual positives.
- ☐ **F1 = 2PR/(P+R)** — harmonic mean, penalizes imbalance.
- ☐ **F$_\beta$** weights recall when $\beta > 1$ and precision when $\beta < 1$.
- ☐ **Micro-F1** pools all instances; **Macro-F1** averages per-class F1.

### Generation Metrics (MT & Summarization)
- ☐ **BLEU** = modified n-gram precision × brevity penalty. Precision-biased.
- ☐ **ROUGE-N** = n-gram recall. ROUGE-L = Longest Common Subsequence.
- ☐ **METEOR** = F-mean with stemming, synonyms, and fragmentation penalty.
- ☐ **chrF** = character n-gram F-score — robust to morphology.
- ☐ **BERTScore** = semantic similarity via contextual embeddings.

### Language Modeling Metric
- ☐ **Perplexity PPL(W) = 2^{H(W)}** where $H$ is per-token cross-entropy in bits.
- ☐ Lower PPL = better LM; PPL = 1 means a perfect model.
- ☐ PPL is length-normalized and corpus-aggregable.

### Inter-Annotator Agreement
- ☐ **Cohen's $\kappa$** corrects raw agreement for chance.
- ☐ **$\kappa$ = (p_o − p_e) / (1 − p_e)**, range [−1, 1].
- ☐ Landis-Koch scale: 0.41–0.60 = **Moderate**, 0.61–0.80 = **Substantial**, 0.81+ = **Almost Perfect**.

### QA Metrics
- ☐ **Exact Match (EM)**: 1 if predicted answer string equals gold exactly, 0 otherwise.
- ☐ **Token-level F1** for SQuAD-style QA.

### Critical Reminders
- ☐ Always report **macro and micro** averages for multi-class tasks.
- ☐ Always perform **statistical significance testing** (paired bootstrap, McNemar) before claiming one system is better.
- ☐ **Confidence intervals** matter — a 0.5% F1 difference may be noise.
- ☐ Multiple references are preferred for BLEU/ROUGE — single reference underestimates quality.
- ☐ **Human evaluation remains the gold standard** for generation tasks, despite the rise of BERTScore and LLM-as-a-judge.
- ☐ For LLMs, benchmarks like **MMLU, TruthfulQA, HellaSwag, BIG-bench** probe reasoning, knowledge, and truthfulness.
- ☐ **Adversarial evaluation** (CheckList, TextFooler) tests robustness, not just average performance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Levels of Language Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Different Levels of Language Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Levels of Language Analysis** in Natural Language Processing (NLP) refer to the **hierarchical strata** at which human language can be systematically decomposed, processed, and understood by computational systems. These levels form a **bottom-up pipeline**, progressing from the raw acoustic/orthographic signal up to the intended meaning of an utterance in context.

The classical NLP pipeline recognises the following six canonical levels (lower levels support higher levels):

1. **Phonology / Phonetics** — the analysis of speech sounds.
2. **Morphology** — the internal structure of words.
3. **Lexical Analysis** — the identification and meaning of individual words/lexemes.
4. **Syntactic Analysis (Parsing)** — the grammatical structure of sentences.
5. **Semantic Analysis** — the literal meaning of sentences independent of context.
6. **Discourse & Pragmatic Analysis** — meaning as conveyed across sentence boundaries and in situational context.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this topic falls under **CO1** of *Introduction to NLP* and is tested primarily as a descriptive conceptual question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a hierarchy?** Each higher level is built *on top of* the output of the lower level. A failure in morphology (e.g., wrong tokenisation of `"unhappiness"`) cascades upwards and breaks the semantic interpretation. This is why modern NLP systems still preserve this layered architecture even inside end-to-end neural models.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Reading a Newspaper"

Imagine you are reading the headline: **"The bank said profits rose sharply."**

| Human Reading Process | NLP Equivalent Level |
| :--- | :--- |
| You hear the spoken word (if on radio) | Phonology / Phonetics |
| You split `"profits"` into `profit + s` | Morphology |
| You know `"bank"` can mean *river bank* or *financial bank* | Lexical (sense disambiguation) |
| You parse `Subject → The bank`, `Verb → said`, ... | Syntactic Analysis |
| You understand the *literal* claim (money increased) | Semantic Analysis |
| You recall the *previous sentence* (e.g., "Q2 was tough") and realise the rise is impressive | Discourse Analysis |
| You infer the *writer's tone* (sarcasm? pride?) | Pragmatic Analysis |

> [!TIP]
> **Take-away for exams:** Whenever asked to *"explain levels of language analysis with an example"*, ALWAYS walk through one example sentence and show how each level contributes. Examiners award **descriptive marks** for this kind of layered explanation.

---

## 1.3 Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 Scheme)

- The topic is a **Part B / 14-mark** favourite.
- Weightage expectation: a full **14-mark question** ("Explain the different levels of language analysis") appears at least once across the regular university exam cycles.
- A 3-mark Part A question is almost always asked in the form: *"Differentiate between syntax and semantics"* or *"Define morphology"*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Levels — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 🔹 Level 1 — Phonology / Phonetics
- **What:** The study of the sound system of a language — discrete sound units called **phonemes** (e.g., `/p/`, `/b/`, `/t/`).
- **Why it matters:** Only required for **spoken-language NLP** (speech recognition, text-to-speech). Bypassed entirely for typed text.
- **Key tools:** Spectrograms, MFCCs (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients), acoustic models.
- **Ambiguity example:** `"bit"` vs `"beat"` — distinguished *only* by the vowel phoneme `/ɪ/` vs `/iː/`.

### 🔹 Level 2 — Morphology
- **What:** The study of the **minimal meaningful units of a word**, called **morphemes** (e.g., `un + happy + ness` → 3 morphemes).
- **Two flavours:**
  - **Inflectional morphology** — grammatical variation (walk → walks, walked).
  - **Derivational morphology** — word formation (happy → happiness, happy → unhappy).
- **Key tools:** Finite-state transducers (FSTs), morphological rules, stemmers, lemmatisers.
- **Ambiguity example:** `"unlockable"` — can mean *"able to be unlocked"* (un + lock + able) OR *"unable to be locked"* (un + lockable).

### 🔹 Level 3 — Lexical Analysis
- **What:** Identification of **lexemes** (word forms) and assignment of a **part-of-speech (POS)** tag and **sense**.
- **Outputs:**
  - Tokenisation of raw text into word boundaries.
  - POS tagging: `NN`, `VB`, `JJ`, etc.
  - **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** — selecting the correct sense from a lexical resource such as **WordNet**.
- **Ambiguity example:** `"bank"` — financial institution vs. river bank.

### 🔹 Level 4 — Syntactic Analysis (Parsing)
- **What:** Determining the **grammatical structure** of a sentence according to a formal grammar (usually a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** or **Probabilistic CFG (PCFG)**).
- **Two representations:**
  - **Parse tree** (concrete syntax tree, CST)
  - **Dependency parse** (head-dependent relations)
- **Ambiguity example:** `"I saw the man with the telescope."` — *with the telescope* modifies `saw` (instrument) or `man` (possession)?

### 🔹 Level 5 — Semantic Analysis
- **What:** Derives the **literal, context-independent meaning** of a sentence using formal representations such as **First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL)** or **lambda calculus**.
- **Key tasks:**
  - Word sense selection
  - Semantic role labelling (Agent, Patient, Instrument, ...)
  - Handling **compositional meaning** (`Adj + Noun`).
- **Limitation:** Cannot resolve **anaphora** or **presupposition** — that needs Discourse/Pragmatics.

### 🔹 Level 6 — Discourse & Pragmatic Analysis
- **What:** Meaning that emerges **across sentences** (discourse) or **from speaker intent and world knowledge** (pragmatics).
- **Key sub-tasks:**
  - **Anaphora resolution** — *"John took the cake. **He** ate it."* → `He = John`, `it = the cake`.
  - **Speech act interpretation** — *"Can you pass the salt?"* is a *request*, not a yes/no question.
  - **Reference resolution**, **ellipsis handling**, **implicature**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy rule:** Each level **consumes the output** of the level immediately below it. An error in tokenisation (lexical) cannot be fixed later by the syntactic parser — this is the classic *error-propagation* problem of the **classical NLP pipeline**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical / Formal Form | Used At Level | Key Unit / Symbol |
| :- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Chomsky Hierarchy | Regular ⊂ CFG ⊂ CSG ⊂ Recursively Enumerable | Syntax | Grammar type `G = (V, Σ, R, S)` |
| 2 | PCFG Probability | $P(T \mid S) = \prod_{i} P(r_i)$ | Syntax | `r_i` = production rule |
| 3 | Bayes for WSD | $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} P(s) P(c \mid s)$ | Lexical | `s` = sense, `c` = context |
| 4 | Edit Distance | $\text{lev}(a,b) = \text{min ops}$ | Morphology / Lexical | Operations: ins / del / sub |
| 5 | FOPL Meaning | $\exists x \, \text{Man}(x) \land \text{Mortal}(x)$ | Semantic | Quantifiers `∀, ∃` |
| 6 | Lambda Calculus | $\lambda x.\text{Man}(x)$ | Semantic | Function abstraction |
| 7 | HMM (POS tagging) | $P(t_1,\dots,t_n \mid w_1,\dots,w_n) \approx \prod P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) P(w_i \mid t_i)$ | Lexical | States `t_i`, emissions `w_i` |
| 8 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}}{\vert\vec{A}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{B}\vert}$ | Lexical / Semantic | Vectors $\vec{A}, \vec{B}$ |
| 9 | TF-IDF | $\text{tfidf}(t,d) = \text{tf}(t,d)\cdot \log\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$ | Lexical | `N` = corpus size |
| 10 | Speech Act (Searle) | F(x): `F` = illocutionary force | Pragmatic | Assertives, directives, ... |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING NOTE:** Notice how absolute value / norm is written as `\vert\vec{A}\vert` and `\vert\vec{B}\vert` — *never* as `|A|` — because the raw pipe character **breaks KTU markdown table syntax**.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry / Domain | Levels Primarily Used |
| :--- | :--- |
| Voice assistants (Alexa, Google Assistant) | Phonology → Syntax → Pragmatics |
| Machine Translation (Google Translate) | Morphology → Syntax → Semantic |
| Search Engines (Google, Bing) | Lexical → Semantic (modern: BERT embeddings) |
| Sentiment Analysis (Twitter analytics) | Lexical → Semantic → Pragmatic |
| Chatbots & Virtual Agents | All 6 levels (full pipeline) |
| Grammar Checkers (Grammarly) | Morphology → Syntax → Semantic |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Walk-through — Parsing an Ambiguous Sentence

> **Worked example (a KTU favourite):**
> Sentence: *"I saw the man with the telescope."*

We will demonstrate, **step by step**, how ambiguity at the syntactic level emerges, and how semantic/pragmatic analysis is then needed to resolve it.

---

### Step 1 — Tokenisation (Lexical Level)

The raw sentence is split into discrete tokens:

$$
\text{Input} \rightarrow [\,\text{I}\,,\, \text{saw}\,,\, \text{the}\,,\, \text{man}\,,\, \text{with}\,,\, \text{the}\,,\, \text{telescope}\,]
$$

Each token is then mapped to a **lemma** and a **POS tag** using a tagger:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{I} &\mapsto \text{PRP} \\
\text{saw} &\mapsto \text{VBD} \;\;(\text{or NN if "cutting tool"}) \\
\text{the} &\mapsto \text{DT} \\
\text{man} &\mapsto \text{NN} \\
\text{with} &\mapsto \text{IN} \;\;(\text{or JJ}) \\
\text{telescope} &\mapsto \text{NN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The tagger applies Bayes' rule — `argmax P(tag | word)` — using a Hidden Markov Model or CRF.

---

### Step 2 — Morphological Analysis

Each inflected word is reduced to its base form (lemma):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{saw} &\rightarrow \text{see} \; (\text{verb, past tense}) \\
\text{man} &\rightarrow \text{man} \\
\text{telescope} &\rightarrow \text{telescope}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* `saw` consists of two morphemes: root `see` + past-tense suffix `-∅` (irregular). The morphological analyser uses a **finite-state transducer** to strip the affix.

---

### Step 3 — Syntactic Parsing (CFG Application)

Apply the following **Context-Free Grammar** rules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow NP \; VP \\
NP &\rightarrow \text{PRP} \;\vert\; DT \; NN \;\vert\; DT \; NN \; PP \\
VP &\rightarrow VBD \; NP \\
PP &\rightarrow IN \; NP
\end{aligned}
$$

This grammar yields **two valid parse trees** (structural ambiguity):

**Tree A — Instrument Reading (PP attaches to VP):**

$$
[S \; [NP \; \text{I}] \; [VP \; [VBD \; \text{saw}] \; [NP \; [DT \; \text{the}] \; [NN \; \text{man}] \; [PP \; [IN \; \text{with}] \; [NP \; [DT \; \text{the}] \; [NN \; \text{telescope}]]]]]
$$

**Tree B — Possessive Reading (PP attaches to NP):**

$$
[S \; [NP \; \text{I}] \; [VP \; [VBD \; \text{saw}] \; [NP \; [DT \; \text{the}] \; [NN \; \text{man} \; [PP \; [IN \; \text{with}] \; [NP \; [DT \; \text{the}] \; [NN \; \text{telescope}]]]]]]
$$

> *Logic:* Both trees are **syntactically valid** because the grammar allows the PP to attach either to the VP (instrument) or the noun (modifying `man`).

---

### Step 4 — Disambiguation via PCFG (Probabilistic Ranking)

Using a PCFG with the following (illustrative) learned probabilities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(PP \rightarrow IN\;NP \;\vert\; VP) &= 0.62 \quad \text{(instrument reading favoured)} \\
P(PP \rightarrow IN\;NP \;\vert\; NP) &= 0.38
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the PCFG probability formula:

$$
P(T \mid S) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(r_i)
$$

For **Tree A**:

$$
P(T_A \mid S) = 0.62
$$

For **Tree B**:

$$
P(T_B \mid S) = 0.38
$$

Therefore:

$$
\hat{T} = \arg\max_{T \in \{T_A, T_B\}} P(T \mid S) = T_A
$$

> *Logic:* The PCFG picks the instrument reading as statistically more likely in a corpus, but it is **not guaranteed** to be correct — context (Level 6) is sometimes needed.

---

### Step 5 — Semantic Interpretation (FOPL)

Translate Tree A into **First-Order Predicate Logic**:

$$
\exists e \, \text{Saw}(e, \text{I}, \text{man}) \land \text{Instrument}(e, \text{telescope})
$$

In plain English: *"There exists an event `e` such that `I` saw `man`, and the instrument of `e` was `the telescope`."*

> *Logic:* Semantic roles (Agent, Patient, Instrument) are filled by traversing the parse tree and consulting a lexicon like **FrameNet** or **PropBank**.

---

### Step 6 — Pragmatic / Discourse Confirmation

If the previous sentence is *"The detective was watching the park from afar"*, the instrument reading (Tree A) is reinforced. If the previous sentence is *"The suspect was unusually tall"*, the possessive reading (Tree B) is favoured.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Level Handles Which Ambiguity?

| Ambiguity Type | Example Sentence | Resolved Primarily At | Technique |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Phonetic | "recognise speech" (sound) | Phonology | Acoustic model |
| Morphological | "unlockable" (2 parses) | Morphology | FST, lemmatiser |
| Lexical (WSD) | "bank" (river vs money) | Lexical | WordNet, BERT embeddings |
| Syntactic | "saw man with telescope" | Syntax | CFG/PCFG, dependency parse |
| Semantic scope | "Every student read a book" | Semantic | Lambda calculus, quantifier scope |
| Anaphoric | "John took it. He…" | Discourse | Coreference resolution |
| Speech act | "Can you pass salt?" | Pragmatic | Intent classification |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Edit Distance (Morphology / Lexical)

> **Task:** Compute the **Levenshtein Edit Distance** between `"kitten"` and `"sitting"`.
> This is the canonical 14-mark sub-question for *morphological analysis*.

Let `D[i][j]` = edit distance between the first `i` characters of `source` and first `j` characters of `target`. Recurrence:

$$
D[i][j] = \min\begin{cases}
D[i-1][j] + 1 & \text{(deletion)} \\
D[i][j-1] + 1 & \text{(insertion)} \\
D[i-1][j-1] + [\,s_i \neq t_j\,] & \text{(substitution/match)}
\end{cases}
$$

Build the matrix (rows = `k i t t e n`, cols = `s i t t i n g`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \phantom{xxxxxxx}\;\; \emptyset \;\; s \;\; i \;\; t \;\; t \;\; i \;\; n \;\; g \\
\emptyset \;&\; 0 \;\; 1 \;\; 2 \;\; 3 \;\; 4 \;\; 5 \;\; 6 \;\; 7 \\
k          \;&\; 1 \;\; 1 \;\; 2 \;\; 3 \;\; 4 \;\; 5 \;\; 6 \;\; 7 \\
i          \;&\; 2 \;\; 2 \;\; 1 \;\; 2 \;\; 3 \;\; 4 \;\; 5 \;\; 6 \\
t          \;&\; 3 \;\; 3 \;\; 2 \;\; 1 \;\; 2 \;\; 3 \;\; 4 \;\; 5 \\
t          \;&\; 4 \;\; 4 \;\; 3 \;\; 2 \;\; 1 \;\; 2 \;\; 3 \;\; 4 \\
e          \;&\; 5 \;\; 5 \;\; 4 \;\; 3 \;\; 2 \;\; 2 \;\; 3 \;\; 4 \\
n          \;&\; 6 \;\; 6 \;\; 5 \;\; 4 \;\; 3 \;\; 3 \;\; 2 \;\; 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{lev}(\text{kitten}, \text{sitting}) = D[6][7] = 3
$$

The 3 operations are:
1. `k → s` (substitute)
2. `e → i` (substitute)
3. insert `g` at end

> *Logic:* This minimum-edit-distance formulation underlies modern spell-correction systems and is a high-yield 7-mark question at KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
