---

## 4.2 Diagram: Forward and Backward Propagation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input x] --> B[Initialize weights W and bias b]
    B --> C{Forward Pass}
    C --> D[Compute z = Wx + b]
    D --> E[Apply activation a = sigma z]
    E --> F[Compute prediction y hat]
    F --> G[Compute Loss L]
    G --> H{Backward Pass}
    H --> I[Compute gradient dL dW]
    I --> J[Update weights W = W minus alpha dL dW]
    J --> K{Converged?}
    K -- No --> C
    K -- Yes --> L[Final Trained Model]
```

**Process explanation:**
1. **Forward Pass:** Input $\mathbf{x}$ is transformed layer-by-layer to produce a prediction $\hat{y}$.
2. **Loss Computation:** The prediction is compared with the ground truth to compute $\mathcal{L}$.
3. **Backward Pass:** Gradients are computed from the loss back to every weight using the chain rule.
4. **Parameter Update:** Weights are nudged in the direction that reduces loss.
5. **Iteration:** Steps 1–4 are repeated for many epochs until convergence.

---

## 4.3 Diagram: Deep Learning for NLP — End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Raw Text Corpus] --> P2[Tokenization]
    P2 --> P3[Vocabulary Building]
    P3 --> P4[Word Embedding Layer]
    P4 --> P5[Deep Neural Network]
    P5 --> P6[Output Predictions]
    P6 --> P7[Evaluation and Deployment]

    P5A[MLP for Classification] -.-> P5
    P5B[CNN for Text] -.-> P5
    P5C[RNN LSTM for Sequences] -.-> P5
    P5D[Transformer for Context] -.-> P5
```

**Pipeline stages explained:**

| Stage | Operation | Deep Learning Component |
|-------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **1. Raw Text** | Input corpus of documents | None (data preparation) |
| **2. Tokenization** | Split text into words/subwords | Rule-based + statistics |
| **3. Vocabulary** | Build word-to-index mapping | Hash table / BPE tokenizer |
| **4. Embedding** | Convert tokens to dense vectors | **Embedding layer** (learned) |
| **5. Deep Network** | Process embeddings hierarchically | MLP, CNN, RNN, or Transformer |
| **6. Output** | Produce task-specific predictions | Softmax / Sigmoid layer |
| **7. Evaluation** | Measure performance on test set | Accuracy, F1, BLEU, Perplexity |

---

## 4.4 Diagram: Vanishing Gradient Problem in Deep Networks

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Layer 0 Input] --> L1[Layer 1]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Output Loss]

    G1[Gradient at Layer 1 approx 0.001] -.-> L1
    G2[Gradient at Layer 2 approx 0.0001] -.-> L2
    G3[Gradient at Layer 3 approx 0.00001] -.-> L3
    G4[Gradient at Layer 4 approx 0.000001] -.-> L4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** When using **Sigmoid** or **Tanh** activations in deep networks, the gradient **shrinks exponentially** as it propagates backward. This means early layers learn **extremely slowly** (or not at all), a phenomenon called the **vanishing gradient problem**.

**Solutions in modern deep learning:**
- Use **ReLU** (derivative is 1 for positive inputs, preventing gradient shrinkage).
- Use **Batch Normalization** to stabilize layer inputs.
- Use **Residual connections** (skip connections) as in ResNet and Transformers.
- Use **LSTM/GRU** cells for sequential data (gating mechanisms preserve gradient flow).

---

## 4.5 Diagram: Gradient Descent Optimization Landscape

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Start at random point W0 on loss surface] --> B[Compute gradient direction]
    B --> C[Move W in negative gradient direction]
    C --> D{At local minimum?}
    D -- No --> B
    D -- Yes --> E[Convergence achieved]

    A -.-> A1[High loss region]
    D -.-> D1[Low loss region - minimum]
```

**Optimization in practice:**
- **Convex landscapes** (e.g., linear regression): A single global minimum exists.
- **Non-convex landscapes** (deep networks): Many local minima, but experience shows most are "good enough."
- **Saddle points** are more common than local minima in high-dimensional loss surfaces.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**Define Deep Learning. List any three advantages of deep learning over traditional machine learning in NLP applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Deep Learning is a branch of machine learning that uses artificial neural networks with multiple hidden layers to automatically learn hierarchical feature representations from data, without requiring manual feature engineering.

**Advantages (2 marks — any three of the following, each carrying 0.67 marks):**

1. **Automatic Feature Extraction:** Eliminates the need for hand-crafted features like n-grams, POS tags, or parse trees.
2. **Hierarchical Representation Learning:** Captures low-level patterns (characters) to high-level abstractions (semantic meaning) across layers.
3. **End-to-End Learning:** A single unified model is trained from raw input to final output, reducing pipeline complexity.
4. **Scalability with Data:** Performance continues to improve as more training data becomes available.
5. **Superior Performance on Complex Tasks:** State-of-the-art results in machine translation, question answering, and text generation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the vanishing gradient problem. How does the ReLU activation function help mitigate it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Vanishing Gradient Problem (1.5 marks):**
The vanishing gradient problem occurs during backpropagation in deep neural networks when gradients of the loss function become extremely small (close to zero) as they are propagated from the output layer to the input layer. This causes the weights in early layers to update very slowly, effectively preventing the network from learning meaningful representations. It is especially severe with Sigmoid and Tanh activations because their derivatives are bounded (maximum 0.25 for Sigmoid, 1.0 for Tanh) and the repeated multiplication across layers causes exponential decay.

**ReLU Solution (1.5 marks):**
The ReLU activation function $\text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0, x)$ has a derivative of exactly **1 for all positive inputs** and **0 for negative inputs**. Because the derivative does not saturate for positive values, gradients can flow backward through deep networks without being multiplied by small values at every layer, thereby preserving their magnitude and enabling effective learning in early layers.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the forward propagation equations for a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) with one hidden layer. Clearly define all symbols used. Explain the role of the activation function with a suitable example.**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture Description (1 mark):**
Consider an MLP with:
- Input layer: $n_x$ neurons
- Hidden layer: $n_h$ neurons with activation $\sigma_h$
- Output layer: $n_y$ neurons with activation $\sigma_y$

**Forward Propagation Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1: Hidden Layer Pre-activation**

The pre-activation vector $\mathbf{z}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h}$ at the hidden layer is computed as the affine transformation of the input:

$$\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$$

where $\mathbf{W}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h \times n_x}$ is the weight matrix, $\mathbf{b}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h}$ is the bias vector, and $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_x}$ is the input.

**Step 2: Hidden Layer Activation**

The activation vector is obtained by applying the non-linear activation function element-wise:

$$\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \sigma_h(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})$$

**Step 3: Output Layer Pre-activation**

$$\mathbf{z}^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$$

**Step 4: Output Layer Activation (Prediction)**

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \sigma_y(\mathbf{z}^{(2)})$$

**Role of Activation Function (2 marks):**
The activation function introduces **non-linearity** into the network. Without it, stacking multiple layers would be equivalent to a single linear transformation, regardless of depth. For example, a network with ReLU activation can learn the XOR function, which a single linear perceptron cannot. ReLU also helps mitigate the vanishing gradient problem by maintaining a constant gradient of 1 for positive inputs.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the backpropagation algorithm in detail. Derive the gradient of the loss with respect to the weights of the output layer for a binary classification task using Sigmoid output and Binary Cross-Entropy loss.**

**Model Solution:**

**Backpropagation Algorithm Explanation (3 marks):**

Backpropagation is a two-phase algorithm for efficiently computing gradients in a neural network:

**Phase 1 — Forward Pass:** Input is propagated through the network to compute the output prediction $\hat{y}$ and the loss $\mathcal{L}$.

**Phase 2 — Backward Pass:** Starting from the loss, the chain rule of calculus is applied recursively to compute the gradient of the loss with respect to every weight in the network. The gradient flows backward from output to input.

**Why Backpropagation? (0.5 marks):** It avoids redundant computation by reusing intermediate results, making gradient computation tractable for deep networks with millions of parameters.

**Derivation of Output Layer Gradient (4 marks):**

Given:
- Output pre-activation: $z^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)} + b^{(2)}$
- Output prediction: $\hat{y} = \sigma(z^{(2)}) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z^{(2)}}}$
- True label: $y \in \{0, 1\}$
- Loss: $\mathcal{L} = -[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$

**Step 1: Gradient of Loss w.r.t. $\hat{y}$**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} = -\frac{y}{\hat{y}} + \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{y}} = \frac{\hat{y} - y}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})}$$

**Step 2: Gradient of $\hat{y}$ w.r.t. $z^{(2)}$**

Using $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$$

**Step 3: Combine using Chain Rule**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = \frac{\hat{y} - y}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})} \cdot \hat{y}(1-\hat{y}) = \hat{y} - y$$

This elegant simplification is a special property of the **BCE + Sigmoid** combination.

**Step 4: Gradient w.r.t. Output Weights**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} = (\hat{y} - y) \mathbf{a}^{(1)}$$

**Step 5: Update Rule**

$$\mathbf{W}^{(2)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(2)} - \alpha (\hat{y} - y) (\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^T$$

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the various activation functions used in deep learning. Compare Sigmoid, Tanh, and ReLU in terms of their mathematical formulation, output range, and suitability for hidden layers of deep networks.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Activation functions introduce non-linearity into neural networks, enabling them to learn complex, non-linear mappings from inputs to outputs.

**Mathematical Formulations and Analysis (4 marks):**

| Function | Formula | Range | Derivative | Hidden Layer Suitability |
|----------|---------|-------|------------|--------------------------|
| **Sigmoid** | $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | $\sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$ | Poor (vanishing gradient) |
| **Tanh** | $\tanh(x) = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $1 - \tanh^2(x)$ | Moderate (zero-centered) |
| **ReLU** | $\text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0, x)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $0$ if $x<0$, $1$ if $x \geq 0$ | Excellent (no saturation for $x>0$) |

**Detailed Comparison (2 marks):**

- **Sigmoid** squashes inputs to $(0,1)$, but its maximum derivative is only $0.25$. In deep networks, repeated multiplication by $0.25$ causes gradients to vanish exponentially, making early layers train very slowly. Additionally, its output is not zero-centered, leading to zig-zagging gradient updates.

- **Tanh** is similar to Sigmoid but zero-centered with range $(-1, 1)$. It performs better than Sigmoid in hidden layers because its gradient is stronger (peak derivative of $1.0$) and the zero-centered output helps optimization. However, it still saturates for large positive/negative inputs.

- **ReLU** has become the default choice for hidden layers. It does not saturate for positive inputs (derivative is exactly $1$), which prevents vanishing gradients. It is computationally very cheap (just a max operation). Its main drawbacks are the "dying ReLU" problem (neurons stuck at zero) and the fact that it is not differentiable at $x=0$, though this is handled in practice by subgradient methods.

**Conclusion:** ReLU is the recommended default for hidden layers in modern deep networks, with Leaky ReLU or PReLU used when the dying ReLU problem is observed.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Adam optimization algorithm. How does it improve upon vanilla gradient descent and SGD with momentum? Mention any three hyperparameters of Adam and their typical values.**

**Model Solution:**

**Vanilla Gradient Descent Limitation (1.5 marks):**
Vanilla gradient descent uses a single learning rate $\alpha$ for all parameters and updates as:
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$$
It has three issues: (1) slow convergence on non-spherical loss surfaces, (2) oscillation in steep directions, and (3) equal treatment of all parameters regardless of gradient magnitude or frequency.

**SGD with Momentum (1.5 marks):**
Momentum adds a velocity term to accelerate convergence:
$$v_t = \beta v_{t-1} + \alpha \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$$
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_t$$
This helps navigate ravines and reduces oscillation but still uses a single learning rate for all parameters.

**Adam Algorithm Explanation (3 marks):**
Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation) maintains two exponentially decaying moving averages for each parameter:
- First moment (mean of gradients): $m_t = \beta_1 m_{t-1} + (1-\beta_1) \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$
- Second moment (uncentered variance of gradients): $v_t = \beta_2 v_{t-1} + (1-\beta_2)(\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L})^2$

**Bias correction** is applied to counteract initialization bias:
$$\hat{m}_t = \frac{m_t}{1-\beta_1^t}, \quad \hat{v}_t = \frac{v_t}{1-\beta_2^t}$$

**Update rule:**
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \frac{\hat{m}_t}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_t} + \epsilon}$$

**Why Adam is Superior (1 mark):**
Adam combines the benefits of momentum (smooth updates using first moment) with **per-parameter adaptive learning rates** (using second moment). Parameters with large gradients get smaller effective learning rates, while parameters with small gradients get larger ones. This leads to faster, more stable convergence.

**Three Hyperparameters (1 mark — 0.33 each):**
1. **Learning rate** ($\alpha$): typically $10^{-3}$ (default).
2. **$\beta_1$** (decay rate for first moment): typically $0.9$.
3. **$\beta_2$** (decay rate for second moment): typically $0.999$.
4. **$\epsilon$** (numerical stability term): typically $10^{-8}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing Sigmoid with Softmax:** Sigmoid is for **binary** classification (single output), while Softmax is for **multi-class** classification (mutually exclusive classes, outputs sum to 1). Mixing these up costs full marks on the relevant sub-question.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Bias Term:** When asked to write the forward propagation equation, students often write only $\mathbf{W}\mathbf{x}$ and forget $+\mathbf{b}$. The bias term is **mandatory** and carries a full mark in KTU papers.
>
> 3. **Using Linear Activations in Hidden Layers:** Writing $\sigma(x) = x$ (identity) in hidden layers is a critical error because it collapses the network to a single linear transformation, defeating the entire purpose of depth.
>
> 4. **Incorrect Cross-Entropy Sign:** The cross-entropy loss has a **negative** sign in front. Forgetting this is a frequent and heavily penalized error.
>
> 5. **Writing ReLU Derivative Incorrectly:** The derivative of ReLU is **0 for $x < 0$** and **1 for $x \geq 0$**. Writing it as a constant value (like 1 everywhere) is wrong.
>
> 6. **Not Stating the Vanishing Gradient Mitigation:** When discussing ReLU, students often forget to mention that its derivative is **1 for positive inputs**, which is the key reason it mitigates vanishing gradients.
>
> 7. **Confusing Epoch, Batch, and Iteration:** A clear KTU favorite for 1-mark sub-questions. **Iteration** = one update step. **Batch** = samples per update. **Epoch** = one pass through the entire training set.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Deep Learning in NLP**

### Core Concepts (Must Memorize)

- [ ] Deep learning uses **multi-layer neural networks** to learn hierarchical representations.
- [ ] A **perceptron** computes $y = \sigma(\sum_i w_i x_i + b)$.
- [ ] **MLP** = input layer + one or more hidden layers + output layer, fully connected.
- [ ] Forward propagation: $\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}^{(l)}\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)})$.
- [ ] Backpropagation uses the **chain rule** to compute gradients layer by layer.
- [ ] The combination **Softmax + CCE** simplifies the gradient to $\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$.
- [ ] The combination **Sigmoid + BCE** simplifies the gradient to $\hat{y} - y$.

### Activation Functions

- [ ] **Sigmoid** → Binary output, suffers from vanishing gradient.
- [ ] **Tanh** → Zero-centered, slightly better than Sigmoid.
- [ ] **ReLU** → Default for hidden layers, mitigates vanishing gradient.
- [ ] **Softmax** → Multi-class output, probabilities sum to 1.
- [ ] Always use **non-linear** activations in hidden layers.

### Loss Functions

- [ ] **MSE** → Regression tasks.
- [ ] **Binary Cross-Entropy** → Binary classification.
- [ ] **Categorical Cross-Entropy** → Multi-class classification (with Softmax).

### Optimization

- [ ] **SGD** updates one sample at a time.
- [ ] **Mini-Batch GD** uses small batches (e.g., 32 or 64).
- [ ] **Momentum** adds a velocity term for faster convergence.
- [ ] **Adam** is the default modern optimizer; combines momentum with adaptive learning rates.
- [ ] The update rule: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$.

### Common Problems & Solutions

- [ ] **Vanishing Gradient** → Use ReLU, BatchNorm, or Residual connections.
- [ ] **Exploding Gradient** → Use gradient clipping.
- [ ] **Overfitting** → Use Dropout, L2 regularization, or early stopping.
- [ ] **Dying ReLU** → Use Leaky ReLU or PReLU.

### NLP-Specific Deep Learning

- [ ] Deep learning powers **machine translation, sentiment analysis, NER, and LLMs**.
- [ ] **Word embeddings** (Word2Vec, GloVe) are learned dense vector representations of words.
- [ ] **RNNs/LSTMs** handle sequential data; **Transformers** handle long-range dependencies via attention.
- [ ] **Perplexity** is the standard evaluation metric for language models.

### Key Numbers to Remember

- [ ] ReLU derivative: **1** for $x \geq 0$, **0** for $x < 0$.
- [ ] Sigmoid derivative: $\sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$, max value **0.25**.
- [ ] Softmax outputs sum to **1**.
- [ ] Adam default learning rate: $\alpha = 10^{-3}$.
- [ ] Adam default $\beta_1 = 0.9$, $\beta_2 = 0.999$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: and NLP: An Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Language and NLP: An Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Language

In the context of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and computational linguistics, **Language** is formally defined as a structured, rule-governed system of arbitrary vocal, written, or signed symbols used by human beings to communicate meaning, intent, emotion, and information. It operates at multiple interacting levels — **phonology, morphology, syntax, semantics, pragmatics, and discourse** — and exhibits the two core properties of *productivity* (ability to produce infinitely many new sentences from a finite lexicon) and *displacement* (ability to refer to events removed in time or space).

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Natural Language Processing (NLP)

**Natural Language Processing (NLP)** is the sub-field of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Computational Linguistics concerned with the theory and computational implementation of enabling machines to **understand, interpret, generate, and respond to human language** in a valuable, contextually appropriate manner. It bridges the gap between *human communication* and *machine representation of knowledge*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Universal Translator

Imagine you are a person who only understands **Binary (0s and 1s)**, but your friend only speaks **Malayalam**. To communicate, you need a *Universal Translator* who can:
- **Listen** to Malayalam (Input/Understanding)
- **Decode** the meaning (Analysis)
- **Translate** it into Binary (Representation)
- **Construct** a reply in Malayalam (Generation)

NLP acts as exactly this *Universal Translator* between **humans** and **computers**. While humans think in fuzzy, contextual, emotional language, computers need precise, mathematical instructions. NLP provides the bi-directional pipeline (NLU + NLG) to make this dialogue possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **NLU (Natural Language Understanding)** = Machine reading/listening (Input side)
> **NLG (Natural Language Generation)** = Machine writing/speaking (Output side)
> **NLP = NLU + NLG**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The very first Module lays the conceptual foundation — students are expected to clearly differentiate between *language* (a cognitive-linguistic phenomenon), *linguistics* (the scientific study of language), and *NLP* (the computational engineering of language).

### 1.4 Why Study NLP? — The Industrial Motivation

NLP is no longer an academic curiosity. It underpins **Search Engines (Google BERT)**, **Virtual Assistants (Alexa, Siri)**, **Machine Translation (Google Translate)**, **Sentiment Analysis (Brand Monitoring)**, **Healthcare (Clinical Text Mining)**, **Legal Tech (Contract Analysis)**, and **Code Generation (GitHub Copilot)**. The global NLP market is one of the fastest-growing segments in AI, making it a *high-yield* skill for KTU graduates entering the software industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Wing Architecture of NLP

Modern NLP theory recognizes two complementary wings:

**A. Natural Language Understanding (NLU)** — *Decoding Wing*
Goal: Convert unstructured, ambiguous human text/speech into a **structured, machine-readable formal representation** (parse trees, logical forms, semantic frames, embeddings).

Steps involved:
1. **Tokenization** — Splitting text into atomic units (words/sub-words).
2. **Morphological Analysis** — Identifying roots, prefixes, suffixes.
3. **POS Tagging** — Labelling each token as Noun, Verb, Adjective, etc.
4. **Syntactic Parsing** — Building a grammar tree.
5. **Semantic Analysis** — Extracting meaning (entities, relations).
6. **Pragmatic / Discourse Analysis** — Resolving context, anaphora, intent.

**B. Natural Language Generation (NLG)** — *Encoding Wing*
Goal: Convert structured data into coherent, fluent, human-readable natural language text.
Classical pipeline (Reiter & Dale, 2000): **Content Determination → Document Planning → Microplanning → Surface Realization → Text.**

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Layer

| Layer | The Core Question it Answers | Why Computers Struggle |
|---|---|---|
| Phonology | How are sounds organized? | Accents, ASR noise |
| Morphology | What are the smallest meaning units? | Inflection, compounding |
| Syntax | How are words combined legally? | Long-distance dependencies |
| Semantics | What does the sentence *mean*? | Polysemy, metaphor |
| Pragmatics | What did the speaker *intend*? | Sarcasm, implicature |
| Discourse | How do sentences cohere? | Anaphora, coherence |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Definition / Equation | Boundary / Constraint | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Productivity of Language | $\text{Sentences} = \text{finite lexicon}^{\infty}$ | Finite vocabulary → infinite sentences | Motivates statistical/neural models over rule-based ones |
| 2 | Shannon Entropy of a Language | $H(L) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(w_i) \log_2 p(w_i)$ | $0 \le H(L) \le \log_2 \vert V \vert$ bits/symbol | Information-theoretic bound on compression & perplexity |
| 3 | Perplexity (PPL) of LM | $\text{PPL}(W) = 2^{H(W)} = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid w_{<i})} \right)^{1/N}$ | Lower is better; $\text{PPL} \ge 1$ | Evaluates language models |
| 4 | Zipf's Law (Word Frequency) | $f(r) \approx \frac{C}{r^{\alpha}}, \quad \alpha \approx 1$ | Rank-frequency relationship | Guides vocabulary pruning & stop-word design |
| 5 | BLEU Score (Generation) | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | $\text{BP} = \min\!\left(1, e^{1-r/c}\right)$ | Evaluates MT/Generation quality |
| 6 | Ambiguity Ratio | $A = \frac{\text{Number of valid parses}}{\text{Number of sentences}}$ | $A \gg 1$ for natural languages | Quantifies NLP difficulty |
| 7 | Chomsky Hierarchy of Formal Languages | $\text{Regular} \subset \text{CFG} \subset \text{CSG} \subset \text{Recursively Enumerable}$ | Strict subset chain | Selects parser type for grammar |
| 8 | F1 Score (Information Retrieval) | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $0 \le F_1 \le 1$ | Evaluates tagging, NER, parsing |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Formula Sheet questions usually target **Zipf's Law, Perplexity, BLEU, and F1-score**. Memorize both the equation and the *interpretation* (higher/lower is better).

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of NLP Theory

The hierarchy in §2.3 directly drives **industrial system design**. For instance, *finite-state transducers* handle morphology, *CYK / Earley parsers* handle context-free syntax, and *transformer architectures* approximate the semantic + pragmatic layers jointly. Every modern NLP system you will study in higher semesters maps back to one of these levels.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Decoding "I saw the man with the telescope."

This sentence is the **classic KTU illustration** of *syntactic ambiguity*. We will decode it step-by-step across all linguistic levels.

**Input String:** `I saw the man with the telescope.`

#### Step 1 — Phonological Level (Spoken form)
The acoustic signal is digitized into a spectrogram. We do not analyze this in detail for text NLP, but the *lexical stress* on "saw" (past tense vs. cutting tool) and "man" (vs. *Mann*) would already start disambiguating.

#### Step 2 — Tokenization & Morphological Analysis
The string is split into morphemes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sentence} &\rightarrow [I] \, [saw] \, [the] \, [man] \, [with] \, [the] \, [telescope] \, [.] \\[4pt]
\text{Morphemes} &\rightarrow \text{I} + \text{saw} + \text{the} + \text{man} + \text{with} + \text{the} + \text{tele} + \text{scope} + \text{.}
\end{aligned}
$$

- *tele + scope* = Greek roots meaning *far + instrument* → compound noun.
- *saw* = root "see" + past-tense morpheme *-ed* (spelled irregularly as *-w*).
- *with* = preposition (function word).

#### Step 3 — POS Tagging
Using a statistical tagger (e.g., HMM or CRF):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{I/PRP} \quad &\text{saw/VBD} \quad \text{the/DT} \quad \text{man/NN} \quad \text{with/IN} \quad \text{the/DT} \quad \text{telescope/NN} \quad \text{./.}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where **PRP = Personal Pronoun, VBD = Verb Past Tense, DT = Determiner, NN = Noun Singular, IN = Preposition**.

#### Step 4 — Syntactic Parsing (The Ambiguity Emerges)
At the syntax level, the prepositional phrase **(PP) "with the telescope"** can attach at *two valid attachment points*, producing two distinct parse trees. Let **NP = Noun Phrase, VP = Verb Phrase, PP = Prepositional Phrase**.

**Parse Tree 1 — Instrument Attachment (Low/Right):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\text{S} &\rightarrow \text{NP}_1 \, \text{VP}_1] \\[2pt]
\text{NP}_1 &\rightarrow \text{I} \\[2pt]
\text{VP}_1 &\rightarrow \text{VBD} \, \text{NP}_2 \, \text{PP}_1 \\[2pt]
\text{NP}_2 &\rightarrow \text{DT} \, \text{NN} \\[2pt]
\text{PP}_1 &\rightarrow \text{IN} \, \text{NP}_3 \\[2pt]
\text{NP}_3 &\rightarrow \text{DT} \, \text{NN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Meaning:** "I used the telescope to see the man." → **Telescope = instrument of *seeing*.**

**Parse Tree 2 — Modifier Attachment (High/Left):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\text{S} &\rightarrow \text{NP}_1 \, \text{VP}_1] \\[2pt]
\text{NP}_1 &\rightarrow \text{I} \\[2pt]
\text{VP}_1 &\rightarrow \text{VBD} \, \text{NP}_2 \\[2pt]
\text{NP}_2 &\rightarrow \text{DT} \, \text{NN} \, \text{PP}_1 \\[2pt]
\text{PP}_1 &\rightarrow \text{IN} \, \text{NP}_3 \\[2pt]
\text{NP}_3 &\rightarrow \text{DT} \, \text{NN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Meaning:** "I saw the man who was holding the telescope." → **Telescope = property of the *man*.**

#### Step 5 — Semantic Role Labelling (SRL)
For each parse, roles are assigned:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parse 1: Agent} &= \text{I}, \quad \text{Verb} = \text{saw (perception)}, \quad \text{Theme} = \text{man}, \quad \text{Instrument} = \text{telescope} \\[2pt]
\text{Parse 2: Agent} &= \text{I}, \quad \text{Verb} = \text{saw (perception)}, \quad \text{Theme} = \text{man[with telescope]}, \quad \text{Instrument} = \varnothing
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Pragmatic / Discourse Resolution
World knowledge (Pragmatics) and anaphora resolution are applied. If the *previous discourse* is about astronomy, **Parse 1** is more probable. If the *previous discourse* is about a group of tourists, **Parse 2** wins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Pedagogical Takeaway:** A single string produces **two semantically distinct but syntactically valid interpretations**. This *structural ambiguity* is the central engineering challenge that motivates statistical and neural NLP — pure rule-based grammar cannot *rank* the parses; it merely enumerates them.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Rule-Based, Statistical & Neural NLP

| Dimension | Rule-Based (1950s–1990s) | Statistical (1990s–2010s) | Neural / LLM Era (2013–Present) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Knowledge Source | Hand-crafted linguistic rules | Annotated corpora, n-gram counts | Self-supervised on raw text |
| Core Algorithm | CFG, DCG, FST | HMM, CRF, SVM, MEMM | RNN, LSTM, Transformer |
| Strength | Interpretable, linguistically pure | Data-driven, robust | Contextual, scalable |
| Weakness | Brittle, knowledge bottleneck | Feature engineering bottleneck | Black-box, data-hungry |
| Example System | ELIZA, SHRDLU | Stanford NER, Moses MT | BERT, GPT, Llama |
| KTU Exam Weight | Moderate (theoretical) | Moderate (mathematical) | High (current industry) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Architecture of an NLP System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Human Text Input] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Tokenization]
    C --> D[Morphological Analysis]
    D --> E[POS Tagging]
    E --> F[Syntactic Parsing]
    F --> G[Semantic Analysis]
    G --> H[Pragmatic Context]
    H --> I[Structured Representation]
    I --> J[Application Layer]
    J --> K1[Sentiment Analysis]
    J --> K2[Machine Translation]
    J --> K3[Question Answering]
    J --> K4[Chatbot Response]
    K4 --> L[NLG Pipeline]
    L --> M[Human Readable Output]
```

### 4.2 The NLP Pipeline — Stages and Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Raw Text] --> P2[Sentence Segmentation]
    P2 --> P3[Word Tokenization]
    P3 --> P4[Morphological Stemming]
    P4 --> P5[POS Tagging]
    P5 --> P6[Chunking]
    P6 --> P7[Parsing Tree]
    P7 --> P8[Semantic Frames]
    P8 --> P9[Discourse Coherence]
    P9 --> P10[Final Meaning Representation]
```

### 4.3 NLU vs NLG — Two Wings of NLP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_SIDE
        H1[Human Natural Language] --> N1[Lexical Analysis] --> N2[Syntactic Analysis] --> N3[Semantic Analysis] --> N4[Pragmatic Analysis]
    end
    N4 --> SR[Structured Knowledge Base]
    SR --> G1[Content Planning] --> G2[Sentence Planning] --> G3[Surface Realization] --> H2[Generated Natural Language]
```

### 4.4 Levels of Language Analysis (Lattice View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Phonology - Sound] --> L2[Morphology - Word Structure] --> L3[Syntax - Sentence Structure] --> L4[Semantics - Literal Meaning] --> L5[Pragmatics - Intended Meaning] --> L6[Discourse - Multi-sentence Coherence]
    L1 -.- A1[Examples: allophones, intonation]
    L2 -.- A2[Examples: un + do + ing]
    L3 -.- A3[Examples: S -> NP VP rules]
    L4 -.- A4[Examples: synonymy, entailment]
    L5 -.- A5[Examples: sarcasm, politeness]
    L6 -.- A6[Examples: anaphora resolution]
```

### 4.5 Ambiguity Taxonomy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AMB[Ambiguity in NLP] --> AMB1[Lexical Ambiguity]
    AMB --> AMB2[Syntactic Ambiguity]
    AMB --> AMB3[Semantic Ambiguity]
    AMB --> AMB4[Pragmatic Ambiguity]
    AMB1 --> AMB1A[Polysemy: bank river vs money]
    AMB1 --> AMB1B[Homonymy: bat animal vs cricket bat]
    AMB2 --> AMB2A[PP Attachment: with telescope]
    AMB2 --> AMB2B[Coordination Scope]
    AMB3 --> AMB3A[Quantifier Scope]
    AMB3 --> AMB3B[Metaphor]
    AMB4 --> AMB4A[Sarcasm]
    AMB4 --> AMB4B[Speech Acts]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Natural Language Processing. List any four major applications of NLP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Natural Language Processing (NLP) is the sub-field of Artificial Intelligence and Linguistics concerned with enabling computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language in a meaningful and useful way. It combines computational linguistics with statistical, machine-learning, and deep-learning models.
- **Any Four Applications (1.5 marks):**
  1. **Machine Translation** (e.g., Google Translate — English to Malayalam)
  2. **Sentiment Analysis** (e.g., product review classification)
  3. **Virtual Assistants** (e.g., Alexa, Google Assistant)
  4. **Spam Detection** in e-mails
  5. **Chatbots & Question Answering** systems
  6. **Named Entity Recognition** in legal/medical documents

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Explain the term "ambiguity" in NLP. Differentiate between lexical and syntactic ambiguity with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Ambiguity (1 mark):** Ambiguity is the property of a language expression (word, phrase, or sentence) having **more than one valid interpretation**. It is the central challenge of NLP because machines must pick the *correct* interpretation based on context.
- **Lexical Ambiguity (1 mark):** A *single word* has multiple meanings.
  *Example:* "**Bank**" → river bank / financial bank / to bank on something.
- **Syntactic Ambiguity (1 mark):** A *single sentence* has multiple valid grammatical structures.
  *Example:* "I saw the man **with the telescope**." → Telescope used as instrument OR held by the man.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Module Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Rule:** Answer **any ONE full question** from the choice provided. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically carrying **7 marks each**.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the different levels of language analysis in NLP. Illustrate with a suitable example sentence, showing the analysis at each level.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The six canonical levels of language analysis are:

1. **Phonology / Phonetics (1 mark):** Studies the sound system of language. Concerned with phonemes, intonation, stress. *Example:* The word "record" changes meaning based on stress — RE-cord (noun) vs. re-CORD (verb).

2. **Morphology (1 mark):** Studies the internal structure of words — roots, prefixes, suffixes. *Example:* "**un** + **believe** + **able**" — three morphemes forming one adjective.

3. **Syntax (1.5 marks):** Studies the rules governing word combination into phrases and sentences. *Example:* "The cat sat on the mat" follows the rule S → NP VP, where NP = The cat and VP = sat on the mat.

4. **Semantics (1.5 marks):** Studies literal meaning — independent of context. *Example:* "The cat is on the mat" denotes a spatial relation between two entities.

5. **Pragmatics (1 mark):** Studies intended meaning depending on context, speaker, and situation. *Example:* "Can you pass the salt?" is a *request*, not a yes/no question.

6. **Discourse (1 mark):** Studies relationships *across* sentences. *Example:* "Ravi bought a car. **It** was red." — The pronoun "It" refers back to "car" via anaphora.

**(b) [7 Marks]** *What is ambiguity in NLP? Explain the four major types of ambiguity with one example for each. How does this challenge motivate the use of statistical NLP?*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

- **Definition of Ambiguity (1 mark):** A linguistic expression has ambiguity when it can be interpreted in more than one valid way.

- **Four Types (4 marks, 1 each):**
  1. **Lexical Ambiguity** — A word has multiple senses. *Example:* "He went to the **bank**." → river bank / financial bank.
  2. **Syntactic Ambiguity** — A sentence has multiple parse trees. *Example:* "I saw the man with the telescope." (instrument vs. possession).
  3. **Semantic Ambiguity** — A sentence has multiple meanings even after disambiguation of words. *Example:* "Every student read a book." (one shared book OR distinct books per student — quantifier scope ambiguity).
  4. **Pragmatic Ambiguity** — Meaning depends on speaker intent. *Example:* "It's cold here." can be a statement, a request to close the window, or a hint to leave.

- **Motivation for Statistical NLP (2 marks):** Pure rule-based grammar enumerates *all* valid parses without ranking them. Statistical NLP uses **probabilistic grammars (PCFGs)** and **machine learning** to assign likelihoods to each parse, choosing the most probable one given the corpus data. This converts the *ambiguity problem* into a *ranking problem* that can be solved with data.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Compare Rule-Based, Statistical, and Neural approaches to NLP. Discuss their strengths, weaknesses, and one representative system for each.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Approach | Core Idea | Strength (1 mark) | Weakness (1 mark) | Example System |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rule-Based** | Hand-coded linguistic rules | Interpretable, linguistically pure, no data needed | Brittle, costly to scale, knowledge-acquisition bottleneck | **ELIZA** (Weizenbaum, 1966) / **SHRDLU** (Winograd, 1970) |
| **Statistical** | Probabilistic models trained on annotated corpora | Data-driven, robust to noise, provides rankings | Requires annotated data, sparse features | **Moses SMT** (Koehn et al., 2007) / **Stanford NER** (Finkel et al., 2005) |
| **Neural / Deep Learning** | Distributed representations + deep nets (RNN, LSTM, Transformer) | Contextual embeddings, end-to-end, scales with compute | Black-box, energy-hungry, data-hungry | **BERT** (Devlin et al., 2018) / **GPT-4** (OpenAI, 2023) |

*Plus brief commentary (1 mark):* The shift has been *paradigmatic* — from symbolic to subsymbolic — driven by Moore's Law, large corpora (Common Crawl), and the invention of the Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017).

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Write short notes on: (i) Zipf's Law, (ii) BLEU Score, (iii) Perplexity. State one engineering use of each.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

(i) **Zipf's Law (2.5 marks):** In any large natural language corpus, the frequency of a word is **inversely proportional** to its rank in the frequency table.

$$f(r) = \frac{C}{r^{\alpha}}, \quad \alpha \approx 1$$

**Engineering Use:** Used to design **stop-word lists**, **sub-word tokenizers (BPE, WordPiece)**, and to **prune huge vocabularies** in large language models — only the top-K frequent tokens need to be explicitly stored.

(ii) **BLEU Score (2.5 marks):** **Bilingual Evaluation Understudy** measures the similarity of machine-translated text to one or more human reference translations using modified n-gram precision and a brevity penalty.

$$\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right), \quad \text{BP} = \min\!\left(1, e^{1-r/c}\right)$$

**Engineering Use:** Standard automatic metric for evaluating **machine translation systems**; also used in **image captioning** and **text summarization** evaluation.

(iii) **Perplexity (2 marks):** The exponentiated cross-entropy of a language model over a test set. It measures how *surprised* the model is by unseen text.

$$\text{PPL}(W) = 2^{H(W)} = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid w_{<i})} \right)^{1/N}$$

**Engineering Use:** The standard intrinsic metric for **language models** (both classical n-gram and modern neural). Lower PPL means a better model. Used during **pre-training and fine-tuning** of LLMs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing NLU and NLP** — They are *not* synonyms. NLP ⊃ NLU + NLG. Always write the full breakdown.
> 2. **Mixing up Ambiguity Types** — Lexical = one word; Syntactic = sentence structure; Semantic = scope/metaphor; Pragmatic = speaker intent. Examiners love to test this exact distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting the formula form of Zipf's Law** — Many students write only prose. KTU wants the equation $\,f(r) = C/r^{\alpha}\,$.
> 4. **BLEU without the Brevity Penalty** — A common 2-mark deduction. Always show BP.
> 5. **No examples** — Every conceptual answer **must** carry an example sentence to score full marks in Part B.
> 6. **Writing only the final answer without intermediate derivation steps** in numerical sub-parts — KTU follows step-marking, so always show the algebraic path.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Language** = rule-governed symbolic system for human communication; exhibits **productivity** and **displacement**.
- **Linguistics** = the *scientific study* of language; **NLP** = the *computational engineering* of language.
- **NLP = NLU (Understanding) + NLG (Generation)**.
- **Six Levels of Language Analysis:** Phonology → Morphology → Syntax → Semantics → Pragmatics → Discourse.
- **Ambiguity** is the *core challenge* of NLP. Four types: Lexical, Syntactic, Semantic, Pragmatic.
- **Three Paradigms:** Rule-Based (symbolic) → Statistical (probabilistic) → Neural/Deep Learning (subsymbolic).
- **Key Metrics to Memorize:** Zipf's Law (word frequency), Perplexity (language model quality), BLEU (generation/translation quality), F1-score (classification/IR).
- **Foundational Formulas (KTU High-Yield):** $f(r)=C/r^{\alpha}$, $\text{PPL}=2^{H(W)}$, $\text{BLEU}=\text{BP}\cdot\exp(\sum w_n\log p_n)$, $F_1=2PR/(P+R)$.
- **Classic Ambiguity Example:** "I saw the man with the telescope." — know the *two parse trees* by heart.
- **Historical Milestones:** Turing Test (1950) → ELIZA (1966) → SHRDLU (1970) → Statistical MT (1990s) → Word2Vec (2013) → Transformer (2017) → BERT/GPT (2018+).
- **Industrial Applications (must list 4+):** Machine Translation, Sentiment Analysis, Chatbots, NER, Spam Filtering, Voice Assistants, Text Summarization, Question Answering.
- **Always end answers with an example sentence** — KTU valuation key rewards applied understanding.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approaches to NLP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Approaches to NLP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned):**
> *Approaches to NLP* refer to the distinct **computational paradigms** and **methodological frameworks** adopted for building systems that can automatically process, understand, and generate human language. Formally, an NLP approach is a **theoretically grounded strategy** that combines (a) a *linguistic model of language structure*, (b) a *mathematical model of uncertainty/disambiguation*, and (c) a *learning/engineering strategy* for acquiring knowledge from data or rules.

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes these approaches along a **historical–conceptual axis** spanning five major paradigms:

1. **Rationalist / Symbolic (Rule-Based) Approach**
2. **Empiricist / Statistical (Corpus-Based) Approach**
3. **Connectionist / Neural Approach**
4. **Hybrid (Symbolic + Statistical) Approach**
5. **Deep Learning / Transformer-Based Approach**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "How a Child Learns a Language"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine teaching a friend a new language.
>
> - **Symbolic Approach** = Giving them a **grammar textbook** and a dictionary. They learn *“if-then”* rules (*if a sentence ends in a question mark, invert the auxiliary verb*).
> - **Statistical Approach** = Dropping them into the country and letting them **listen to millions of sentences**; they figure out that the word *“rain”* often follows *“heavy”* and *“light”* but rarely follows *“yellow”*.
> - **Connectionist / Neural Approach** = Building a **simulated brain** with interconnected neurons that automatically extracts patterns from text.
> - **Hybrid Approach** = Using a **grammar book for sentence structure** but **statistical frequency for word choice**.
> - **Deep Learning / Transformer Approach** = Giving them an **encyclopedia + massive exposure + a supercharged attention mechanism** that lets them *focus* on relevant parts of any sentence.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used Across Approaches

| Metric | Symbol | Purpose | Standard Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | $\text{Acc}$ | Correct predictions / Total | $[0, 1]$ |
| Precision | $P$ | True positives among predicted positives | $[0, 1]$ |
| Recall | $R$ | True positives among actual positives | $[0, 1]$ |
| F1-Score | $F_1$ | Harmonic mean of $P$ and $R$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Perplexity | $\text{PP}$ | Quality of probabilistic language model | $[1, \infty)$, lower is better |
| BLEU | $\text{BLEU}$ | Translation quality (n-gram overlap) | $[0, 1]$ |

### 1.4 The Two Grand Philosophical Pillars

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Philosophical Dichotomy (High-Yield for KTU):**
> Almost every NLP approach can be traced back to one of two competing views of language:
>
> - **Rationalism (Chomsky, 1957)** → Language is *innate* and *rule-governed* → **Symbolic NLP**
> - **Empiricism** → Language is *learned from exposure* → **Statistical & Neural NLP**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Approach

#### A. Rationalist / Symbolic (Rule-Based) Approach

- **Core Premise:** Language is governed by **finite, explicit rules** that can be hand-crafted.
- **Knowledge Source:** Linguists encode grammar, morphology, syntax, and semantic relations.
- **Architecture:** A pipeline of *lexicon → parser → semantic interpreter → reasoning engine*.
- **Key Techniques:** *Context-Free Grammars (CFG)*, *Definite Clause Grammars*, *augmented transition networks*, *feature unification*.
- **Famous Systems:** **ELIZA (1966)**, **SHRDLU (1960s)**, **LUNAR (Woods, 1973)**, **Chat-80**.
- **Strengths:** Interpretable, deterministic, no training data required, linguistically motivated.
- **Weaknesses:** *Knowledge-acquisition bottleneck*, brittle, poor at handling ambiguity, expensive to scale.

#### B. Empiricist / Statistical (Corpus-Based) Approach

- **Core Premise:** Language patterns can be *induced from large annotated or unannotated corpora* using probability theory.
- **Knowledge Source:** A **corpus** of real text (e.g., Brown Corpus, Penn Treebank).
- **Architecture:** Probabilistic model $P(\text{output} \mid \text{input})$ is learned from data.
- **Key Techniques:** *N-gram language models*, *Hidden Markov Models (HMM)*, *Maximum Entropy (MaxEnt)*, *Conditional Random Fields (CRF)*, *Naïve Bayes*.
- **Famous Systems:** *Speech taggers of the 1990s (e.g., TnT)*, *statistical machine translation (IBM Models)*.
- **Strengths:** Robust to noise, handles ambiguity probabilistically, scalable.
- **Weaknesses:** Requires massive labeled data, sparse-data issues, weak at long-range dependencies, less interpretable.

#### C. Connectionist / Neural Approach

- **Core Premise:** Language is an emergent property of **distributed representations** learned by artificial neural networks.
- **Knowledge Source:** Vectors (embeddings) learned from raw text.
- **Architecture:** Networks of artificial neurons with **distributed word embeddings** (*word2vec*, *GloVe*).
- **Key Techniques:** *Feed-forward neural networks*, *Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)*, *Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)*, *Gated Recurrent Units (GRU)*.
- **Famous Systems:** *Bengio’s neural language model (2003)*, *Collobert & Weston (2008)*.
- **Strengths:** Learns rich representations, handles non-linearity, generalization.
- **Weaknesses:** Computationally expensive, opaque ("black box"), data-hungry, vanishing-gradient issues in vanilla RNNs.

#### D. Hybrid Approach

- **Core Premise:** Combine the **interpretability of rules** with the **robustness of statistics/neural networks**.
- **Architecture:** Symbolic front-end + statistical/neural back-end.
- **Example:** A rule-based POS tagger pre-processes text, then an HMM or neural tagger refines ambiguous cases.
- **Strengths:** Best of both worlds, higher accuracy, more reliable.
- **Weaknesses:** Engineering complexity, integration overhead.

#### E. Deep Learning / Transformer-Based Approach

- **Core Premise:** Use **very deep neural networks** with **self-attention** to model long-range dependencies.
- **Knowledge Source:** Massive corpora + unsupervised pre-training + task-specific fine-tuning.
- **Key Architectures:** *Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017)*, *BERT, GPT, T5, LLaMA*.
- **Strengths:** State-of-the-art across virtually all NLP benchmarks; pre-train once, fine-tune for many tasks.
- **Weaknesses:** Enormous compute cost, huge carbon footprint, hallucination issues, ethical concerns.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in the KTU 2024 ESE/Module tests.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes Theorem (foundation of statistical NLP) | $$P(C \mid D) \;=\; \dfrac{P(D \mid C)\cdot P(C)}{P(D)}$$ | $C$=class, $D$=document |
| 2 | N-gram Probability (Markov Assumption) | $$P(w_n \mid w_1^{n-1}) \;\approx\; P(w_n \mid w_{n-1})$$ | Bigram simplification |
| 3 | Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE) for n-grams | $$P(w_n \mid w_{n-1}) \;=\; \dfrac{C(w_{n-1}\,w_n)}{C(w_{n-1})}$$ | $C(\cdot)$ = count in corpus |
| 4 | Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing | $$P^{*}(w_n \mid w_{n-1}) \;=\; \dfrac{C(w_{n-1}\,w_n)+1}{C(w_{n-1})+V}$$ | $V$ = vocabulary size |
| 5 | Perplexity of a Language Model | $$\text{PP}(W) \;=\; \sqrt[N]{\dfrac{1}{P(w_1 w_2 \dots w_N)}}$$ | Lower $\text{PP}$ = better model |
| 6 | Precision | $$P \;=\; \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| 7 | Recall | $$R \;=\; \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$$ | Coverage of actual positives |
| 8 | F1-Score (Harmonic Mean) | $$F_1 \;=\; \dfrac{2\cdot P\cdot R}{P+R}$$ | Balanced single metric |
| 9 | BLEU Score (Geometric Mean of n-gram precisions, with brevity penalty $BP$) | $$\text{BLEU} \;=\; BP \cdot \exp\!\Bigl(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\Bigr)$$ | Typically $w_n=\tfrac{1}{N}$ |
| 10 | Sigmoid Activation (logistic) | $$\sigma(z) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$$ | Used in binary classifiers |
| 11 | Softmax (multi-class) | $$\text{softmax}(z_i) \;=\; \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$$ | Output layer of neural LM |
| 12 | Self-Attention Score | $$\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) \;=\; \text{softmax}\!\Bigl(\dfrac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\Bigr)V$$ | Core of Transformer |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Symbolic** systems still power **grammar checkers in MS Word** and **industrial command-language interfaces**.
- **Statistical** models dominate **speech recognition** engines (e.g., earlier versions of Google Voice).
- **Neural/Deep** models now drive **chatbots, sentiment analysis, machine translation, search engines, code completion**.
- **Hybrid** systems are used in **legal/medical NLP** where both interpretability and accuracy are critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Theorem to a Working Text Classifier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked derivation — *Naïve Bayes Document Classifier*** (a classic statistical NLP approach).

**Step 1 — Goal.** Given a document $D$ represented by a bag of words $\{w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n\}$, classify it into one of $K$ classes $C = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_K\}$.

**Step 2 — Decision Rule (Maximum A Posteriori).** We pick the class that maximizes the posterior probability:

$$\hat{C} \;=\; \underset{c \,\in\, C}{\arg\max}\; P(c \mid D)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem.** Substitute the posterior:

$$P(c \mid D) \;=\; \dfrac{P(D \mid c)\cdot P(c)}{P(D)}$$

**Step 4 — Drop the Evidence Term.** Since $P(D)$ is constant across all classes, it does not affect the $\arg\max$:

$$\hat{C} \;=\; \underset{c}{\arg\max}\; P(D \mid c)\cdot P(c)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Naïve Conditional Independence Assumption.** Assume each word position $w_i$ is independent of every other word given the class $c$:

$$P(D \mid c) \;=\; P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n \mid c) \;\approx\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

**Step 6 — Combine.**

$$\hat{C} \;=\; \underset{c}{\arg\max}\; P(c) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

**Step 7 — Estimate Priors from Corpus.**

$$P(c) \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Number of documents in class } c}{\text{Total number of documents}} \;=\; \dfrac{N_c}{N}$$

**Step 8 — Estimate Likelihoods (with Laplace smoothing to avoid zero probabilities):**

$$P(w_i \mid c) \;=\; \dfrac{\text{count of } w_i \text{ in class } c + 1}{\text{total words in class } c + V}$$

where $V$ is the vocabulary size. The “$+1$” in numerator and “$+V$” in denominator are the **Laplace add-1 smoothing** to handle out-of-vocabulary / unseen words.

**Step 9 — Final Classifier.**

$$\boxed{\hat{C} \;=\; \underset{c}{\arg\max}\; \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid c) \right]}$$

(The $\log$ is taken to convert the product into a sum and avoid numerical underflow.)

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is one of the most frequently tested structures in KTU ESE for Module 1.**

| Criterion | Symbolic | Statistical | Neural | Deep Learning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge Source** | Hand-crafted rules | Annotated corpus | Labeled data + embeddings | Massive unlabeled text |
| **Representation** | Symbols, parse trees | Probability distributions | Distributed vectors | Contextual embeddings |
| **Data Need** | None | Moderate | High | Very high |
| **Interpretability** | Very high | Medium | Low | Very low |
| **Handles Ambiguity** | Poorly | Probabilistically | Learned implicitly | Learned implicitly |
| **Scalability** | Low | Medium | Medium | Very high |
| **Compute Cost** | Low | Medium | High | Extremely high |
| **Maintenance** | Hard (manual) | Moderate | Re-training | Re-training + fine-tuning |
| **Example System** | ELIZA, SHRDLU | HMM Tagger, IBM MT | word2vec, LSTM LM | BERT, GPT, T5 |
| **Era** | 1950s–1980s | 1990s–2000s | 2003–2013 | 2014–present |

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Perplexity of a Bigram Model

**Given a tiny corpus of three sentences:**

| Sentence | Tokens |
|---|---|
| $S_1$ | `<s> I love NLP </s>` |
| $S_2$ | `<s> I love AI </s>` |
| $S_3$ | `<s> AI loves NLP </s>` |

**Step 1 — Compute bigram counts.**

| Bigram | Count |
|---|---|
| `<s> I` | 2 |
| `<s> AI` | 1 |
| `I love` | 2 |
| `love NLP` | 1 |
| `love AI` | 1 |
| `AI loves` | 1 |
| `loves NLP` | 1 |
| `NLP </s>` | 2 |
| `AI </s>` | 1 |

**Step 2 — Compute bigram probabilities (with add-1 smoothing, $V=8$):**

$$P(\text{love} \mid I) \;=\; \dfrac{2+1}{2+8} \;=\; 0.30$$

$$P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) \;=\; \dfrac{1+1}{2+8} \;=\; 0.20$$

$$P(</s> \mid \text{NLP}) \;=\; \dfrac{2+1}{2+8} \;=\; 0.30$$

**Step 3 — Compute probability of test sentence `<s> I love NLP </s>`:**

$$P(\text{test}) \;=\; 0.30 \times 0.30 \times 0.20 \times 0.30 \;=\; 0.0054$$

**Step 4 — Compute Perplexity (test sentence has $N=4$ tokens).**

$$\text{PP} \;=\; (0.0054)^{-1/4} \;\approx\; 3.71$$

**Interpretation:** The model is “surprised” by an average branching factor of about 3.71 when predicting the next word. Lower perplexity = a better language model.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Evolution of NLP Approaches (Conceptual Timeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["1950s Symbolic Era ELIZA SHRDLU"] --> node2["1990s Statistical Era HMM CRF IBM Models"]
    node2 --> node3["2013 Neural Era word2vec LSTM Encoder Decoder"]
    node3 --> node4["2017 Transformer Era Attention Is All You Need"]
    node4 --> node5["2018 Onwards Pretrained Era BERT GPT T5 LLaMA"]

    node1 -.- nodeA["Hand Crafted Rules Interpretable"]
    node2 -.- nodeB["Probability Theory Annotated Corpora"]
    node3 -.- nodeC["Distributed Vectors Learned Representations"]
    node4 -.- nodeD["Self Attention Parallelism"]
    node5 -.- nodeE["Transfer Learning Few Shot Capabilities"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: How a Hybrid NLP System Works

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Raw Text Input"] --> nodeB["Tokenizer"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Rule Based Pre Processor"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Statistical or Neural Model"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Symbolic Post Processor"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Final Output"]
    nodeD -.-> nodeG["Confidence Scores"]
    nodeG -.-> nodeE
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing an NLP Approach

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ["Start Do you have large labeled data"] -->|Yes| nodeN["Use Neural or Deep Learning"]
    nodeQ -->|No| nodeS["Do you have medium sized annotated corpus"]
    nodeS -->|Yes| nodeST["Use Statistical Approach HMM CRF"]
    nodeS -->|No| nodeR["Use Symbolic Rule Based Approach"]
    nodeN --> nodeCheck["Need interpretability"]
    nodeCheck -->|Yes| nodeH["Hybrid Approach"]
    nodeCheck -->|No| nodeDL["Pure Deep Learning Transformer"]
```

### 4.4 Venn Diagram — Overlap of Modern Paradigms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS["Symbolic Rules"] --> nodeH["Modern Hybrid NLP System"]
    nodeStat["Statistical Probabilities"] --> nodeH
    nodeN["Neural Representations"] --> nodeH
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - Students often *just list* the approaches without contrasting them — you **must** mention at least *one strength and one weakness* of each to score full marks.
> - For Bayes/Naïve Bayes questions, students forget the **denominator** $P(D)$ is dropped but should be *mentioned* in working.
> - For deep-learning questions, *do not confuse* "Neural approach" with "Deep Learning approach" — they are **not identical** (Neural = shallow networks, Deep = many-layer networks).
> - When asked "Compare", always use a **table** — it earns easier partial marks than paragraph form.
> - Always **state assumptions** (e.g., conditional independence in Naïve Bayes) explicitly.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**
*List any three approaches to NLP and give one example system for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Symbolic / Rule-Based Approach** — *Example:* ELIZA (Weizenbaum, 1966), a chatbot that used pattern–response rules.
2. **Statistical / Corpus-Based Approach** — *Example:* Hidden Markov Model (HMM) based POS tagger.
3. **Connectionist / Neural Approach** — *Example:* word2vec (Mikolov, 2013), which learns distributed word embeddings using a shallow neural network.
*(1 mark for each correct approach + example. Total 3.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**
*Distinguish between the Rationalist and Empiricist views of language.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Rationalist | Empiricist |
|---|---|---|
| **Philosopher** | Noam Chomsky (1957) | Behaviorist / probabilistic school |
| **View of Language** | Innate, rule-governed | Learned from sensory data/exposure |
| **Knowledge Source** | Explicit grammar rules | Corpus-derived probabilities |
| **NLP Paradigm** | Symbolic / Rule-based | Statistical / Neural |
| **Limitation** | Knowledge-acquisition bottleneck | Sparse-data problem |

*(1 mark for definition of each view + 1 mark for the contrast in NLP approach.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **five major approaches to NLP** with at least one representative system or algorithm for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe a **hybrid NLP architecture** and justify why such systems are preferred in real-world applications. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Answer for Q-A (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Symbolic / Rule-Based** (2 marks)
   - Hand-crafted linguistic rules (CFG, definite-clause grammars).
   - *Example:* ELIZA, SHRDLU.
   - *Strength:* Interpretable. *Weakness:* Brittle, knowledge-acquisition bottleneck.

2. **Statistical / Empirical** (2 marks)
   - Probabilistic models trained on corpora.
   - *Example:* HMM POS tagger, IBM Model 1 for MT, n-gram language model.
   - *Strength:* Robust to noise. *Weakness:* Requires large annotated corpora.

3. **Connectionist / Neural** (1 mark)
   - Distributed representations in artificial neural networks.
   - *Example:* word2vec, Bengio’s 2003 neural language model.

4. **Hybrid** (1 mark)
   - Combines symbolic rules + statistical/neural layers.
   - *Example:* Rule-based preprocessor feeding a CRF tagger.

5. **Deep Learning / Transformer-Based** (1 mark)
   - Very deep networks with self-attention; pre-train + fine-tune.
   - *Example:* BERT, GPT, T5.

#### **Model Answer for Q-A (b) — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram of a Hybrid NLP Pipeline** (3 marks for diagram):

```
[Raw Text] → [Tokenizer] → [Rule-Based Normalizer] → [Statistical/Neural Engine] → [Symbolic Post-Processor] → [Output]
                                          ↑                                     ↓
                                  (confidence feedback) ←—————— (sub-symbolic scores)
```

**Justification (4 marks):**
- **Interpretability:** Symbolic layer gives traceable decisions, essential in *medical*, *legal*, and *financial* NLP.
- **Robustness:** Statistical/neural layer handles noisy, ambiguous, out-of-distribution inputs.
- **Domain Adaptation:** Rules can encode *expert knowledge*, reducing labeled-data demand.
- **Accuracy:** Confidence feedback from neural sub-system can *trigger* symbolic fallbacks for low-confidence predictions → higher macro-F1.
- **Real-world examples:** *IBM Watson Oncology* (rules + ML), *Apple Siri* (grammar + neural ASR), *Google Translate* (statistical + neural hybrids).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *(ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)*

**(a)** Derive the **Naïve Bayes classification rule** for text documents. State all assumptions clearly. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A tiny corpus contains the following four sentences (each tokenized into words):

| # | Sentence | Class |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `chinese beijing chinese` | $c$ |
| 2 | `chinese chinese shanghai` | $c$ |
| 3 | `tokyo japan chinese` | $\neg c$ |

Using **Laplace smoothing** with vocabulary $V=\{\text{Chinese, Beijing, Shanghai, Tokyo, Japan, be}\}$ (size $V=6$), classify the test sentence: `Chinese Chinese Chinese Tokyo Japan` using Naïve Bayes. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Answer for Q-B (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Bayes' Theorem statement** (1 mark):
   $$P(c \mid D) = \dfrac{P(D \mid c)\,P(c)}{P(D)}$$

2. **Naïve Conditional Independence Assumption** (2 marks): The probability of each word $w_i$ in document $D$ is conditionally independent of every other word given the class $c$:
   $$P(D \mid c) = P(w_1,\dots,w_n \mid c) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

3. **Decision rule** (2 marks):
   $$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c}\, P(c) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

4. **Log-form to prevent underflow** (1 mark):
   $$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c}\, \Bigl[\log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n}\log P(w_i \mid c)\Bigr]$$

5. **Laplace-smoothed estimates** (1 mark):
   $$P(w_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c) + 1}{\sum_{w}\text{count}(w, c) + V}$$

#### **Model Answer for Q-B (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Priors** (1 mark):
- $P(c) = 2/3$ (2 of 3 sentences in class $c$)
- $P(\neg c) = 1/3$

**Step 2 — Token counts per class** (1 mark):

| Vocabulary | $c$ | $\neg c$ |
|---|---|---|
| Chinese | 3 | 1 |
| Beijing | 1 | 0 |
| Shanghai | 1 | 0 |
| Tokyo | 0 | 1 |
| Japan | 0 | 1 |
| **Total (non-`be`)** | **5** | **2** |

*(The token `be` is ignored by convention; $V=6$ includes `be`.)*

**Step 3 — Smoothed likelihoods** (1 mark):

For class $c$ (denominator = $5 + 6 = 11$):

| Word | $P(w \mid c)$ |
|---|---|
| Chinese | $(3+1)/11 = 4/11$ |
| Tokyo   | $(0+1)/11 = 1/11$ |
| Japan   | $(0+1)/11 = 1/11$ |

For class $\neg c$ (denominator = $2 + 6 = 8$):

| Word | $P(w \mid \neg c)$ |
|---|---|
| Chinese | $(1+1)/8 = 2/8$ |
| Tokyo   | $(1+1)/8 = 2/8$ |
| Japan   | $(1+1)/8 = 2/8$ |

**Step 4 — Posterior for class $c$** (2 marks):

Test sentence has three *Chinese*, one *Tokyo*, one *Japan*.

$$P(c)\cdot P(D \mid c) = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{4}{11}\right)^{3} \cdot \frac{1}{11} \cdot \frac{1}{11} \;\approx\; \frac{2}{3} \cdot 0.0481 \cdot 0.00827 \;\approx\; 2.65\times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5 — Posterior for class $\neg c$** (2 marks):

$$P(\neg c)\cdot P(D \mid \neg c) = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{8}\right)^{3} \cdot \frac{2}{8} \cdot \frac{2}{8} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{3} \cdot 0.015625 \cdot 0.0625 \;\approx\; 3.26\times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 — Decision** (part of the 2 marks above):

$$P(\neg c \mid D) > P(c \mid D) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Predicted class} = \boxed{\neg c\ \text{(not-China)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The test sentence contains the word *Tokyo* and *Japan* which never appear in the "China" class; Laplace smoothing ensures the unknown-word effect is *attenuated* but still dominates because of the multiplicative n-gram effect — a classic illustration of how a single strong cue can flip a Naïve Bayes decision.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MODULE 1 — "APPROACHES TO NLP" — KEY TAKEAWAYS**

- **Five approaches:** *Symbolic*, *Statistical*, *Connectionist/Neural*, *Hybrid*, *Deep Learning/Transformer*.
- **Two philosophies:** *Rationalist* (rules) vs *Empiricist* (data).
- **Core symbolic systems to remember:** ELIZA, SHRDLU, LUNAR, Chat-80.
- **Core statistical systems to remember:** HMM tagger, IBM translation models, n-gram LM.
- **Core neural systems to remember:** word2vec, GloVe, LSTM, encoder-decoder.
- **Core deep-learning systems to remember:** Transformer, BERT, GPT, T5, LLaMA.
- **Bayes' theorem** is the *backbone* of statistical NLP — must be memorized exactly.
- **Naïve Bayes assumptions** = (1) bag-of-words, (2) conditional independence of words given class, (3) position of words does not matter.
- **Laplace smoothing** $P(w \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w,c)+1}{\sum_{w}\text{count}(w,c)+V}$ — must be applied when a word does not occur in training data for a class.
- **Perplexity** $PP(W) = P(w_1\dots w_N)^{-1/N}$ — *lower is better*; inversely related to model confidence.
- **BLEU** measures *n-gram overlap* between machine and reference translation — *higher is better*; includes a *brevity penalty*.
- **F1-score** is the *harmonic mean* of precision and recall — preferred over accuracy for *imbalanced* classes.
- **Hybrid systems** are *practically preferred* in industry because they combine interpretability (rules) with robustness (statistics/neural networks).
- **Transformer self-attention** formula: $\text{softmax}\!\bigl(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k}\bigr)V$ — definitional must-know.
- **Always** present a **comparison table** when asked to "compare" or "differentiate" — KTU examiners award partial marks generously for tabular contrasts.
- For *numerical* Naïve Bayes / n-gram questions, **show every arithmetic step**; KTU valuation rewards working, not just the final number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heuristics-Based NLP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Heuristics-Based NLP ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Heuristics-Based Natural Language Processing (Heuristics-Based NLP)** is the foundational paradigm of computational linguistics in which language understanding, generation, and analysis are performed by **hand-crafted rule systems, pattern-matching engines, regular expressions, finite-state automata, and symbolic linguistic knowledge bases** — without relying on statistical learning or neural network parameters. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEIET864), Heuristics-Based NLP is positioned as the **historical and logical precursor** to modern statistical and deep-learning approaches. It is grounded in the principle that *human language can be decomposed into explicit, deterministic (or semi-deterministic) rules* that a machine can apply mechanically. The core ingredients of a heuristics-based NLP pipeline are: | Component | Role in the Pipeline | |---|---| | **Regular Expressions (Regex)** | Lexical pattern matching for tokens, dates, e-mails, etc. | | **Finite-State Automata (FSA / FST)** | Morphological analysis, tokenization, sandhi splitting | | **Context-Free Grammars (CFG)** | Phrase-structure parsing, syntactic trees | | **Production Rules (If–Then)** | Tagging (e.g., Brill tagger), chunking, semantic interpretation | | **Lexicons / Gazetteers** | Lookup tables for named entities, stopwords, domain terms | | **Hand-coded Disambiguation Heuristics** | Tie-breaking in cases of rule conflict (e.g., "longest match") | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Strict Grammar Teacher > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine an English grammar school teacher who has memorised **every rule in the Oxford English Grammar** book. Whenever a student writes a sentence, the teacher does not "guess" what the sentence means — instead, the teacher applies a **sequence of explicit rules** in a fixed order: > 1. First, split the sentence by punctuation. > 2. Then, identify nouns by their suffixes (-tion, -ment, -er). > 3. Then, find verbs by their position relative to nouns. > 4. Apply rule R17: "If a word ends in -ing and follows an auxiliary verb, it is a gerund." > > The teacher never *learns* from data; he merely *consults* his pre-written rulebook. This is **exactly** what a heuristics-based NLP system does. ### 1.3 Why Heuristics? — The Engineering Rationale - **Interpretability:** Every decision can be traced back to an explicit rule → critical in legal, medical, and compliance NLP. - **Data Efficiency:** Zero training corpus required; ideal for **low-resource languages** (e.g., Malayalam, Sanskrit). - **Determinism:** Given the same input, the system always produces the same output — vital for safety-critical applications. - **Cost:** No GPUs, no hyper-parameter tuning, no model retraining. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The syllabus explicitly contrasts Heuristics-Based NLP with **Statistical NLP** and **Neural NLP** as the *three chronological generations*. Expect an exam question asking: *"Compare heuristics-based, statistical, and neural approaches to NLP."* ### 1.4 Limitations (to be contrasted later in the module) - **Coverage vs. Precision Trade-off:** Rules either fire too often (over-generation) or miss edge cases. - **Knowledge Acquisition Bottleneck:** Hand-coding rules is labour-intensive and non-scalable. - **Brittleness:** A single unanticipated construction can break the cascade. - **No Generalisation:** The system cannot infer rules for unseen constructions. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operational Stack of a Heuristics-Based NLP System A canonical heuristics-based NLP pipeline executes the following sequence of rule-driven stages. Each stage is an **independent module** with a clearly defined input/output contract. **Stage 1 — Sentence Segmentation** - Rule: Split the text on the regex `[.!?]+\s+[A-Z]` *and* verify the next token is a known sentence-initial word. - Heuristic for abbreviations: *If a period is preceded by a known abbreviation (Mr., Dr., U.S.), do **not** split.* **Stage 2 — Tokenisation** - Rule: Match tokens via a finite-state transducer that walks the input character stream and emits token boundaries. - Heuristic: *Longest-match-wins (Maximal Munch).* **Stage 3 — Morphological Analysis** - Rule: Apply an FST that strips affixes, consults the lexicon, and returns the lemma + morphological features. - Heuristic: *If two analyses are possible, choose the one requiring the fewest affix-stripping operations (Minimum Edit Distance).* **Stage 4 — Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** - Rule: Apply a cascade of **lexical rules** (e.g., "If word ∈ {the, a, an} → DT") followed by **contextual rules** (e.g., Brill's transformation rules: "Change VBD → VBN if previous tag is HVD"). - Heuristic: *Default tag for unknown words = NN (common noun).* **Stage 5 — Chunking / Shallow Parsing** - Rule: Identify NP, VP, PP chunks via regex over POS tags, e.g., `(DT | JJ)* NN` → NP. - Heuristic: *Greedy left-to-right consumption of tokens.* **Stage 6 — Full Syntactic Parsing** - Rule: Apply a CFG using a **chart parser** (Earley, CYK) to build a parse forest. - Heuristic: *If multiple parses exist, pick the one with the fewest unary productions (lowest branching cost).* **Stage 7 — Semantic / Pragmatic Interpretation** - Rule: Map parse-tree nodes to predicates using rule templates (e.g., S → NP VP → predicate(VP.head, NP.head)). ### 2.2 Formal Building Blocks #### 2.2.1 Regular Expressions (Regex) A **regular expression** over an alphabet $\Sigma$ is built from: - The empty string $\varepsilon$ - Symbols $a \in \Sigma$ - Concatenation $r_1 r_2$ - Alternation $r_1 \vert r_2$ - Kleene star $r^*$ The language denoted by a regex $r$ is written $L(r)$. Regexes are *equivalent in expressive power to finite-state automata* (Kleene's Theorem). **Core Regex Operators used in NLP Tokenisation:** | Operator | Meaning | NLP Use-Case | |---|---|---| | `.` | Any single character | Generic character class | | `*` | Zero or more repetitions | Plural endings, repeated suffixes | | `+` | One or more repetitions | Sequences of letters | | `?` | Zero or one (optional) | Optional determiner | | `[A-Z]` | Character class | Capitalised words (proper nouns) | | `^` | Start of line/string | Sentence-initial word | | `$` | End of line/string | Sentence-final punctuation | | `\d` | Digit | Extracting numbers, dates | | `\w` | Word character | Token boundaries | | `(…)` | Capture group | Extracting sub-patterns | #### 2.2.2 Finite-State Automata (FSA) A **deterministic finite automaton (DFA)** is the 5-tuple: $$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$ where: - $Q$ = finite set of states - $\Sigma$ = finite input alphabet - $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ = transition function - $q_0 \in Q$ = start state - $F \subseteq Q$ = set of accepting (final) states A **non-deterministic finite automaton (NFA)** relaxes the transition function to: $$\delta: Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow \mathcal{P}(Q)$$ allowing multiple next-states or zero-symbol (ε) transitions. > [!NOTE] > **Theoretical Guarantee:** Every NFA can be converted to an equivalent DFA via the **subset construction** (Rabin-Scott theorem). The number of DFA states can in the worst case be $2^{\vert Q \vert}$, but for NLP lexicons the blow-up is empirically small. #### 2.2.3 Finite-State Transducer (FST) An FST is an FSA whose transitions emit an **output symbol** in addition to consuming an input symbol. It is the workhorse of morphological analysers (e.g., the **Kleene/Koskenniemi two-level morphology**, **XFST/LEXC** formalism used for Malayalam and Hindi). Formally, an FST is a 6-tuple: $$T = (Q, \Sigma, \Delta, \delta, q_0, F)$$ with $\delta: Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow Q \times (\Delta \cup \{\varepsilon\})^*$ and $\Delta$ being the output alphabet. #### 2.2.4 Context-Free Grammar (CFG) A CFG is a 4-tuple: $$G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$$ where: - $V$ = finite set of non-terminals (e.g., NP, VP) - $\Sigma$ = finite set of terminals (words) - $R \subseteq V \times (V \cup \Sigma)^*$ = set of production rules - $S \in V$ = start symbol The language generated is $L(G) = \{ w \in \Sigma^* : S \Rightarrow^* w \}$. #### 2.2.5 The Chomsky Hierarchy (Essential for KTU) | Type | Grammar | Automaton | Language Family | NLP Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Type-3** | Regular | FSA | Regular Languages | Tokenisation, morphology | | **Type-2** | Context-Free | Pushdown Automaton | Context-Free Languages | Syntactic parsing | | **Type-1** | Context-Sensitive | Linear-Bounded Automaton | Context-Sensitive Languages | Cross-serial dependencies | | **Type-0** | Unrestricted | Turing Machine | Recursively Enumerable | (Theoretical limit) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield:** English syntax is **not context-free** (cross-serial dependencies in Swiss German, agreement in Arabic), but the CFG approximation is good enough for most practical parsers — this is itself a *heuristic simplification*. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (One-Page Cheat Sheet) | #
