| Concept | Formula / Definition | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Regex language | $L(r_1 r_2) = L(r_1) \cdot L(r_2)$ | Tokenisation rules | | 2 | Regex alternation | $L(r_1 \vert r_2) = L(r_1) \cup L(r_2)$ | POS tag patterns | | 3 | Regex Kleene star | $L(r^*) = \bigcup_{k=0}^{\infty} L(r)^k$ | Repeated affixes | | 4 | DFA transition | $\delta(q, a) = q'$ | State diagram for morphology | | 5 | NFA → DFA conversion | $Q' = \mathcal{P}(Q)$ (subset construction) | Compiler construction | | 6 | FST output | $\delta(q, a) = (q', b)$ with $b \in \Delta^*$ | Stemming, transliteration | | 7 | CFG derivation | $S \Rightarrow^* w$ | Parse-tree construction | | 8 | CYK parsing condition | Chomsky Normal Form: $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | $O(n^3)$ parse decision | | 9 | Precision | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Rule-quality metric | | 10 | Recall | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Rule-coverage metric | | 11 | F1-score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Balancing precision and recall | | 12 | Levenshtein edit distance | $d(i, j) = \min\{d(i-1, j)+1, d(i, j-1)+1, d(i-1, j-1) + \mathbb{1}[a_i \neq b_j]\}$ | Spell-correction heuristic | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Apache Lucene / Elasticsearch:** Heavily regex-driven tokenisers and stemmers in inverted-index search engines. - **Lex/Yacc (and modern ANTLR):** Used in compilers to parse programming languages — a direct industrial descendant of CFG heuristics. - **Postal Address Parsers** (e.g., **libpostal**): Layered regex cascades for street/city extraction. - **Spelling & Grammar Checkers** in early MS-Word: Based on hand-coded rule sets. - **Information Extraction** in legal-tech (e.g., **CUAD**, **Kira Systems**): Use rule-based templates for clause identification. - **Bot Frameworks** (e.g., **AIML** of ALICE / ELIZA): Pure pattern-template-response engine — the canonical heuristics chatbot. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pedagogical Note:** This section builds up three concrete worked examples that KTU examiners love: (i) a regex for date extraction, (ii) a DFA for English plural morphology, and (iii) a CFG parse of a simple English sentence. Every algebraic and logical step is shown explicitly. --- ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Designing a Regex for Date Extraction **Problem Statement:** Extract all dates of the form `DD-MM-YYYY` from the text *"The deadline is 25-12-2024 and the review is on 01-03-2025."* **Step 1 — Decompose the format.** A date `DD-MM-YYYY` consists of: - 2 digits for the day - 1 hyphen - 2 digits for the month - 1 hyphen - 4 digits for the year **Step 2 — Write the regex component by component.** | Component | Regex Fragment | Justification | |---|---|---| | Day | `\d{2}` | Exactly 2 digits | | Hyphen | `-` | Literal hyphen | | Month | `(0[1-9]\vert 1[0-2])` | Months 01–12, with leading zero enforced | | Hyphen | `-` | Literal hyphen | | Year | `\d{4}` | Exactly 4 digits | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing just `\d{2}-\d{2}-\d{4}` — this matches the *pattern* but also `99-99-9999` (an invalid date). Always tighten month and day ranges. **Step 3 — Combine with word boundaries** so the regex does not match inside a longer number string. $$\boxed{\texttt{\textbackslash b(0[1-9]\vert[12]\textbackslash d\vert 3[01])-(0[1-9]\vert 1[0-2])-\textbackslash d\{4\}\textbackslash b}}$$ **Step 4 — Verify against the input.** Apply the regex to *"The deadline is 25-12-2024 and the review is on 01-03-2025."*: - `25-12-2024` → Day `25` ∈ [01–31] ✓, Month `12` ∈ [01–12] ✓, Year `2024` ✓ → **MATCH** - `01-03-2025` → Day `01` ∈ [01–31] ✓, Month `03` ∈ [01–12] ✓, Year `2025` ✓ → **MATCH** **Heuristic Tie-Breaker Rule:** *If two regex matches overlap, prefer the longer one; if equal, prefer the leftmost.* This is the *leftmost-longest match* heuristic of the POSIX NFA standard. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — DFA for English Plural Morphology **Problem:** Design a DFA that accepts English singular nouns in the input and emits their plural form as output (i.e., design a **Finite-State Transducer** for the rules $N \rightarrow N+s$ and $N \rightarrow N+es$). **Step 1 — Identify the morphological rules (heuristics).** | Rule # | Input Suffix (Singular) | Output Suffix (Plural) | Example | |---|---|---|---| | R1 | (consonant) + `y` | (consonant) + `ies` | city → cities | | R2 | `s`, `x`, `z`, `ch`, `sh` | `es` | bus → buses, box → boxes | | R3 | Default | `s` | cat → cats | **Step 2 — Formalise as a transducer.** States: $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_{acc}\}$. The transducer reads the singular noun left-to-right, buffers characters, and on reaching the end-state, applies one of the three rules. **Transition function $\delta$:** $$\begin{aligned} \delta(q_0, c) &= (q_0, c) \quad \text{for } c \in \Sigma \setminus \{y, s, x, z, c, h, s, h\} \quad \text{(default pass-through)} \\ \delta(q_0, y) &= (q_1, y) \quad \text{(remember last char is `y`)} \\ \delta(q_1, \text{end}) &= (q_{acc}, \text{ies}) \quad \text{(apply R1)} \\ \delta(q_0, s) &= (q_{acc}, \text{es}) \quad \text{(apply R2 for s)} \\ \delta(q_0, x) &= (q_{acc}, \text{es}) \quad \text{(apply R2 for x)} \\ \delta(q_0, c) &= (q_c, c) \quad \text{(remember `c` for `ch` rule)} \\ \delta(q_c, h) &= (q_{acc}, \text{es}) \quad \text{(apply R2 for ch)} \\ \delta(q_0, s) &= (q_s, s) \quad \text{(remember `s` for `sh` rule)} \\ \delta(q_s, h) &= (q_{acc}, \text{es}) \quad \text{(apply R2 for sh)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Trace the input `box`.** $$\begin{aligned} &(q_0, b) \rightarrow (q_0, b) \quad \text{(default pass-through, emit `b`)} \\ &(q_0, o) \rightarrow (q_0, o) \quad \text{(default pass-through, emit `o`)} \\ &(q_0, x) \rightarrow (q_{acc}, \text{es}) \quad \text{(apply R2: emit `es` instead of `x`)} \end{aligned}$$ Output: `b o es` → **`boxes`** ✓ **Step 4 — Heuristic for unknown words.** If the noun is not in the lexicon, fall back to default R3 (add `s`). This is the *unknown-word heuristic*. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Production-grade morphological FSTs (e.g., the **Helsinki Finite-State Transducer (HFST)**) compactly encode thousands of such rules into a single optimised binary file often only 1–2 MB in size, yet can analyse 100,000+ words/second on a single CPU core. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — CFG Parse of an English Sentence **Problem:** Using the CFG below, produce a parse tree for the sentence *"The cat saw the rat."* **Grammar $G$:** $$\begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow NP \; VP \\ NP &\rightarrow DT \; NN \\ VP &\rightarrow VB \; NP \\ DT &\rightarrow \text{`the'} \\ NN &\rightarrow \text{`cat'} \;\vert\; \text{`rat'} \\ VB &\rightarrow \text{`saw'} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 1 — Apply the production $S \rightarrow NP \; VP$.** Root node becomes `S`, with two children `NP` and `VP`. **Step 2 — Expand `NP` (left branch).** Apply $NP \rightarrow DT \; NN$. Children become `DT` and `NN`. **Step 3 — Expand `VP` (right branch).** Apply $VP \rightarrow VB \; NP$. Children become `VB` and `NP`. **Step 4 — Lexical expansion of terminals.** Walk down to leaves: $$\begin{aligned} DT &\rightarrow \text{`the'} \quad (\text{first DT}) \\ NN &\rightarrow \text{`cat'} \\ VB &\rightarrow \text{`saw'} \\ DT &\rightarrow \text{`the'} \quad (\text{second DT}) \\ NN &\rightarrow \text{`rat'} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Render the parse tree (textual):** ``` S / \ NP VP /\ /\ DT NN VB NP | | | |\ The cat saw DT NN | | The rat ``` **Step 6 — Heuristic for parse ambiguity.** If the grammar admitted the alternative `NN → 'rat' | 'dog'`, the sentence *"The cat saw the rat."* would have only **one** parse. But a sentence like *"I saw the man with the telescope."* has **two** parses (instrumental vs. possessive). The standard heuristic is: > *Prefer the parse with the lowest attachment cost — attach new constituents to the **nearest** available non-terminal.* (This is the **Right-Association** or **Late Closure** heuristic of Gibson's dependency-locality theory.) --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Heuristics vs. Statistical vs. Neural NLP | Dimension | Heuristics-Based NLP | Statistical NLP | Neural NLP | |---|---|---|---| | **Knowledge Source** | Hand-coded rules | Annotated corpus | Raw corpus + embeddings | | **Representation** | Symbolic (rules, trees) | Probabilistic (HMM, CRF) | Distributed (vectors) | | **Training Data Needed** | None | Tens of thousands of tokens | Millions of tokens | | **Inference Cost** | Very low (CPU) | Low–Medium | High (GPU preferred) | | **Interpretability** | **Very high** | Medium | Low (black box) | | **Generalisation** | Poor | Good | Excellent | | **Best Use Case** | Low-resource, safety-critical | Mid-resource, mature | High-resource, SOTA tasks | | **Classic Example** | ELIZA, MITalk, Lex/Yacc | HMM Tagger, MEMM, CRF | BERT, GPT, T5 | | **Failure Mode** | Brittle on unseen input | Data bias | Adversarial inputs, hallucination | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > All diagrams below use Mermaid syntax. Node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `stageA`, `node1`) to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. Labels are plain text — no markdown or HTML inside double quotes. ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Heuristics-Based NLP Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode[Raw Text Input] --> stageA[Sentence Segmenter
Regex over punctuation] stageA --> stageB[Tokeniser
FSA with longest match] stageB --> stageC[Morphological Analyser
FST lookup in lexicon] stageC --> stageD[POS Tagger
Lexical then contextual rules] stageD --> stageE[Chunker
Regex over POS tags] stageE --> stageF[CFG Parser
Earley or CYK chart] stageF --> stageG[Semantic Interpreter
Rule templates on parse tree] stageG --> stageH[Tie Breaker Module
Selects among ambiguous outputs] stageH --> outNode[Structured Representation
Tree, frame, or predicate logic] ``` ### 4.2 State Diagram of the Plural-Morphology FST ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> q0 q0 --> q0 : default char pass through q0 --> q_y : read y at word end q_y --> q_acc : ies substitution q0 --> q_acc : x s z es substitution q0 --> q_c : read c q_c --> q_acc : h es substitution q0 --> q_s : read s q_s --> q_acc : h es substitution q0 --> q_acc : default s addition q_acc --> [*] ``` ### 4.3 Rule Cascade in Brill's POS Tagger ```mermaid flowchart LR inpNode[Tokenised Sentence] --> lexLayer[Lexical Rule Layer
word to tag dictionary lookup] lexLayer --> ctxLayer[Contextual Rule Layer
transform previous tag] ctxLayer --> unkLayer[Unknown Word Heuristic
default NN capitalisation check] unkLayer --> postLayer[Post Processing Rules
sentence start capitalisation] postLayer --> outNode2[Final POS Tag Sequence] ``` ### 4.4 Decision Flow for Heuristic Disambiguation ```mermaid flowchart TD ambigNode[Ambiguous Input Received] --> checkA{Check Lexicon First} checkA -- Hit --> outA[Return Lexicon Entry] checkA -- Miss --> checkB{Check Rule Cascade} checkB -- Single Match --> outB[Apply Single Rule] checkB -- Multiple Matches --> tieNode[Apply Tie Breaker] tieNode --> tieA{Prefer Longest Match} tieA -- Yes --> outC[Return Longest] tieA -- No --> tieB{Prefer Leftmost} tieB -- Yes --> outD[Return Leftmost] tieB -- No --> outE[Return Most Frequent Rule] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** *Define Heuristics-Based NLP. List any four of its key components.* **Model Answer:** > **Heuristics-Based NLP** is the paradigm of Natural Language Processing in which language analysis is performed using **hand-crafted rules, regular expressions, finite-state automata, and symbolic linguistic knowledge**, without any statistical learning from data. **Four key components:** 1. **Regular Expressions (Regex)** — used for lexical pattern matching (e.g., tokenisation, date extraction). 2. **Finite-State Automata / Transducers (FSA / FST)** — used for morphological analysis and tokenisation. 3. **Context-Free Grammars (CFG)** — used for syntactic parsing. 4. **Production Rules (If–Then)** — used for POS tagging, chunking, and semantic interpretation. 5. *(Optional additional)* Lexicons / Gazetteers for lookups. 6. *(Optional additional)* Hand-coded disambiguation heuristics (longest match, leftmost match, minimum edit distance). *(Any four → 3 marks; complete definition → 1 mark.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]** *Explain the "longest match" heuristic with an example.* **Model Answer:** The **longest match heuristic** (also called *Maximal Munch*) is a disambiguation rule used in tokenisation, morphological analysis, and pattern matching. **Principle:** *When multiple rules or patterns can match a substring of the input, the system prefers the match that consumes the **maximum number of characters**.* **Example:** For the input string `banking`: - Rule A: match `bank` (4 characters) as a noun. - Rule B: match `bank` + `ing` (7 characters) as a verb (gerund / present participle). Applying the longest-match heuristic, the tokeniser selects the 7-character match `banking` as a single token, which is the correct verb form. **Where it is used:** POSIX regex engines, English tokenisers, FST-based morphologies, and many early POS taggers. *(Definition → 1 mark, example → 1 mark, application → 1 mark.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) regulations, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers **internal choice**. Below are **two independent alternatives** (Question A or Question B) — students may answer either. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme ESE]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **heuristics-based NLP pipeline**, describing the function of each stage. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Design a **finite-state transducer (FST)** for the morphological rule that converts English singular nouns ending in `y` (preceded by a consonant) into their plural form by replacing `y` with `ies`. Give the formal definition $(Q, \Sigma, \Delta, \delta, q_0, F)$ and trace it for the input `city`. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution (a):** The architecture of a heuristics-based NLP pipeline consists of the following sequential stages (refer Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram): 1. **Sentence Segmentation:** Uses regex `[.!?]+\s+[A-Z]` to detect sentence boundaries. Heuristic: ignore periods inside known abbreviations. 2. **Tokenisation:** A finite-state scanner walks the character stream and emits tokens using the **longest-match** rule. 3. **Morphological Analysis:** An FST consults the lexicon and produces lemma + morphological features (tense, number, case). 4. **POS Tagging:** A two-stage process — first lexical (dictionary lookup), then contextual (Brill's transformation rules). 5. **Chunking:** Regex over POS tags groups words into NP, VP, PP chunks using patterns like `(DT | JJ)* NN` → NP. 6. **Parsing:** A CFG parser (Earley, CYK, or top-down) builds a syntactic parse tree. 7. **Semantic Interpretation:** Rule templates map parse-tree nodes to logical predicates. 8. **Disambiguation Module:** Applies heuristics (lowest attachment, longest match) to choose the most plausible output. **Model Solution (b):** **FST for the `y → ies` rule:** $$T = (Q, \Sigma, \Delta, \delta, q_0, F)$$ - $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_f\}$ - $\Sigma = \{a, \ldots, z\}$ (input alphabet of lower-case English letters) - $\Delta = \{a, \ldots, z\}$ (output alphabet, same as $\Sigma$) - $q_0 = q_0$ (start state) - $F = \{q_f\}$ (accepting state) **Transition function $\delta$:** $$\begin{aligned} \delta(q_0, c) &= (q_0, c) \quad \forall c \in \Sigma \setminus \{y\} \quad \text{(copy non-`y` chars to output)} \\ \delta(q_0, y) &= (q_1, y) \quad \text{(buffer the `y` and enter state } q_1) \\ \delta(q_1, \text{end}) &= (q_f, \text{ies}) \quad \text{(replace `y` with `ies` at word-end)} \end{aligned}$$ **Trace for `city`:** | Step | Current State | Input Symbol | Next State | Output | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $q_0$ | `c` | $q_0$ | `c` | | 2 | $q_0$ | `i` | $q_0$ | `i` | | 3 | $q_0$ | `t` | $q_0$ | `t` | | 4 | $q_0$ | `y` | $q_1$ | `y` | | 5 | $q_1$ | end | $q_f$ | `ies` (replacing `y`) | **Final output:** `c` + `i` + `t` + `ies` = **`cities`** ✓ **Alternative (vowel-before-y) handling:** If the `y` is preceded by a vowel (as in `boy` → `boys`, NOT `boies`), an additional state $q_v$ is required. The rule becomes: $$\begin{aligned} \delta(q_0, a) &= (q_v, a) \\ \delta(q_0, e) &= (q_v, e) \\ \delta(q_0, i) &= (q_v, i) \\ \delta(q_0, o) &= (q_v, o) \\ \delta(q_0, u) &= (q_v, u) \\ \delta(q_v, y) &= (q_f, \text{ys}) \quad \text{(just add `s`)} \end{aligned}$$ This makes the transducer linguistically accurate. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme ESE]** **(a)** Explain **Regular Expressions** and their role in NLP. Write a regex (with justification) to extract valid email addresses from a piece of text. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Define a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)**. Construct a CFG that generates the language of balanced parentheses and demonstrate a parse tree for the string `((()))`. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution (a):** **Regular Expressions:** A regular expression is a formal language for specifying patterns over strings. It is built from atomic symbols using concatenation, alternation (`|`), and Kleene star (`*`). Regexes are **equivalent in expressive power to finite-state automata** and are widely used in NLP for **tokenisation, sentence segmentation, named-entity recognition, and pattern-based information extraction**. **Regex for Email Extraction:** $$\boxed{\texttt{[A-Za-z0-9.\_\%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\textbackslash .[A-Za-z]\{2,\}}}$$ **Component breakdown:** | Component | Matches | Justification | |---|---|---| | `[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+` | Local part of the email | One or more allowed characters (letters, digits, dot, underscore, percent, plus, hyphen) | | `@` | Literal `@` symbol | Mandatory separator | | `[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` | Domain name part | Letters, digits, dots, hyphens | | `\.` | Literal dot before TLD | Escaped to match the actual dot character | | `[A-Za-z]{2,}` | Top-level domain (TLD) | At least 2 alphabetic characters (e.g., `com`, `org`, `in`) | **Engineering Caveat:** The RFC 5321 email grammar is actually **not regular** in the strict sense (it allows nested comments and quoted local parts). Hence, the regex above is a *heuristic approximation* — this is a classic illustration of how Heuristics-Based NLP trades **strict correctness for practical utility**. **Model Solution (b):** **Definition of CFG:** A Context-Free Grammar is a 4-tuple $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ where $V$ is a finite set of non-terminals, $\Sigma$ a finite set of terminals, $R \subseteq V \times (V \cup \Sigma)^*$ a finite set of production rules, and $S \in V$ the start symbol. CFGs generate context-free languages and are recognised by **pushdown automata**. **CFG for Balanced Parentheses:** $$G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$$ - $V = \{S\}$ (one non-terminal) - $\Sigma = \{ (, ) \}$ (two terminal symbols) - $S = S$ (the start symbol) - $R$ contains the following rules: $$\begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow ( \; S \; ) \quad \text{(wrap an `S` in matching parentheses)} \\ S &\rightarrow S \; S \quad \text{(concatenate two balanced strings)} \\ S &\rightarrow \varepsilon \quad \text{(the empty string is balanced)} \end{aligned}$$ **Parse Tree for `((()))`:** Apply the rule $S \rightarrow (S)$ three times, then $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ at the innermost level: ``` S | ( S ) | ( S ) | ( S ) | ε ``` Read leaves left-to-right: `( ( ( ε ) ) )` = **`((()))`** ✓ **Verification by CYK Algorithm (Chomsky Normal Form):** For the string to be in $L(G)$, the CYK table entry $T[1, 6]$ must contain $S$. The conversion to CNF replaces $S \rightarrow (S)$ with binary rules: $$\begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow L \; R \quad \text{where } L \rightarrow (, \; R \rightarrow S \; P, \; P \rightarrow ) \\ \end{aligned}$$ CYK runs in $O(n^3)$ time and confirms that `((()))` is generated by the grammar. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Heuristics-Based NLP Questions (KTU 2024):** > 1. **Forgetting to define all five components of a DFA/FST** — students often write only the transition function and skip $Q$, $\Sigma$, $\Delta$, $q_0$, $F$. Each missing element costs **½ to 1 mark**. > 2. **Confusing Regex with CFG** — Regexes cannot handle nested structures (e.g., balanced parentheses). A KTU trap question asks *"Can regex match balanced parentheses?"* — the correct answer is **NO**, because balanced parens require a **counting** mechanism (pushdown automaton), which is a CFG-level construct. > 3. **Skipping the Heuristic Justification** — the examiner wants to see *why* a particular rule is applied, not just *that* it is applied. Always state the heuristic (longest match, leftmost, minimum cost, etc.). > 4. **Confusing Tokenisation with Sentence Segmentation** — they are two distinct pipeline stages. Sentence segmentation splits on punctuation at the sentence level; tokenisation splits a sentence into words/sub-words. > 5. **Forgetting Chomsky Hierarchy** — at least one Part B question in the last three KTU papers has asked students to *classify a given grammar* in the Chomsky hierarchy. Memorise the four levels and the corresponding automaton. > 6. **Ignoring Edge Cases** — in regex questions, always consider the case where the pattern occurs at the start/end of the string (use `\b` word boundaries). > 7. **No diagram for 7-mark questions** — KTU examiners explicitly state *"neat block diagram"* in the question paper. A missing diagram typically costs **2 marks** in the architecture-style question. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Heuristics-Based NLP** - ✅ **Heuristics-Based NLP** = rule-based, symbolic, hand-coded NLP without statistical or neural learning. - ✅ **Key building blocks:** Regex, FSA/FST, CFG, Production Rules, Lexicons. - ✅ **Regex operators** to memorise: `.`, `*`, `+`, `?`, `[]`, `^`, `$`, `\d`, `\w`, `\b`, `()`. - ✅ **DFA definition** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ and **NFA definition** (subset construction for conversion). - ✅ **FST** extends FSA by emitting output symbols per transition — used in morphology. - ✅ **CFG definition** $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ — used in syntactic parsing. - ✅ **Chomsky Hierarchy:** Type-3 (Regular, FSA) ⊂ Type-2 (Context-Free, PDA) ⊂ Type-1 (Context-Sensitive, LBA) ⊂ Type-0 (Unrestricted, TM). - ✅ **Disambiguation heuristics:** Longest match (Maximal Munch), Leftmost match, Right Association, Minimum edit distance. - ✅ **Brill Tagger** is the canonical *transformation-based learning* approach — a hybrid between heuristics and statistics. - ✅ **Heuristics-Based NLP Pros:** Interpretable, deterministic, data-free, low-cost, ideal for low-resource languages. - ✅ **Heuristics-Based NLP Cons:** Brittle, knowledge-acquisition bottleneck, poor generalisation, no learning. - ✅ **Classic systems to mention in answers:** ELIZA (1966), SHRDLU, Lex/Yacc, MITalk, Kleene/Koskenniemi morphology, HFST, libpostal, AIML. - ✅ **Formula to memorise:** $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ and the Levenshtein recursion (Section 2.3, rows 9–12). - ✅ **Engineering insight:** Even modern systems like *Elasticsearch* tokenisers and *regex-based log parsers* rely on heuristics-based NLP. The paradigm is **not obsolete** — it is **complementary** to statistical and neural approaches. - ✅ **KTU 2024 contrast requirement:** Always be ready to compare Heuristics vs. Statistical vs. Neural NLP along the dimensions: knowledge source, training data, interpretability, generalisation, and cost. ---

topics.title: Machine Learning & Deep Learning for NLP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning & Deep Learning for NLP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Machine Learning (ML) for NLP** is the computational paradigm of designing algorithms that can automatically learn statistical patterns, linguistic structures, and semantic representations from large corpora of natural language text, without being explicitly programmed with handcrafted rules. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, ML for NLP encompasses **supervised**, **unsupervised**, **semi-supervised**, and **reinforcement learning** approaches that transform raw text into numerical feature spaces amenable to statistical generalization.

**Deep Learning (DL) for NLP** is a specialized sub-branch of ML that employs **multi-layered artificial neural networks** (typically with more than three hidden layers) to learn hierarchical representations of language. DL models such as **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)**, **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** networks, **Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs)**, and the **Transformer architecture** have become the de-facto standard for modern NLP tasks including machine translation, sentiment classification, named entity recognition, and question answering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 mandates understanding of how ML and DL underpin every downstream NLP pipeline. Students MUST be able to differentiate classical feature-engineered ML approaches from end-to-end representation-learning DL approaches.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Child Learning Language"**

Imagine teaching a child to recognize whether a movie review is *positive* or *negative*.

- **Classical Machine Learning** is like giving the child a **checklist**: count the number of "good" words vs. "bad" words, check the presence of exclamation marks, measure average word length, etc. The parent (engineer) decides *what* to look at (features), and the child simply plugs these numbers into a learned formula (model).

- **Deep Learning** is like letting the child **read thousands of reviews by themselves** and figure out, layer by layer, that individual letters form words, words form phrases, phrases form sentiments, and sentiments form reviews. The DL model *automatically discovers* the features.

In both cases, the child improves with **experience (data)**. ML is like learning with flashcards and rules; DL is like learning by immersion and intuition.

## 1.3 Why ML & DL Matter for NLP

Language is **ambiguous, context-dependent, and high-dimensional**. Handcrafted linguistic rules (regular expressions, context-free grammars) cannot scale to the **exponential combinations** of vocabulary, syntax, and semantics found in real text.

| Challenge | ML/DL Solution |
|-----------|----------------|
| Polysemy (one word, many meanings) | Contextual embeddings from Transformers |
| Unknown words (Out-of-Vocabulary) | Sub-word tokenization (BPE, WordPiece) |
| Sarcasm and implicit sentiment | Deep semantic encoders (BERT, LSTM) |
| Domain adaptation | Transfer learning & fine-tuning |
| Data sparsity | Distributed representations |

## 1.4 Key Metrics & Constants

- **Vocabulary Size (\(V\))**: Typically ranges from **\(10^4\)** to **\(10^6\)** for large corpora.
- **Embedding Dimension (\(d\))**: Usually **50, 100, 200, 300** for static embeddings; **768, 1024, 2048** for Transformer-based contextual embeddings.
- **Learning Rate (\(\eta\))**: Typically **\(10^{-3}\) to \(10^{-5}\)** for DL models.
- **Perplexity (PPL)**: Standard evaluation metric for language models, where **lower is better**.
- **BLEU Score**: Ranges from **0 to 1** (or 0–100 scale); measures n-gram overlap in translation.
- **F1-Score**: Harmonic mean of precision and recall; the **standard classification metric**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Machine Learning for NLP

### 2.1.1 Supervised Learning
The model learns a mapping function from labeled input-output pairs \(\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}\).

**How it works for NLP:**
1. Collect a labeled corpus (e.g., movie reviews with positive/negative tags).
2. Convert text to numerical features (Bag-of-Words, TF-IDF, embeddings).
3. Train a classifier (Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, SVM, Neural Network).
4. Evaluate on a held-out test set.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common NLP Supervised Tasks:** Text Classification, Named Entity Recognition (NER), Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging, Machine Translation (parallel corpora).

### 2.1.2 Unsupervised Learning
The model discovers hidden structure in unlabeled data.

**How it works for NLP:**
1. Take a raw, unlabeled text corpus.
2. Apply algorithms that find patterns, clusters, or low-dimensional representations.
3. Use the discovered structure for downstream tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common NLP Unsupervised Tasks:** Topic Modeling (LDA, NMF), Word Embedding Learning (Word2Vec, GloVe), Document Clustering, Language Model Pre-training.

### 2.1.3 Semi-Supervised Learning
Combines a small amount of labeled data with a large amount of unlabeled data. Modern NLP relies heavily on this paradigm (e.g., pre-train on unlabeled text, fine-tune on labeled text).

### 2.1.4 Reinforcement Learning
An agent learns to perform actions in an environment to maximize a cumulative reward signal. In NLP, used for **dialogue systems**, **summarization**, and **machine translation with non-differentiable metrics**.

## 2.2 The Classical ML Pipeline for NLP

```
Raw Text → Preprocessing → Feature Extraction → Model Training → Prediction
```

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Text Preprocessing**: Tokenization, lowercasing, stop-word removal, stemming, lemmatization.
2. **Feature Extraction**:
   - **Bag-of-Words (BoW)**: Represents a document as a vector of word counts.
   - **TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency)**: Weights words by their importance.
   - **N-grams**: Captures local word-order information.
3. **Model Training**: Apply Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, SVM, or Random Forests.
4. **Prediction**: Use the trained model to infer labels for unseen text.

## 2.3 The Deep Learning Paradigm Shift

DL eliminates the manual feature engineering step by **learning representations end-to-end**. Instead of handcrafting features, the network's hidden layers learn increasingly abstract representations:

- **Layer 1**: Character-level features (n-grams of characters).
- **Layer 2**: Word-level features (morphology).
- **Layer 3**: Syntactic features (parts of speech, dependencies).
- **Layer 4+**: Semantic features (meaning, sentiment, entailment).

## 2.4 Core Neural Architectures for NLP

### 2.4.1 Feed-Forward Neural Networks (FFNN)
The simplest DL architecture. Each neuron in a layer is connected to every neuron in the next layer. Used as a baseline or as the final classification layer in larger networks.

### 2.4.2 Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)
Designed to process **sequential data**. Maintains a hidden state \(h_t\) that is updated at each time step:

$$h_t = f(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t + b)$$

Where:
- \(x_t\): input at time step \(t\) (e.g., word embedding).
- \(h_{t-1}\): previous hidden state.
- \(W_h, W_x\): learnable weight matrices.
- \(f\): non-linear activation (typically \(\tanh\) or ReLU).

**Problem:** Suffer from the **vanishing gradient** problem, making it hard to learn long-range dependencies.

### 2.4.3 Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)
An advanced RNN that uses **three gates** (input, forget, output) and a **cell state** to selectively remember or forget information over long sequences.

$$f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$$
$$i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$$
$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$$
$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$
$$o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$$
$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

### 2.4.4 Gated Recurrent Units (GRU)
A simplified LSTM with only two gates (reset and update). Often performs comparably to LSTM with fewer parameters.

### 2.4.5 Transformer Architecture
The current state-of-the-art for most NLP tasks. Uses **self-attention** to model relationships between all positions in a sequence in parallel.

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$

Where \(Q\), \(K\), \(V\) are the Query, Key, and Value matrices derived from the input embeddings, and \(d_k\) is the dimension of the keys.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Equation** | **Description** | **Typical Range/Units** |
|-------------|------------------------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| TF-IDF Weight | $\text{tfidf}(t, d) = \text{tf}(t, d) \cdot \log\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$ | Importance of term \(t\) in doc \(d\) | \(0\) to \(\infty\) |
| Softmax Activation | $\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | Converts logits to probabilities | \(0\) to \(1\), sums to \(1\) |
| Cross-Entropy Loss | $L = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Standard loss for classification | \(0\) to \(\infty\) |
| RNN Hidden Update | $h_t = f(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t + b)$ | Sequential state update | Vector of dim \(d\) |
| LSTM Forget Gate | $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | Controls memory retention | \(0\) to \(1\) per dim |
| Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | Transformer attention mechanism | Weighted sum of values |
| Perplexity | $\text{PPL} = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(w_i \vert w_{<i})\right)$ | Language model evaluation | Lower is better; \(\geq 1\) |
| BLEU Score | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | Translation quality metric | \(0\) to \(1\) (or \(0\) to \(100\)) |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision/Recall | \(0\) to \(1\) |
| Gradient Descent Update | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_{\theta} L(\theta)$ | Parameter optimization rule | \(\theta\) updated per step |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application** | **ML/DL Technique Used** | **Industry Example** |
|------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Email Spam Filtering | Naive Bayes / SVM | Gmail, Outlook |
| Chatbots & Virtual Assistants | RNN / Transformer + RL | ChatGPT, Alexa, Google Assistant |
| Machine Translation | Encoder-Decoder LSTM / Transformer | Google Translate, DeepL |
| Sentiment Analysis | LSTM / BERT | Brand monitoring tools |
| Search Engines | Learning-to-Rank (LambdaMART) | Google, Bing |
| Voice Assistants (ASR + NLU) | Deep CNNs + Transformers | Siri, Google Assistant |
| Code Autocompletion | Large Language Models | GitHub Copilot, Tabnine |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: TF-IDF Weight

**Problem Setup:** Given a corpus \(\mathcal{D} = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_N\}\) containing \(N\) documents, compute the importance of a term \(t\) in a specific document \(d\).

**Step 1 — Term Frequency (TF):**
The simplest choice is the raw count of term \(t\) in document \(d\):
$$\text{tf}(t, d) = \text{count of } t \text{ in } d$$

A normalized variant divides by the total number of terms in \(d\):
$$\text{tf}_{\text{norm}}(t, d) = \frac{\text{count}(t, d)}{\sum_{t' \in d} \text{count}(t', d)}$$

**Step 2 — Document Frequency (DF):**
The number of documents in which term \(t\) appears:
$$\text{df}(t) = \mid \{d \in \mathcal{D} : t \in d\} \mid$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):**
A measure of how "rare" or "informative" term \(t\) is across the entire corpus:
$$\text{idf}(t) = \log \frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$$

**Why log?** Without log, the IDF would be dominated by very rare terms, causing numerical instability. Log dampens this effect.

**Step 4 — TF-IDF Product:**
$$\text{tfidf}(t, d) = \text{tf}(t, d) \cdot \text{idf}(t) = \text{tf}(t, d) \cdot \log \frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$$

**Interpretation:** A term gets a high TF-IDF score when it appears frequently in a *specific* document but rarely in the *overall* corpus.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Softmax Function

**Problem Setup:** Convert a vector of real-valued logits \(\mathbf{z} = (z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_K)\) into a probability distribution over \(K\) classes.

**Step 1 — Exponential Transformation:**
Apply \(\exp(\cdot)\) to ensure all values are positive:
$$e^{z_1}, e^{z_2}, \ldots, e^{z_K}$$

**Step 2 — Normalization (Divide by Sum):**
$$\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

**Step 3 — Verification of Properties:**
- **Non-negativity:** Since \(\exp(\cdot) > 0\), the numerator is positive.
- **Sums to 1:** $\sum_{i=1}^{K} \sigma(z_i) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{K} e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} = 1$.
- **Preserves order:** Larger \(z_i\) yields larger \(\sigma(z_i)\).

**Step 4 — Numerical Stability Trick:**
For large \(z_i\) values, \(e^{z_i}\) can overflow. Subtract the maximum logit \(M = \max(z)\) before exponentiation:
$$\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i - M}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j - M}}$$

This does not change the output (the \(M\) terms cancel) but keeps exponentials bounded.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cross-Entropy Loss

**Problem Setup:** Given predicted probabilities \(\hat{y} = (\hat{y}_1, \ldots, \hat{y}_N)\) and true one-hot labels \(y = (y_1, \ldots, y_N)\), measure the dissimilarity.

**Step 1 — Information Content (Surprise):**
The information content of an event with probability \(p\) is:
$$I(p) = -\log(p)$$

**Step 2 — Expected Surprise Over the True Distribution:**
$$L = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$$

Since \(y\) is one-hot, only the term corresponding to the correct class survives:
$$L = -\log(\hat{y}_{i^*}) \quad \text{where } i^* \text{ is the true class}$$

**Step 3 — Connection to Maximum Likelihood:**
Minimizing cross-entropy is equivalent to maximizing the log-likelihood of the correct class under the model's predicted distribution.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Attention

**Problem Setup:** Given a sequence of \(n\) input embeddings, compute a weighted representation where each position attends to all other positions.

**Step 1 — Project Inputs to Q, K, V:**
Using learned weight matrices \(W_Q, W_K, W_V\):
$$Q = X W_Q, \quad K = X W_K, \quad V = X W_V$$

**Step 2 — Compute Compatibility Scores:**
The dot product \(QK^T\) produces an \(n \times n\) matrix where entry \((i, j)\) measures how much position \(i\) should attend to position \(j\).

**Step 3 — Scale by \(\sqrt{d_k}\):**
$$\text{scores} = \frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Why scale?** Without scaling, for large \(d_k\), the dot products grow in magnitude, pushing the softmax into regions of very small gradients (saturation).

**Step 4 — Apply Softmax Row-Wise:**
$$A = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)$$

Each row of \(A\) is a probability distribution over positions.

**Step 5 — Weighted Sum with Values:**
$$\text{Output} = A \cdot V = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Classical ML vs. Deep Learning for NLP

| **Dimension** | **Classical ML** | **Deep Learning** |
|---------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Feature Engineering | Manual (TF-IDF, BoW, n-grams) | Automatic (learned representations) |
| Data Requirement | Moderate (\(10^3\)–\(10^5\) samples) | Large (\(10^5\)–\(10^9\) tokens) |
| Computational Cost | Low (CPU sufficient) | High (GPU/TPU required) |
| Interpretability | High (weights, coefficients) | Low (black-box) |
| Long-Range Dependencies | Weak (bag-of-words assumption) | Strong (attention, LSTM) |
| Out-of-Vocabulary Handling | Poor (must handle explicitly) | Good (sub-word tokenization) |
| Training Time | Seconds to minutes | Hours to weeks |
| Inference Speed | Fast | Moderate (depends on model size) |
| Example Models | Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression | RNN, LSTM, GRU, Transformer, BERT |
| Best Use Case | Small datasets, explainability-critical | Large datasets, SOTA accuracy |

---

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Inp["Input x_t"] --> Concat1["Concatenate with h_t-1"]
    PrevH["Previous Hidden h_t-1"] --> Concat1
    Concat1 --> FGate["Forget Gate sigmoid"]
    Concat1 --> IGate["Input Gate sigmoid"]
    Concat1 --> Cand["Candidate tanh"]
    FGate --> Mult1["Multiply with C_t-1"]
    PrevC["Previous Cell C_t-1"] --> Mult1
    Mult1 --> Add["Add Operation"]
    IGate --> Mult2["Multiply with Candidate"]
    Cand --> Mult2
    Mult2 --> Add
    Concat1 --> OGate["Output Gate sigmoid"]
    Add --> TanhOut["tanh of New Cell"]
    OGate --> Mult3["Multiply with tanh"]
    TanhOut --> Mult3
    Mult3 --> NewH["New Hidden h_t"]
    Add --> NewC["New Cell C_t"]
```

    E --> ProjK["Linear Projection W_K"]
    E --> ProjV["Linear Projection W_V"]

    ProjK --> Dot
    Dot --> Scale["Divide by sqrt of d_k"]
    Scale --> Soft["Softmax over keys"]
    Soft --> Weight["Attention Weights"]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Machine Learning for NLP. List the four main paradigms of machine learning with one NLP application for each.**

**Model Answer:**

Machine Learning for NLP is a subfield of artificial intelligence that develops algorithms capable of learning linguistic patterns from text data without being explicitly programmed.

The four main paradigms are:

1. **Supervised Learning** — learns from labeled input-output pairs. *NLP Application:* Text classification (spam detection).
2. **Unsupervised Learning** — discovers hidden structure in unlabeled data. *NLP Application:* Topic modeling using LDA.
3. **Semi-Supervised Learning** — combines small labeled data with large unlabeled data. *NLP Application:* Pre-training + fine-tuning (e.g., BERT).
4. **Reinforcement Learning** — learns via reward signals from an environment. *NLP Application:* Dialogue system optimization.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs. How does the LSTM architecture address it?**

**Model Answer:**

The **vanishing gradient problem** occurs during backpropagation through time (BPTT) in RNNs. When gradients are propagated across many time steps, they are repeatedly multiplied by values less than 1, causing them to shrink exponentially toward zero. As a result, early time steps receive negligible gradient updates, and the network cannot learn long-range dependencies.

**LSTM addresses this** through three key mechanisms:

1. **Cell State (\(C_t\))**: A separate memory highway that carries information across time steps with minimal transformation.
2. **Forget Gate (\(f_t\))**: Selectively retains or discards information using a sigmoid-activated gate, allowing gradients to flow unimpeded when \(f_t \approx 1\).
3. **Input and Output Gates (\(i_t, o_t\))**: Control what new information is written to the cell state and what is read out as the hidden state.

This gated design creates a near-linear path for gradient flow, mitigating the vanishing gradient problem and enabling learning of long-range dependencies.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the classical Machine Learning pipeline for an NLP text classification task. Explain with a diagram how TF-IDF features are computed and used with a Naive Bayes classifier.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the Pipeline Stages:**
A classical ML pipeline for NLP text classification consists of:
1. Text preprocessing
2. Feature extraction (TF-IDF)
3. Model training (Naive Bayes)
4. Prediction and evaluation

**Step 2 — Text Preprocessing:**
- **Tokenization**: Split text into words (e.g., "Great movie!" → ["great", "movie"]).
- **Lowercasing**: Convert all characters to lowercase.
- **Stop-word Removal**: Remove common words like "the", "is", "a".
- **Stemming/Lemmatization**: Reduce words to root form (e.g., "running" → "run").

**Step 3 — TF-IDF Feature Extraction:**
Given a corpus with \(N\) documents:
- Compute TF: $\text{tf}(t, d) = \text{count}(t, d)$
- Compute IDF: $\text{idf}(t) = \log \frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$
- TF-IDF: $\text{tfidf}(t, d) = \text{tf}(t, d) \cdot \text{idf}(t)$

**Step 4 — Naive Bayes Classification:**
Naive Bayes applies Bayes' theorem with the "naive" assumption of conditional independence between features:

$$P(c \vert d) \propto P(c) \prod_{t \in d} P(t \vert c)$$

Where:
- \(c\) = class label (e.g., "spam" or "ham")
- \(d\) = document
- \(P(c)\) = prior probability of class
- \(P(t \vert c)\) = likelihood of term \(t\) given class \(c\)

The predicted class is:
$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \prod_{t \in d} P(t \vert c)$$

**Step 5 — Smoothing (Laplace):**
To handle zero probabilities for unseen terms:
$$P(t \vert c) = \frac{\text{count}(t, c) + 1}{\sum_{t'} \text{count}(t', c) + V}$$

Where \(V\) is vocabulary size.

**Step 6 — Evaluation:**
Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-score on a held-out test set.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast classical Machine Learning approaches with Deep Learning approaches for NLP. Discuss at least four key differences with examples.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

| **Aspect** | **Classical ML** | **Deep Learning** |
|------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Feature Engineering** | Manual: TF-IDF, BoW | Automatic: learned embeddings |
| **Data Requirement** | Works well with small datasets | Requires large datasets |
| **Interpretability** | High (feature weights) | Low (black-box) |
| **Sequential Modeling** | Weak (bag-of-words loses order) | Strong (RNN, LSTM, Transformer) |
| **Computational Cost** | Low (CPU) | High (GPU/TPU) |
| **Example Task** | Spam classification with SVM | Sentiment analysis with BERT |

**Detailed Discussion:**

1. **Feature Engineering:** Classical ML requires the engineer to manually craft features (e.g., TF-IDF vectors). DL learns features automatically from raw text, reducing human effort but requiring more data.

2. **Data Requirements:** Classical ML like Naive Bayes can work effectively with thousands of samples. DL models typically require millions of tokens to learn meaningful representations.

3. **Sequential Dependencies:** Classical BoW ignores word order. DL architectures (LSTM, Transformer) explicitly model sequential and contextual relationships.

4. **Interpretability:** Classical ML models offer transparent decision-making (e.g., top-weighted features). DL models are often "black boxes," though tools like attention visualization provide partial insight.

5. **Computational Resources:** Classical ML can be trained on a laptop in seconds. DL training of large models like GPT-3 requires thousands of GPUs and weeks of compute time.

**Conclusion:** The choice depends on data availability, computational resources, interpretability requirements, and task complexity. For small datasets and explainable systems, classical ML is preferable; for large-scale, state-of-the-art performance, DL is the standard.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of an LSTM network. Derive the equations for the input gate, forget gate, output gate, and cell state update. Discuss why LSTM outperforms vanilla RNN for long sequences.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Motivation:**
Vanilla RNNs suffer from vanishing/exploding gradients when processing long sequences. LSTM solves this by introducing a **cell state** and **gating mechanisms**.

**Step 2 — LSTM Architecture Components:**
An LSTM cell at time \(t\) takes three inputs:
- Current input \(x_t\)
- Previous hidden state \(h_{t-1}\)
- Previous cell state \(C_{t-1}\)

It produces two outputs:
- Current hidden state \(h_t\)
- Current cell state \(C_t\)

**Step 3 — Forget Gate (\(f_t\)):**
Controls what information to discard from the cell state:
$$f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$$

Output range: \([0, 1]\). \(f_t = 0\) means "completely forget," \(f_t = 1\) means "completely retain."

**Step 4 — Input Gate (\(i_t\)) and Candidate Values (\(\tilde{C}_t\)):**
Decides what new information to store:
$$i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$$
$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$$

**Step 5 — Cell State Update:**
$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

The forget gate scales the old cell state, and the input gate scales the new candidate values.

**Step 6 — Output Gate (\(o_t\)) and Hidden State:**
$$o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$$
$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

**Step 7 — Why LSTM Outperforms Vanilla RNN:**
- **Additive Cell State Update**: The cell state update is *additive* (\(C_{t-1} + \text{new}\)), not multiplicative. Gradients flow through this additive path with minimal decay.
- **Selective Memory**: Gates allow the network to *selectively* remember or forget information, unlike vanilla RNN which compresses all information into a single hidden state.
- **Long-Range Dependencies**: Effective for sequences of length \(>100\), where vanilla RNN fails.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the Transformer architecture's self-attention mechanism. Explain the scaled dot-product attention formula and discuss why scaling by \(\sqrt{d_k}\) is necessary.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Background:**
Introduced in "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., 2017), the Transformer replaces recurrence with **self-attention**, enabling parallel processing of all positions.

**Step 2 — Self-Attention Concept:**
Each position in the sequence computes a weighted sum of all other positions, where the weights represent *relevance*. This allows the model to capture long-range dependencies directly.

**Step 3 — Q, K, V Projections:**
Given input embeddings \(X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}\):
$$Q = X W_Q, \quad K = X W_K, \quad V = X W_V$$

Where \(W_Q, W_K, W_V\) are learned projection matrices.

**Step 4 — Scaled Dot-Product Attention:**
$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

**Step 5 — Step-by-Step Computation:**
1. Compute compatibility scores: \(QK^T\) (shape \(n \times n\)).
2. Scale by \(\sqrt{d_k}\) to control variance.
3. Apply softmax row-wise to get attention weights.
4. Multiply by \(V\) to get the contextualized output.

**Step 6 — Why Scale by \(\sqrt{d_k}\)?**

Assume the components of \(Q\) and \(K\) are independent random variables with mean 0 and variance 1. Then the dot product \(q \cdot k = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i\) has:
- Mean: 0
- Variance: \(d_k\)

For large \(d_k\), the dot products have **large magnitude**, pushing the softmax into regions where it outputs values close to 0 or 1. This results in **extremely small gradients** (saturation), making learning difficult.

By dividing by \(\sqrt{d_k}\), we normalize the variance back to 1:
$$\text{Var}\left(\frac{q \cdot k}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) = 1$$

This keeps the softmax in a well-behaved regime where gradients remain informative.

**Step 7 — Multi-Head Attention:**
In practice, the Transformer uses multiple attention heads in parallel:
$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) W_O$$
$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_Q^i, KW_K^i, VW_V^i)$$

Each head learns to attend to different aspects of the sequence (e.g., syntactic vs. semantic).

**Step 8 — Advantages over RNN:**
- **Parallelization**: All positions are processed simultaneously, unlike sequential RNNs.
- **Long-Range Dependencies**: Direct connections between any two positions, regardless of distance.
- **Constant Path Length**: Information travels directly from any position to any other in \(O(1)\) steps.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing RNN cell state with hidden state.** The cell state \(C_t\) is the long-term memory; the hidden state \(h_t\) is the short-term output. Mixing them up loses marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to scale by \(\sqrt{d_k}\) in the attention formula.** Examiners specifically test for this. Writing \(\text{softmax}(QK^T)V\) without scaling is a 2-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Writing TF-IDF without explaining the intuition.** Students often write the formula but forget to explain *why* IDF uses log (to dampen the effect of very rare terms).
>
> 4. **Treating "Deep Learning" and "Machine Learning" as synonyms.** DL is a *subset* of ML. Examiners test this distinction.
>
> 5. **Omitting the activation function in LSTM gates.** The forget gate uses \(\sigma\) (sigmoid), the candidate uses \(\tanh\). Conflating them is a frequent error.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning vanishing gradients** when discussing why LSTM is preferred over RNN. This is a compulsory 2-mark point in most answers.
>
> 7. **Diagram-Less Answers:** In 7-mark sub-questions, students must include at least one labeled diagram (pipeline, architecture, or flow). Missing diagrams typically cost 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Machine Learning & Deep Learning for NLP**

- **ML Paradigms:** Supervised (labeled), Unsupervised (unlabeled), Semi-Supervised (mixed), Reinforcement (reward-based).
- **Classical ML Pipeline:** Preprocessing → Feature Extraction (BoW/TF-IDF) → Classifier (NB/SVM/LR) → Evaluation.
- **TF-IDF Formula:** $\text{tfidf}(t, d) = \text{tf}(t, d) \cdot \log\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$ — high for terms frequent in a document but rare overall.
- **Softmax:** Converts logits to probabilities; numerically stabilized by subtracting max logit.
- **Cross-Entropy Loss:** $-\sum y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ — standard loss for multi-class classification.
- **RNN Equation:** $h_t = f(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t + b)$ — sequential, suffers from vanishing gradients.
- **LSTM Gates:** Forget (\(f_t\)), Input (\(i_t\)), Output (\(o_t\)) — all use sigmoid; candidate uses tanh.
- **LSTM Cell Update:** $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ — additive update enables long-range memory.
- **GRU:** Simplified LSTM with reset and update gates only; fewer parameters, comparable performance.
- **Transformer Attention:** $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ — parallel, no recurrence.
- **Scaling Factor:** $\sqrt{d_k}$ prevents softmax saturation by normalizing variance to 1.
- **Multi-Head Attention:** Multiple attention heads in parallel, each learning different relationship types.
- **Key Metrics:** Perplexity (language models, lower is better), BLEU (translation, 0–100), F1 (classification, 0–1).
- **ML vs. DL:** Classical ML = manual features, small data, interpretable. DL = automatic features, large data, state-of-the-art accuracy.
- **Modern NLP Trend:** Pre-train on large unlabeled corpora (e.g., BERT, GPT) → fine-tune on task-specific labeled data (semi-supervised paradigm).
- **Real-World Applications:** Spam filtering (NB), translation (Transformer), chatbots (RNN+RL), sentiment analysis (LSTM/BERT), search ranking (Learning-to-Rank).

---

# Module 1 – NLP Pipeline: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NLP Pipeline (Natural Language Processing Pipeline):** A sequential, modular architecture of text-processing stages that transforms unstructured human language data into structured, machine-readable representations suitable for downstream computational analysis, statistical modeling, or deep learning inference.

In the context of **PEIET864 – Introduction to Natural Language Processing (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, an NLP Pipeline is treated as the **backbone workflow** that every NLP application — from a simple spam filter to a state-of-the-art Large Language Model — must traverse (either explicitly or implicitly). Each stage **consumes the output of the previous stage**, applies a transformation, and passes refined information forward.

The classical pipeline is also referred to in literature as the **"Text Preprocessing and Linguistic Annotation Stack"** or the **"NLP Stack."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1, KTU 2024):** The pipeline stages most emphasized at KTU board level are **Tokenization → Stopword Removal → Stemming/Lemmatization → POS Tagging → Named Entity Recognition (NER) → Parsing**. Students are expected to describe each stage, its input, output, and the linguistic level it operates on (morphological, syntactic, semantic, pragmatic).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a **raw ingredient (unstructured text)** entering a restaurant kitchen:

1. **Receiving (Data Acquisition)** — Raw vegetables/text arrive.
2. **Washing (Text Cleaning)** — Dirt/HTML tags/emoji noise is removed.
3. **Chopping (Tokenization)** — The text is sliced into small uniform pieces (tokens).
4. **Sorting (Stopword Removal)** — Useless bits (peels, seeds) are discarded.
5. **Cooking (Lemmatization/Stemming)** — Pieces are softened/normalized into a base form.
6. **Plating with labels (POS Tagging, NER)** — Each piece is labeled (noun, verb, person, location).
7. **Recipe validation (Parsing)** — The dish's grammar/structure is verified.
8. **Serving (Modeling)** — A trained chef (model) tastes it and produces an output (classification, translation, summary).

Just as no dish can be served directly from raw unwashed produce, **no NLP algorithm can operate on raw text without traversing the pipeline.**

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Definition | Linguistic Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Corpus** | A large structured collection of texts | Lexical |
| **Token** | The smallest meaningful unit (word, subword, character) | Morphological |
| **Lemma** | The dictionary/base form of a word | Morphological |
| **Stem** | The crude root form after suffix stripping | Morphological |
| **Parse Tree** | A syntactic representation of grammatical structure | Syntactic |
| **NER** | Identification of named entities (Persons, Organizations, Locations) | Semantic |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stages of the NLP Pipeline — Structured Breakdown

The pipeline is not strictly linear in production systems (feedback loops exist), but for **KTU Module 1 examination purposes**, it is presented in a **canonical 7-stage linear flow**.

### Stage 1 — Text Acquisition (Data Collection)
- **Why:** NLP models require raw text corpora (e.g., news, tweets, Wikipedia dumps).
- **How:** Web scraping, APIs, sensor data, OCR.
- **Output:** A raw text string (often noisy).

### Stage 2 — Text Cleaning / Preprocessing
- **Why:** Real-world text contains noise (HTML, emojis, encoding issues).
- **How:** Remove URLs, punctuation (optional), normalize case, handle Unicode.
- **Output:** Cleaned raw text.

### Stage 3 — Tokenization
- **Why:** Models operate on discrete units, not continuous streams.
- **How:** Split text into tokens (whitespace, regex, BPE, WordPiece).
- **Output:** A list of tokens $\big[ t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n \big]$.

### Stage 4 — Stopword Removal
- **Why:** Common words (the, is, at) carry low semantic weight.
- **How:** Filter tokens against a stopword dictionary (NLTK, spaCy).
- **Output:** Filtered token list.

### Stage 5 — Stemming and Lemmatization (Normalization)
- **Why:** Reduce inflectional variants to a common base to lower dimensionality.
- **How:**
  - **Stemming:** Rule-based chopping (Porter, Snowball).
  - **Lemmatization:** Dictionary + morphological analysis (WordNet).
- **Output:** Normalized tokens.

### Stage 6 — POS Tagging & Named Entity Recognition (NER)
- **Why:** Inject grammatical and semantic labels.
- **How:** HMM, CRF, RNN, Transformers.
- **Output:** Annotated tuples $\big(t_i, \text{tag}_i\big)$ or $\big(t_i, \text{entity}_i\big)$.

### Stage 7 — Parsing (Syntactic & Semantic)
- **Why:** Understand grammatical relationships.
- **How:** Constituency parsing, Dependency parsing.
- **Output:** Parse tree / dependency graph.

### Stage 8 — Feature Representation & Modeling
- **Why:** Convert symbolic tokens into numerical vectors.
- **How:** Bag-of-Words, TF-IDF, Word2Vec, BERT embeddings.
- **Output:** Numerical tensor suitable for ML/DL models.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Metrics Table

> [!NOTE]
> Although the NLP Pipeline itself does not contain many "formulas," the **evaluation metrics** of any downstream stage (especially NER, POS tagging, Parsing) are heavily tested. Master the table below.

| Metric | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Balanced classification |
| **Precision** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | When false positives are costly |
| **Recall** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | When false negatives are costly |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean, balanced metric |
| **TF (Term Frequency)** | $\text{tf}(t, d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | Word importance in a doc |
| **IDF** | $\text{idf}(t) = \log \dfrac{N}{\vert \{d : t \in d\} \vert}$ | Word rarity across corpus |
| **TF-IDF** | $\text{tf-idf}(t, d) = \text{tf}(t, d) \cdot \text{idf}(t)$ | Feature weighting |
| **Perplexity** | $\text{PPL}(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)^{-1/N}$ | Language model evaluation |
| **BLEU Score** | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | Machine translation |

> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The pipe (absolute value) operator $\vert \cdot \vert$ is critical in IDF; **do NOT substitute $N - \vert \cdot \vert$** as the denominator — a frequent valuation pitfall.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Use tokenization + TF-IDF for ranking.
- **Healthcare:** NER pipelines extract drug names, dosages, and diseases from clinical notes.
- **Finance:** Sentiment pipelines process earnings calls for stock prediction.
- **Legal:** Contract clause extraction pipelines automate due diligence.
- **Customer Service:** Chatbot pipelines perform intent classification + NER + response generation.

The pipeline is **engineered, not magical** — every chatbot, translator, and voice assistant you interact with is a refined version of this same architecture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 End-to-End Worked Example — A Single Sentence Through the Pipeline

To make the pipeline **fully tangible**, let us trace a real-world sentence through every stage.

**Input Sentence:**
> *"Dr. Anjali Krishnan, the Chief Data Scientist at Infosys, bought 3 AI books from Amazon India on 05/01/2024."*

---

### Stage 1 — Text Acquisition
Raw text is read from a file/stream.

$$
\text{raw} = \text{"Dr. Anjali Krishnan, the Chief Data Scientist at Infosys, ..."}
$$

---

### Stage 2 — Text Cleaning

- Remove URLs (none here).
- Normalize Unicode (smart quotes → straight quotes).
- Lowercase (optional; preserves NER performance so we **skip lowercasing**).

**Result (cleaned):**
> *"Dr. Anjali Krishnan, the Chief Data Scientist at Infosys, bought 3 AI books from Amazon India on 05/01/2024."*

---

### Stage 3 — Tokenization

Using whitespace + punctuation tokenization:

$$
\text{tokens} = \big[\text{"Dr."}, \text{"Anjali"}, \text{"Krishnan,"}, \text{"the"}, \text{"Chief"}, \text{"Data"}, \text{"Scientist"}, \text{"at"}, \text{"Infosys,"}, \text{"bought"}, \text{"3"}, \text{"AI"}, \text{"books"}, \text{"from"}, \text{"Amazon"}, \text{"India"}, \text{"on"}, \text{"05/01/2024."}\big]
$$

**Note (KTU Pitfall):** "Dr." must NOT be split as $\big[\text{"Dr"}, \text{"."}\big]$ — abbreviation handling requires a smart tokenizer.

---

### Stage 4 — Stopword Removal

Stopwords: $\big\{\text{the, at, on, from}\big\}$.

$$
\text{filtered} = \big[\text{"Dr."}, \text{"Anjali"}, \text{"Krishnan,"}, \text{"Chief"}, \text{"Data"}, \text{"Scientist"}, \text{"Infosys,"}, \text{"bought"}, \text{"3"}, \text{"AI"}, \text{"books"}, \text{"Amazon"}, \text{"India"}, \text{"05/01/2024."}\big]
$$

**Why (intuition):** "the" and "at" carry little semantic signal for our analysis.

---

### Stage 5 — Stemming vs. Lemmatization

Apply **lemmatization** using WordNet:

| Token | Stem (Porter) | Lemma (WordNet) |
|---|---|---|
| bought | bought | buy |
| books | book | book |
| Chief | chief | chief |
| Scientists | scientist | scientist |

**Stemming output:** $\big[\text{"Dr."}, \text{"Anjali"}, \text{"Krishnan"}, \text{"Chief"}, \text{"Data"}, \text{"Scientist"}, \text{"Infosys"}, \text{"bought"}, \text{"3"}, \text{"AI"}, \text{"book"}, \text{"Amazon"}, \text{"India"}, \text{"05/01/2024"}\big]$

**Difference explained:** Stemming is **rule-based and crude** ("bought" → "bought"); lemmatization is **linguistically aware** ("bought" → "buy").

---

### Stage 6 — POS Tagging & NER

#### POS Tagging Output:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{"Anjali"} &\rightarrow \text{NNP (Proper Noun, Singular)} \\
\text{"bought"} &\rightarrow \text{VBD (Verb, Past Tense)} \\
\text{"3"} &\rightarrow \text{CD (Cardinal Number)} \\
\text{"books"} &\rightarrow \text{NNS (Noun, Plural)} \\
\text{"on"} &\rightarrow \text{IN (Preposition)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### NER Output:

| Entity Text | Entity Label |
|---|---|
| Anjali Krishnan | **PERSON** |
| Infosys | **ORG** |
| Amazon India | **ORG** |
| 05/01/2024 | **DATE** |
| 3 | **CARDINAL** |

---

### Stage 7 — Parsing (Dependency)

The dependency tree of the verb **"bought"** is:

- **bought** $\rightarrow$ *nsubj* $\rightarrow$ **Anjali Krishnan**
- **bought** $\rightarrow$ *dobj* $\rightarrow$ **books**
- **bought** $\rightarrow$ *prep_from* $\rightarrow$ **Amazon India**
- **bought** $\rightarrow$ *prep_on* $\rightarrow$ **05/01/2024**

This tree unlocks **who did what to whom, when, where**.

---

### Stage 8 — Feature Representation (TF-IDF Derivation)

Let us compute TF-IDF for the word **"Scientist"** in a corpus of $N = 1000$ documents where the word "Scientist" appears in $\text{df} = 5$ documents and occurs $f = 2$ times in document $d$ of length $\sum_{t'} f_{t',d} = 50$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{tf}(\text{"Scientist"}, d) &= \dfrac{f}{\sum_{t'} f_{t',d}} = \dfrac{2}{50} = 0.04 \\[6pt]
\text{idf}(\text{"Scientist"}) &= \log \dfrac{N}{\text{df}} = \log \dfrac{1000}{5} = \log(200) \approx 2.301 \\[6pt]
\text{tf-idf}(\text{"Scientist"}, d) &= 0.04 \times 2.301 = 0.092
\end{aligned}
$$

This numerical vector now becomes **input** to a downstream classifier.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Stage-wise Input/Output

| # | Stage | Input | Operation | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acquisition | File / API / Web | Read | Raw text string |
| 2 | Cleaning | Raw text | Strip noise | Cleaned text |
| 3 | Tokenization | Cleaned text | Split | Token list |
| 4 | Stopword Removal | Token list | Filter | Reduced token list |
| 5 | Stemming/Lemma | Token list | Normalize | Base form list |
| 6 | POS/NER | Base tokens | Tag | Annotated tokens |
| 7 | Parsing | Annotated tokens | Parse | Parse tree |
| 8 | Vectorization | Tokens/Tree | Embed | Numerical tensor |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
