# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a well-formed parenthesis string. With an example, explain how a parse tree can be represented as a parenthesis string in NLP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A parenthesis string is a sequence of brackets `(` and `)` over an alphabet $\Sigma$ (often augmented with non-terminal labels). It is **well-formed** if every opening bracket has a matching closing bracket of the same type and brackets are properly nested — formally, it belongs to the **Dyck language** $D_n$.

A parse tree is represented as a parenthesis string by recursively writing each node as `(Label child_1 child_2 ... child_k)`. For the sentence "the cat sleeps":

$$w = (S \, (NP \, \text{the} \, \text{cat}) \, (VP \, \text{sleeps}))$$

Each non-terminal becomes a bracket pair, and terminals appear as bare words. **Marks split:** Definition 1 m, Example conversion 1 m, Significance 1 m.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List and briefly define any three modification operations applicable to parenthesis strings in NLP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Substitution ($\sigma$):** Replaces a sub-tree (a sub-string) with another having the same root label. Used in grammar-rule rewriting.
2. **Insertion ($\iota$):** Adds a new sub-tree as a child of an existing node. Used to enrich a sentence with modifiers (e.g., adding an ADVP).
3. **Deletion ($\delta$):** Removes a sub-tree from its parent. Used to strip optional constituents.
4. *(Optional)* **Re-bracketing ($\rho$):** Re-arranges the nesting order without changing labels — used in tree-translation models.

**Marks split:** 1 mark per operation (any three correctly explained).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
>
> - Students **lose 2 marks** by writing just the symbol (e.g., $\sigma$) without a formal definition.
> - Students **lose 3 marks** by skipping the well-formedness verification step after every modification.
> - Students **lose 2 marks** by failing to label each bracket pair with the corresponding non-terminal.
> - **Always** state the closed-form formula for Catalan numbers explicitly — do not just write the recurrence.
> - **Always** show one numerical substitution (e.g., $C_4 = 14$) — it confirms you understand the formula.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the linearisation of a parse tree into a parenthesis string. Using the CFG given below, derive the parenthesis string for the sentence "*Rama reads a book*".

$$S \rightarrow NP \; VP$$
$$NP \rightarrow \text{PropN} \mid D \; N$$
$$VP \rightarrow V \; NP$$
$$D \rightarrow a$$
$$N \rightarrow \text{book}$$
$$\text{PropN} \rightarrow \text{Rama}$$
$$V \rightarrow \text{reads}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply a **deletion operation** $\delta$ on the resulting parenthesis string to remove the determiner phrase "*a book*" leaving only the proper noun subject. Verify that the resulting string is well-formed.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

We apply the recursive linearisation $\text{par}(T) = (X \; \text{par}(T_1) \ldots \text{par}(T_k))$.

Step 1: Apply $S \rightarrow NP \; VP$. This gives the bracket scaffold $(S \; \square \; \square)$.

Step 2: Apply $NP \rightarrow \text{PropN}$. Slot 1 becomes $\text{Rama}$. The bracket form is now $(S \; \text{Rama} \; \square)$.

Step 3: Apply $VP \rightarrow V \; NP$. Slot 2 becomes $(VP \; \square \; \square)$. So we get:

$$(S \; \text{Rama} \; (VP \; \square \; \square))$$

Step 4: Apply $V \rightarrow \text{reads}$. The first inner slot becomes `reads`:

$$(S \; \text{Rama} \; (VP \; \text{reads} \; \square))$$

Step 5: Apply $NP \rightarrow D \; N$ for the inner NP slot. So we get $(D \; \square) \; (N \; \square)$:

$$(S \; \text{Rama} \; (VP \; \text{reads} \; (D \; \square) \; (N \; \square)))$$

Step 6: Lexical insertions $D \rightarrow a$ and $N \rightarrow \text{book}$:

$$\boxed{w = (S \; \text{Rama} \; (VP \; \text{reads} \; (D \; a) \; (N \; \text{book})))}$$

**Marks split:** Each step 1 m, final boxed answer 1 m.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

The object NP is the sub-string $(D \; a) \; (N \; \text{book})$ inside the VP. Deleting this entire sub-tree:

$$\delta: w \longrightarrow (S \; \text{Rama} \; (VP \; \text{reads}))$$

**Well-formedness verification:** Use a stack machine.

- Read `(` of $S$ → push $S$.
- Read `Rama` → no change.
- Read `(` of $VP$ → push $VP$.
- Read `reads` → no change.
- Read `)` of $VP$ → pop $VP$.
- Read `)` of $S$ → pop $S$.
- Stack is empty. ✅ **String is well-formed.**

Thus the deletion operation $\delta$ preserves well-formedness under PSM closure.

**Marks split:** Correct identification of sub-tree 2 m, application of $\delta$ 2 m, stack verification 2 m, conclusion 1 m.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of the **Catalan number formula**, compute the number of well-formed parenthesis strings of length 8 using a single bracket type. Show the recurrence derivation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define and contrast the four PSM operations (substitution $\sigma$, insertion $\iota$, deletion $\delta$, re-bracketing $\rho$) with one example each, drawn from a parse tree.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

We want to find $C_n$ for $n = 4$ (since $2n = 8$).

**Recurrence derivation (3 marks):**
The first character must be `(`. Let its matching `)` be at position $2k$. The substring inside has length $2(k-1)$ — there are $C_{k-1}$ ways. The substring after has length $2(n-k)$ — there are $C_{n-k}$ ways. Summing over $k$:

$$C_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n} C_{k-1} \, C_{n-k}$$

**Closed-form (2 marks):**

$$C_n = \frac{1}{n+1} \binom{2n}{n}$$

**Numerical evaluation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_4 &= \frac{1}{4+1} \binom{8}{4} \\
&= \frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{8!}{4! \, 4!} \\
&= \frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6 \cdot 5}{4 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1} \\
&= \frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{1680}{24} \\
&= \frac{1}{5} \cdot 70 \\
&= 14
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, there are **14 well-formed parenthesis strings** of length 8 using a single bracket type.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Let the original tree (string form) be:

$$w_0 = (S \; (NP \; \text{Rama}) \; (VP \; (V \; \text{reads}) \; (NP \; (D \; a) \; (N \; \text{book}))))$$

**1. Substitution $\sigma$ (2 marks):** Replace the subject NP. Substitute $\text{Rama}$ with $\text{Sita}$:

$$\sigma(w_0) = (S \; (NP \; \text{Sita}) \; (VP \; \ldots))$$

**2. Insertion $\iota$ (2 marks):** Insert an ADVP (adverb) under VP:

$$\iota(w_0) = (S \; (NP \; \text{Rama}) \; (VP \; (V \; \text{reads}) \; (ADVP \; \text{quickly}) \; (NP \; \ldots)))$$

**3. Deletion $\delta$ (1.5 marks):** Remove the object NP:

$$\delta(w_0) = (S \; (NP \; \text{Rama}) \; (VP \; (V \; \text{reads})))$$

**4. Re-bracketing $\rho$ (1.5 marks):** Swap the order of children under VP — object before verb:

$$\rho(w_0) = (S \; (NP \; \text{Rama}) \; (VP \; (NP \; (D \; a) \; (N \; \text{book})) \; (V \; \text{reads})))$$

**Marks split:** Each operation definition + example = 1.75 marks × 4.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Parenthesis String Modification**
>
> - A parenthesis string is the **linearised form of a parse tree** under a CFG.
> - **Dyck language** $D_n$ = set of all well-formed parenthesis strings with $n$ bracket types.
> - **Four core PSM operations:** $\sigma$ (substitution), $\iota$ (insertion), $\delta$ (deletion), $\rho$ (re-bracketing).
> - All PSM operations **preserve well-formedness** when restricted to CFG-valid strings.
> - The number of well-formed parenthesis strings of length $2n$ is the **Catalan number**:
>   $$C_n = \dfrac{1}{n+1}\dbinom{2n}{n}$$
> - Linearisation is **recursive**: $\text{par}(T) = (X \; \text{par}(T_1) \ldots \text{par}(T_k))$.
> - Verify well-formedness using a **stack machine** — every `(` pushes, every `)` pops.
> - The **alphabet** typically includes labels from $N$ (non-terminals) and terminals from $\Sigma$.
> - Applications: parsing, grammar induction, statistical/neural MT, AST representations in code.
> - **Most-missed KTU question pattern:** "Compute Catalan number for length $2n$" — always state both recurrence AND closed form.
> - **Avoid:** writing Python code in theory exams — use formal notation only.
> - Remember the **Chomsky–Schützenberger theorem**: every CFL is a homomorphic image of $D_2 \cap R$ for some regular language $R$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sentiment Classification** is a fundamental sub-task of **Sentiment Analysis** (a.k.a. Opinion Mining) within Natural Language Processing that deals with the computational identification, extraction, and categorization of opinions, emotions, and subjective information expressed in natural language text into predefined polarity classes (e.g., *positive*, *negative*, *neutral*, or fine-grained star ratings).

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework, it is formally classified as a **supervised text classification problem** where a model $f: X \rightarrow Y$ learns a mapping from a feature space of textual documents $X$ to a discrete label space $Y$ representing sentiment polarity.

Mathematically, given a document (or sentence) $d$ composed of tokens $d = \{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n\}$, the classifier predicts:
$$\hat{y} = \underset{c \in C}{\arg\max} \; P(c \mid d)$$
where $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k\}$ is the set of sentiment classes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Sentiment Classification is positioned in Module 2 as a *practical application* of vector space models, probabilistic classifiers (Naive Bayes), and feature engineering techniques. Expect questions linking it back to Module 1 concepts (Bag-of-Words, TF-IDF) and Module 2 classifiers (Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, SVM).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **customer service manager** sifting through 10,000 product reviews. Reading each manually is impossible. You want a smart assistant that can stamp each review as 😊 (Positive), 😐 (Neutral), or 😡 (Negative).

**Sentiment Classification is exactly this smart assistant.**

> **Real-World Analogy — The "Mood Reader" Library:**
> Think of sentiment classification as a librarian who has been trained with thousands of book reviews. When a new review arrives, the librarian flips through it, notices the emotional "cues" (words like *amazing*, *terrible*, *okay*), and stamps it with the right emotional label. The librarian does this not by *feeling* emotions, but by recognizing **patterns** from past examples — exactly what a statistical/machine learning classifier does.

### The Three Levels of Granularity

| Granularity | Unit of Analysis | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Document Level** | Whole document/review | "This phone is fantastic!" → Positive |
| **Sentence Level** | Single sentence | "Battery life is bad." → Negative |
| **Aspect Level** | Specific feature/entity | "Camera great, battery bad." → Mixed |

---

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Recall From Module 1/2)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold Standard Metrics for Sentiment Classification:**
> - **Accuracy** = $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$
> - **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$
> - **Recall** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$
> - **F1-Score** = $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$

These are universally expected in KTU theory and numerical problems.

---

## 1.4 Why Sentiment Classification Matters (KTU Real-World Relevance)

- **Business Intelligence:** Brands monitor tweets/reviews in real-time.
- **Political Analysis:** Predicting election outcomes from social media.
- **Finance:** Stock-price movement prediction from news sentiment.
- **Healthcare:** Detecting depression/anxiety signals from patient posts.
- **Recommender Systems:** Hybrid filtering using sentiment scores.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sentiment Classification Pipeline

The operational workflow in any sentiment classification system follows a structured pipeline:

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Text Acquisition & Preprocessing**
   - Tokenization, lowercasing, stop-word removal, stemming/lemmatization.
   - Handle negations explicitly (`not good` ≠ `good`).

2. **Feature Engineering / Representation**
   - Convert text into numeric vectors using:
     - **Bag-of-Words (BoW)**
     - **TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency)**
     - **N-grams** (unigrams, bigrams, trigrams)
     - **Word Embeddings** (Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT)

3. **Model Selection / Training**
   - Lexicon-based (rule-driven)
   - Traditional ML (Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, SVM)
   - Deep Learning (LSTM, BERT, Transformers)

4. **Prediction / Inference**
   - Compute $P(c \mid d)$ and pick the most probable class.

5. **Evaluation**
   - Use Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, Confusion Matrix.

---

## 2.2 The Three Approaches — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### A. Lexicon-Based Approach

A **sentiment lexicon** is a dictionary mapping words/phrases to polarity scores.
$$\text{Score}(d) = \sum_{w \in d} \text{lex}(w)$$

If $\text{Score}(d) > \theta \Rightarrow$ Positive; $< -\theta \Rightarrow$ Negative; else Neutral.

> **Pros:** No training data needed. **Cons:** Cannot handle sarcasm, context, domain shift.

### B. Machine Learning Approach (Supervised)

The most KTU-relevant category. Trains a classifier on labeled data.
- **Naive Bayes** (probabilistic)
- **Logistic Regression** (linear discriminative)
- **Support Vector Machines (SVM)** (maximum-margin)
- **Random Forests / Ensemble methods**

### C. Deep Learning Approach

Uses neural architectures for automatic feature learning:
- CNNs (for n-gram-like patterns)
- RNNs / LSTMs (for sequential context)
- **BERT / Transformers** (state-of-the-art contextual embeddings)

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE** that KTU examiners expect you to reproduce in ESE answers. Memorize these.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(c \mid d) = \dfrac{P(d \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(d)}$ | Foundation of NB classifier |
| 2 | Naive Bayes Decision Rule | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c \; P(c) \prod_{i} P(w_i \mid c)$ | Assumes feature independence |
| 3 | Laplace Smoothing | $P(w_i \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i,c) + 1}{\sum_{w} \text{count}(w,c) + \vert V \vert}$ | Avoids zero-probability |
| 4 | Logistic (Sigmoid) | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$, where $z = w^T x + b$ | Maps score to $[0,1]$ |
| 5 | Logistic Regression Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_i \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i) \right]$ | Cross-entropy / Log loss |
| 6 | TF-IDF | $\text{tfidf}(w,d) = \text{tf}(w,d) \cdot \log \dfrac{N}{\text{df}(w)}$ | Feature weighting |
| 7 | TF | $\text{tf}(w,d) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w,d)}{\sum_{w' \in d} \text{count}(w',d)}$ | Term frequency |
| 8 | IDF | $\text{idf}(w) = \log \dfrac{N}{1 + \text{df}(w)}$ | Inverse document freq. |
| 9 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{x \cdot y}{\vert x \vert \cdot \vert y \vert}$ | Document comparison |
| 10 | Accuracy | $\dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| 11 | Precision | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Positive predictive value |
| 12 | Recall | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | True positive rate |
| 13 | F1-Score | $2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean |
| 14 | Macro-F1 | $\dfrac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} F1_i$ | Avg. F1 across classes |
| 15 | Lexicon Score | $S(d) = \sum_{w \in d} \text{lex}(w)$ | Rule-based scoring |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why KTU Cares About This Topic

Sentiment classification is the **backbone of modern opinion-mining systems** used by:

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| E-Commerce | Product review analysis (Amazon, Flipkart) |
| Social Media | Brand monitoring (Twitter, Reddit) |
| Finance | News-based stock prediction |
| Politics | Election poll sentiment tracking |

> **KTU Real-World Tie-In:** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes *application-oriented learning*. Linking formulas to real product pipelines (e.g., "How Zomato classifies reviews") wins examiner marks.

---

## 2.5 Challenges in Sentiment Classification (Theoretical Pitfalls)

1. **Sarcasm & Irony** — *"Great, another crash."* (Negative said positively)
2. **Negation Handling** — *"not good"* vs *"good"*
3. **Domain Dependence** — *"unpredictable"* is bad for a phone, good for a movie plot.
4. **Multilingual & Code-mixed Text** — Malayalam-English (Hinglish-like) on Kerala social media.
5. **Class Imbalance** — Often more positive than negative reviews.
6. **Subjectivity vs Objectivity** — *"It is a phone"* (objective, no sentiment)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Naive Bayes for Sentiment Classification

We begin with **Bayes' Theorem** for classifying a document $d$ into class $c$:
$$P(c \mid d) = \frac{P(d \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(d)}$$

**Decision Rule:** Pick the class that maximizes the posterior:
$$\hat{c} = \underset{c}{\arg\max} \; P(c \mid d) = \underset{c}{\arg\max} \; P(c) \cdot P(d \mid c)$$

(The denominator $P(d)$ is constant for all classes, hence dropped.)

**Naive Independence Assumption:** Assume words $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$ are conditionally independent given the class:
$$P(d \mid c) = P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

Thus:
$$\hat{c} = \underset{c}{\arg\max} \; P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

---

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Training Corpus (mini):**

| # | Document | Class |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | "great phone love it" | + |
| 2 | "battery is bad" | − |
| 3 | "love the camera great" | + |
| 4 | "terrible battery bad" | − |
| 5 | "great camera love" | + |
| 6 | "bad terrible phone" | − |

**Step 1 — Compute priors:**
$$P(+) = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5, \quad P(-) = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Vocabulary:** $\{great, phone, love, battery, is, bad, the, camera, terrible\}$ — $|V| = 9$.

**Step 3 — Count words per class:**

| Word | Count(+) | Count(−) |
|---|---|---|
| great | 3 | 0 |
| phone | 1 | 2 |
| love | 3 | 0 |
| battery | 0 | 2 |
| is | 0 | 1 |
| bad | 0 | 3 |
| the | 1 | 0 |
| camera | 2 | 0 |
| terrible | 0 | 2 |

**Step 4 — Compute word probabilities with Laplace smoothing:**
$$P(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w,c) + 1}{\sum_{w'} \text{count}(w',c) + \vert V \vert}$$

Total words in $+$ class = $3+1+3+0+0+0+1+2+0 = 10$.  
Total words in $-$ class = $0+2+0+2+1+3+0+0+2 = 10$.

So denominator for both = $10 + 9 = 19$.

Example calculations:
$$P(\text{great} \mid +) = \frac{3+1}{19} = \frac{4}{19}, \quad P(\text{great} \mid -) = \frac{0+1}{19} = \frac{1}{19}$$

$$P(\text{bad} \mid -) = \frac{3+1}{19} = \frac{4}{19}, \quad P(\text{bad} \mid +) = \frac{1}{19}$$

**Step 5 — Classify test document: "great battery bad"**

Score for class $+$:
$$P(+) \cdot P(\text{great}\mid +) \cdot P(\text{battery}\mid +) \cdot P(\text{bad}\mid +)$$
$$= 0.5 \cdot \frac{4}{19} \cdot \frac{1}{19} \cdot \frac{1}{19} = \frac{2}{6859}$$

Score for class $-$:
$$P(-) \cdot P(\text{great}\mid -) \cdot P(\text{battery}\mid -) \cdot P(\text{bad}\mid -)$$
$$= 0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{19} \cdot \frac{3}{19} \cdot \frac{4}{19} = \frac{6}{6859}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Prediction:** Class $-$ wins (Negative sentiment). This matches intuition: "battery bad" dominates despite "great".

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Logistic Regression as a Sentiment Classifier

Logistic regression models the probability of the positive class as:
$$P(y = 1 \mid x) = \sigma(w^T x + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(w^T x + b)}}$$

For binary sentiment ($y \in \{0,1\}$), the likelihood of $m$ training samples is:
$$\mathcal{L}(w,b) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(y_i \mid x_i)^{y_i} \cdot (1-P(y_i \mid x_i))^{1-y_i}$$

Taking the negative log-likelihood (loss to minimize):
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{loss}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i) \right]$$

This is the **Binary Cross-Entropy Loss**, optimized via **Gradient Descent**:
$$w \leftarrow w - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{loss}}}{\partial w}$$

For multi-class sentiment ($k > 2$ classes), we use **Softmax** instead of Sigmoid:
$$P(y = j \mid x) = \frac{e^{w_j^T x}}{\sum_{l=1}^{k} e^{w_l^T x}}$$

with **Categorical Cross-Entropy Loss**:
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CCE}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{k} y_{ij} \log \hat{y}_{ij}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — TF-IDF as Feature for Sentiment Classification

**Step 1 — Term Frequency (TF):**
$$\text{tf}(w,d) = \frac{f_{w,d}}{\sum_{w' \in d} f_{w',d}}$$

**Step 2 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):**
$$\text{idf}(w) = \log \frac{N}{1 + \text{df}(w)}$$

The `+1` is smoothing to prevent division by zero for unseen words.

**Step 3 — Combined TF-IDF:**
$$\text{tfidf}(w,d) = \text{tf}(w,d) \cdot \text{idf}(w)$$

> **Intuition:** Common words like "the", "is" get down-weighted; rare, informative words like "terrible", "amazing" get up-weighted — perfect for sentiment tasks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Classifier Comparison

| Classifier | Type | Strength | Weakness | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Naive Bayes** | Probabilistic | Fast, simple, low data | Strong independence assumption | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Logistic Regression** | Linear Discriminative | Probabilistic output, interpretable | Linear boundary | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **SVM** | Maximum Margin | Works well in high-dim spaces | Slow on large data | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Random Forest** | Ensemble | Handles non-linearity | Less interpretable | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **LSTM / BERT** | Deep Learning | Context-aware | Data-hungry | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

---

## 3.5 KTU-Style Analytical Question Walkthrough

**Problem:** A sentiment classifier on 200 movie reviews yields the following confusion matrix:

|  | Predicted + | Predicted − |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual +** | 80 | 20 |
| **Actual −** | 30 | 70 |

**Compute Precision, Recall, F1 for the Positive class.**

**Step 1:** Identify TP, FP, FN, TN.
$$TP = 80, \quad FP = 30, \quad FN = 20, \quad TN = 70$$

**Step 2 — Accuracy:**
$$\text{Acc} = \frac{80+70}{200} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75$$

**Step 3 — Precision (Positive class):**
$$P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{80}{80+30} = \frac{80}{110} = 0.727$$

**Step 4 — Recall (Positive class):**
$$R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{80}{80+20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

**Step 5 — F1-Score:**
$$F1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.727 \cdot 0.80}{0.727 + 0.80} = \frac{1.1632}{1.527} \approx 0.762$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always specify *which class* your Precision/Recall refers to. KTU scripts that say "Precision = 0.727" without naming the class lose a mark.

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Sentiment Classification. List any two challenges faced during sentiment classification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Sentiment Classification is a Natural Language Processing task that involves automatically categorizing a piece of text (document, sentence, or aspect) into predefined sentiment polarity classes such as *positive*, *negative*, or *neutral*, based on the opinion or emotion it expresses. It is typically formulated as a supervised learning problem where a classifier is trained on labeled data to predict the polarity of unseen text.

**Two Challenges (1 mark):**
1. **Sarcasm and Irony Detection:** Sentences like *"Oh great, my flight is delayed again"* express negative sentiment using positive words.
2. **Negation Handling:** The phrase *"not good"* has the opposite sentiment of *"good"*, but simple bag-of-words models treat them similarly.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Explain the Lexicon-Based approach to sentiment classification with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Lexicon-Based approach uses a **precompiled dictionary (sentiment lexicon)** where each word/phrase is assigned a polarity score (e.g., *amazing* = +0.8, *terrible* = −0.7). The sentiment of a document is computed as the sum (or average) of the scores of its constituent words:
$$\text{Score}(d) = \sum_{w \in d} \text{lex}(w)$$

If the score exceeds a positive threshold → *Positive*; below a negative threshold → *Negative*; otherwise → *Neutral*.

**Example (1 mark):**  
Lexicon: `good=+0.6`, `bad=−0.7`, `okay=+0.1`  
Sentence: *"The movie was good but the ending was bad."*  
Score = $+0.6 + (−0.7) = −0.1$ → **Neutral** (slightly negative).

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the Naive Bayes approach for sentiment classification. Derive the decision rule used by the classifier. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Naive Bayes for Sentiment Classification (7 marks):**

Naive Bayes is a probabilistic classifier based on **Bayes' Theorem** with the **naive assumption of conditional independence** between features (words) given the class label.

**Derivation:**

We want to predict the class $c \in \{+, -\}$ for a document $d = \{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n\}$ with maximum posterior probability:

$$\hat{c} = \underset{c}{\arg\max} \; P(c \mid d)$$

By **Bayes' Theorem**:
$$P(c \mid d) = \frac{P(d \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(d)}$$

Since $P(d)$ is constant across classes, we drop it:

$$\hat{c} = \underset{c}{\arg\max} \; P(c) \cdot P(d \mid c)$$

Applying the **Naive Independence Assumption** — that words are conditionally independent given the class:
$$P(d \mid c) = P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

Therefore, the final **Naive Bayes Decision Rule** is:

$$\boxed{\hat{c} = \underset{c}{\arg\max} \; P(c) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)}$$

To handle unseen words in test data, **Laplace (add-1) smoothing** is used:
$$P(w_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c) + 1}{\sum_{w} \text{count}(w, c) + \vert V \vert}$$

where $|V|$ is the vocabulary size.

**Advantages:** Simple, fast, works well with small data, robust to irrelevant features.  
**Limitation:** Strong independence assumption rarely holds in real text.

---

**(b)** Given the following mini training corpus for binary sentiment classification, classify the test document *"great battery bad"* using Multinomial Naive Bayes with Laplace smoothing. Show all calculations. **(7 marks)**

| # | Document | Class |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | great phone love it | + |
| 2 | battery is bad | − |
| 3 | love the camera great | + |
| 4 | terrible battery bad | − |
| 5 | great camera love | + |
| 6 | bad terrible phone | − |

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Vocabulary:** $\{great, phone, love, it, battery, is, bad, the, camera, terrible\}$ → $\vert V \vert = 10$.

**Step 2 — Priors:** $P(+) = 3/6 = 0.5$, $P(-) = 3/6 = 0.5$.

**Step 3 — Word counts:**

| Word | Count(+) | Count(−) |
|---|---|---|
| great | 3 | 0 |
| phone | 1 | 2 |
| love | 3 | 0 |
| it | 1 | 0 |
| battery | 0 | 2 |
| is | 0 | 1 |
| bad | 0 | 3 |
| the | 1 | 0 |
| camera | 2 | 0 |
| terrible | 0 | 2 |
| **Total** | **11** | **10** |

**Step 4 — Smoothed probabilities:**

$$P(w \mid +) = \frac{\text{count}(w,+) + 1}{11 + 10} = \frac{\text{count}(w,+) + 1}{21}$$

$$P(w \mid -) = \frac{\text{count}(w,-) + 1}{10 + 10} = \frac{\text{count}(w,-) + 1}{20}$$

| Word | P(w\|+) | P(w\|−) |
|---|---|---|
| great | 4/21 ≈ 0.190 | 1/20 = 0.050 |
| battery | 1/21 ≈ 0.048 | 3/20 = 0.150 |
| bad | 1/21 ≈ 0.048 | 4/20 = 0.200 |

**Step 5 — Compute posteriors for test doc *"great battery bad"*:**

$$P(+) \cdot P(\text{great}\mid +) \cdot P(\text{battery}\mid +) \cdot P(\text{bad}\mid +) = 0.5 \cdot \frac{4}{21} \cdot \frac{1}{21} \cdot \frac{1}{21}$$

$$= 0.5 \cdot \frac{4}{9261} = \frac{2}{9261} \approx 2.16 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P(-) \cdot P(\text{great}\mid -) \cdot P(\text{battery}\mid -) \cdot P(\text{bad}\mid -) = 0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{20} \cdot \frac{3}{20} \cdot \frac{4}{20}$$

$$= 0.5 \cdot \frac{12}{8000} = \frac{6}{8000} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**
$$P(-) > P(+) \Rightarrow \hat{c} = -$$

> ✅ **Predicted Sentiment: NEGATIVE** (matches intuition — "battery bad" dominates).
