## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define tokenization in NLP. Differentiate between word-level and character-level tokenization with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tokenization** is the process of splitting a text into smaller linguistic units called tokens, which serve as input to downstream NLP models. It is the first step in any NLP pipeline.

**Word-level tokenization** splits text on whitespace and punctuation, treating each word as one token. Example: `"NLP is fun"` → `["NLP", "is", "fun"]` (3 tokens). It produces a large vocabulary and fails on out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words.

**Character-level tokenization** treats every character as a token. Example: `"NLP"` → `["N", "L", "P"]` (3 tokens). It produces a very small vocabulary ($\sim 26$ for English) and completely eliminates OOV issues but results in long sequences and weaker semantic meaning per token.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for word-level, 1 mark for character-level with contrast.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)? State one advantage and one disadvantage.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **BPE** is a greedy, data-driven subword tokenization algorithm that iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pair of tokens in a corpus to form a new subword unit. The process continues until a target vocabulary size is reached.

**Advantage**: Handles out-of-vocabulary words elegantly by decomposing unseen words into known subword units (e.g., `"unhappiness"` → `["un", "happiness"]`).

**Disadvantage**: The greedy merge strategy is deterministic and may not produce linguistically optimal splits; also, the algorithm is language-dependent unless adapted via SentencePiece.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: In tokenization questions, students frequently lose marks by (1) confusing BPE with WordPiece, (2) forgetting to handle the end-of-word marker `</w>`, (3) skipping the corpus frequency calculation step, and (4) not specifying the final vocabulary size. Always show the **complete iterative table** for full marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)** algorithm for subword tokenization. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the corpus:
`{"the cat sat", "the cat slept", "the dog sat", "the dog slept"}` (each appearing with equal frequency 1),
manually execute the first **3 merge operations** of the BPE algorithm and list the resulting vocabulary. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 Marks)**: BPE is a subword segmentation algorithm that builds a vocabulary through iterative merging of the most frequent adjacent character/subword pairs in a corpus.

**Algorithm Steps (3 Marks)**:
1. Initialize vocabulary with all unique characters.
2. Count frequencies of all adjacent token pairs in the corpus.
3. Select the most frequent pair and merge it into a new token.
4. Add the new token to vocabulary and update the corpus.
5. Repeat steps 2-4 until the vocabulary reaches a predefined size $M$.

**Formula (1 Mark)**: The merge selection rule is $(t_a^*, t_b^*) = \arg\max_{(t_i, t_j)} \text{count}(t_i, t_j)$.

**Advantages (1 Mark)**: OOV handling, small fixed vocabulary, language flexibility.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 0 — Character vocabulary (1 Mark)**:
$V_0 = \{t, h, e, \_, c, a, s, l, p, d, o, g\}$ where `_` represents space.

**Corpus represented as character sequences (1 Mark)**:
- `the cat sat`: `t h e _ c a t _ s a t`
- `the cat slept`: `t h e _ c a t _ s l e p t`
- `the dog sat`: `t h e _ d o g _ s a t`
- `the dog slept`: `t h e _ d o g _ s l e p t`

**Pair counts at iteration 0 (2 Marks)**:

| Pair | Count |
|---|---|
| `(t, h)` | 4 |
| `(h, e)` | 4 |
| `(e, _)` | 4 |
| `(_, c)` | 2 |
| `(_, d)` | 2 |
| `(c, a)` | 2 |
| `(a, t)` | 2 |
| `(a, _)` | 2 |
| `(s, a)` | 2 |
| `(s, l)` | 2 |
| `(l, e)` | 2 |
| `(e, p)` | 2 |
| `(p, t)` | 2 |
| `(d, o)` | 2 |
| `(o, g)` | 2 |
| `(g, _)` | 2 |

**Most frequent pair**: `(t, h)`, `(h, e)`, `(e, _)` all tied at count 4. Convention: pick `(t, h)`.

**Iteration 1 — Merge `(t, h)` → `th`** (1 Mark):
$V_1 = V_0 \cup \{th\}$

**Iteration 2 — Recount and merge (1 Mark)**:
After merging, new pair `(th, e)` appears 4 times → next merge: `the`.

$V_2 = V_1 \cup \{the\}$

**Iteration 3 — Recount and merge (1 Mark)**:
After merging `the`, pair `(the, _)` appears 4 times → next merge: `the_`.

$V_3 = V_2 \cup \{the_\}$, so $\vert V_3 \vert = 12 + 3 = 15$ tokens.

**Final answer**: After 3 merges, vocabulary includes `th`, `the`, `the_` in addition to base characters.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Compare and contrast **BPE, WordPiece, and Unigram Language Model** tokenization schemes with respect to merge criterion, training direction, and OOV handling. Use a comparison table. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **out-of-vocabulary (OOV) problem**. How does subword tokenization solve it? Illustrate with the example of the unseen word `"unaffordability"`. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

| Aspect | BPE | WordPiece | Unigram LM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Merge Criterion** | Maximum raw frequency | Maximum likelihood gain $\Delta L$ | Minimum likelihood loss on removal |
| **Direction** | Bottom-up (small → large vocab) | Bottom-up | Top-down (large → small vocab) |
| **OOV Handling** | Decompose into subwords | Decompose into subwords | Probabilistic segmentation |
| **Used By** | GPT-2/3/4 | BERT | XLNet, ALBERT |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic | Deterministic | Probabilistic (sampling possible) |

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark per correct row, plus 2 marks for explanatory coherence.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**OOV Definition (2 Marks)**: The OOV problem occurs when a token in the test data does not exist in the training vocabulary $V_{\text{train}}$. Word-level tokenizers typically replace such tokens with a generic `UNK` (unknown) marker, losing all semantic information.

**Subword Solution (3 Marks)**: Subword tokenization decomposes any novel word into a sequence of known subword units drawn from $V$. For example, with vocabulary containing `{un, afford, ability, ity}`, the unseen word `"unaffordability"` is segmented as `["un", "afford", "ability", "ity"]` — all known subwords, retaining full semantic content.

**Illustration (2 Marks)**:
- Word-level: `"unaffordability"` → `[UNK]` (1 token, no meaning).
- Subword (BPE): `"unaffordability"` → `["un", "afford", "ability"]` (3 tokens, full meaning preserved).
- Sequence length increases but information loss is eliminated.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Tokenization**

- ☐ **Tokenization** = segmentation of raw text into atomic units (tokens).
- ☐ **Vocabulary** $V$ = set of unique tokens; size $\vert V \vert$ is a key hyperparameter.
- ☐ **Three granularities**: character, subword, word — subword is the modern standard.
- ☐ **Whitespace tokenization** is naive; fails on punctuation and non-space languages.
- ☐ **Penn Treebank tokenizer** uses rule-based regex cascade with contextual rules.
- ☐ **BPE** merges the **most frequent adjacent pair** iteratively until target vocab size.
- ☐ **WordPiece** merges pairs that **maximize likelihood gain**, not raw frequency.
- ☐ **Unigram LM** starts large and **prunes** tokens with lowest likelihood contribution.
- ☐ **SentencePiece** is **language-agnostic**, treating whitespace as a visible symbol.
- ☐ **OOV problem** is solved by subword tokenization (e.g., `"unknown"` → `["un", "known"]`).
- ☐ **Normalization** (lowercasing, Unicode NFC) is a **companion step** to tokenization.
- ☐ Modern transformers (BERT/GPT) use **subword tokenization WITHOUT stemming/lemmatization**.
- ☐ BERT uses **WordPiece** with vocab size $\sim 30{,}000$; GPT-4 uses **BPE** with $\sim 100{,}000$ tokens.
- ☐ Tokenization is **Stage 1** of the NLP pipeline: Raw Text → Tokens → Normalized → Embeddings.
- ☐ **Compression ratio** = characters per token; typically **$3$ – $5$** for English BPE.
- ☐ **Type-Token Ratio (TTR)** = $\vert V \vert / \text{total tokens}$ — measures lexical diversity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stopwords
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stopwords

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STOPWORDS (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> In Natural Language Processing (NLP), **stopwords** are defined as a set of highly frequent, functionally significant but semantically low-weight words of a language that are conventionally filtered out (removed) during the preprocessing phase of text analytics pipelines. These words include articles, prepositions, conjunctions, pronouns, auxiliary verbs, and common adverbs which, while essential for grammatical correctness, contribute negligible discriminative information for tasks such as text classification, information retrieval, sentiment analysis, and topic modeling.

In the formal mathematical notation used in Computational Linguistics, a given text corpus $\mathcal{C}$ is processed by a stopword removal function $\mathcal{F}_{stop}$ such that:

$$\mathcal{C}_{filtered} = \mathcal{F}_{stop}(\mathcal{C}) = \{w \in \mathcal{C} \mid w \notin \mathcal{S}\}$$

where $\mathcal{S}$ represents the predefined **stopword lexicon** (or stoplist) for the target language $L$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of stopwords as the "filler words" of language — like scaffolding on a building.**

Imagine you walk into a library to find a book about **"Machine Learning Algorithms."** The librarian does not need to read every single word in every book title to identify the right shelf. Words like *"the," "a," "of," "in," "is," "and," "to"* appear in nearly EVERY book title. They are grammatically necessary but informationally redundant.

For example, consider these two sentences:
- *"The movie was **good**."*
- *"A film is **great**."*

If we strip out stopwords (*the, was, a, is*), the core sentiment-carrying words survive: **"movie good"** and **"film great."** Now the machine can easily recognize that both sentences express positive sentiment about a visual entertainment medium. The stopwords were just **grammatical glue** — removing them sharpens the model's focus on the *signal* (the meaningful content words) and reduces the *noise*.

---

## 1.3 Categories of Stopwords

Stopwords can be broadly classified into the following linguistic categories:

| # | Category | Example Words | Functional Role |
|---|----------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Articles** | a, an, the | Definite/indefinite reference |
| 2 | **Prepositions** | in, on, at, by, for, with, to, of, from | Spatial/temporal/relational linkage |
| 3 | **Conjunctions** | and, or, but, so, yet, nor | Clause/phrase connection |
| 4 | **Pronouns** | he, she, it, they, we, I, you, his, her | Anaphoric reference |
| 5 | **Auxiliary Verbs** | is, am, are, was, were, be, been, being, have, has, do, does | Tense, aspect, voice formation |
| 6 | **Common Adverbs** | very, just, also, too, only, even | Degree/intensity modifiers |
| 7 | **Demonstratives** | this, that, these, those | Deictic reference |

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does KTU include Stopwords in Module 3 (Text Preprocessing & Normalization)?**
> Stopword removal is one of the **four foundational preprocessing steps** in any NLP pipeline:
> 1. Tokenization
> 2. Stopword Removal ← *This Topic*
> 3. Stemming / Lemmatization
> 4. Vectorization (BoW / TF-IDF)
>
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to **justify** why stopword removal is performed, **list** standard stopword lists (NLTK, spaCy, sklearn), and **demonstrate** the removal process on a sample text.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of Stopword Removal

The stopword removal process follows a strict logical sequence. Understanding each step is critical for KTU's application-based questions.

### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown:

1. **Input Acquisition** — The system receives a raw text document $D$ consisting of characters, punctuation, and whitespace.
2. **Tokenization (Pre-requisite)** — The raw string $D$ is segmented into a sequence of lexical tokens:
$$D = \{t_1, t_2, t_3, \ldots, t_n\}$$
3. **Case Normalization** — Tokens are converted to lowercase (uniform casing) to ensure case-insensitive matching against the stoplist:
$$t_i^{norm} = \text{lower}(t_i)$$
4. **Stoplist Lookup** — Each normalized token $t_i^{norm}$ is checked against the stopword set $\mathcal{S}$. A binary decision is made:
$$\phi(t_i^{norm}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } t_i^{norm} \notin \mathcal{S} \text{ (KEEP)} \\ 0 & \text{if } t_i^{norm} \in \mathcal{S} \text{ (REMOVE)} \end{cases}$$
5. **Filtering** — Tokens where $\phi(t_i^{norm}) = 1$ are retained; tokens where $\phi(t_i^{norm}) = 0$ are discarded.
6. **Output Reconstruction** — The filtered tokens are joined back (optionally) into a processed string $D_{filtered}$.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Stopword Removal

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Rationale (KTU High-Yield):**
> Stopword removal is performed for **four engineering reasons:**

| # | Reason | Engineering Impact |
|---|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Dimensionality Reduction** | Vocabulary size shrinks by **20–35%** on average, reducing storage and compute cost |
| 2 | **Noise Suppression** | High-frequency low-information tokens dilute statistical models (e.g., TF-IDF) |
| 3 | **Focus on Semantics** | Content words carry the *meaning payload*; stopwords carry *grammatical scaffolding* |
| 4 | **Improved Accuracy** | Many ML classifiers (Naive Bayes, SVM) improve in precision when stopwords are removed |

---

## 2.3 Standard Stopword Lexicons (KTU Examiner Favorites)

KTU examiners expect familiarity with the following industry-standard stoplists:

- **NLTK Stopwords** — 179 words for English (in `nltk.corpus.stopwords.words('english')`)
- **spaCy Stopwords** — 326 words (`spacy.lang.en.stop_words.STOP_WORDS`)
- **scikit-learn Stopwords** — 318 words (`sklearn.feature_extraction.text.ENGLISH_STOP_WORDS`)
- **Glasgow / SMART Stoplist** — Historical academic stoplist (571 words)
- **Google Stopwords** — First open-source web stopword list

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Domain-Specific Stopwords:** Modern NLP often builds **custom stoplists** for specialized domains. For example, in a medical corpus, the word *"patient"* may be a stopword. In a Twitter sentiment task, words like *"http"* and *"@user"* become stopwords.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Notation | Description |
|---------|---------------------|-------------|
| Stopword Filter Function | $\mathcal{C}_{filtered} = \{w \in \mathcal{C} \mid w \notin \mathcal{S}\}$ | Removes all tokens present in stoplist $\mathcal{S}$ |
| Token Decision Function | $\phi(t_i) \in \{0, 1\}$ | Binary decision: keep (1) or remove (0) |
| Reduction Ratio | $R = \frac{\mid \mathcal{S} \cap \mathcal{C} \mid}{\mid \mathcal{C} \mid} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of corpus tokens removed |
| TF-IDF with Stopwords | $w_{ij} = tf_{ij} \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{df_j}\right)$ | Stopwords have low $df_j \approx N$, so weight → 0 (natural suppression) |
| Vocabulary Size | $V_{after} = V_{before} - \mid \mathcal{S} \cap V \mid$ | Post-removal vocabulary shrinkage |

**CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical pipe symbol has been replaced with `\vert` where required to preserve table integrity.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Stopword removal is a **production-grade technique** used across:
- **Google Search Engine** — Indexing efficiency
- **Twitter/X Sentiment Analysis** — Removing "@", "RT", "the"
- **Spam Filters** — Eliminating common email boilerplate
- **Chatbots & LLMs** — Reducing context window bloat
- **Legal Document Analysis** — Removing boilerplate clauses
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Demonstration

## 3.1 Worked Example: Stopword Removal on a Sample Sentence

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a typical KTU 2-mark / 3-mark question pattern:**
> *"Apply stopword removal on the following sentence and show the output."*

### Given:
**Input Sentence:**
> *"Natural Language Processing is a subfield of Artificial Intelligence, and it deals with the interaction between computers and human language."*

**Stoplist Used:** NLTK English stopwords (subset shown)

$$\mathcal{S} = \{is, a, of, and, it, the, with, between, in, on, at, for, to, from\}$$

---

### Step 1: Tokenization

The input sentence is split into individual tokens:

$$D = \{\text{"Natural", "Language", "Processing", "is", "a", "subfield", "of", "Artificial", "Intelligence", ",", "and", "it", "deals", "with", "the", "interaction", "between", "computers", "and", "human", "language", "."}\}$$

Total tokens $n = 22$.

---

### Step 2: Case Normalization

All tokens converted to lowercase:

$$D_{norm} = \{\text{"natural", "language", "processing", "is", "a", "subfield", "of", "artificial", "intelligence", ",", "and", "it", "deals", "with", "the", "interaction", "between", "computers", "and", "human", "language", "."}\}$$

---

### Step 3: Punctuation Removal (Optional Pre-step)

In strict pipelines, punctuation is stripped:

$$D_{clean} = \{\text{"natural", "language", "processing", "is", "a", "subfield", "of", "artificial", "intelligence", "and", "it", "deals", "with", "the", "interaction", "between", "computers", "and", "human", "language"}\}$$

Total clean tokens = **20**.

---

### Step 4: Stoplist Lookup & Filtering

Each token is compared against $\mathcal{S}$:

| Token | In Stoplist? | Decision |
|-------|--------------|----------|
| natural | No | KEEP |
| language | No | KEEP |
| processing | No | KEEP |
| **is** | **Yes** | **REMOVE** |
| **a** | **Yes** | **REMOVE** |
| subfield | No | KEEP |
| **of** | **Yes** | **REMOVE** |
| artificial | No | KEEP |
| intelligence | No | KEEP |
| **and** | **Yes** | **REMOVE** |
| **it** | **Yes** | **REMOVE** |
| deals | No | KEEP |
| **with** | **Yes** | **REMOVE** |
| **the** | **Yes** | **REMOVE** |
| interaction | No | KEEP |
| **between** | **Yes** | **REMOVE** |
| computers | No | KEEP |
| **and** | **Yes** | **REMOVE** |
| human | No | KEEP |
| language | No | KEEP |

---

### Step 5: Output Reconstruction

$$\boxed{D_{filtered} = \{\text{"natural", "language", "processing", "subfield", "artificial", "intelligence", "deals", "interaction", "computers", "human", "language"}\}}$$

Count of retained tokens = **11**.

---

### Step 6: Reduction Ratio Calculation

The reduction ratio $R$ is computed as:

$$\begin{aligned} R &= \frac{\mid \mathcal{S} \cap D_{clean} \mid}{\mid D_{clean} \mid} \times 100\% \\[6pt] R &= \frac{9}{20} \times 100\% \\[6pt] R &= 45\% \end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** 45% of the tokens in this sentence were stopwords. This demonstrates how stopwords dominate natural language frequency distributions — often following **Zipf's Law**, where the top ~20 words account for ~50% of all word occurrences.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When to Use vs. NOT Use Stopword Removal

| Task | Use Stopword Removal? | Justification |
|------|----------------------|----------------|
| Text Classification (Spam detection) | ✅ YES | Focus on content keywords |
| Sentiment Analysis | ⚠️ CONTEXTUAL | Remove but preserve negations (*"not," "never," "no"*) |
| Machine Translation | ❌ NO | Grammar is essential for fluency |
| Question Answering Systems | ❌ NO | Stopwords like *"what," "where," "how"* carry query semantics |
| Text Summarization | ✅ YES | Content-word extraction dominates |
| Chatbot Intent Recognition | ⚠️ CONTEXTUAL | May need to retain *"help," "need"* |
| POS Tagging | ❌ NO | Every word has a grammatical role |
| Language Modeling (LLMs) | ❌ NO | Modern Transformers benefit from full context |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** A common error is blanket-applying stopword removal to *all* NLP tasks. Examiners award marks for **critical thinking** — always justify with the task objective.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Stopword Removal Pipeline Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Input] --> B[Tokenization Module]
    B --> C[Case Normalization: lower]
    C --> D[Punctuation Stripper]
    D --> E{For Each Token}
    E --> F[Lookup in Stoplist S]
    F --> G{Token in S?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Discard Token]
    G -->|No| I[Retain Token]
    I --> J[Filtered Token Stream]
    H --> K[Continue Loop]
    K --> E
    J --> L[Reconstruct Output Text]
    L --> M[Cleaned Corpus Output]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture — Stopword Categories

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stopword Lexicon S] --> B[Articles]
    A --> C[Prepositions]
    A --> D[Conjunctions]
    A --> E[Pronouns]
    A --> F[Auxiliary Verbs]
    A --> G[Common Adverbs]

    B --> B1[a, an, the]
    C --> C1[in, on, at, for, with, of]
    D --> D1[and, or, but, so]
    E --> E1[he, she, it, they]
    F --> F1[is, am, are, was, have, do]
    G --> G1[very, just, also, too]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Decision Flow — Should We Remove Stopwords?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([NLP Task Initiated]) --> Q1{Task Type?}
    Q1 -->|Classification| Q2{TF-IDF or BoW?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Remove1[Remove Stopwords]
    Q2 -->|No| Keep1[Retain Stopwords]
    Q1 -->|Translation| Keep2[Retain All Words]
    Q1 -->|QA System| Keep3[Retain All Words]
    Q1 -->|Sentiment| Q3{Negation Words Present?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Custom[Custom Stoplist - Preserve Negations]
    Q3 -->|No| Remove2[Remove Standard Stopwords]
    Q1 -->|Summarization| Remove3[Remove Stopwords]
    Q1 -->|POS Tagging| Keep4[Retain All Words]

    Remove1 --> End([Optimized Pipeline])
    Keep1 --> End
    Keep2 --> End
    Keep3 --> End
    Custom --> End
    Remove2 --> End
    Remove3 --> End
    Keep4 --> End
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Corpus-Level Impact Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Corpus C] -->|Token Count: N| B[Stopword Set S]
    B -->|Removed Tokens: N_S| C[Filtered Corpus C_filtered]
    A -->|| C | D[Vocabulary Size V_before]
    C -->|V_after = V_before - intersection| E[Reduced Vocabulary V_after]
    D --> F[High-Dim Sparse Matrix]
    E --> G[Lower-Dim Dense Matrix]
    F -->|Slow, Noisy| H[ML Model]
    G -->|Fast, Focused| H[ML Model]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Stopwords with Examples** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Stopwords are commonly used words in a language that occur with very high frequency but carry little semantic content. They are filtered out during text preprocessing to reduce noise and dimensionality.

**Examples (1 mark each):**
- *Articles:* the, a, an
- *Prepositions:* in, on, at, for, of
- *Auxiliary verbs:* is, are, was, have, do

---

### **Question 2: List Any Three Standard Stopword Lists Used in NLP** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **NLTK Stopwords** — 179 English words available via `nltk.corpus.stopwords.words('english')`
2. **spaCy Stopwords** — 326 words in the `spacy.lang.en.stop_words.STOP_WORDS` set
3. **scikit-learn Stopwords** — 318 words via `sklearn.feature_extraction.text.ENGLISH_STOP_WORDS`

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Define stopwords. Explain the necessity of stopword removal in NLP pipelines with at least four justifications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following paragraph:
> *"The cat is sitting on the mat. It is a sunny day, and the cat looks very happy."*

Apply the stopword removal process step-by-step using the stoplist $\mathcal{S} = \{the, is, on, a, and, it\}$ and show the final filtered output. Calculate the reduction ratio. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Definition & Justifications (7 Marks)**

**Definition (3 Marks):**
Stopwords are high-frequency, low-semantic-value words (articles, prepositions, conjunctions, pronouns, auxiliary verbs) that are conventionally removed during the preprocessing stage of NLP pipelines. Mathematically, for a corpus $\mathcal{C}$ and stoplist $\mathcal{S}$:

$$\mathcal{C}_{filtered} = \{w \in \mathcal{C} \mid w \notin \mathcal{S}\}$$

**Necessity — Four Justifications (1 Mark Each):**

1. **Dimensionality Reduction:** Removing stopwords shrinks the vocabulary size by 20–35%, reducing memory and compute overhead.
2. **Noise Suppression:** In statistical models like Naive Bayes or TF-IDF, stopwords dominate frequency counts and dilute the signal.
3. **Improved Accuracy:** Models trained on content words show higher precision in classification tasks.
4. **Faster Indexing:** In information retrieval, smaller inverted indexes enable quicker search.

---

**Part (b) — Worked Stopword Removal (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Tokenization (1 Mark)**

$$D = \{\text{the, cat, is, sitting, on, the, mat, ., it, is, a, sunny, day, ,, and, the, cat, looks, very, happy, .}\}$$

**Step 2: Case Normalization & Punctuation Removal (1 Mark)**

$$D_{clean} = \{\text{the, cat, is, sitting, on, the, mat, it, is, a, sunny, day, and, the, cat, looks, very, happy}\}$$

Total tokens $n = 17$.

**Step 3: Apply Stopword Filter (3 Marks)**

| Token | In Stoplist $\mathcal{S}$? | Decision |
|-------|----------------------------|----------|
| **the** (×3) | Yes | REMOVE |
| cat | No | KEEP |
| **is** (×2) | Yes | REMOVE |
| sitting | No | KEEP |
| **on** | Yes | REMOVE |
| mat | No | KEEP |
| **it** | Yes | REMOVE |
| **a** | Yes | REMOVE |
| sunny | No | KEEP |
| day | No | KEEP |
| **and** | Yes | REMOVE |
| looks | No | KEEP |
| very | No (not in $\mathcal{S}$) | KEEP |
| happy | No | KEEP |

**Step 4: Final Output (1 Mark)**

$$\boxed{D_{filtered} = \{\text{cat, sitting, mat, sunny, day, looks, very, happy}\}}$$

Retained token count = **8**.

**Step 5: Reduction Ratio Calculation (1 Mark)**

$$\begin{aligned} R &= \frac{\mid \mathcal{S} \cap D_{clean} \mid}{\mid D_{clean} \mid} \times 100\% \\[6pt] R &= \frac{9}{17} \times 100\% \\[6pt] R &\approx 52.94\% \end{aligned}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Differentiate between stopword removal and stemming. Why might stopword removal be skipped in machine translation tasks? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a custom stopword list for a **medical domain corpus** containing 5 sentences. Justify each entry with reasoning. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Differentiation & Justification (7 Marks)**

**Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Parameter | Stopword Removal | Stemming |
|-----------|------------------|----------|
| **Objective** | Remove high-frequency, low-meaning words | Reduce words to their root form |
| **Operation** | Deletion | Truncation (e.g., "running" → "run") |
| **Linguistic Level** | Lexical filtering | Morphological analysis |
| **Output** | Subset of original tokens | Modified tokens |
| **Example** | "the cat is on mat" → "cat mat" | "running, runs, ran" → "run" |
| **Tool** | NLTK, spaCy stoplists | Porter, Lancaster, Snowball stemmers |

**Why Skip in Machine Translation? (2 Marks):**
In machine translation (e.g., English → Malayalam), grammatical structure is critical. Stopwords like *"the," "of," "in"* carry syntactic meaning that defines the target language's grammar. Removing them disrupts fluency and breaks the syntactic tree parsers rely on. For example, *"the"* in English maps to specific gendered/definite articles in Malayalam. Stripping it causes information loss and ungrammatical outputs.

---

**Part (b) — Custom Medical Stopword List (7 Marks)**

**Sample Medical Corpus (assumed):**
> *"The patient was admitted to the hospital. The doctor prescribed medication. The nurse monitored the patient's vitals. Treatment was administered in the ward. Recovery was observed after therapy."*

**Custom Stoplist Design (1 Mark per entry + reasoning):**

| # | Custom Stopword | Justification |
|---|-----------------|---------------|
| 1 | patient | Appears in every medical record → no discriminative power |
| 2 | doctor | Universal role, not informative for diagnosis-specific tasks |
| 3 | hospital | Generic setting term, low signal |
| 4 | treatment | Broad term, lacks specificity |
| 5 | nurse | Generic role descriptor |
| 6 | ward | Generic location descriptor |
| 7 | medication | Overused term in clinical narratives |

**Why NOT remove "vitals," "therapy," "recovery"?**
These are domain-specific content words carrying diagnostic/temporal information that distinguishes one medical record from another — hence they must be **retained**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:**
>
> 1. **Mistake:** Listing stopwords without categorizing them (articles, prepositions, etc.) — *Examiner's Note: KTU awards 1 mark for classification.*
> 2. **Mistake:** Applying stopword removal WITHOUT first performing tokenization — *Always show tokenization as Step 1.*
> 3. **Mistake:** Using Python code in theory exams — *For 14-mark theory questions, prefer pseudocode or step-by-step tables.*
> 4. **Mistake:** Claiming stopword removal is always beneficial — *Examiner's Note: Critical evaluation earns full marks; blanket statements lose 2–3 marks.*
> 5. **Mistake:** Confusing **stemming** with **stopword removal** — *These are distinct preprocessing steps; examiners specifically test this differentiation.*
> 6. **Mistake:** Forgetting case normalization — *Tokens like "The" and "the" must be lowercased before lookup.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — STOPWORDS**
>
> - **Definition:** High-frequency, low-semantic-value words removed during preprocessing.
> - **Categories:** Articles, prepositions, conjunctions, pronouns, auxiliary verbs, common adverbs.
> - **Mathematical Form:** $\mathcal{C}_{filtered} = \{w \in \mathcal{C} \mid w \notin \mathcal{S}\}$
> - **Standard Stoplists:** NLTK (179 words), spaCy (326 words), sklearn (318 words).
> - **Reduction Ratio Formula:** $R = \frac{\mid \mathcal{S} \cap \mathcal{C} \mid}{\mid \mathcal{C} \mid} \times 100\%$
> - **Pipeline Position:** Comes AFTER tokenization and case normalization, BEFORE stemming/lemmatization.
> - **Use When:** Text classification, spam filtering, summarization, topic modeling.
> - **Don't Use When:** Machine translation, POS tagging, question answering, language modeling.
> - **Domain-Specific:** Always build custom stoplists for specialized corpora (medical, legal, Twitter).
> - **Negation Caveat:** Preserve negation words (*not, never, no*) in sentiment analysis.
> - **Zipf's Law Connection:** Top ~20 words account for ~50% of all word occurrences — empirical justification for stopword removal.
> - **Always Show Steps:** Tokenize → Normalize → Strip Punctuation → Lookup → Filter → Reconstruct.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stemming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stemming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3: STEMMING — SECTION 1: CORE DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** and **Information Retrieval (IR)**, **Stemming** is defined as a *morphological normalization technique* that reduces inflected or derived word forms to their **word stem** — a common base or root form — by **chopping off word endings or prefixes** using heuristic, rule-based procedures.

According to the standard KTU 2024 NLP syllabus formulation:

> **Stemming** is the process of reducing a word to its **stem** (a *pseudo-root* form), which is the part of the word that remains after the removal of its affixes (suffixes and sometimes prefixes). The resulting stem need **not** be a valid dictionary word, but it serves as a compact morphological index for the original word's semantic family.

Mathematically, stemming can be represented as a deterministic transformation function:

$$S : W \rightarrow \Sigma^*$$

Where:
- $W$ is the set of all surface word tokens in a document or corpus.
- $\Sigma^*$ denotes the set of all possible character strings over the alphabet $\Sigma$.
- $S(w)$ returns a string $s \in \Sigma^*$ such that $s$ is the morphological stem of $w$.

For every input word $w \in W$ and its morphological variants $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$ belonging to the **same semantic paradigm**, the stemmer enforces:

$$S(w_1) = S(w_2) = \dots = S(w_n) = s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Stemming is positioned within the **Text Preprocessing and Normalization** sub-module of Module 3. It is treated as a *preprocessing filter* that feeds normalized tokens into downstream components such as **Bag-of-Words**, **TF-IDF**, **Topic Modeling**, and **Indexing Engines**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stem vs. Lemma — Critical Distinction:**
> A **Stem** is produced by *rule-based chopping heuristics* (e.g., "university" → "univers", "studies" → "studi"). It is **not** required to be a real word.
> A **Lemma** is the *canonical dictionary form* of a word produced by **Lemmatization**, which uses linguistic vocabulary and morphological analysis (e.g., "studies" → "study", "better" → "good").
> The KTU 2024 curriculum explicitly treats these as **two distinct normalization paradigms** under the broader umbrella of Morphological Analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **librarian** organizing millions of books. A reader searches for *"running"*. But in your catalog, the book is filed under *"run"*. If the user cannot find the book, the library is useless. The job of the **stemmer** is to act like a **mass-production clerical assistant** who, before filing, rapidly chops off common endings from every incoming word so that:

- *running* → *run*
- *runs* → *run*
- *ran* → *run*  *(in some aggressive stemmers)*
- *runner* → *runn*  *(note: NOT a real word!)*

> [!TIP]
> **The "Harsh Barber" Metaphor:** Think of a stemmer as an overenthusiastic barber who, given a set of words, shaves off predictable word-endings (suffixes like *-ing*, *-ed*, *-ly*, *-s*, *-ation*) using a fixed list of rules. The barber is **fast**, **cheap**, and operates on **rules** — but is sometimes **too aggressive** (cuts too much) or **too mild** (leaves too much). The stem that remains is just a "rough sketch" of the original word's identity — sufficient for search engines, but not for linguistic perfection.

This makes stemming ideal for **high-volume, speed-critical applications** such as search engine indexing, where linguistic accuracy is sacrificed for **computational efficiency** and **recall maximization**.

## 1.3 Why Stemming Matters — Engineering Motivation

| Application Domain | Role of Stemming | Benefit Gained |
|---|---|---|
| **Search Engines (Google, Bing)** | Maps query terms to indexed stems | Higher **recall** — finds *"running shoes"* when user types *"run shoes"* |
| **Text Classification** | Reduces feature space dimensionality | Smaller vocabulary = faster classifiers |
| **Clustering & Topic Modeling** | Groups morphological variants together | Cleaner semantic clusters |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Normalizes review tokens | Better statistical aggregation |
| **Information Extraction** | Standardizes entity mentions | Improved entity matching |

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Standardized Vocabulary)

> [!NOTE]
> The following terms are explicitly tested in KTU 2024 End-Semester Examinations (ESE) under Module 3 of PEIET864.

- **Stem:** The pseudo-root obtained after removing affixes.
- **Affix:** A morpheme attached to a base word (suffix, prefix, infix, circumfix).
- **Suffix Stripping:** The act of removing the trailing portion of a word.
- **Conflicting Suffixes:** Two or more suffix rules that both match a word (algorithm must have a tie-breaking priority).
- **Measure of a Word (m):** A special integer metric used in the **Porter Stemmer** that quantifies the number of vowel–consonant sequences.
- **Over-stemming:** When too much is chopped off, conflating unrelated words (e.g., *university* → *univers* ≈ *universe*).
- **Under-stemming:** When too little is chopped, leaving related words ungrouped (e.g., *connect* ≠ *connected* after a poor stem).

# 🔬 MODULE 3: STEMMING — SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Foundation — How Does a Stemmer Work?

A stemmer operates as a **cascade of rule applications** over an input word $w$. Each rule is of the general form:

> **Condition → Suffix → Replacement**
> *If* the word matches Condition *and* ends with Suffix, *then* replace Suffix with Replacement.

A typical rule schema can be expressed as:

$$(\text{condition } C, \text{ suffix } \sigma, \text{ replacement } \rho)$$

When applied to a word $w$:

$$w' = \begin{cases} w[0..(|w|-|\sigma|)] \oplus \rho & \text{if } C(w) = \text{True} \land w \text{ ends with } \sigma \\ w & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Where:
- $w[0..k]$ denotes the prefix substring from index $0$ to $k$.
- $\oplus$ denotes string concatenation.
- $|w|$ and $|\sigma|$ are the lengths of the word and suffix respectively.

The entire stemming procedure is a finite **state machine** that applies rules in priority order, where the **output of one rule** becomes the **input of the next** until a fixed point or a maximum iteration count is reached.

## 2.2 The Porter Stemmer — Algorithmic Architecture (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

The **Porter Stemmer (1980)** is the **canonical, board-favorite algorithm** for the KTU 2024 NLP course. It operates in **five sequential phases**, each containing a set of suffix-stripping rules with conditions on the *Measure* of the word stem.

### 2.2.1 The "Measure" Function $m(w)$ — Core Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Measure of a Word Stem** is defined as the number of **VC sequences** (a vowel immediately followed by a consonant) in the stem. This metric determines *how "sturdy" or complex* the stem is and is used as a guard against over-stemming short or weak stems.

Formally:

$$m(w) = \left\vert \{ i : 1 \le i \le |w|-1,\ w[i] \in V,\ w[i+1] \in C \} \right\vert$$

Where:
- $V = \{a, e, i, o, u, y\}$ (treated as vowels by Porter, with the **convention** that the **first letter** of a word is *never* a vowel, and $y$ is treated as a vowel *only* if it appears after a consonant).
- $C = \Sigma \setminus V$ (the consonant set).

**Examples of $m(w)$:**

| Word | VC Sequences | $m$ |
|---|---|---|
| `tree` | $(r,e)$ | **1** |
| `trouble` | $(o,u)$, $(u,b)$ | **2** |
| `private` | $(i,v)$, $(a,t)$ | **2** |
| `oaten` | $(o,a)$, $(e,n)$ | **2** |
| `orrery` | $(o,r)$, $(e,r)$, $(y, \text{treated as V})$ | **3** |

**Rule Conditions based on Measure:**

- $m(w) = 0$ — empty measure: $*$condition
- $m(w) = 1$ — first non-empty measure: $*$v$*$condition
- $m(w) > 1$ — double measure: $*$v$*$v$*$condition

### 2.2.2 The Five Porter Steps

| Step | Name | Purpose | Sample Rules (Suffix → Stem) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1a** | Plural Suffixes | Handle plurals and past participles | `sses → ss`, `ies → i`, `ss → ss`, `s → ∅` |
| **1b** | Past Tense / Gerund | Remove `-ed`, `-ing` | `eed → ee` (if $m > 0$), `ed → ∅` (if vowel in stem), `ing → ∅` (if vowel in stem) |
| **1c** | Y to I | Normalize `-y` to `-i` | `y → i` (if stem has vowel & $m > 0$) |
| **2** | Suffix Stripping | Remove derivational suffixes | `ational → ate`, `tional → tion`, `izer → ize`, `ization → ize`, `fulness → ful`, `ousli → ous`, `iveness → ive` |
| **3** | Suffix Stripping (IC) | Remove `-ic`, `-ical`, `-ful`, `-ness` | `icate → ic`, `ative → ∅`, `alize → al`, `ical → ic`, `ful → ∅`, `ness → ∅` |
| **4** | Long-Suffix Removal | Remove derivational suffixes with measure guard | `al → ∅` (if $m > 1$), `ance → ∅`, `ence → ∅`, `er → ∅` (if $m > 1$), `ic → ∅` (if $m > 1$) |
| **5a** | Final E Removal | Remove trailing `e` | `e → ∅` (if $m > 1$) or `e → ∅` (if $m = 1$ and not ending `CVC`) |
| **5b** | Double Letter Reduction | Collapse `ll` → `l` | `ll → l` (if $m > 1$) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following compact reference table consolidates **every** algorithmically essential formula tested in the KTU 2024 ESE for Module 3 — Stemming.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Key Constraint / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stemming Function | $S: W \rightarrow \Sigma^*$ | Maps word to a pseudo-root string |
| 2 | Stem Equivalence | $S(w_1) = S(w_2) = s$ | All morphological variants collapse to the same stem |
| 3 | Porter Measure | $m(w) = \vert VC\text{-pairs} \vert$ | Vowels = $\{a,e,i,o,u,y\}$ with first-letter exclusion |
| 4 | Measure Conditions | $*$ = $m=0$, $*$v$*$ = $m=1$, $*$v$*$v$*$ = $m>1$ | Used as guard in Steps 2, 3, 4, 5 |
| 5 | Suffix Stripping | $w' = w[0..(|w|-|\sigma|)] \oplus \rho$ | Apply only if condition $C(w)$ is true |
| 6 | Vowel-in-Stem Test | $\exists i : w[i] \in V$ | Required for `-ed`, `-ing` removal (Step 1b) |
| 7 | Porter Step 1a | `sses → ss`, `ies → i`, `s → ∅` | Plural normalization |
| 8 | Porter Step 1b | `eed → ee` (if $m>0$), `ed → ∅`, `ing → ∅` | Tense/gerund removal |
| 9 | Porter Step 1c | `y → i` (if stem has vowel) | Standardization |
| 10 | Porter Step 2 | `ational → ate`, `ization → ize` etc. | Only if $m>0$ in stem |
| 11 | Porter Step 3 | `icate → ic`, `ative → ∅` etc. | Only if $m>0$ in stem |
| 12 | Porter Step 4 | `al → ∅` (if $m>1$), `er → ∅` (if $m>1$) | Aggressive suffix removal |
| 13 | Porter Step 5a | `e → ∅` (if $m>1$) or special CVC rule | Final cleanup |
| 14 | Porter Step 5b | `ll → l` (if $m>1$) | Double-letter collapse |
| 15 | Stem Confusion Types | Over-stemming, Under-stemming | Critical evaluation metric |

## 2.4 Comparative Analysis of Popular Stemmers

| Stemmer | Algorithm Type | Strengths | Weaknesses | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Porter (1980)** | Rule-based, 5 phases | Simple, language-agnostic baseline | Aggressive, not linguistic | **Very Fast** |
| **Lovins (1968)** | Rule-based, longest-match lookup | Handles longest suffixes | Single-pass, limited rules | Fast |
| **Paice/Husk (1990)** | Iterative rule-based | Iterative, table-driven | Brittle, complex | Moderate |
| **Lancaster (1990)** | Iterative rule-based (more aggressive than Porter) | Highly aggressive | Severe over-stemming | Fast |
| **Snowball (Porter2)** | Refined Porter | Multi-lingual, well-documented | Slightly slower than Porter | Fast |
| **Krovetz (1993)** | Dictionary-driven | More linguistically accurate | Requires lexicon, slower | Slow |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production-grade search and NLP systems, stemming is **deployed at the indexing layer**, not the query layer. Modern systems (e.g., Elasticsearch) often provide stemming as a **configurable analyzer** with options like `porter_stem`, `snowball`, and `kstem`. The trade-off is captured in two well-known metrics:

- **Precision** — How many retrieved documents are *relevant*? (Under-stemming hurts this.)
- **Recall** — How many relevant documents are *retrieved*? (Over-stemming helps recall but hurts precision.)

The **Stemming Strength Spectrum** can be visualized as:

$$\text{Lancaster (most aggressive)} \succ \text{Porter} \succ \text{Snowball} \succ \text{Krovetz} \succ \text{Lemmatizer (least aggressive)}$$

# 🧮 MODULE 3: STEMMING — SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATION & ANALYTICAL TRACE

## 3.1 Worked Example — Porter Stemmer on `CARRIES`

We will trace the full Porter algorithm on the input word $w_0 = $ `carries`, with detailed step-by-step justification for every rule application.

### Initial Setup
Let $w_0 = $ `carries`. Define the string:

$$w_0 = c \, a \, r \, r \, i \, e \, s$$

Total length $|w_0| = 7$. Vowel set $V = \{a, e, i, o, u, y\}$.

### Step 1a — Plural Suffix Normalization

Inspect the suffix of $w_0$: it ends in `s` (length 1). Check the rules of Step 1a in priority order:

1. `sses → ss`: `carries` does **not** end in `sses`. **Skip.**
2. `ies → i`: `carries` ends in `ies` (the last 3 characters are `i`, `e`, `s`). ✅ **Match!**

Apply the rule:

$$w_1 = w_0[0..(|w_0| - 3)] \oplus \rho = w_0[0..4] \oplus \text{`i'}$$

Compute the prefix:

$$w_0[0..4] = c \, a \, r \, r \, i \rightarrow \text{`carri'}$$

Append the replacement `i`:

$$w_1 = \text{`carri'} \oplus \text{`i'} = \text{`carrii'}$$

> **Halt — Step 1a completes with $w_1 = $ `carrii`.**

### Step 1b — Past Tense & Gerund Cleanup

Inspect the suffix of $w_1 = $ `carrii`:
- Does it end in `eed`? No. **Skip rule `(eed → ee)`.**
- Does it end in `ed`? No. **Skip.**
- Does it end in `ing`? No. **Skip.**

**No changes.** $w_1 = $ `carrii` remains.

### Step 1c — Y → I Normalization

Suffix of $w_1 = $ `carrii` ends in `i`. The rule is:

> Replace `y → i` **if** there is a vowel in the stem (i.e., the prefix before the `y`).

But $w_1$ ends in `i`, not `y`. **Rule does not apply.** No changes.

### Step 2 — Derivational Suffix Stripping

Inspect $w_1 = $ `carrii`. Its suffix `ii` does not match any Step 2 rule (`ational`, `tional`, `ization`, `icate`, `ative`, `alize`, `iciti`, `ical`, `ful`, `ness`, etc.). **No changes.**

### Step 3 — Derivational Suffix Stripping (Continued)

Inspect $w_1 = $ `carrii`. Its suffix `ii` does not match any Step 3 rule. **No changes.**

### Step 4 — Long-Suffix Removal with Measure Guard

Inspect $w_1 = $ `carrii`. Check Step 4 rules (e.g., `al`, `ance`, `ence`, `er`, `ic`, `able`, `ible`, `ant`, `ement`, `ment`, `ent`, `ion`, `ou`, `ism`, `ate`, `iti`, `ous`, `ive`, `ize`).

**None match.** No changes.

### Step 5a — Final `e` Removal

Inspect $w_1 = $ `carrii`. Does it end in `e`? **No.** Skip.

### Step 5b — Double Letter Reduction

Inspect $w_1 = $ `carrii`. Does it end in `ll`? **No.** Skip.

### Final Stem Output

$$S(\text{`carries'}) = w_1 = \text{`carrii'}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The Porter stemmer's output `carrii` is **not a valid English word**. This is a textbook example of why stemming sacrifices *linguistic accuracy* for *morphological compression*. In a downstream search engine, however, `carrii` would still correctly conflate with `carried` and `carrier` (which also stem to `carri`), improving recall.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Porter Stemmer on `STUDIES`

### Step 1a
- `studies` ends in `ies`. Rule `ies → i` applies. ✅
- New word: `studi`

$$w_1 = w_0[0..4] \oplus \text{`i'} = \text{`studi'}$$

### Step 1b
- `studi` does not end in `eed`, `ed`, or `ing`. **No match.**

### Step 1c
- `studi` ends in `i`. The Porter rule for `y → i` only triggers on a literal `y` suffix. **No match.**

### Steps 2, 3, 4
- `studi` does not match any derivational suffix. **No match.**

### Step 5a
- `studi` does not end in `e`. **No match.**

### Step 5b
- `studi` does not end in `ll`. **No match.**

### Final Output

$$S(\text{`studies'}) = \text{`studi'}$$

> **Compare:** If we had used a **Lemmatizer** instead, the output would have been `study` (a valid lemma), not `studi`.

## 3.4 Measure Computation Drill (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Measure $m(w)$ is **the single most-tested concept** in KTU 2024 ESE questions on Porter Stemming. Memorize the algorithm and be prepared to compute $m$ for arbitrary words.

**Rule for identifying Vowels in Porter's algorithm:**

1. The **first letter** of any word is treated as a **consonant** (by convention).
2. $y$ is treated as a **vowel** only if it appears in a position where it is not the first letter and is preceded by a consonant.
3. All other letters $\{a, e, i, o, u\}$ are always vowels.

**Worked Drill:** Compute $m$ for the word `appreciate`.

- Strip conventional first-letter flag: `a` is now treated as a consonant.
- Iterate: `p`(C) `p`(C) `r`(C) `e`(V) `c`(C) `i`(V) `a`(V) `t`(C) `e`(V)
- Identify VC transitions: $(e, c)$, $(i, a)$ — note: $a$ is followed by $t$ (V→C) — and $(a, t)$ counts as one VC if we scan continuously.
- Final count: $m(\text{`appreciate'}) = 3$.

**Result:** The Measure $m$ being $> 1$ means the stem is "robust" enough to permit aggressive derivational suffix removal in Step 2 and beyond.

    I --> J
    J --> K{Step 3: IC Suffixes if m greater than 0}
    K --> K1[icate to ic]

    K --> K3[alize to al]
    K --> K4[ical to ic]
    K --> K5[ful to empty]

    M --> M1[al to empty]

    M --> M3[ence to empty]
    M --> M4[er to empty]
    M --> M5[ic to empty]

    M1 --> N[Word w6]

## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define stemming. How does it differ from lemmatization?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Stemming is a rule-based morphological normalization technique used in NLP that reduces inflected or derived words to their **stem** — a pseudo-root form — by chopping off word endings using heuristic suffix-stripping rules. The resulting stem need not be a valid dictionary word.

**Key Differences (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | Stemming | Lemmatization |
|---|---|---|
| **Output** | Pseudo-root (may not be a real word) | Valid dictionary lemma |
| **Method** | Rule-based, heuristic | Linguistic, vocabulary-driven, uses POS tags |
| **Speed** | Very fast | Slower (requires dictionary lookup & morphological analysis) |
| **Accuracy** | Lower | Higher |
| **Example** | `studies` → `studi` | `studies` → `study` |
| **Example** | `better` → `better` | `better` → `good` |

> *Award full 3 marks if both the definition and at least 3 distinguishing points are present.*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of "Measure of a Word Stem" used in the Porter Stemmer. Compute the measure for the word "nationalize".**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Concept (1.5 Marks):** The Measure $m(w)$ of a word stem, as defined by the Porter Stemmer, is the number of **vowel–consonant (VC) sequences** in the stem. It serves as a heuristic safeguard against over-stemming short or weak stems. The measure is used as a guard condition in Steps 2, 3, 4, and 5 of the algorithm.

**Computation for "nationalize" (1.5 Marks):**

- Treat first letter `n` as a consonant (by convention).
- Sequence: `n`(C) `a`(V) `t`(C) `i`(V) `o`(V) `n`(C) `a`(V) `l`(C) `i`(V) `z`(C) `e`(V)
- Identify VC transitions:
  1. $(a, t)$ — VC pair #1
  2. $(i, n)$ — no (V→C, not the same index)
  3. $(o, n)$ — VC pair #2
  4. $(a, l)$ — VC pair #3
  5. $(i, z)$ — VC pair #4
  6. $(e, \text{end})$ — no following consonant

$$m(\text{`nationalize'}) = 4$$

> *Award full 3 marks for correct concept definition and accurate count.*

---

## 📌 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse the *Measure of a Word* with the *word length*. Many students write "measure = number of characters," which is **wrong** and will cost 2 marks.
> - **Always** state the Porter convention that the **first letter is treated as a consonant**, even if it is a vowel (e.g., `apple` → `a` is consonant).
> - **Apply the rules in strict priority order.** Skipping to Step 5 without exhausting Steps 1–4 will yield an incorrect stem and lose up to 4 marks.
> - **Mention the Vowel-in-Stem condition** explicitly for Steps 1b and 1c. Examiners allocate 1 mark for this.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the **Porter Stemmer algorithm** in detail. List all five steps and describe the role of the **Measure** function in each step where it is used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **Porter Stemmer** step-by-step on the words `relational`, `generalizations`, and `controllable`. Show the rule applied at each step and compute the final stems. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📘 Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture Overview (2 Marks):**
The Porter Stemmer is a **five-phase, rule-based suffix-stripping algorithm** developed by Martin Porter in 1980. Each phase consists of a list of **conditional suffix-stripping rules**. The output of one rule becomes the input of the next, creating a cascade of morphological reductions. The algorithm is **deterministic**, **language-agnostic** (originally English), and **highly efficient** (linear time in word length).

**Step 1a — Plural Suffixes (1 Mark):**
Rules:
- `sses → ss`
- `ies → i`
- `ss → ss`
- `s → ∅`

**Step 1b — Past Tense / Gerund (1 Mark):**
Rules with Vowel-in-Stem condition:
- `(m>0) eed → ee`
- `ed → ∅` if stem contains a vowel
- `ing → ∅` if stem contains a vowel

**Step 1c — Y → I Normalization (0.5 Mark):**
- `y → i` if the stem has a vowel (excluding the first letter).

**Step 2 — Derivational Suffix Removal (1 Mark):**
Rules applied **only if $m > 0$** in the stem: e.g., `ational → ate`, `tional → tion`, `ization → ize`, `iveness → ive`, `fulness → ful`, `ousli → ous`, `ization → ize`.

**Step 3 — Derivational Suffixes (IC Family) (0.5 Mark):**
Rules with $m > 0$: `icate → ic`, `ative → ∅`, `alize → al`, `ical → ic`, `ful → ∅`, `ness → ∅`.

**Step 4 — Long-Suffix Removal (0.5 Mark):**
Aggressive removal with $m > 1$: e.g., `al → ∅`, `ance → ∅`, `ence → ∅`, `er → ∅`, `ic → ∅`, `able → ∅`, `ible → ∅`, `ant → ∅`, `ement → ∅`, `ment → ∅`, `ent → ∅`, `ion → ∅`, `ou → ∅`, `ism → ∅`, `ate → ∅`, `iti → ∅`, `ous → ∅`, `ive → ∅`, `ize → ∅`.

**Step 5 — Final Cleanup (0.5 Mark):**
- 5a: `e → ∅` if $m > 1$, or if $m = 1$ and stem does not end in CVC.
- 5b: `ll → l` if $m > 1$.

**Role of Measure Function (1 Mark):** The Measure $m(w)$ acts as a **morphological robustness guard**. It prevents the stemmer from chopping off too much from short or vowel-light stems, which would lead to over-stemming. Steps 2, 3, 4, and 5 all condition their rule application on the measure being above certain thresholds, ensuring the resulting stem is "sturdy" enough to retain meaning.

---

#### 📘 Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Trace on `relational`** (2.33 Marks)

- $w_0 = $ `relational`
- Step 1a: No plural suffix match. **No change.**
- Step 1b: Does not end in `eed`/`ed`/`ing`. **No change.**
- Step 1c: Does not end in `y`. **No change.**
- Step 2: Ends in `ational`. Measure of stem `rel` is $m = 1$. Rule condition: $m > 0$ ✅. Apply: `ational → ate`. → `relate`
- Step 3: Does not match Step 3 rules. **No change.**
- Step 4: `relate` ends in `ate`. Measure of `rel` is $1$. Rule requires $m > 1$. ❌ **No change.**
- Step 5a: `relate` ends in `e`. Measure of `relat` is $2 > 1$. ✅ Apply: `e → ∅`. → `relat`
- Step 5b: Does not end in `ll`. **No change.**

**Final Stem:** $S(\text{`relational'}) = \text{`relat'}$

**(ii) Trace on `generalizations`** (2.33 Marks)

- $w_0 = $ `generalizations`
- Step 1a: Ends in `s`. Apply: `s → ∅`. → `generalization`
- Step 1b: No match. **No change.**
- Step 1c: No match. **No change.**
- Step 2: Ends in `ization`. Stem `general` has $m = 2 > 0$ ✅. Apply: `ization → ize`. → `generalize`
- Step 3: Ends in `ize`? No, ends in `alize` — does not match Step 3's `alize → al` (that rule requires $m>0$ and applies to `alize` as a whole). Wait, the rule is `alize → al`. The current word is `generalize`, which does **not** end in `alize`. So **no match in Step 3**.
- Step 4: `generalize` ends in `ize`. Measure of `general` is $2 > 1$ ✅. Apply: `ize → ∅`. → `general`
- Step 5a: `general` does not end in `e`. **No change.**
- Step 5b: No `ll`. **No change.**

**Final Stem:** $S(\text{`generalizations'}) = \text{`general'}$

**(iii) Trace on `controllable`** (2.33 Marks)

- $w_0 = $ `controllable`
- Step 1a: No plural suffix match. **No change.**
- Step 1b: No match. **No change.**
- Step 1c: No match. **No change.**
- Step 2: Does not match Step 2 rules. **No change.**
- Step 3: Does not match Step 3 rules. **No change.**
- Step 4: Ends in `able`. Measure of `controll` is $2 > 1$ ✅. Apply: `able → ∅`. → `controll`
- Step 5a: Does not end in `e`. **No change.**
- Step 5b: Ends in `ll`. Measure of `control` is $2 > 1$ ✅. Apply: `ll → l`. → `control`

**Final Stem:** $S(\text{`controllable'}) = \text{`control'}$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Compare the **Porter, Lancaster, and Snowball** stemmers with respect to aggressiveness, speed, and risk of over-stemming. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **over-stemming** and **under-stemming**? Provide two examples of each and discuss their impact on Information Retrieval systems. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📘 Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Criterion | Porter Stemmer | Lancaster Stemmer | Snowball (Porter2) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Year / Author** | 1980, Martin Porter | 1990, Paice/Husk | 2001, Porter |
| **Algorithm Type** | 5-phase rule-based | Iterative rule-based | Refined Porter |
| **Aggressiveness** | Moderate | Very High (most aggressive) | Moderate (slightly less than Porter) |
| **Speed** | Very Fast | Fast | Fast |
| **Over-stemming Risk** | Moderate | **High** | Low–Moderate |
| **Under-stemming Risk** | Moderate | Low | Low |
| **Multilingual Support** | English only originally | English only | Multiple languages (15+) |
| **Documentation** | Excellent (canonical) | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Example** | `university` → `univers` | `university` → `univ` | `university` → `univers` |

**Narrative Discussion (2 Marks):**

- The **Lancaster stemmer** is the most aggressive because it applies rules iteratively without a strong measure-based guard, leading to severe over-stemming in many cases. It is preferred in **high-recall** scenarios (e.g., legal document search) but is generally avoided in production due to unintuitive stems.
- The **Porter stemmer** is the **academic and industry baseline** for English stemming. Its 5-phase design with measure-based guards strikes a balance between aggression and linguistic safety.
- The **Snowball stemmer** is a **cleaner, multi-lingual re-implementation** of Porter with better-documented rules and easier extensibility. It is the **recommended default** in modern libraries like NLTK (`SnowballStemmer("english")`).

---

#### 📘 Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Definitions (2 Marks):**

- **Over-stemming** occurs when the stemmer removes too much of the word, causing **semantically unrelated words to collapse to the same stem**. This *hurts precision* because irrelevant documents get retrieved.
- **Under-stemming** occurs when the stemmer removes too little, causing **semantically related words to remain distinct**. This *hurts recall* because relevant documents are missed.

**Examples of Over-stemming (2 Marks):**

1. `university` → `univers` and `universe` → `univers`. These are completely different concepts (an institution vs. a cosmological concept), but share the same stem.
2. `general` → `gener` and `generic` → `gener`. Although related in root, collapsing them loses semantic specificity.

**Examples of Under-stemming (2 Marks):**

1. `connect` → `connect` and `connected` → `connect` (this is **correctly** stemmed by Porter). However, `connection` → `connect` (also correct). An under-stemmer might leave `connectedly` → `connectedly`, failing to conflate with `connect`.
2. A naive stemmer that only removes `-s` would treat `runs` and `running` as distinct, leaving morphologically related words ungrouped.

**Impact on Information Retrieval (1 Mark):**

In an IR system using the vector space model, over-stemming inflates the **False Positive Rate** (irrelevant documents retrieved), while under-stemming inflates the **False Negative Rate** (relevant documents missed). Modern search engines mitigate this by **combining stemming with lemmatization**, **field-specific stemming rules**, and **semantic embeddings** that go beyond surface morphology.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density, last-minute revision summary for KTU 2024 ESE Module 3 — Stemming.**

- ☐ **Stemming** is a **rule-based morphological normalization** technique that chops off suffixes using heuristics.
- ☐ **Stem ≠ Lemma.** Stems need not be valid words; lemmas must be.
- ☐ The **Porter Stemmer (1980)** is the canonical KTU 2024 algorithm, structured in **5 sequential steps**.
- ☐ The **Measure function** $m(w)$ counts **VC (vowel–consonant) sequences** in the stem.
- ☐ First letter of a word is always treated as a **consonant** in Porter's measure.
- ☐ **Step 1a** handles plurals; **Step 1b** handles past tense & gerund; **Step 1c** handles `y → i`.
- ☐ **Steps 2, 3** apply derivational suffix rules gated by $m > 0$.
- ☐ **Step 4** is the aggressive suffix removal gated by $m > 1$.
- ☐ **Step 5a** removes a final `e` (if $m > 1$ or special CVC condition); **Step 5b** collapses `ll → l` (if $m > 1$).
- ☐ **Over-stemming** = chopping too much → hurts **precision**.
- ☐ **Under-stemming** = chopping too little → hurts **recall**.
- ☐ Stemmer aggressiveness order: **Lancaster ≻ Porter ≻ Snowball ≻ Krovetz ≻ Lemmatizer**.
- ☐ Stemming is used at the **indexing layer** of search engines; lemmatization is used where **linguistic accuracy** is critical.
- ☐ Stemming **trades accuracy for speed** — ideal for high-volume, recall-oriented applications.

> **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination!** 🚀
