## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Define **TF-IDF**. Explain why **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)** uses a logarithm in its formulation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 mark):** TF-IDF stands for *Term Frequency – Inverse Document Frequency*. It is a statistical measure used to evaluate the importance of a word to a document in a collection (corpus). It is computed as the product of TF and IDF:
>
> $$\text{TF-IDF}(t, d) = \text{TF}(t, d) \times \text{IDF}(t)$$
>
> **Why IDF uses logarithm (2 marks):**
> 1. **Dampening effect:** Without log, a term in 1 document vs. 1000 documents would have an IDF ratio of 1000 — disproportionately large. Log compresses this scale.
> 2. **Multiplicative property:** $\log(a \cdot b) = \log a + \log b$ makes TF-IDF scores additive in nature, easing computation and comparison.
> 3. **Intuition:** Doubling the rarity of a term should not double its weight — logarithmic growth reflects diminishing returns.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**

Distinguish between **Cosine Similarity** and **Euclidean Distance** as vector similarity measures. State one scenario where each is preferred.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cosine Similarity | Euclidean Distance |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| Formula | $\frac{\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}}{\Vert\vec{A}\Vert\Vert\vec{B}\Vert}$ | $\sqrt{\sum (A_i - B_i)^2}$ |
| Range | $[0,1]$ for non-negative vectors | $[0, \infty)$ |
| Magnitude-sensitive? | No (direction-only) | Yes |
| Preferred when | Document length varies; only word *proportions* matter (e.g., search engines) | Absolute magnitudes matter (e.g., word counts, sensor readings) |

(1 mark for each distinction row + 1 mark for scenario.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *TF-IDF Heavy*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Given the following corpus of **three documents**:

- **D1:** *"NLP is fun NLP is powerful"*
- **D2:** *"Machine learning is fun"*
- **D3:** *"Deep learning is powerful"*

Compute the **TF-IDF vector** for D1 using the *normalized TF* and *classic IDF* (log base 10). Show all intermediate calculations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Jaccard Similarity** measure. Compute the Jaccard similarity between the *vocabulary sets* of D1 and D3 after removing stop-words "is" and "the". Why is Jaccard inappropriate for weighted TF-IDF vectors without modification? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a): TF-IDF for D1 (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the vocabulary across all three documents (1 mark):**

After tokenization, the unique terms are:
$$V = \{\text{NLP, is, fun, powerful, machine, learning, deep}\}$$

(We ignore the article "the" since it does not appear in this corpus, and we treat "is" as a stop-word for IDF computation only if asked; per KTU norms, we retain all terms.)

So $\vert V \vert = 7$ and $N = 3$.

**Step 2 — Compute raw term counts and document frequencies (1 mark):**

| Term | D1 | D2 | D3 | df(t) |
|------|----|----|----|-------|
| NLP | 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| is | 2 | 1 | 1 | 3 |
| fun | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 |
| powerful | 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| machine | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| learning | 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| deep | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |

**Step 3 — Normalized TF for D1 (1 mark):**

Total terms in D1: $\vert D1 \vert = 6$ (NLP ×2, is ×2, fun ×1, powerful ×1).

$$\text{TF}(\text{NLP}, D1) = 2/6 \approx 0.3333$$

$$\text{TF}(\text{is}, D1) = 2/6 \approx 0.3333$$

$$\text{TF}(\text{fun}, D1) = 1/6 \approx 0.1667$$

$$\text{TF}(\text{powerful}, D1) = 1/6 \approx 0.1667$$

**Step 4 — Compute IDF (log base 10) (1 mark):**

$$\text{IDF}(\text{NLP}) = \log_{10}(3/1) = \log_{10}(3) \approx 0.4771$$

$$\text{IDF}(\text{is}) = \log_{10}(3/3) = \log_{10}(1) = 0$$

$$\text{IDF}(\text{fun}) = \log_{10}(3/2) = \log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.1761$$

$$\text{IDF}(\text{powerful}) = \log_{10}(3/2) \approx 0.1761$$

**Step 5 — Multiply TF × IDF to get the TF-IDF vector for D1 (2 marks):**

$$\text{TF-IDF}(\text{NLP}, D1) = 0.3333 \times 0.4771 \approx 0.1590$$

$$\text{TF-IDF}(\text{is}, D1) = 0.3333 \times 0 = 0$$

$$\text{TF-IDF}(\text{fun}, D1) = 0.1667 \times 0.1761 \approx 0.0294$$

$$\text{TF-IDF}(\text{powerful}, D1) = 0.1667 \times 0.1761 \approx 0.0294$$

$$\boxed{\vec{D1}_{\text{TF-IDF}} = [0.1590,\; 0,\; 0.0294,\; 0.0294,\; 0,\; 0,\; 0]}$$

in the order $[NLP, is, fun, powerful, machine, learning, deep]$.

**Step 6 — Key observation (1 mark):** Because "is" appears in *all three* documents, its IDF is 0 and consequently its TF-IDF contribution is 0 — it carries no discriminative power, exactly as the IDF mechanism intends.

---

#### Part (b): Jaccard Similarity (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jaccard Similarity** between two sets $A$ and $B$ is the ratio of the size of their intersection to the size of their union:
>
> $$J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$$
>
> Range: $[0, 1]$; 1 = identical, 0 = disjoint.

**Step 1 — Build vocabulary sets for D1 and D3 after removing stop-words (1 mark):**

Unique terms in **D1** (excluding "is"): $\{NLP, fun, powerful\}$

Unique terms in **D3** (excluding "is"): $\{deep, learning, powerful\}$

**Step 2 — Compute intersection and union (1 mark):**

$$A \cap B = \{\text{powerful}\} \implies \vert A \cap B \vert = 1$$

$$A \cup B = \{\text{NLP, fun, powerful, deep, learning}\} \implies \vert A \cup B \vert = 5$$

**Step 3 — Compute Jaccard similarity (1 mark):**

$$J(D1, D3) = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$$

**Step 4 — Why Jaccard is inappropriate for weighted TF-IDF vectors (2 marks):**

1. Jaccard is defined on **sets**, treating every term as either present or absent (binary). It discards the **magnitude information** captured by TF-IDF weights (e.g., that "NLP" appears twice in D1).
2. Two documents could share *no* terms but be semantically related — Jaccard would give 0, missing that nuance.
3. To use Jaccard on weighted vectors, one must *threshold* or *binarize* first, which loses ordering information.
4. **Soft alternatives** like *continuous Jaccard* or *Tanimoto coefficient* exist but are beyond KTU Module 3 scope.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Vector Similarity Heavy*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define **Cosine Similarity**. Derive the formula for cosine similarity between two vectors $\vec{A} = (3, 2, 0, 5)$ and $\vec{B} = (1, 0, 4, 2)$. Compute the result and interpret. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the TF-IDF vectors:

- $\vec{D1} = (0.8, 0.0, 0.3, 0.5)$
- $\vec{D2} = (0.6, 0.4, 0.0, 0.5)$

Compute their **cosine similarity** and **Euclidean distance**. Compare the two metrics and explain why cosine is preferred for **document retrieval**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a): Cosine Similarity Definition & Computation (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cosine Similarity** is a measure of similarity between two non-zero vectors defined as the cosine of the angle between them in the vector space. It is given by:
>
> $$\text{cos\_sim}(\vec{A}, \vec{B}) = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert \vec{A} \Vert \, \Vert \vec{B} \Vert} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i B_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i^2}}$$

**Step 1 — Dot product $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}$ (1 mark):**

$$\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = (3)(1) + (2)(0) + (0)(4) + (5)(2) = 3 + 0 + 0 + 10 = 13$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude of $\vec{A}$ (1 mark):**

$$\Vert \vec{A} \Vert = \sqrt{3^2 + 2^2 + 0^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{9 + 4 + 0 + 25} = \sqrt{38} \approx 6.1644$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of $\vec{B}$ (1 mark):**

$$\Vert \vec{B} \Vert = \sqrt{1^2 + 0^2 + 4^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0 + 16 + 4} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.5826$$

**Step 4 — Cosine similarity (1 mark):**

$$\text{cos\_sim}(\vec{A}, \vec{B}) = \frac{13}{6.1644 \times 4.5826} = \frac{13}{28.2507} \approx 0.4602$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):** A value of $\approx 0.46$ indicates **moderate similarity** between $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$. The vectors are not orthogonal (would be 0) and not identical (would be 1); they share some directional alignment because of the strong overlap in the 4th coordinate (5 × 2 = 10) but differ significantly in dimensions 2, 3, and the relative magnitudes.

---

#### Part (b): Cosine vs Euclidean Comparison (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute cosine similarity (2 marks):**

Dot product:
$$\vec{D1} \cdot \vec{D2} = (0.8)(0.6) + (0.0)(0.4) + (0.3)(0.0) + (0.5)(0.5) = 0.48 + 0 + 0 + 0.25 = 0.73$$

Magnitudes:
$$\Vert \vec{D1} \Vert = \sqrt{0.64 + 0 + 0.09 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.98} \approx 0.9899$$

$$\Vert \vec{D2} \Vert = \sqrt{0.36 + 0.16 + 0 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.77} \approx 0.8775$$

Cosine similarity:
$$\text{cos\_sim}(D1, D2) = \frac{0.73}{(0.9899)(0.8775)} = \frac{0.73}{0.8686} \approx 0.8404$$

**Step 2 — Compute Euclidean distance (2 marks):**

$$\text{dist}(D1, D2) = \sqrt{(0.8-0.6)^2 + (0.0-0.4)^2 + (0.3-0.0)^2 + (0.5-0.5)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{0.04 + 0.16 + 0.09 + 0} = \sqrt{0.29} \approx 0.5385$$

**Step 3 — Comparison and Justification (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cosine (≈ 0.84) | Euclidean (≈ 0.54) |
|--------|----------------|---------------------|
| Semantic interpretation | High directional similarity | Moderate absolute difference |
| Effect of length | Insensitive | Sensitive |

**Why cosine is preferred for document retrieval:**

1. **Length invariance:** Two documents of vastly different sizes (one 10 words, another 10,000 words) but identical term proportions will have Euclidean distance ≈ 99 but cosine similarity = 1. Cosine correctly identifies them as *conceptually identical*.
2. **Stop-word dilution:** Common words inflate all magnitudes; cosine normalizes by magnitude, focusing on relative term distribution.
3. **Empirical performance:** Standard IR benchmarks (TREC, etc.) show cosine-based ranking outperforms Euclidean for document retrieval.
4. **Score interpretation:** Cosine is bounded in $[0,1]$, making thresholding for relevance ranking straightforward.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in TF-IDF & Vector Similarity Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize TF** — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if raw counts are used when the question says "normalized TF".
> 2. **Using log base inconsistently** — If you use $\log_{10}$, stick to it throughout; switching to $\ln$ midway is a 1-mark penalty.
> 3. **Computing IDF with df(t) = 0** — This causes division by zero. Use smoothed IDF: $\log((N+1)/(\text{df}+1)) + 1$.
> 4. **Mixing up document count $N$ with vocabulary size $\vert V \vert$** — $N$ = number of documents; $\vert V \vert$ = number of unique terms. They are different.
> 5. **Magnitude vs. dot product confusion** — Cosine ≠ dot product unless vectors are unit-normalized. Many students skip the denominator.
> 6. **Off-by-one in df(t)** — A term that appears *twice* in one document still contributes df(t) = 1 (because df = *document* frequency, not raw frequency).
> 7. **Forgetting to handle empty/zero vectors** — If a query has no overlap with a document, cosine is undefined ($0/0$). Examiners often award 0 marks if the student doesn't mention edge-case handling.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Vector Similarity & TF-IDF**
>
> - **TF-IDF = TF × IDF.** TF measures local importance; IDF measures global rarity.
> - **Normalized TF** uses the total word count of the *document* as denominator.
> - **Classic IDF:** $\log(N / \text{df}(t))$; **Smoothed IDF:** $\log((N+1)/(\text{df}(t)+1)) + 1$.
> - **df(t) ≠ raw frequency** of t — it counts how many *documents* contain t.
> - **Cosine similarity** is in $[-1, 1]$ generally and $[0, 1]$ for non-negative vectors (e.g., TF-IDF).
> - **Cosine = 1 → identical direction; Cosine = 0 → orthogonal (no shared terms).**
> - **Euclidean distance** is magnitude-sensitive; convert to similarity via $1/(1+d)$.
> - **Jaccard** is set-based; useless on weighted vectors without binarization.
> - **Cosine ignores document length**, making it ideal for retrieval where length varies.
> - **TF-IDF dampens stop-words** by assigning them low or zero weight automatically.
> - **Order of operations:** Tokenize → Remove stop-words (optional) → Build vocab → Compute TF → Compute IDF → Multiply → Vectorize → Cosine / Distance.
> - **Edge case:** If a query has zero overlap with a document, cosine is 0/0; handle via smoothing or thresholding.
> - **Memory aid:** "TF = how *often*, IDF = how *unique*, TF-IDF = how *informative*, Cosine = how *similar*."

---

*End of Module 3 — Topic: Vector Similarity & TF-IDF | PEIET864 | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Word-to-Index Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Word-to-Index Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Word-to-Index Mapping** (also called *Word-to-ID Mapping* or *Lexicon Construction*) is a fundamental preprocessing technique in Natural Language Processing (NLP) that constructs a deterministic, bijective (or injective) lookup dictionary assigning a unique non-negative integer index to every distinct lexical token observed in a training corpus.

Formally, given a tokenized corpus $C = \{t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots, t_n\}$, a Word-to-Index Mapping function $\mathcal{M}$ is defined as:

$$\mathcal{M}: \mathcal{V} \rightarrow \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$$

where $\mathcal{V}$ is the set of unique vocabulary words extracted from $C$, and the resulting dictionary is denoted $D = \{(w_i, \text{idx}_i) \mid w_i \in \mathcal{V}\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a foundational prerequisite for *Bag-of-Words*, *TF-IDF*, *Word Embeddings*, and *Neural Language Models*. Mastering this mapping is **mandatory** before tackling Module 4 representation schemes.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Library Card Catalogue"

Imagine you walk into a massive library containing **millions of books**, but the librarian tells you: *"To find any book, you must know its 7-digit catalogue number."* 

- **Books** → unique words in your corpus (e.g., *"cat", "run", "neural"*)
- **Catalogue Number** → the integer index assigned (e.g., `0`, `1`, `2`, …)
- **The Card Catalogue Book** → the mapping dictionary $\mathcal{M}$
- **The Librarian** → the tokenizer + mapping function

When you later submit a sentence to a machine learning model, the model cannot read the word *"cat"* directly. It looks up *"cat"* in the catalogue and retrieves the number `42` (for example). The model then works entirely with numbers — exactly like a librarian working with numeric catalogue codes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Word-to-Index Mapping is simply *assigning every unique word in the dataset a unique number*, so that computers (which understand numbers, not strings) can process language efficiently.

## 1.3 Why It Matters in NLP Pipelines

| # | Engineering Rationale |
|---|----------------------|
| 1 | Machine learning models require **numeric tensor inputs**; raw strings are incompatible with matrix operations. |
| 2 | Index lookups run in **O(1)** time complexity via hash-table dictionaries, enabling real-time preprocessing. |
| 3 | It serves as the gateway step for **One-Hot Encoding**, **TF-IDF matrices**, **Embedding Layers**, and **Vocabulary Constraints**. |
| 4 | It enables **out-of-vocabulary (OOV) handling** through reserved indices for special tokens. |
| 5 | It produces a **fixed-size vocabulary**, which bounds the dimensionality of subsequent representations. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Decomposition — Building the Mapping

The construction of a word-to-index mapping proceeds through **five systematic phases**, each contributing to a robust final dictionary.

### Phase 1 — Corpus Collection & Tokenization
- Aggregate all training documents into a single corpus or maintain a per-document list.
- Apply a chosen tokenization scheme (whitespace, regex, Byte-Pair Encoding, WordPiece, etc.).
- **Output:** A flat token sequence $C = (t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n)$.

### Phase 2 — Vocabulary Extraction
- Apply the set-comprehension operation to extract unique tokens:

$$\mathcal{V} = \text{unique}(C)$$

- **Output:** A set of unique words with duplicates removed. Cardinality $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ is called the **vocabulary size** $V$.

### Phase 3 — Frequency Profiling (Optional but Standard Practice)
- Count occurrences of each word $w \in \mathcal{V}$:

$$\text{freq}(w) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}(t_i = w)$$

where $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function. Words are then **sorted in descending frequency order**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why sort by frequency?** Frequent words receive lower indices, which optimizes embedding lookup patterns in modern frameworks (TensorFlow, PyTorch) where low indices map to frequently-accessed memory regions.

### Phase 4 — Index Assignment
- Iterate through the sorted vocabulary, assigning indices sequentially:

$$\text{idx}(w_k) = k, \quad k \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, V-1\}$$

- Reserve index `0` (or `1`, depending on convention) for the padding token **`<PAD>`** and a high index for the unknown-word token **`<UNK>`**.

### Phase 5 — Dictionary Construction & Inversion
- Build the forward dictionary $D_{\text{fwd}}: w \mapsto \text{idx}$.
- Build the inverse dictionary $D_{\text{inv}}: \text{idx} \mapsto w$ (used during decoding/translation tasks).

## 2.2 Special Tokens Convention (KTU Board-Favorite Topic)

| Token | Standard Index | Purpose |
|-------|---------------|---------|
| `<PAD>` | **0** | Pads shorter sequences to a uniform length in batches. |
| `<UNK>` | **1** (or last index) | Replaces any word absent from the vocabulary. |
| `<SOS>` | varies | *Start of Sentence* marker for sequence generation. |
| `<EOS>` | varies | *End of Sentence* marker. |
| `<BOS>` | varies | *Beginning of Sequence* marker. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Vocabulary Size | $V = \vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ | Cardinality; dimensionless count |
| 2 | Word Frequency | $\text{freq}(w) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}(t_i = w)$ | Integer count |
| 3 | Coverage Ratio | $\text{coverage} = \frac{\text{words in vocab}}{\text{words in corpus}}$ | Fraction in $[0, 1]$ |
| 4 | OOV Rate | $\text{OOV} = \frac{\text{OOV tokens in test}}{\text{total test tokens}}$ | Should be **minimized** |
| 5 | Mapping Function | $\mathcal{M}: w \in \mathcal{V} \to k \in \{0,1,\dots,V-1\}$ | Deterministic |
| 6 | Inverse Mapping | $\mathcal{M}^{-1}: k \to w$ | Used for decoding |
| 7 | Sparsity of One-Hot Vector | $\rho = 1 - \frac{1}{V}$ | Approaches 1 as $V \to \infty$ |
| 8 | Memory Footprint (Hash Dict) | $M \approx O(V \cdot L)$ | $L$ = avg. token length (bytes) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Word-to-Index Mapping is **not merely an academic exercise**; it powers:

- **Search Engines** (Google, Bing) — token → inverted-index lookup.
- **Chatbots & LLMs** — preprocessing layer before token embedding matrices $E \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$.
- **Speech Recognition Systems** — decoding acoustic models' outputs back to text.
- **Sentiment Analysis Pipelines** — converting product reviews to feature vectors.
- **Machine Translation** — source-side and target-side vocabularies with shared indices.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Hand-Traceable Demonstration

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This example is intentionally constructed to be fully reproducible on paper — exactly the style KTU examiners expect in 14-mark derivations.

### Step 0 — Given Corpus
Suppose our training corpus $C$ contains the following three tokenized sentences:

$$C = \{ \text{"the cat sat"}, \text{"the dog ran"}, \text{"the cat ran"} \}$$

### Step 1 — Flatten the Corpus
Concatenate all tokens into a single sequence:

$$C_{\text{flat}} = (\text{the}, \text{cat}, \text{sat}, \text{the}, \text{dog}, \text{ran}, \text{the}, \text{cat}, \text{ran})$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Vocabulary Set $\mathcal{V}$
Apply uniqueness operation:

$$\mathcal{V} = \{\text{the}, \text{cat}, \text{sat}, \text{dog}, \text{ran}\}$$

Therefore, $V = \vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 5$.

### Step 3 — Compute Word Frequencies

The frequency count is computed as:

$$\text{freq}(w) = \sum_{i=1}^{9} \mathbb{1}(t_i = w)$$

Evaluating term-by-term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{freq}(\text{the}) &= \mathbb{1}(\text{the}) + \mathbb{1}(\text{the}) + \mathbb{1}(\text{the}) = 3 \\
\text{freq}(\text{cat}) &= \mathbb{1}(\text{cat}) + \mathbb{1}(\text{cat}) = 2 \\
\text{freq}(\text{ran}) &= \mathbb{1}(\text{ran}) + \mathbb{1}(\text{ran}) = 2 \\
\text{freq}(\text{sat}) &= \mathbb{1}(\text{sat}) = 1 \\
\text{freq}(\text{dog}) &= \mathbb{1}(\text{dog}) = 1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Sort Vocabulary by Descending Frequency
Resolution of frequency ties (e.g., *cat* vs *ran*) is performed alphabetically or by first-occurrence — KTU accepts either convention.

$$\mathcal{V}_{\text{sorted}} = (\text{the}, \text{cat}, \text{ran}, \text{dog}, \text{sat})$$

### Step 5 — Assign Integer Indices
Apply the mapping rule $\text{idx}(w_k) = k$ starting from $k = 0$ (or $k = 1$ if reserving 0 for `<PAD>`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{the}  &\mapsto 0 \quad (\text{freq} = 3) \\
\text{cat}  &\mapsto 1 \quad (\text{freq} = 2) \\
\text{ran}  &\mapsto 2 \quad (\text{freq} = 2) \\
\text{dog}  &\mapsto 3 \quad (\text{freq} = 1) \\
\text{sat}  &\mapsto 4 \quad (\text{freq} = 1) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Construct the Forward Dictionary $D_{\text{fwd}}$

$$D_{\text{fwd}} = \{ (\text{the}, 0), (\text{cat}, 1), (\text{ran}, 2), (\text{dog}, 3), (\text{sat}, 4) \}$$

### Step 7 — Construct the Inverse Dictionary $D_{\text{inv}}$

$$D_{\text{inv}} = \{ (0, \text{the}), (1, \text{cat}), (2, \text{ran}), (3, \text{dog}), (4, \text{sat}) \}$$

### Step 8 — Encode a Test Sentence
Encode the test sentence *"the dog sat"*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{encode}(\text{the})  &= D_{\text{fwd}}[\text{the}] = 0 \\
\text{encode}(\text{dog}) &= D_{\text{fwd}}[\text{dog}] = 3 \\
\text{encode}(\text{sat}) &= D_{\text{fwd}}[\text{sat}] = 4 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Final encoded vector:

$$\mathbf{x} = [0, 3, 4]$$

### Step 9 — Decode Back to Original Sentence (Round-Trip Verification)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{inv}}[0] &= \text{the} \\
D_{\text{inv}}[3] &= \text{dog} \\
D_{\text{inv}}[4] &= \text{sat} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Round-trip confirmed: *"the dog sat"* is faithfully recovered.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Handling Out-of-Vocabulary Words

Suppose a new test sentence arrives containing the unseen word *"elephant"*:

$$\text{test} = \text{"the elephant sat"}$$

Since *"elephant"* $\notin \mathcal{V}$, the mapping function must invoke the fallback strategy:

$$
\text{idx}(\text{elephant}) = \begin{cases}
D_{\text{fwd}}[\text{elephant}] & \text{if elephant} \in \mathcal{V} \\
\text{idx}(\text{<UNK>}) = 1 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Encoded vector:

$$\mathbf{x} = [0, 1, 4]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** The OOV mechanism prevents the system from *crashing* when encountering novel vocabulary. Without `<UNK>` handling, the lookup $D_{\text{fwd}}[\text{elephant}]$ raises a `KeyError`, breaking the entire pipeline.

## 3.3 Mathematical Property Summary

| Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Implication |
|----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Determinism** | $\forall w \in \mathcal{V}: \mathcal{M}(w) = \text{unique } k$ | Same word always → same index |
| **Injectivity** | $\mathcal{M}(w_i) = \mathcal{M}(w_j) \Rightarrow w_i = w_j$ | No two words share an index |
| **Surjectivity** | $\forall k \in \{0,\dots,V-1\}, \exists w \in \mathcal{V}: \mathcal{M}(w) = k$ | Every index is "used" |
| **Computability** | $\mathcal{M}$ implemented via hash table | $O(1)$ average-case lookup |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — End-to-End Word-to-Index Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Corpus C] --> B[Tokenizer Module]
    B --> C[Token Sequence T]
    C --> D[Vocabulary Extractor]
    D --> E[Unique Word Set V]
    E --> F[Frequency Counter]
    F --> G[Sorted Vocabulary Vsort]
    G --> H[Index Assigner]
    P[Special Tokens PAD UNK] --> H
    H --> I[Forward Dictionary Dfwd]
    H --> J[Inverse Dictionary Dinv]
    I --> K[Encoded Sentence Vector]
    J --> L[Decoded Original Sentence]
    K --> M[ML Model / Embedding Layer]
    M --> N[Downstream NLP Task]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Tokens flow top-down. The same Vocabulary Extractor feeds both frequency analysis (left branch) and the index assignment stage (right branch). Special tokens are injected as auxiliary inputs to the Index Assigner.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Mapping Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Word w] --> B{w in Vocabulary V?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Lookup Dfwd w]
    C --> D[Return Integer Index]
    B -- No --> E[Return UNK Index]
    E --> D
    D --> F[Store in Sequence Buffer]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Computational Layer Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER
        A1[Raw Text Documents]
    end
    subgraph PREPROCESSING_LAYER
        B1[Tokenization]
        B2[Stop-word Removal]
        B3[Stemming or Lemmatization]
    end
    subgraph LEXICON_LAYER
        C1[Word to Index Mapping]
        C2[Index to Word Mapping]
        C3[Special Token Handler]
    end
    subgraph REPRESENTATION_LAYER
        D1[One-Hot Encoding]
        D2[TF-IDF Vectorizer]
        D3[Word Embedding Matrix]
    end
    A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    C1 --> D3
    D3 --> E1[Neural Network Input]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This block-level schematic positions Word-to-Index Mapping as the critical **bridge** between symbolic preprocessing (text) and numerical representation (vectors). Without this bridge, downstream neural networks cannot ingest language data.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` 
**Define Word-to-Index Mapping. Why is it considered an essential preprocessing step in any NLP pipeline?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Word-to-Index Mapping is the process of constructing a deterministic dictionary that assigns a unique non-negative integer index to every distinct word in a tokenized corpus. Formally, $\mathcal{M}: \mathcal{V} \to \{0, 1, \dots, V-1\}$.

It is essential because:
1. **(1 mark)** Machine learning models operate on numeric tensors; raw strings cannot be processed directly by matrix operations.
2. **(1 mark)** It enables fast $O(1)$ lookup via hash tables, critical for real-time applications.
3. **(1 mark)** It forms the prerequisite for One-Hot Encoding, TF-IDF, and Embedding Layers — all downstream representations depend on a numerical lexicon.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What are special tokens in a vocabulary? List any four with their typical purposes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Special tokens are reserved entries in the vocabulary that do not correspond to natural language words but serve structural roles in NLP pipelines.

| Token | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `<PAD>` | Pads sequences to uniform batch length |
| `<UNK>` | Replaces out-of-vocabulary words |
| `<SOS>` | Marks start of a sequence (generation tasks) |
| `<EOS>` | Marks end of a sequence |

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for any two tokens with correct purpose = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, students must answer **one** full question from each module, with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the step-by-step procedure to construct a Word-to-Index Mapping for a given corpus. Include the role of frequency sorting and special token reservation in your answer.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Given the corpus:  
$C = \{ \text{"NLP is fun"}, \text{"Deep learning powers NLP"}, \text{"learning is deep"} \}$  
Construct the complete Word-to-Index dictionary (with `<PAD>` = 0 and `<UNK>` reserved at index = last). Then encode the test sentence: *"fun learning NLP"*.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

The construction proceeds through five stages:

1. **(1.5 marks)** **Tokenization** — split each sentence into tokens using whitespace or regex rules.
2. **(1 mark)** **Vocabulary Extraction** — apply uniqueness: $\mathcal{V} = \text{unique}(C)$.
3. **(1.5 marks)** **Frequency Counting & Sorting** — count each word's occurrences and sort in descending order. Low-frequency words are often pruned to control $V$.
4. **(1.5 marks)** **Index Assignment with Special Tokens** — reserve index `0` for `<PAD>` and last index for `<UNK>`; assign remaining indices sequentially.
5. **(1.5 marks)** **Dictionary Storage** — persist $D_{\text{fwd}}$ and its inverse $D_{\text{inv}}$ for use during training and inference.

> Frequency sorting ensures that **high-utility** words receive **low indices**, optimizing embedding lookup speed in production.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Flatten the corpus.**

$$C_{\text{flat}} = (\text{NLP}, \text{is}, \text{fun}, \text{Deep}, \text{learning}, \text{powers}, \text{NLP}, \text{learning}, \text{is}, \text{deep})$$

**Step 2: Vocabulary (case-normalized).**

$$\mathcal{V} = \{\text{nlp}, \text{is}, \text{fun}, \text{deep}, \text{learning}, \text{powers}\}, \quad V = 6$$

**Step 3: Frequency counts.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{freq}(\text{nlp}) &= 2 \\
\text{freq}(\text{is}) &= 2 \\
\text{freq}(\text{learning}) &= 2 \\
\text{freq}(\text{fun}) &= 1 \\
\text{freq}(\text{deep}) &= 2 \\
\text{freq}(\text{powers}) &= 1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Sorted vocabulary (descending freq, alphabetical tie-break).**

$$\mathcal{V}_{\text{sort}} = (\text{deep}, \text{is}, \text{learning}, \text{nlp}, \text{fun}, \text{powers})$$

**Step 5: Assign indices with special tokens.**

- `<PAD>` → index `0`
- Vocabulary words → indices `1` to `6`
- `<UNK>` → index `7`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{<PAD>}  &\mapsto 0 \\
\text{deep}   &\mapsto 1 \\
\text{is}     &\mapsto 2 \\
\text{learning} &\mapsto 3 \\
\text{nlp}    &\mapsto 4 \\
\text{fun}    &\mapsto 5 \\
\text{powers} &\mapsto 6 \\
\text{<UNK>}  &\mapsto 7 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Encode the test sentence *"fun learning NLP"*.**

$$\mathbf{x} = [5, 3, 4]$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the limitations of Word-to-Index Mapping and explain how OOV (Out-of-Vocabulary) handling addresses them. Differentiate between frequency-based pruning and `<UNK>` substitution strategies.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Consider a corpus with vocabulary size $V = 10{,}000$. After constructing the Word-to-Index Mapping, calculate: (i) the dimensionality of the one-hot encoded vector for any word, (ii) the memory required to store the full one-hot matrix for the entire vocabulary assuming 32-bit floating-point representation, (iii) the sparsity ratio of each one-hot vector.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Limitations of Word-to-Index Mapping:**

1. **(1.5 marks)** **Vocabulary Explosion** — large corpora produce $V$ in millions, making one-hot vectors memory-prohibitive.
2. **(1 mark)** **No Semantic Information** — indices carry no meaning; "happy" and "joyful" are treated as unrelated.
3. **(1 mark)** **OOV Problem** — unseen words during inference are dropped or replaced.
4. **(1 mark)** **Sequence Length Variance** — sentences have different lengths, requiring padding.
5. **(0.5 marks)** **Case Sensitivity Issues** — "Apple" vs "apple" treated as different tokens without normalization.

**OOV Handling Strategies:**

- **(1 mark)** **`<UNK>` Substitution** — replace unseen words with a single reserved token; simple but loses information.
- **(1 mark)** **Frequency-based Pruning** — keep only the top-$K$ most frequent words in vocabulary; rarer words become `<UNK>`. Reduces $V$ but may discard rare but meaningful terms.

> The two strategies are **complementary**: pruning minimizes $V$ at construction time, while `<UNK>` substitution handles residual OOVs at inference time.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $V = 10{,}000$.

**(i) Dimensionality of one-hot vector:**

$$\dim(\mathbf{e}_w) = V = 10{,}000 \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(ii) Memory for full one-hot matrix:**

The full one-hot matrix is $E \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times V}$, containing $V^2$ entries.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Entries} &= V^2 = (10{,}000)^2 = 10^8 \\
\text{Memory} &= 10^8 \times 32 \text{ bits} = 3.2 \times 10^9 \text{ bits} \\
&= 3.2 \times 10^9 \div 8 \text{ bytes} = 4 \times 10^8 \text{ bytes} \\
&\approx \mathbf{400 \text{ MB}} \quad \text{(3 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Sparsity ratio:**

Each one-hot vector has exactly 1 non-zero entry out of $V$:

$$\rho = 1 - \frac{1}{V} = 1 - \frac{1}{10{,}000} = 0.9999 = \mathbf{99.99\%} \quad \text{(2.5 marks)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to reserve `<PAD>` and `<UNK>` indices** — KTU examiners *explicitly deduct 1–2 marks* if special tokens are missing from the dictionary.
> 2. **Incorrect frequency tie-breaking** — when two words share the same frequency, students often skip the alphabetical convention explanation. **Always state your tie-breaking rule explicitly.**
> 3. **Memory calculation unit errors** — confusing bits vs bytes in Q.B(b)(ii) is the #1 mark-losing mistake. **Convert carefully: 8 bits = 1 byte.**
> 4. **Case normalization** — students frequently treat *"Deep"* and *"deep"* as different words. **Always normalize to lowercase before computing $\mathcal{V}$.**
> 5. **OOV handling confusion** — confusing *frequency pruning* (corpus-time) with *`<UNK>` substitution* (inference-time) costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Word-to-Index Mapping assigns a unique integer to each unique word in the corpus.
- **Mathematical Form:** $\mathcal{M}: w \in \mathcal{V} \to k \in \{0, 1, \dots, V-1\}$.
- **Pipeline Stages:** Tokenize → Extract Vocabulary → Count Frequencies → Sort → Assign Indices → Store Dictionary.
- **Special Tokens:** Always reserve `<PAD>` (index 0), `<UNK>` (last index), and optionally `<SOS>`/`<EOS>`.
- **Frequency Sorting:** Sort in descending order; ties broken alphabetically or by first-occurrence.
- **Lookup Complexity:** $O(1)$ via hash tables; $O(V)$ via arrays.
- **OOV Strategy:** Use `<UNK>` substitution; mitigate OOV rate via frequency-based vocabulary pruning.
- **Dimensionality:** One-hot encoding produces vectors of dimension $V$ (highly sparse).
- **Memory Footprint:** One-hot matrix scales as $O(V^2)$; for $V = 10^4$, requires ~400 MB.
- **Inverse Mapping:** Always construct $\mathcal{M}^{-1}$ to enable decoding for sequence generation.
- **Limitations:** No semantic meaning captured; high memory cost; OOV vulnerability.
- **Prerequisite For:** Bag-of-Words, TF-IDF, Embedding Layers, Sequence Models, Transformers.
- **KTU Board Expectation:** Demonstrate hand-computed dictionaries in numerical problems; show both forward and inverse maps; explain OOV handling explicitly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building TF-IDF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Building TF-IDF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**TF-IDF (Term Frequency – Inverse Document Frequency)** is a classical statistical weighting scheme widely used in Information Retrieval (IR) and Natural Language Processing (NLP) to convert textual data into a meaningful numerical representation. Formally, it is a *product of two heuristic metrics* — a local term-relevance score (**Term Frequency**) and a global term-specificity score (**Inverse Document Frequency**) — that quantifies how important a word is to a single document relative to the entire document collection (corpus).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim alignment):** TF-IDF is a numerical statistic that reflects *how important a word is to a document in a collection or corpus*. The importance increases proportionally to the number of times a word appears in the document but is offset (normalized) by the frequency of the word in the corpus.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, building a TF-IDF matrix is treated as a **feature engineering pipeline** — a mandatory step before feeding textual data into machine learning models such as Naive Bayes classifiers, Support Vector Machines (SVM), or cosine-similarity-based document retrieval engines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Detective Bookstore" Intuition)

Imagine you are a detective walking into a massive library of 10,000 crime novels, and you need to find which book is most likely about a *bank heist*. 

- **Term Frequency (TF)** is like how many times the word **"heist"** appears in a specific novel. If the book mentions "heist" 30 times, the term frequency is high — local relevance.
- **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)** is like asking the librarian, *"In how many of the 10,000 books does the word 'heist' appear?"* If the answer is *only 12 books*, then this word is *rare and highly discriminative*. If another word like **"the"** appears in 9,999 books, it carries no discriminative power at all.
- **TF-IDF** multiplies these two. A word like *"heist"* (high TF in our book, very high IDF across the library) gets a **massive score** → the algorithm recognizes it as a signature term. The word *"the"* (high TF but extremely low IDF) gets a **near-zero score** → it is treated as stop-word noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Principle:** TF-IDF answers the question — *"Which words make THIS document UNIQUE compared to everyone else?"* Words that are frequent in one document but rare globally get the highest weights.

### 1.3 Terminology Anchors

For the rest of this document, the following symbols are standardized (matching KTU question paper conventions):

- **D** $\rightarrow$ the entire document collection (corpus)
- **N** $\rightarrow$ total number of documents in the corpus
- **d** $\rightarrow$ a single document
- **t** $\rightarrow$ a single term (word/token)
- **f(t,d)** $\rightarrow$ raw count of term *t* in document *d*
- **df(t)** $\rightarrow$ *document frequency* — number of documents in which term *t* appears at least once
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why TF-IDF Exists (The Problem It Solves)

A simple "Bag of Words" (BoW) representation only counts word occurrences. This causes two critical problems:

1. **Stop-word Domination:** Common words like *"the", "is", "of"* dominate the frequency count but carry no semantic meaning.
2. **Long-Document Bias:** A 5000-word document will naturally have higher raw counts than a 50-word document, even if the shorter one is more focused on a topic.

TF-IDF neutralizes both problems. **Term Frequency** normalizes counts within a document, and **Inverse Document Frequency** penalizes globally common words.

### 2.2 The Three Core Formulas

#### A. Term Frequency (TF) Variants

| Variant | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Raw Count** | $\text{tf}(t,d) = f(t,d)$ | Simplest form, rarely used alone |
| **Boolean Frequency** | $\text{tf}(t,d) = 1$ if $t \in d$, else $0$ | When presence matters more than count |
| **Log-Normalized** | $\text{tf}(t,d) = \log(1 + f(t,d))$ | Dampens effect of high counts |
| **Augmented Frequency** | $\text{tf}(t,d) = 0.5 + 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{f(t,d)}{\max\{f(w,d) : w \in d\}}$ | Prevents bias toward longer documents |
| **Proportional Frequency** *(most common in KTU exam)* | $\text{tf}(t,d) = \dfrac{f(t,d)}{\sum_{w \in d} f(w,d)}$ | Standard textbook TF |

#### B. Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)

The standard IDF formula is:

$$\text{idf}(t, D) = \log\left(\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)$$

However, the **smoothed IDF** is preferred in practice to avoid division-by-zero and to prevent extreme values when $\text{df}(t)$ is very small:

$$\text{idf}(t, D) = \log\left(\frac{1 + N}{1 + \text{df}(t)}\right) + 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> The "+1" inside the log and the "+1" outside are the **Lidstone smoothing** corrections introduced by scikit-learn and the official KTU recommended textbook references. KTU exam answers expecting "smoothed IDF" must use this form to score full marks.

#### C. Final TF-IDF Weight

$$\text{tfidf}(t, d, D) = \text{tf}(t, d) \times \text{idf}(t, D)$$

After computation, each document vector is typically **L2-normalized**:

$$v_{\text{norm}} = \frac{v}{\sqrt{\sum_{i} v_i^2}}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Raw TF | $\text{tf}(t,d) = f(t,d)$ | Count of $t$ in $d$ |
| Normalized TF | $\text{tf}(t,d) = f(t,d) \,/\, \sum_{w} f(w,d)$ | Probability of $t$ in $d$ |
| Document Frequency | $\text{df}(t) = \sum_{d \in D} \mathbb{1}\{t \in d\}$ | Number of docs containing $t$ |
| IDF (Standard) | $\text{idf}(t) = \log \vert D \vert \,/\, \text{df}(t)$ | Log of inverse document frequency |
| IDF (Smoothed) | $\text{idf}(t) = \log\bigl[(1+\vert D \vert)\,/\,(1+\text{df}(t))\bigr] + 1$ | Numerically stable version |
| TF-IDF | $\text{tfidf}(t,d) = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \text{idf}(t)$ | Final weight of $t$ in $d$ |
| Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(d_1, d_2) = \dfrac{\vec{d_1} \cdot \vec{d_2}}{\Vert \vec{d_1} \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert \vec{d_2} \Vert_2}$ | Used downstream on TF-IDF vectors |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

TF-IDF is the **workhorse baseline** of modern NLP pipelines:

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Originally ranked documents using TF-IDF before deep learning.
- **Spam Filters:** Spam emails contain rare-to-corpora words like *"Viagra", "lottery"*, which TF-IDF boosts.
- **Recommendation Systems:** Article recommendation engines compute cosine similarity between TF-IDF vectors of articles a user has read.
- **Document Clustering & Topic Modeling (LDA):** Acts as the input feature matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times \vert V \vert}$ where $V$ is vocabulary size.
- **Plagiarism Detection:** Flags documents whose TF-IDF vector angles (cosine similarity) are suspiciously close.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

Consider a mini-corpus of **three documents**:

- **$d_1$:** *"the cat sat on the mat"*
- **$d_2$:** *"the dog sat on the log"*
- **$d_3$:** *"cats and dogs are pets"*

**Total documents:** $N = 3$

**Step 1 — Tokenize and build vocabulary:**
$$V = \{\text{the, cat, sat, on, mat, dog, log, cats, and, dogs, are, pets}\}$$
**Vocabulary size:** $\vert V \vert = 12$

**Step 2 — Compute Raw Counts $f(t,d)$:**

| Term | $d_1$ | $d_2$ | $d_3$ | df(t) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| the | 2 | 2 | 0 | 2 |
| cat | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| cats | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| sat | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 |
| on | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 |
| mat | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| dog | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| dogs | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| log | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| and | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| are | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| pets | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| **Total words** | **6** | **5** | **4** | — |

**Step 3 — Compute Normalized TF:** $\text{tf}(t,d) = f(t,d) / \sum_{w} f(w,d)$

For $d_1$ (length 6):
$$\text{tf}(\text{`the'}, d_1) = \frac{2}{6} = 0.3333$$
$$\text{tf}(\text{`cat'}, d_1) = \frac{1}{6} = 0.1667$$
$$\text{tf}(\text{`mat'}, d_1) = \frac{1}{6} = 0.1667$$

For $d_2$ (length 5):
$$\text{tf}(\text{`the'}, d_2) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4000$$
$$\text{tf}(\text{`dog'}, d_2) = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2000$$

For $d_3$ (length 4):
$$\text{tf}(\text{`cats'}, d_3) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.2500$$
$$\text{tf}(\text{`dogs'}, d_3) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.2500$$

**Step 4 — Compute Smoothed IDF:** $\text{idf}(t) = \log\bigl[(1+N)/(1+\text{df}(t))\bigr] + 1$

$$\text{idf}(\text{`the'}) = \log\left(\frac{1+3}{1+2}\right) + 1 = \log(1.333) + 1 = 0.2877 + 1 = 1.2877$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{`cat'}) = \log\left(\frac{1+3}{1+1}\right) + 1 = \log(2.0) + 1 = 0.6931 + 1 = 1.6931$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{`cats'}) = \log\left(\frac{1+3}{1+1}\right) + 1 = 1.6931$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{`sat'}) = \log\left(\frac{1+3}{1+2}\right) + 1 = 1.2877$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{`on'}) = \log\left(\frac{1+3}{1+2}\right) + 1 = 1.2877$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{`mat'}) = \log\left(\frac{1+3}{1+1}\right) + 1 = 1.6931$$

**Step 5 — Compute Final TF-IDF:** $\text{tfidf}(t,d) = \text{tf}(t,d) \times \text{idf}(t)$

$$\text{tfidf}(\text{`the'}, d_1) = 0.3333 \times 1.2877 = 0.4292$$

$$\text{tfidf}(\text{`cat'}, d_1) = 0.1667 \times 1.6931 = 0.2822$$

$$\text{tfidf}(\text{`the'}, d_2) = 0.4000 \times 1.2877 = 0.5151$$

$$\text{tfidf}(\text{`dog'}, d_2) = 0.2000 \times 1.6931 = 0.3386$$

$$\text{tfidf}(\text{`cats'}, d_3) = 0.2500 \times 1.6931 = 0.4233$$

$$\text{tfidf}(\text{`dogs'}, d_3) = 0.2500 \times 1.6931 = 0.4233$$

**Step 6 — Final TF-IDF Matrix (showing a few key cells):**

| Term | $d_1$ | $d_2$ | $d_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| the | 0.4292 | 0.5151 | 0.0000 |
| cat | 0.2822 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 |
| cats | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.4233 |
| dog | 0.0000 | 0.3386 | 0.0000 |
| dogs | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.4233 |
| mat | 0.2822 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 |

### 3.2 Mathematical Insight — Why the Logarithm?

The log in IDF is not arbitrary. It is the **information-theoretic surprise** of the term:

$$\text{idf}(t) \approx -\log\bigl[P(\text{term } t \text{ appears})\bigr]$$

This comes from Shannon's information theory. A rare term carries **high information content** (high bits of surprise), and a common term carries **low information**. The log function compresses the dynamic range so that a word appearing in 1 document vs 2 documents does not get an absurdly larger weight than a word in 100 vs 200.

### 3.3 Sparse Matrix Representation

For a real corpus with $\vert V \vert = 50{,}000$ and $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ documents, the TF-IDF matrix has **50 billion cells**, but more than **99.99% of them are zero**. Hence TF-IDF matrices are stored as **Compressed Sparse Row (CSR)** matrices, reducing memory by orders of magnitude.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The TF-IDF Building Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Corpus D = d1, d2, ..., dN] --> B[Tokenization & Lowercasing]
    B --> C[Stopword Removal and Stemming]
    C --> D[Build Vocabulary V = t1, t2, ..., tV]
    D --> E[Step 1: Compute Term Frequency TF table]
    D --> F[Step 2: Compute Document Frequency DF for each term]
    F --> G[Step 3: Compute IDF vector using log formula]
    E --> H[Step 4: Element-wise Multiply TF and IDF]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Step 5: L2 Normalize each document row]
    I --> J[Output TFIDF Matrix X of shape N x V]
    J --> K[Downstream Task: Cosine Similarity or ML Classifier]
```

### 4.2 Interpretive Walkthrough of the Pipeline

1. **Raw Corpus** — Input is an unstructured collection of plain-text documents.
2. **Tokenization** — Sentences are split into individual word tokens; punctuation is stripped.
3. **Stopword Removal & Stemming** — Words like *"the"*, *"is"*, *"running"*→*"run"* are normalized. *Note: even after stopword removal, TF-IDF still computes them but assigns them near-zero weight.*
4. **Vocabulary Construction** — A unique sorted set of all terms is built — this defines the feature space.
5. **TF Computation** — Each cell $X_{ij}$ starts as the normalized frequency of term $j$ in document $i$.
6. **DF & IDF Computation** — Count how many documents each term appears in, then take the smoothed log.
7. **Multiplication** — Element-wise product fuses local and global statistics.
8. **L2 Normalization** — Every document vector becomes a unit vector so cosine similarity reduces to a dot product.
9. **Output** — The sparse matrix is ready for retrieval, clustering, or classification.

### 4.3 Weight Distribution Behaviour (Conceptual Sketch)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[High TF, Low DF] -->|Rare and Frequent in Doc| B[VERY HIGH TFIDF Score]
    C[High TF, High DF] -->|Common Stop Word| D[LOW TFIDF Score]
    E[Low TF, Low DF] -->|Rare but Trivial| F[LOW TFIDF Score]
    G[High TF, Medium DF] -->|Topic Defining Word| H[MEDIUM TFIDF Score]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Define **Term Frequency (TF)** and **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)**. Why is the logarithm applied in the IDF computation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Term Frequency (TF):** The normalized count of a term $t$ in a document $d$, given by $\text{tf}(t,d) = f(t,d) \,/\, \sum_{w} f(w,d)$. It captures the *local importance* of a word inside a single document. *(1 mark)*
- **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):** A global statistic that measures how *rare or informative* a term is across the entire corpus $D$. It is computed as $\text{idf}(t) = \log\bigl[(1+N)/(1+\text{df}(t))\bigr] + 1$. *(1 mark)*
- **Why log?** The logarithm compresses the dynamic range of raw document frequencies. A term that appears in 1 vs 2 documents should not have an absurdly larger weight than one in 1000 vs 2000 documents. It also gives a **monotonically decreasing** weight as df increases and prevents TF-IDF values from exploding. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** What is the **smoothed IDF formula** used in TF-IDF? How does it differ from the standard IDF, and why is smoothing necessary?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Smoothed IDF Formula:** $\text{idf}(t) = \log\bigl[(1+N)/(1+\text{df}(t))\bigr] + 1$ — adds 1 to both numerator and denominator (Lidstone smoothing) and adds 1 outside the log to ensure all IDF values are strictly positive. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Difference from Standard:** The standard formula $\text{idf}(t) = \log[N/\text{df}(t)]$ breaks (returns $\infty$ or NaN) when $\text{df}(t) = 0$ for an unseen term. Smoothing prevents this. *(1 mark)*
- **Necessity:** In real-world corpora, new test documents will contain vocabulary absent from the training set. Without smoothing, their vectors would contain `NaN` values, breaking any downstream computation. *(0.5 marks)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

**Q3. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]` — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

Consider the following corpus of three documents:
- $d_1$: *"NLP is fun and NLP is powerful"*
- $d_2$: *"Deep learning powers modern NLP"*
- $d_3$: *"Fun with deep learning and NLP"*

**(a)** Construct the **Term Frequency (TF)** table using normalized proportional frequency. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compute the **smoothed IDF** for every unique term, and then build the **TF-IDF matrix** for the corpus. Identify the term with the highest TF-IDF weight in $d_1$. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q3(a):**

**Step 1 — Tokenize and compute raw counts.**

Tokens (after lowercasing):
- $d_1$: {nlp: 2, is: 2, fun: 1, and: 1, powerful: 1} → total = 7
- $d_2$: {deep: 1, learning: 1, powers: 1, modern: 1, nlp: 1} → total = 5
- $d_3$: {fun: 1, with: 1, deep: 1, learning: 1, and: 1, nlp: 1} → total = 6

**Step 2 — Normalized TF** = raw count / total tokens in document.

| Term | $d_1$ | $d_2$ | $d_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| nlp | 2/7 = 0.2857 | 1/5 = 0.2000 | 1/6 = 0.1667 |
| is | 2/7 = 0.2857 | 0 | 0 |
| fun | 1/7 = 0.1429 | 0 | 1/6 = 0.1667 |
| and | 1/7 = 0.1429 | 0 | 1/6 = 0.1667 |
| powerful | 1/7 = 0.1429 | 0 | 0 |
| deep | 0 | 1/5 = 0.2000 | 1/6 = 0.1667 |
| learning | 0 | 1/5 = 0.2000 | 1/6 = 0.1667 |
| powers | 0 | 1/5 = 0.2000 | 0 |
| modern | 0 | 1/5 = 0.2000 | 0 |
| with | 0 | 0 | 1/6 = 0.1667 |

*(Award full 7 marks for the complete normalized TF table with correct denominators and divisions shown.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q3(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute document frequency df(t):**

| Term | df(t) | Documents |
|---|---|---|
| nlp | 3 | $d_1, d_2, d_3$ |
| is | 1 | $d_1$ |
| fun | 2 | $d_1, d_3$ |
| and | 2 | $d_1, d_3$ |
| powerful | 1 | $d_1$ |
| deep | 2 | $d_2, d_3$ |
| learning | 2 | $d_2, d_3$ |
| powers | 1 | $d_2$ |
| modern | 1 | $d_2$ |
| with | 1 | $d_3$ |

**Step 2 — Smoothed IDF** using $\text{idf}(t) = \log[(1+3)/(1+\text{df}(t))] + 1$:

$$\text{idf}(\text{nlp}) = \log(4/4) + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1.0000$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{is}) = \log(4/2) + 1 = 0.6931 + 1 = 1.6931$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{fun}) = \log(4/3) + 1 = 0.2877 + 1 = 1.2877$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{and}) = 1.2877$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{powerful}) = 1.6931$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{deep}) = 1.2877$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{learning}) = 1.2877$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{powers}) = 1.6931$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{modern}) = 1.6931$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{with}) = 1.6931$$

**Step 3 — TF-IDF Matrix** (showing a sample of high-weight cells):

$$\text{tfidf}(\text{`is'}, d_1) = 0.2857 \times 1.6931 = 0.4837$$

$$\text{tfidf}(\text{`fun'}, d_1) = 0.1429 \times 1.2877 = 0.1840$$

$$\text{tfidf}(\text{`nlp'}, d_1) = 0.2857 \times 1.0000 = 0.2857$$

$$\text{tfidf}(\text{`deep'}, d_2) = 0.2000 \times 1.2877 = 0.2575$$

**Term with highest TF-IDF in $d_1$:** The term **"is"** with weight **0.4837** wins, because it has a moderately high TF (2/7) and the highest IDF (it appears in only 1 document out of 3). *(7 marks awarded for the complete table and identification.)*

---

**Q4. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]` — QUESTION B (14 Marks) (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**

**(a)** Explain the **five major variants of Term Frequency** with formulas. State which variant is most suitable for long documents and justify your answer. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations of TF-IDF** in modern NLP pipelines. Compare it briefly with the Bag-of-Words (BoW) approach. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q4(a):**

The five major TF variants are:

1. **Raw Count:** $\text{tf}(t,d) = f(t,d)$ — simplest, but biased toward long documents.
2. **Boolean:** $\text{tf}(t,d) = 1$ if $t \in d$ else $0$ — ignores repetition.
3. **Log-Normalized:** $\text{tf}(t,d) = \log(1 + f(t,d))$ — dampens high counts logarithmically.
4. **Augmented Frequency:** $\text{tf}(t,d) = 0.5 + 0.5 \cdot \frac{f(t,d)}{\max_{w} f(w,d)}$ — scales TF to [0.5, 1.0], excellent for long documents.
5. **Proportional (Normalized):** $\text{tf}(t,d) = f(t,d) / \sum_{w} f(w,d)$ — converts to a probability distribution.

*(5 marks for listing with formulas; 2 marks for the justification.)*

**Most suitable for long documents: Augmented Frequency** — because it normalizes against the maximum count in that same document rather than the total count, making it robust when a single term dominates (e.g., a 10,000-word research paper where one term appears 200 times).

---

**Model Solution for Q4(b):**

**Advantages of TF-IDF:** *(3.5 marks)*
- Simple, interpretable, computationally cheap.
- Reduces stop-word dominance via IDF.
- Produces a sparse high-dimensional feature space ideal for linear models.
- Language-agnostic — works for any tokenizable script.

**Limitations of TF-IDF:** *(2 marks)*
- **No semantic understanding:** *"good"* and *"excellent"* are treated as completely different dimensions.
- **Out-of-vocabulary problem:** Cannot handle new words not seen in training.
- **High dimensionality:** Vocabulary of 100k+ leads to memory issues.
- **Ignores word order and context:** Loses syntactic information.

**Comparison with BoW:** *(1.5 marks)*
- BoW is purely frequency-based and treats all words equally, so it is dominated by stop-words. TF-IDF re-weights words globally, making it strictly superior for tasks like information retrieval, document classification, and clustering. BoW is still preferred when simplicity is paramount or when downstream models (e.g., topic models) prefer raw counts.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the +1 smoothing term** in IDF — using the raw $\log[N/\text{df}(t)]$ instead of the smoothed version. KTU follows scikit-learn convention; you lose **1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Using raw counts instead of normalized TF** in the multiplication step — TF-IDF must use *proportional* or *log-normalized* TF, not raw count. This single mistake **wipes out 3-4 marks** in Part B.
> 3. **Not showing intermediate DF table** — students often jump from vocabulary straight to TF-IDF matrix. You must show df(t) for every term or you lose **2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing TF and IDF axes** — TF is a *row* property (depends on the document), IDF is a *column* property (depends on the term across corpus). Mixing these up is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
> 5. **Skipping the L2 normalization step** in the final answer — many KTU questions explicitly mention "normalized TF-IDF", and omitting this costs **1 mark**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TF-IDF = Term Frequency × Inverse Document Frequency** — always remember this single product formula.
- **TF is LOCAL** (per document), **IDF is GLOBAL** (per corpus).
- **Standard TF in KTU syllabus:** $\text{tf}(t,d) = f(t,d) / \sum_{w \in d} f(w,d)$.
- **Standard IDF in KTU syllabus:** $\text{idf}(t) = \log\bigl[(1+N)/(1+\text{df}(t))\bigr] + 1$ (smoothed).
- **Vocabulary size $\vert V \vert$** is the number of unique terms after tokenization, stopword removal, and stemming.
- **L2 normalization** is the final step: divide each document row by its Euclidean norm.
- **High TF-IDF** = a word is frequent in *this* document *and* rare in the rest of the corpus → strong topic signature.
- **Low TF-IDF** = the word is either rare in the document *or* common across the corpus → weak signal.
- **Common stop-words** (the, is, of) automatically get low TF-IDF because their high df(t) makes IDF small.
- **TF-IDF matrix is sparse** — store as CSR (Compressed Sparse Row) format in practice.
- **Downstream usage:** cosine similarity for retrieval, Naive Bayes / SVM for classification, K-Means for clustering, LDA for topic modeling.
- **Limitation:** TF-IDF is *syntactic, not semantic* — modern transformers (BERT, Word2Vec) address this but TF-IDF remains the indispensable baseline.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neural Word Embeddings. Vector Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Vector Space Models (VSMs)** represent words, documents, or linguistic units as dense, continuous-valued vectors in a low-dimensional real-valued space $\mathbb{R}^{d}$, where $d$ typically ranges from **50 to 300**. A **Neural Word Embedding** is a parametric function $f: \mathcal{V} \to \mathbb{R}^{d}$ learned via a shallow neural network, which maps every word $w$ in a finite vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$ to a vector $\vec{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ such that semantically and syntactically similar words occupy geometrically proximate regions in the embedding space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distributional Hypothesis (Firth, 1957):** "You shall know a word by the company it keeps." Words appearing in similar linguistic contexts tend to have similar meanings. Neural embeddings operationalize this hypothesis by learning representations from large unlabeled corpora in a self-supervised manner.

The two foundational architectures under the KTU 2024 NLP syllabus for Module 3 are:

1. **Word2Vec** (Mikolov et al., 2013) — includes the **Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBOW)** model and the **Skip-gram** model.
2. **GloVe** (Pennington et al., 2014) — **Global Vectors for Word Representation**, which factorizes the global word–word co-occurrence matrix.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Semantic Atlas"

Imagine you are a cartographer building a map of an enormous city where every street name is a word. Instead of drawing a literal map, you assign **GPS coordinates** (the embedding vector) to each street. The rule you enforce is simple: streets that often appear on the same postcard (co-occur in text) should have coordinates that are numerically close. After processing millions of postcards (a corpus), you discover a remarkable pattern — a vector arithmetic like:

$$\vec{\text{King}} - \vec{\text{Man}} + \vec{\text{Woman}} \approx \vec{\text{Queen}}$$

This is the magic of neural word embeddings: **meaning becomes geometry**, and relationships become direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Classical VSMs:** Earlier techniques like **TF-IDF** and **LSA (Latent Semantic Analysis)** produced high-dimensional, sparse vectors (one entry per vocabulary word). Neural embeddings produce **low-dimensional, dense vectors** where every dimension contributes to meaning — a far more efficient representation for downstream tasks.

### 1.3 Core Metrics & Hyperparameters

- **$V$** = Vocabulary size (often $10^5$–$10^6$).
- **$d$** = Embedding dimension (commonly **50, 100, 200, 300**).
- **$c$** = Context window radius (commonly **2 to 10**).
- **$N$** = Number of negative samples (commonly **5 to 20** for small datasets, **2 to 5** for large).
- **$\eta$** = Learning rate (e.g., **0.025** with linear decay).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Word2Vec Family

#### 2.1.1 Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBOW)

- **Goal:** Predict a center word $w_t$ from a symmetric context window $\{w_{t-c}, \dots, w_{t-1}, w_{t+1}, \dots, w_{t+c}\}$.
- **Mechanism:** All context word one-hot vectors are multiplied by a shared input embedding matrix $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$, averaged, and then projected through an output matrix $\mathbf{W}' \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times V}$ followed by a softmax to yield a probability distribution over the vocabulary.
- **Why averaging works:** CBOW treats context as a "bag" — it disregards word order, which makes it faster but slightly less accurate for syntactic tasks.

#### 2.1.2 Skip-gram

- **Goal:** Predict the surrounding context words given a single center word $w_t$.
- **Mechanism:** The center word's embedding is used $2c$ times to predict each context word independently.
- **Why it wins:** Skip-gram treats each (center, context) pair as a separate training example, producing richer embeddings for rare words. It is the workhorse of most production embedding systems.

### 2.2 The GloVe Model

GloVe bridges the gap between **global matrix factorization** (LSA) and **local context window methods** (Word2Vec). It explicitly factorizes the **word–word co-occurrence matrix** $\mathbf{X}$, where $X_{ij}$ counts how often word $j$ appears in the context of word $i$.

### 2.3 Similarity & Distance Metrics

Two vectors in embedding space can be compared using:

- **Cosine Similarity:** Measures the angle between vectors, ignoring magnitude.
- **Euclidean Distance:** Measures straight-line distance.
- **Dot Product:** Magnitude-sensitive similarity.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notation & Notes |
|---|---|---|
| One-hot vector | $\vec{x}_w \in \{0,1\}^{V}$, $\Vert \vec{x}_w \Vert_0 = 1$ | $V$ is vocabulary size |
| Embedding lookup | $\vec{v}_w = \mathbf{W}^{\top} \vec{x}_w$ | $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$ |
| Softmax classifier | $P(w_O \mid w_I) = \dfrac{\exp(\vec{v}'_{w_O}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{V} \exp(\vec{v}'_{w}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I})}$ | $w_O$ = output, $w_I$ = input |
| Cosine similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\Vert \vec{u} \Vert \, \Vert \vec{v} \Vert}$ | Range: $[-1, 1]$ |
| Euclidean distance | $d(\vec{u},\vec{v}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(u_i - v_i)^2}$ | Range: $[0, \infty)$ |
| GloVe objective (core term) | $J = \sum_{i,j=1}^{V} f(X_{ij}) \left( \vec{w}_i^{\top} \tilde{\vec{w}}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{ij} \right)^{2}$ | $f$ is the weighting function |
| Weighting function $f$ | $f(x) = (x/x_{\max})^{\alpha}$ if $x < x_{\max}$, else $1$ | Standard: $x_{\max}=100$, $\alpha=0.75$ |
| Negative Sampling (SGNS) loss | $E = -\log \sigma(\vec{v}'_{w_O}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I}) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} \log \sigma(-\vec{v}'_{w_k}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I})$ | $\sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})$ |
| Learning rate decay | $\eta_t = \eta_0 \cdot \max\left(0, 1 - \dfrac{t}{T}\right)$ | Linear decay, $T$ = total steps |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Applications in B.Tech NLP Pipelines:**
> - **Search engines:** Replace TF-IDF with neural embeddings for semantic search.
> - **Recommendation systems:** Use item embeddings to find similar products.
> - **Machine translation:** Embeddings form the input layer of encoder–decoder models.
> - **Sentiment analysis:** Pre-trained embeddings accelerate convergence by **20–40%**.
> - **Named Entity Recognition (NER):** Embeddings provide robust features for low-resource languages.

### 2.6 Why Negative Sampling?

The full softmax over a vocabulary of size $V$ (e.g., $V = 10^6$) is computationally prohibitive — $O(V)$ per training step. Negative sampling converts the multi-class problem into $N+1$ binary classification problems, reducing cost to $O(N)$ where $N \ll V$. The probability of sampling a word $w_i$ as a negative example is:

$$P(w_i) = \frac{f(w_i)^{3/4}}{\sum_{j=0}^{V} f(w_j)^{3/4}}$$

The **3/4 power** empirically down-weights the dominance of high-frequency words during negative sampling.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Cosine Similarity from First Principles

**Step 1: Define the dot product.**
For two real vectors $\vec{u}, \vec{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, the dot product is defined as:
$$\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = \sum_{i=1}^{d} u_i v_i = \Vert \vec{u} \Vert \, \Vert \vec{v} \Vert \cos \theta$$
This is the geometric identity we will invert.

**Step 2: Solve for the angle.**
Rearranging the identity:
$$\cos \theta = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\Vert \vec{u} \Vert \, \Vert \vec{v} \Vert}$$
This is the cosine similarity. We then express the norms in expanded form:
$$\cos \theta = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{d} u_i v_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} u_i^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} v_i^{2}}}$$

**Step 3: Sanity-check the bounds.**
By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, $-1 \leq \cos \theta \leq 1$, with $\cos \theta = 1$ when $\vec{u} = \lambda \vec{v}$ (perfectly aligned) and $\cos \theta = -1$ when they are anti-parallel.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Skip-gram with Negative Sampling (SGNS) Objective

**Step 1: The vanilla skip-gram objective.**
We want to maximize the log-probability of observing context words given the center word:
$$J_{\text{naive}} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{-c \leq j \leq c,\, j \neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)$$
where $P(w_O \mid w_I)$ uses the full softmax:
$$P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp(\vec{v}'_{w_O}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{V} \exp(\vec{v}'_{w}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I})}$$

**Step 2: Replace the softmax with sigmoid-based binary classification.**
Instead of classifying into $V$ classes, we frame the task as: "Is word $w_O$ a *real* context of $w_I$?" This uses the logistic sigmoid:
$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$
For a positive (real) pair $(w_I, w_O)$:
$$P(\text{positive} = 1 \mid w_I, w_O) = \sigma(\vec{v}'_{w_O}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I})$$

**Step 3: Introduce negative samples.**
For each positive pair, we draw $N$ negative words $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N$ from the unigram distribution raised to the 3/4 power. Each negative should be classified as 0:
$$P(\text{negative} = 0 \mid w_I, w_k) = 1 - \sigma(\vec{v}'_{w_k}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I}) = \sigma(-\vec{v}'_{w_k}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I})$$

**Step 4: Combine into the SGNS objective.**
Taking the log-likelihood and negating (to convert to a minimization problem):
$$E = -\log \sigma(\vec{v}'_{w_O}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I}) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} \log \sigma(-\vec{v}'_{w_k}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I})$$

**Step 5: Compute the gradient with respect to the input embedding $\vec{v}_{w_I}$.**
Let $u = \vec{v}'_{w_O}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I}$ and $u_k = \vec{v}'_{w_k}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I}$. Then:
$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial \vec{v}_{w_I}} = \left(\sigma(u) - 1\right) \vec{v}'_{w_O} - \sum_{k=1}^{N} \sigma(u_k) \vec{v}'_{w_k}$$
This gradient is then used in standard stochastic gradient descent with the learning rate schedule.

### 3.3 Derivation of the GloVe Objective Function

**Step 1: Start from co-occurrence ratios.**
The key insight is that the ratio of co-occurrence probabilities $P(w_k \mid w_i) / P(w_k \mid w_j)$ encodes meaning. For example, the ratio is large for $w_k$ related to $w_i$ (e.g., "ice") but not $w_j$ ("steam"), and small for the opposite.

**Step 2: Encode this ratio as a function of word vectors.**
We require:
$$F(\vec{w}_i, \vec{w}_j, \tilde{\vec{w}}_k) = \frac{P(w_k \mid w_i)}{P(w_k \mid w_j)}$$

**Step 3: Enforce linearity in the vector space.**
A natural choice is to constrain $F$ to be a difference of embeddings and a bias term:
$$F = \exp\!\left(\vec{w}_i^{\top} \tilde{\vec{w}}_k - \vec{w}_j^{\top} \tilde{\vec{w}}_k\right) = \frac{\exp(\vec{w}_i^{\top} \tilde{\vec{w}}_k)}{\exp(\vec{w}_j^{\top} \tilde{\vec{w}}_k)}$$

**Step 4: Match to the probability ratio.**
Setting $F$ equal to the ratio and solving yields:
$$\vec{w}_i^{\top} \tilde{\vec{w}}_k = \log P(w_k \mid w_i) = \log(X_{ik}) - \log(X_i)$$
where $X_i = \sum_k X_{ik}$ is the row sum of the co-occurrence matrix.

**Step 5: Introduce bias terms to absorb the marginals.**
The term $-\log(X_i)$ depends only on $i$, so we introduce a bias $b_i$. Similarly for $j$. This gives the prediction:
$$\vec{w}_i^{\top} \tilde{\vec{w}}_k + b_i + \tilde{b}_k = \log X_{ik}$$

**Step 6: Formulate the weighted least-squares loss.**
$$J = \sum_{i,j=1}^{V} f(X_{ij}) \left( \vec{w}_i^{\top} \tilde{\vec{w}}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{ij} \right)^{2}$$
The weighting function $f$ ensures that:
- **Rare co-occurrences** (small $X_{ij}$) are not over-weighted.
- **Very frequent co-occurrences** (large $X_{ij}$) do not dominate.
- **Zero co-occurrences** ($X_{ij} = 0$) contribute $\log 0$, which is undefined, so $f(0) = 0$.

**Step 7: Closed form for the standard weighting function.**
$$f(x) = \begin{cases} (x / x_{\max})^{\alpha} & \text{if } x < x_{\max} \\ 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
with standard values $x_{\max} = 100$ and $\alpha = 0.75$.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Embedding Arithmetic

> [!NOTE]
> **The Famous "King − Man + Woman ≈ Queen" Example:** This is not a magic trick — it falls out of how the embeddings are trained. The vector $\vec{\text{King}} - \vec{\text{Man}}$ captures the "royalty" direction, which is approximately gender-neutral.

**Step 1:** Assume learned vectors in $d = 4$ (simplified):
$\vec{\text{King}} = (0.5, 0.8, 0.1, 0.4)$, $\vec{\text{Man}} = (0.3, 0.2, 0.5, 0.1)$, $\vec{\text{Woman}} = (0.4, 0.3, 0.5, 0.2)$.

**Step 2:** Compute $\vec{r} = \vec{\text{King}} - \vec{\text{Man}} + \vec{\text{Woman}}$ componentwise:
$$\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= 0.5 - 0.3 + 0.4 = 0.6 \\
r_2 &= 0.8 - 0.2 + 0.3 = 0.9 \\
r_3 &= 0.1 - 0.5 + 0.5 = 0.1 \\
r_4 &= 0.4 - 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compare $\vec{r}$ to the true $\vec{\text{Queen}} = (0.6, 0.9, 0.2, 0.5)$ using cosine similarity. The result is typically > 0.95 for a well-trained model.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Skip-gram Model Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    inpA["INPUT LAYER - One Hot Vector of Center Word w_t"]
    embA["EMBEDDING MATRIX W transpose - Shape V by d"]
    hidA["HIDDEN LAYER - Embedding Vector v_w of size d"]
    outA["OUTPUT MATRIX W prime - Shape d by V"]
    smA["SOFTMAX or NEGATIVE SAMPLING over Vocabulary"]
    ctxA["PREDICTED CONTEXT WORDS - w_t-c to w_t+c"]
    inpA --> embA
    embA --> hidA
    hidA --> outA
    outA --> smA
    smA --> ctxA
```

### 4.4 Cosine Similarity Geometry

```mermaid
graph TD
    origA["ORIGIN - Zero Vector"]
    vecU["Vector u - Word KING"]
    vecV["Vector v - Word QUEEN"]
    angleA["Angle theta between u and v"]
    origA --> vecU
    origA --> vecV
    vecU --- angleA
    vecV --- angleA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** A **small angle** $\theta$ between two word vectors means **high semantic similarity**. For opposite words (e.g., "hot" vs "cold"), $\theta \approx \pi$ and $\cos \theta \approx -1$. This geometric intuition is the foundation of every modern NLP retrieval system.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the term "neural word embedding." State the distributional hypothesis with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A neural word embedding is a **dense, low-dimensional, real-valued vector representation** of a word, learned by a shallow neural network from large unlabeled text corpora. The distributional hypothesis (Firth, 1957) states that "**a word is characterized by the company it keeps**" — words appearing in similar contexts share similar meanings. For example, the words "king" and "queen" tend to appear in similar syntactic contexts (e.g., followed by "of the realm"), so their embeddings are placed close together in the vector space. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Why is cosine similarity preferred over Euclidean distance for comparing word embeddings?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Cosine similarity is preferred because it measures the **angular separation** between vectors rather than their absolute magnitudes. Since word embeddings can vary in norm (a frequent word like "the" may have a longer vector than a rare word), Euclidean distance is sensitive to these magnitude differences and can produce misleading results. Cosine similarity, given by:
$$\cos \theta = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\Vert \vec{u} \Vert \, \Vert \vec{v} \Vert}$$
is **bounded in $[-1, 1]$**, scale-invariant, and directly captures the semantic orientation of words — making it the gold standard for embedding comparison. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often confuse the **input matrix $\mathbf{W}$** and the **output matrix $\mathbf{W}'$** in Word2Vec — make sure to clearly state which is which.
> 2. In derivations, **forgetting the negative sign** when converting log-likelihood to a loss function costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. Drawing a diagram without labels on the **matrix dimensions** is considered incomplete.
> 4. Failing to mention the **3/4 power** in the negative sampling distribution loses a mark.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the architecture of the Skip-gram model with a neat diagram. Compare it with the CBOW model.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The Skip-gram model, introduced by Mikolov et al. (2013), is a shallow two-layer neural network that predicts the surrounding **context words** given a single **center word**. The architecture is as follows:

1. **Input Layer:** A one-hot encoded vector $\vec{x} \in \{0,1\}^{V}$ representing the center word $w_I$. Exactly one entry is 1 (at the index of the word in the vocabulary).
2. **Embedding (Projection) Layer:** Multiplication by the input embedding matrix $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$ effectively *selects* the row corresponding to $w_I$, producing the embedding $\vec{v}_{w_I} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.
3. **Output Layer:** A scoring vector $\vec{z} = \mathbf{W}'^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I} \in \mathbb{R}^{V}$, followed by a softmax to yield $P(w_O \mid w_I)$ for every word in the vocabulary. The network is trained to make $P(w_O \mid w_I)$ large for true context words and small for all others.

**Comparison with CBOW:**

| Aspect | Skip-gram | CBOW |
|---|---|---|
| Prediction direction | Context $\leftarrow$ Center | Center $\leftarrow$ Context |
| Input | One word | Multiple context words |
| Aggregation | Not needed (one embedding) | Average of context embeddings |
| Speed | Slower (2c predictions per center) | Faster |
| Performance on rare words | **Better** | Worse |
| Performance on frequent words | Slightly worse | **Better** |

**(7 marks — 3 for architecture description, 2 for diagram, 2 for comparison)**

---

**(b) Derive the Skip-gram with Negative Sampling (SGNS) objective function and explain why negative sampling is used.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The full softmax-based skip-gram objective requires computing a denominator summed over the entire vocabulary $V$ at every step:
$$P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp(\vec{v}'_{w_O}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{V} \exp(\vec{v}'_{w}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I})}$$
This is $O(V)$ per step — prohibitively expensive for $V \approx 10^6$.

**SGNS reformulation:**
Replace the $V$-way classification with a binary classification for each (center, context) pair: "Is this context word real or fake?" We sample $N$ negative (fake) words from the unigram distribution raised to the 3/4 power. The objective becomes:
$$E = -\log \sigma(\vec{v}'_{w_O}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I}) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} \log \sigma(-\vec{v}'_{w_k}{}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I})$$
where $\sigma(z) = 1/(1 + e^{-z})$ is the sigmoid function. The first term pushes the positive pair's dot product toward $+\infty$, and the second term pushes each negative pair's dot product toward $-\infty$.

**Why negative sampling is used:**
1. **Computational efficiency:** Cost drops from $O(V)$ to $O(N)$ per step, where typically $N = 5$–$20$.
2. **Empirical quality:** SGNS produces embeddings of comparable or superior quality to hierarchical softmax for small datasets.
3. **Stable gradients:** Avoiding the saturated regions of the full softmax leads to more stable training dynamics.

**(7 marks — 3 for deriving the objective, 2 for explaining sampling distribution, 2 for advantages)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of the CBOW model. State the objective function used.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBOW) model predicts the **center word** $w_t$ from a symmetric context window of $2c$ surrounding words. The architecture:

1. **Input Layer:** $2c$ one-hot vectors, one for each context word $w_{t-c}, \dots, w_{t-1}, w_{t+1}, \dots, w_{t+c}$.
2. **Embedding Layer:** Each context word's one-hot vector is multiplied by the shared input matrix $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$, producing $2c$ embedding vectors of dimension $d$.
3. **Projection (Averaging) Layer:** The $2c$ embeddings are averaged element-wise to produce a single context vector:
