# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**Differentiate between Autoregressive (Causal) Language Modeling and Masked Language Modeling. Give one example model for each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Autoregressive (Causal) LM:** Predicts the next token using only leftward (past) context. The model is trained to maximize $P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$. It is **unidirectional** and is naturally suited for **text generation** tasks. *Example: GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer).*
- **Masked LM:** Randomly masks $\sim 15\%$ of input tokens and trains the model to predict them using **bidirectional** (both left and right) context. It is **non-generative** but produces rich contextual representations. *Example: BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers).*
- **Key difference:** Causal LM uses unidirectional context for next-token prediction; Masked LM uses bidirectional context for filling in masked tokens.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**
**What is Perplexity? Why is it considered the standard evaluation metric for language models?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Perplexity (PPL)** is defined as the exponentiated cross-entropy of a language model on a test corpus:
$$\text{PPL}(W) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ln P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right)$$
- **Interpretation:** It represents the effective number of choices the model is uncertain among at each step. A PPL of $k$ means the model is as confused as choosing among $k$ equi-probable words.
- **Why standard?** It is (i) monotonically decreasing with model improvement, (ii) comparable across corpora/vocabularies via normalization, (iii) directly tied to likelihood — the native training objective.
- Lower PPL ⇒ better language model.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Statistical & Neural LM Deep Dive

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the working of an **N-gram Language Model** with a suitable example. Derive the Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE) for bigram probabilities. How does the **zero-probability problem** arise, and how does **Laplace smoothing** solve it? Demonstrate with a numerical example.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare and contrast **Statistical (N-gram)**, **Neural Feedforward**, and **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN/LSTM)** Language Models along the dimensions of: context handling, handling of Out-Of-Vocabulary (OOV) words, capability to capture long-range dependencies, and parallelization.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define an N-gram LM:**
An N-gram LM approximates the probability of a word given its history using only the previous $(n-1)$ words (Markov Assumption of order $n-1$):
$$P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-(n-1)}, \ldots, w_{i-1})$$

**Step 2 — Example with $n = 2$ (Bigram):**
Training corpus: "I love NLP", "I love AI", "I study NLP"

**Step 3 — MLE derivation for bigrams:**
Start from the relative frequency definition:
$$\hat{P}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{\text{Count of bigram } (w_{i-1}, w_i)}{\text{Count of word } w_{i-1}}$$
This is the MLE because it maximizes $P(\text{corpus} \mid \theta) = \prod_{i} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$ subject to $\sum_{w_i} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = 1$.

Numerically:
$$P(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) = \frac{C(\text{I, love})}{C(\text{I})} = \frac{2}{3}$$
$$P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) = \frac{C(\text{love, NLP})}{C(\text{love})} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Zero-probability problem:**
For unseen bigrams (e.g., "study AI" never appearing), the MLE assigns $P = 0$. This causes the entire sentence probability to become zero — known as the **zero-probability (data sparsity) problem**.

**Step 5 — Laplace (Add-$\delta$) smoothing:**
$$P^*(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + \delta}{C(w_{i-1}) + \delta \cdot \vert \mathcal{V} \vert}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical demonstration** with $\delta = 1$, $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 5$, $C(\text{study}) = 1$, $C(\text{study, AI}) = 0$:
$$P^*(\text{AI} \mid \text{study}) = \frac{0 + 1}{1 + 1 \cdot 5} = \frac{1}{6}$$

This non-zero probability ensures unseen n-grams receive a small but finite probability mass.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Dimension | N-gram LM | Neural FFN LM | RNN/LSTM LM |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| **Context Handling** | Fixed $(n-1)$ words | Fixed window $k$ | Variable-length sequential |
| **OOV Handling** | Poor (needs back-off) | Better (subword embeddings) | Better (subword + shared params) |
| **Long-Range Dependencies** | Very poor (limited to $n-1$) | Poor (limited to $k$) | Moderate (LSTM gates) |
| **Parallelization** | Full (count-based) | Full (parallel matrix ops) | None (sequential dependency) |
| **Parameter Efficiency** | Table grows exponentially | Shared embeddings (compact) | Shared recurrent weights (compact) |
| **Training Cost** | Very low | Moderate | High (BPTT) |

**Conclusion:** N-gram LMs are simple and fast but suffer from sparsity. Neural FFN LMs introduced distributed representations solving sparsity. RNN/LSTM LMs extend context arbitrarily but lose parallelism — a limitation overcome by Transformers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Transformer-Based Language Models

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the architecture and training objective of **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)** as used in BERT. How does it differ fundamentally from the **Causal Language Modeling (CLM)** objective used in GPT? Justify why BERT cannot be used directly for open-ended text generation.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Derive and explain the **Perplexity** metric for evaluating language models. A trigram language model assigns the following probabilities to a test sentence of 5 words: $P_1 = 0.5$, $P_2 = 0.25$, $P_3 = 0.5$, $P_4 = 0.1$, $P_5 = 0.2$. Compute the perplexity of this model on the test sentence.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — BERT MLM Objective:**
Given an input token sequence, randomly mask $\sim 15\%$ of tokens. The model's task is to predict the original token at each masked position using the **entire bidirectional context**:

$$\mathcal{L}_{MLM}(\theta) = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} \log P_{\theta}(w_i \mid w_{\setminus i})$$

where $\mathcal{M}$ is the set of masked indices.

**Step 2 — BERT MLM Architecture Flow:**

| Layer | Function |
|-------|----------|
| Input Embedding | Token + Segment + Positional embedding |
| Transformer Encoder × N | Bidirectional self-attention |
| Output Layer | Per-position classifier over vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$ |

**Step 3 — GPT CLM Objective:**
GPT uses **masked self-attention** so that position $i$ attends only to positions $\leq i$:
$$\mathcal{L}_{CLM}(\theta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P_{\theta}(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$

**Step 4 — Key Differences:**

| Aspect | BERT (MLM) | GPT (CLM) |
|--------|------------|-----------|
| Context Direction | Bidirectional | Unidirectional (left only) |
| [MASK] Tokens Used | Yes (during pretraining) | No |
| Pretraining Task | Fill-in-the-blank | Next-token prediction |
| Output Type | Per-token classification scores | Per-token next-token distribution |
| Best For | NLU (classification, NER, QA) | NLG (generation, completion) |

**Step 5 — Why BERT cannot generate open-ended text:**
- BERT's bidirectional attention allows each token to "see the future", but during generation there is no future.
- Replacing generated tokens with [MASK] during inference produces incoherent output because the model was never trained to autoregressively decode from its own predictions — it expects a full input sequence.
- Hence, BERT is fundamentally an **encoder**, not a generator.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Derive Perplexity:**
Average per-token cross-entropy on a test corpus of $N$ tokens:
$$H(W_{\text{test}}) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$

Perplexity is the exponentiated cross-entropy:
$$\text{PPL} = 2^{H(W_{\text{test}})} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ln P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the joint probability of the test sentence** (assuming conditional probabilities are given):
$$P(\text{sentence}) = \prod_{i=1}^{5} P_i = 0.5 \times 0.25 \times 0.5 \times 0.1 \times 0.2$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the product step by step:**
$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{sentence}) &= 0.5 \times 0.25 = 0.125 \\
&= 0.125 \times 0.5 = 0.0625 \\
&= 0.0625 \times 0.1 = 0.00625 \\
&= 0.00625 \times 0.2 = 0.00125
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the average per-token negative log-likelihood** (using natural log):
$$\begin{aligned}
-\frac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{5} \ln P_i &= -\frac{1}{5} [\ln(0.5) + \ln(0.25) + \ln(0.5) + \ln(0.1) + \ln(0.2)] \\
&= -\frac{1}{5} [-0.6931 - 1.3863 - 0.6931 - 2.3026 - 1.6094] \\
&= -\frac{1}{5} \times (-6.6845) \\
&= 1.3369
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Perplexity:**
$$\text{PPL} = \exp(1.3369) \approx 3.808$$

**Interpretation:** The model is roughly as uncertain as choosing uniformly among ~3.8 words at each step.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 3 Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the chain rule base:** Students often define an LM only as "next-word prediction" without stating the chain-rule decomposition — examiners deduct up to **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing Causal and Masked LM:** Writing "BERT is autoregressive" or "GPT is bidirectional" is a **direct 3-mark penalty** in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Skipping numerical evaluation:** When the question asks to "compute", merely stating the formula and not plugging in values loses **2–3 marks**. Always end with a boxed numerical answer.
> 4. **Misapplying smoothing:** Using Add-$\delta$ smoothing but forgetting to update the denominator (must include $+\delta \vert \mathcal{V} \vert$) is a frequent error — lose **1 mark**.
> 5. **Confusing Perplexity vs Cross-Entropy:** PPL is *not* a probability — students lose marks by stating $0 \leq \text{PPL} \leq 1$. Correct: $\text{PPL} \geq 1$.
> 6. **OOV and Sparsity:** N-gram OOV problem must be explicitly linked to Laplace/Kneser-Ney smoothing — a single mention without justification deducts **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Language Model** = a probability distribution over token sequences; foundation is the **chain rule**.
- **Types of LM**: Statistical (N-gram) → Neural (FFN) → Recurrent (RNN/LSTM/GRU) → Transformer-based (Causal GPT, Masked BERT, Permutation XLNet, Seq2Seq T5).
- **N-gram LM** uses the Markov Assumption; estimated via **MLE**; suffers from **data sparsity**; fixed by **smoothing** (Laplace, Kneser-Ney).
- **Neural LM** = distributed word embeddings + neural network; solves sparsity but has **fixed context** (FFN) or **sequential bottleneck** (RNN).
- **Causal/Autoregressive LM (GPT)**: unidirectional, $P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$, used for **generation**.
- **Masked LM (BERT)**: bidirectional, predicts [MASK] tokens from full context, used for **understanding**.
- **Permutation LM (XLNet)**: combines both via random order factorization — no [MASK] tokens needed.
- **Seq2Seq LM (T5/BART)**: text-to-text framework, encoder + autoregressive decoder.
- **Perplexity (PPL)**: $2^{H}$ where $H$ is cross-entropy; **lower is better**; $\text{PPL} \geq 1$.
- **MLE for bigrams**: $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$.
- **Laplace smoothing**: add $\delta$ to every numerator and $\delta \vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ to the denominator.
- **Kneser-Ney smoothing** is the industry-standard advanced technique — preferred over Laplace.
- **Transformer self-attention** uses $\text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$ and enables full parallelization.
- **OOV problem** in classical LMs is addressed via **subword tokenization** (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece) in modern LMs.
- **Bidirectional ≠ bidirectional generation**: BERT encodes bidirectionally but does not generate bidirectionally.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Curse of Dimensionality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Curse of Dimensionality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Curse of Dimensionality** is a foundational concept in computational linguistics and machine learning, originally coined by **Richard Ernest Bellman** in **1961** within the context of dynamic programming. It refers to the various exponential phenomena that emerge when data is analyzed and organized in **high-dimensional spaces**, which do not occur in low-dimensional settings.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Syllabus)**
> The *Curse of Dimensionality* describes the problem that arises when working with high-dimensional data, where the volume of the feature space grows exponentially with the number of dimensions, causing data points to become sparse, distances to lose their meaning, and computational complexity to explode.

In the specific context of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, this phenomenon is acutely visible because text data, when represented numerically (e.g., via **Bag-of-Words**, **TF-IDF**, or **one-hot encodings**), naturally produces feature spaces with dimensions equal to the size of the vocabulary — often ranging from **10,000 to 500,000** features for a single corpus.

## 1.2 The NLP Connection

When we convert text into vectors:

- A vocabulary of size $V$ creates a $V$-dimensional vector space.
- Every document is represented as a single point in this $V$-dimensional space.
- With $V$ in the tens of thousands, even a corpus of millions of documents occupies a vanishingly small fraction of the available space.

This results in:
1. **Data Sparsity** — Most vector entries are zero.
2. **Distance Concentration** — All pairwise distances converge to the same value.
3. **Overfitting** — Models memorize training points rather than generalizing.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Searching for a Friend in a City" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are trying to find a specific friend in **a tiny village** with 10 houses. You can walk to all 10 houses in minutes, and the address (location) of your friend is highly *informative*.
>
> Now, imagine the same problem in **a 100-dimensional city**, where every house has 100 coordinates. Even with a billion houses, almost every location is empty, and "the nearest neighbor" of your friend is now statistically almost as far as the farthest possible neighbor. The address has lost its discriminative power.
>
> This is precisely what happens with text vectors: as the vocabulary (dimensions) grows, the *relative* distance between any two documents shrinks, defeating the purpose of similarity-based NLP algorithms.

## 1.4 The Bellman Perspective

Bellman originally observed this in the context of **adaptive control processes**, where the number of function evaluations required to optimize a function grows exponentially with dimension. For NLP, this manifests as the computational cost of building language models, where adding one extra feature dimension can double memory requirements.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why the Curse Occurs — A Structured Breakdown

The phenomenon is driven by **three interconnected mathematical truths**:

- **Exponential Volume Growth**:
  The volume of a unit hypercube in $n$ dimensions remains 1, but the volume occupied by an inscribed hypersphere shrinks drastically. The "corners" of the cube (where data tends to live in high dimensions) become unreachable.

- **Distance Concentration**:
  In high dimensions, the ratio of the *standard deviation* of distances to the *mean* of distances shrinks toward zero. This means $\sigma(d) \to 0$ while $E[d] \to \infty$, making all distances look approximately equal.

- **Combinatorial Explosion**:
  To maintain the same *density* of sampling as the dimensionality increases by $\Delta n$, the number of required samples must grow exponentially.

## 2.2 The Core Logic Steps

1. **Step 1 — Vectorization**: Convert text into a $V$-dimensional vector using one-hot, BoW, or TF-IDF.
2. **Step 2 — Embedding into $\mathbb{R}^V$**: Each document becomes a point in $\mathbb{R}^V$.
3. **Step 3 — Spacing Analysis**: As $V$ increases, the average distance between random points grows as $\sqrt{V}$.
4. **Step 4 — Sparsity Manifestation**: The fraction of the unit cube $[0,1]^V$ occupied by data approaches zero rapidly.
5. **Step 5 — Algorithmic Failure**: Distance-based algorithms (k-NN, k-means, cosine similarity) lose discriminative power.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **high-yield formula cluster** for KTU 2024 ESE questions on the Curse of Dimensionality. Master these before attempting numerical problems.

| **Formula** | **Name** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{\text{sphere}} = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma(n/2+1)} r^n$ | $n$-Ball Volume | Volume of an $n$-dimensional hypersphere of radius $r$ |
| $V_{\text{cube}} = (2r)^n$ | Hypercube Volume | Volume of an $n$-dimensional cube enclosing the sphere |
| $\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{V_{\text{sphere}}}{V_{\text{cube}}} = 0$ | Volume Ratio Limit | Shows sphere volume vanishes relative to cube |
| $E[\Vert X - Y \Vert_2] = \sqrt{2n} \cdot \sigma$ | Expected L2 Distance | Mean Euclidean distance between two random points in $[0,1]^n$ |
| $\frac{\sigma(d)}{E[d]} \to \frac{1}{\sqrt{2n}}$ | Relative Std. Dev. | Shows distance concentration in high $n$ |
| $N \propto (1/\epsilon)^n$ | Sample Complexity | Samples needed to cover space within error $\epsilon$ |
| $P(\text{point in shell}) = 1 - (1-\epsilon)^n$ | Corner Probability | Probability a point lies in the outer shell of thickness $\epsilon$ |
| $\text{Sparsity} = 1 - \frac{\text{nonzero entries}}{\text{total entries}}$ | Vector Sparsity | Fraction of zero entries in a feature vector |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

In **real-world NLP systems**, awareness of the curse drives critical engineering decisions:

- **Search Engines** use **dimensionality reduction** (LSA, SVD) precisely to avoid this curse.
- **Word Embeddings** (Word2Vec, GloVe) compress $V \approx 10^5$ down to $\approx 300$ dimensions.
- **Recommender Systems** use **matrix factorization** to avoid the user-item curse.
- **Topic Models** (LDA) operate in low-dimensional latent topic spaces.
- **Transformer Models** use **low-rank attention** to mitigate the same issue.

## 2.5 The 'How' Behind Each Step

- The volume of a hypersphere in $\mathbb{R}^n$ grows as $r^n$, but the *normalization constant* $\Gamma(n/2+1)$ grows **faster** (super-exponentially) than $r^n$, so the sphere's relative volume shrinks.
- The expected distance between two random points in $[0,1]^n$ grows as $\sqrt{n}$, while the standard deviation also grows as $\sqrt{n}$ but with a smaller leading constant, so the **coefficient of variation** $\sigma / \mu$ shrinks.
- For text data, the dimensionality equals the vocabulary size, and because most words appear only in a few documents, **sparsity is intrinsic**, not accidental.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Volume Ratio of Hypersphere to Hypercube

**Goal**: Show that as $n \to \infty$, an inscribed hypersphere occupies vanishingly small volume inside its bounding cube.

**Starting Premise**:
The volume of an $n$-dimensional ball of radius $r$ is given by the well-known formula.

$$
V_{\text{sphere}}(n) = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2} + 1\right)} \cdot r^n
$$

**Starting Premise (Cube)**:
The volume of an $n$-dimensional cube of side $2r$ (the smallest cube enclosing the ball of radius $r$).

$$
V_{\text{cube}}(n) = (2r)^n = 2^n \cdot r^n
$$

**Step 1 — Form the Ratio**:
Divide the sphere volume by the cube volume to get the *fraction of space* occupied by the sphere.

$$
\text{Ratio}(n) = \frac{V_{\text{sphere}}(n)}{V_{\text{cube}}(n)} = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2} + 1\right) \cdot 2^n}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the Logarithm**:
To analyze the limit, take the natural log of the ratio to convert exponentiation into a sum.

$$
\ln \text{Ratio}(n) = \frac{n}{2} \ln \pi - \ln \Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2} + 1\right) - n \ln 2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Stirling's Approximation**:
For large $n$, use the asymptotic form $\ln \Gamma(z) \approx (z - \tfrac{1}{2})\ln z - z + \tfrac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi)$ with $z = n/2 + 1$.

$$
\ln \Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2} + 1\right) \approx \frac{n}{2} \ln\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) - \frac{n}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\ln(n\pi)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify**:
Insert the Stirling approximation back into the log-ratio expression and collect terms in $n$.

$$
\ln \text{Ratio}(n) \approx \frac{n}{2} \ln \pi - \frac{n}{2} \ln\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + \frac{n}{2} - \frac{1}{2}\ln(n\pi) - n \ln 2
$$

**Step 5 — Group Linear Terms in $n$**:
Factor out $n/2$ from the leading terms.

$$
\ln \text{Ratio}(n) \approx \frac{n}{2}\left[\ln \pi - \ln\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1 - 2\ln 2\right] - \frac{1}{2}\ln(n\pi)
$$

**Step 6 — Take the Limit**:
As $n \to \infty$, the term $\frac{n}{2}\ln(n/2)$ dominates over $\frac{n}{2}\ln \pi$, so the bracket becomes $\approx -\frac{n}{2}\ln(n/2\pi)$ which goes to $-\infty$.

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \ln \text{Ratio}(n) = -\infty
$$

**Step 7 — Exponentiate to Conclude**:
Since the log-ratio goes to $-\infty$, the ratio itself goes to zero.

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{V_{\text{sphere}}(n)}{V_{\text{cube}}(n)} = 0
$$

**Conclusion**: In high dimensions, almost **all** the volume of a unit cube lies in its *corners*, not in the inscribed ball. Data uniformly distributed in the cube effectively lives only in the corners.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Distance Concentration in $[0,1]^n$

**Goal**: Show that pairwise Euclidean distances between uniformly distributed points in $[0,1]^n$ become nearly equal as $n \to \infty$.

**Starting Premise**:
Let $X = (X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$ and $Y = (Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_n)$ be two random points in $[0,1]^n$, where each $X_i, Y_i$ are i.i.d. uniform on $[0,1]$.

$$
d^2(X, Y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - Y_i)^2
$$

**Step 1 — Mean of Each Squared Component**:
For independent $X_i, Y_i \sim U(0,1)$, compute the expectation of $(X_i - Y_i)^2$.

$$
E[(X_i - Y_i)^2] = \text{Var}(X_i) + \text{Var}(Y_i) = \frac{1}{12} + \frac{1}{12} = \frac{1}{6}
$$

**Step 2 — Variance of Each Squared Component**:
Compute the variance of $(X_i - Y_i)^2$. By the moment formula for a uniform difference, $E[(X_i - Y_i)^4] = \frac{1}{15}$.

$$
\text{Var}\left[(X_i - Y_i)^2\right] = \frac{1}{15} - \left(\frac{1}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{15} - \frac{1}{36} = \frac{7}{180}
$$

**Step 3 — Mean of Total Squared Distance**:
By linearity of expectation, sum the $n$ component means.

$$
E[d^2] = n \cdot \frac{1}{6} = \frac{n}{6}
$$

**Step 4 — Variance of Total Squared Distance**:
Since the $n$ components are independent, variances add.

$$
\text{Var}[d^2] = n \cdot \frac{7}{180} = \frac{7n}{180}
$$

**Step 5 — Mean and Std. Dev. of Distance $d$**:
For large $n$, $d^2$ is approximately Gaussian, so $d \approx \sqrt{E[d^2]}$ and the standard deviation of $d$ is approximately $\sigma(d) \approx \frac{\sigma(d^2)}{2 \sqrt{E[d^2]}}$.

$$
E[d] \approx \sqrt{\frac{n}{6}}, \qquad \sigma(d) \approx \frac{\sqrt{7n/180}}{2\sqrt{n/6}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{7}{30}}
$$

**Step 6 — Coefficient of Variation**:
Compute the ratio $\sigma(d)/E[d]$ and observe its behavior.

$$
\frac{\sigma(d)}{E[d]} \approx \frac{\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{7/30}}{\sqrt{n/6}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{7}{30} \cdot \frac{6}{n}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{7}{5n}} = \sqrt{\frac{7}{20n}}
$$

**Step 7 — Take the Limit**:
As $n \to \infty$, the coefficient of variation shrinks to zero.

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\sigma(d)}{E[d]} = 0
$$

**Conclusion**: In high dimensions, all pairwise distances become **statistically indistinguishable**. This is the **Distance Concentration** phenomenon and is the reason k-NN, cosine similarity, and clustering algorithms fail in raw high-dimensional text spaces.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Probability of Landing in the Outer Shell

**Goal**: Derive the probability that a uniformly random point in $[0,1]^n$ lies within an $\epsilon$-thick shell near the surface.

**Starting Premise**:
Define the *outer shell* as points with at least one coordinate in $[0, \epsilon] \cup [1-\epsilon, 1]$.

$$
P(\text{in shell}) = 1 - P(\text{all coords in } [\epsilon, 1-\epsilon])
$$

**Step 1 — Probability All Coordinates are Interior**:
Each coordinate has probability $1 - 2\epsilon$ of being in $[\epsilon, 1-\epsilon]$. By independence.

$$
P(\text{interior}) = (1 - 2\epsilon)^n
$$

**Step 2 — Complement (In Shell)**:
Take the complement to get the shell probability.

$$
P(\text{shell}) = 1 - (1 - 2\epsilon)^n
$$

**Step 3 — Take the Limit as $n \to \infty$**:
For any fixed $\epsilon > 0$, the term $(1-2\epsilon)^n \to 0$ exponentially.

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} P(\text{shell}) = 1
$$

**Conclusion**: In high dimensions, *every* random point effectively lies on the boundary of the cube. This means text documents (viewed as points in vocabulary space) are essentially "corner cases", which makes generalization extremely difficult.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Conceptual Architecture of the Curse of Dimensionality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Text Corpus Input] --> proc1[Tokenization]
    proc1 --> proc2[Vocabulary Building]
    proc2 --> proc3[Vectorization BoW or TFIDF]
    proc3 --> proc4[VDimensional Feature Space]
    proc4 --> nodeA{Sample Density Check}
    nodeA --> lowD[Low D Dimension less than 50]
    nodeA --> midD[Mid D Dimension 50 to 500]
    nodeA --> highD[High D Dimension greater than 1000]
    lowD --> res1[Dense Data, Good Similarity]
    midD --> res2[Sparse Data, Distance Bias Emerges]
    highD --> res3[Curse of Dimensionality Manifests]
    res3 --> effect1[Data Sparsity]
    res3 --> effect2[Distance Concentration]
    res3 --> effect3[Computational Explosion]
    res3 --> effect4[Overfitting in Models]
    effect1 --> mitig[Apply Mitigation Strategies]
    effect2 --> mitig
    effect3 --> mitig
    effect4 --> mitig
    mitig --> tech1[LSA using SVD]
    mitig --> tech2[Word Embeddings]
    mitig --> tech3[PCA and LDA]
    mitig --> tech4[Autoencoders]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dim2[Dimension 2] --> circle2[Circle inside Square]
    dim3[Dimension 3] --> sphere3[Sphere inside Cube]
    dim10[Dimension 10] --> sph10[Hypersphere inside Hypercube]
    dim100[Dimension 100] --> sph100[Hypertiny Sphere inside Hyperlarge Cube]

```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: NLP Pipeline with Curse Mitigation

```mermaid

    cleanA --> tokA[Tokenization and Stopword Removal]

    vecA --> curseCheck[Curse of Dimensionality Detected]
    curseCheck --> dimRed[Dimensionality Reduction Module]
    dimRed --> pca1[Principal Component Analysis]

```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Distance Concentration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Input Two Vectors X and Y in R_n] --> compA[Compute Per Coordinate Difference]
    compA --> sqA[Square Each Difference]
    sqA --> sumA[Sum all n Squared Differences]
    sumA --> sqrtA[Take Square Root for Euclidean Distance]
    sqrtA --> statA[Compute Mean and Std Dev over Corpus]
    statA --> branchA{Dimensionality n is High}
    branchA --> yesPath[Yes, n is large]
    branchA --> noPath[No, n is small]
    yesPath --> conc[Distances Concentrate near Common Value]
    conc --> fail[Similarity Search Fails]
    noPath --> good[Distances Discriminate Well]
    good --> success[Similarity Search Succeeds]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Curse of Dimensionality" in the context of NLP. Give one example where it arises.**

**Model Answer**:
The Curse of Dimensionality, a term coined by Richard Bellman in 1961, refers to the exponential growth in computational complexity and data sparsity that occurs as the number of features (dimensions) in a dataset increases. In NLP, this arises when text is represented using the Bag-of-Words model with a vocabulary of $V$ words, producing $V$-dimensional sparse vectors. For $V = 50{,}000$, even a corpus of one million documents occupies a negligible fraction of the feature space, causing distance-based algorithms like k-NN and clustering to fail due to indistinguishable pairwise distances.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is meant by "distance concentration" in high-dimensional spaces? Mention its consequence for cosine similarity-based retrieval.**

**Model Answer**:
Distance concentration is the phenomenon where, in high-dimensional spaces, the ratio of the standard deviation of pairwise Euclidean distances to the mean distance approaches zero. Mathematically, $\sigma(d)/E[d] \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. The consequence for cosine similarity-based retrieval is severe: because the angular separation between any two document vectors becomes statistically uniform, cosine similarity scores cluster tightly around their mean value, making it impossible to distinguish genuinely similar documents from dissimilar ones.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]**
**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the Curse of Dimensionality. Derive the limit showing that the volume of a hypersphere becomes negligible compared to its enclosing hypercube as dimensions grow.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss three real-world NLP scenarios where the curse manifests and the techniques used to mitigate it.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Definition**: The Curse of Dimensionality, introduced by Richard Bellman (1961), describes the exponential explosion in the volume of feature space as the number of dimensions increases, leading to data sparsity and algorithmic failure.

**Derivation Setup**:
The volume of an $n$-dimensional ball of radius $r$ is:

$$
V_{\text{sphere}}(n) = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma(n/2+1)} \cdot r^n
$$

The enclosing hypercube has volume:

$$
V_{\text{cube}}(n) = (2r)^n
$$

**Step 1**: Form the ratio $R(n) = V_{\text{sphere}}/V_{\text{cube}}$.

$$
R(n) = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{2^n \cdot \Gamma(n/2+1)}
$$

**Step 2**: Apply Stirling's approximation $\Gamma(z+1) \approx \sqrt{2\pi z}(z/e)^z$ with $z = n/2$.

$$
R(n) \approx \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{2^n \cdot \sqrt{\pi n} \cdot (n/2e)^{n/2}} = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{2^n \cdot \sqrt{\pi n} \cdot n^{n/2} \cdot e^{-n/2}}
$$

**Step 3**: Simplify the numerator and denominator powers of $\pi$ and $n$.

$$
R(n) \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi n}} \cdot \frac{\pi^{n/2} \cdot e^{n/2}}{2^n \cdot n^{n/2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi n}} \cdot \left(\frac{\pi e}{4n}\right)^{n/2}
$$

**Step 4**: For any $n > \pi e / 4 \approx 2.13$, the bracketed term is less than 1, and the ratio $R(n) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.

**Conclusion**: Therefore, $\lim_{n\to\infty} R(n) = 0$, proving that in high dimensions, the inscribed hypersphere occupies a vanishing fraction of the hypercube. Practically, this means that uniform sampling of a high-dimensional space yields points that lie almost exclusively in the "corners" of the space, never in the central region.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Scenario 1 — Document Classification with BoW**:
In a 50,000-word vocabulary, each document becomes a 50,000-dimensional vector with only 100–500 nonzero entries. Logistic regression overfits. **Mitigation**: Apply TF-IDF weighting and Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA) to reduce dimensionality to 100–300.

**Scenario 2 — Word Representations**:
One-hot word vectors of dimension $V \approx 10^5$ cannot capture semantic similarity (orthogonal vectors have zero dot product). **Mitigation**: Use dense word embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText) of dimension 100–300 that place semantically similar words near each other in continuous space.

**Scenario 3 — Recommender Systems / Topic Modeling**:
User-item matrices with millions of items create sparse high-dimensional spaces where collaborative filtering fails. **Mitigation**: Matrix factorization (SVD, NMF) or Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) projects users and items into a low-dimensional latent topic space of dimension 50–200.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | 14 Marks]**
**(a)** [7 Marks] State and prove the distance concentration theorem for uniformly distributed points in $[0,1]^n$. State its implications for k-NN classifiers.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare and contrast three dimensionality reduction techniques used in NLP to combat the curse.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Statement**:
Let $X, Y$ be two random vectors uniformly distributed in $[0,1]^n$. Then as $n \to \infty$, the ratio $\sigma(d(X,Y))/E[d(X,Y)] \to 0$, where $d$ is the Euclidean distance.

**Proof**:
**Step 1** — Setup. Let $Z_i = X_i - Y_i$, where each $Z_i$ has mean 0 and variance $1/6$.

**Step 2** — Compute $E[d^2]$. Using the result that $E[Z_i^2] = 1/6$:

$$
E[d^2] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[Z_i^2] = \frac{n}{6}
$$

**Step 3** — Compute $\text{Var}[d^2]$. With $\text{Var}[Z_i^2] = 7/180$:

$$
\text{Var}[d^2] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}[Z_i^2] = \frac{7n}{180}
$$

**Step 4** — Convert to mean and standard deviation of $d$ using delta method:

$$
E[d] \approx \sqrt{n/6}, \qquad \sigma(d) \approx \frac{\sqrt{7n/180}}{2\sqrt{n/6}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{7}{30}}
$$

**Step 5** — Form the ratio:

$$
\frac{\sigma(d)}{E[d]} = \frac{\sqrt{7/30}}{2\sqrt{n/6}} = \sqrt{\frac{7}{20n}} \to 0 \quad \text{as } n \to \infty
$$

**Implications for k-NN**:
In high dimensions, the nearest neighbor of a query point $q$ is statistically *almost as far* as the farthest neighbor. The relative ranking of neighbors becomes essentially random, causing classification accuracy of k-NN to degrade toward the majority-class baseline. This is the empirical observation that k-NN accuracy in NLP applications plateaus or collapses as vocabulary size grows beyond $\sim 10^4$.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

| **Technique** | **Principle** | **Linearity** | **NLP Use Case** | **Limitation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PCA (Principal Component Analysis)** | Projects data onto directions of maximum variance | Linear | Feature reduction in BoW/TF-IDF pipelines | Captures only linear structure |
| **LSA (Latent Semantic Analysis)** | Truncated SVD on term-document matrix | Linear | Document similarity, topic discovery | Slow for very large corpora |
| **LDA (Latent Dirichlet Allocation)** | Probabilistic generative topic model | Linear (probabilistic) | Topic modeling, document representation | Assumes bag-of-words and exchangeability |
| **Word2Vec / GloVe** | Predicts word context in dense space | Non-linear | Semantic similarity, downstream tasks | Requires large corpora to train |
| **Autoencoders** | Neural encoder-decoder reconstruction | Non-linear | Dense sentence/document embeddings | Computationally expensive to train |

The choice depends on data size, interpretability requirements, and downstream task.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Stirling step**: Many students jump from the ratio to the limit without justifying the intermediate asymptotics. KTU examiners award partial credit for the *setup* (forming the ratio) but full credit requires applying Stirling's approximation.
> 2. **Confusing "sparsity" with "distance concentration"**: These are *related* but distinct consequences. Sparsity is about zero entries; concentration is about distance distributions. Examiners mark them as separate concepts.
> 3. **Confusing $E[d^2]$ with $E[d]$**: A common mistake is to confuse the mean of the *squared* distance with the mean of the *distance itself*. Always use the delta method (linearization) for the conversion.
> 4. **Skipping the unit cube assumption**: Distance concentration results depend on the underlying distribution. State $[0,1]^n$ explicitly.
> 5. **In Part B, students often list techniques without comparison**: KTU 2024 scheme awards marks for *contrast*, not just enumeration. Use comparison tables or explicit "X is better than Y because..." statements.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Originator**: Richard Bellman, 1961, in the context of dynamic programming.
- **Core Definition**: Exponential growth in volume and sparsity of feature space as dimensionality increases.
- **NLP Trigger**: Vocabulary size $V$ acts as dimensionality. For $V > 10^4$, the curse becomes severe.
- **Three Core Manifestations**: (1) Data sparsity, (2) Distance concentration, (3) Sample complexity explosion.
- **Key Formula Cluster**:
  - Hypersphere volume: $V_n = \dfrac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma(n/2+1)} r^n$
  - Mean pairwise distance: $E[d] \approx \sqrt{n/6}$ in $[0,1]^n$
  - Concentration ratio: $\sigma(d)/E[d] \approx \sqrt{7/(20n)} \to 0$
  - Shell probability: $1 - (1-2\epsilon)^n \to 1$
- **Mitigation Techniques (NLP)**: PCA, LSA/SVD, LDA, Word Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe), Autoencoders.
- **Engineering Rule of Thumb**: If $V > 100 \cdot N$ (vocabulary exceeds documents by 100×), dimensionality reduction is mandatory.
- **Exam Tip**: Always specify the *space* (e.g., $[0,1]^n$) and the *metric* (Euclidean, cosine) when stating concentration results.
- **Memory Aid**: "As $n$ rises, corners rise, distances equalize, data disappears."
- **Common Mistake to Avoid**: Do not confuse "high-dimensional" with "many features per sample" — it specifically refers to the **feature space dimension**, not the dataset size.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Language Model Markov Assumption and N-Grams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Language Model Markov Assumption and N-Grams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Language Model?

A **Language Model (LM)** is a probabilistic model that assigns a probability distribution over sequences of words (or tokens) drawn from a vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$. Formally, given a sequence of words $W = w_1, w_2, w_3, \dots, w_n$, a language model computes:

$$P(W) = P(w_1, w_2, w_3, \dots, w_n)$$

This single scalar probability tells us how *likely* a given sentence is to occur in a natural language. The higher the probability, the more "natural" or grammatically/statistically expected the sentence is to a native speaker.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standard):** A Language Model is a statistical tool used in Natural Language Processing that estimates the probability of a word sequence, enabling tasks such as speech recognition, machine translation, spell correction, and text generation.

### 1.2 The Core Problem: Why Can't We Compute $P(W)$ Directly?

In theory, if we had an infinitely large corpus, we could estimate $P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$ using **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** by simply counting:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \frac{\text{Count}(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)}{\text{Total number of word sequences in corpus}}$$

**However**, the practical problems are devastating:
1. **Data Sparsity:** A vocabulary of just 50,000 words gives $50{,}000^n$ possible sequences of length $n$. Most sequences will have **zero count** in any finite corpus.
2. **Computational Intractability:** Storing probabilities for all possible sequences is memory-impossible.
3. **Lack of Generalization:** Even if we somehow observed a sentence once, the model gives it zero probability if observed sequences differ even slightly.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Predictable Conversation"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a person listening to a conversation. They don't memorize the *exact* sentence; instead, they predict the **next word** based on the **last few words** they heard. If someone says *"The weather is ___",* you instinctively guess *"sunny", "cloudy", or "cold"* — not *"elephant"*. A Language Model formalizes this intuition: it doesn't model the *entire* sentence jointly, it predicts one word at a time using only a **small window of recent context**. This "small window" assumption is the **Markov Assumption**.

### 1.4 The Markov Assumption — Formal Definition

The **Markov Assumption** (named after Russian mathematician Andrey Markov, 1856–1922) states that the probability of the next word depends **only on a limited history of preceding words**, not the entire sentence.

A stochastic process satisfies the **first-order Markov property** if:

$$P(w_{i} \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1}) = P(w_{i} \mid w_{i-1})$$

Generalizing to the $k$-th order Markov property:

$$P(w_{i} \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1}) = P(w_{i} \mid w_{i-k}, \dots, w_{i-1})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Markov Assumption (KTU Board Definition):** The future state (next word) is conditionally independent of all past states given a finite window of the most recent $k$ states. This is the foundational principle enabling tractable N-Gram language models.

### 1.5 What is an N-Gram?

An **N-Gram** is a contiguous sequence of $N$ items (words or characters) from a given text. When we use the Markov Assumption with a history of $N-1$ words to predict the next word, the resulting model is called an **N-Gram Language Model**.

| N | N-Gram Name | Context Window | Example from "I love NLP deeply" |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Unigram** | 0 preceding words | "I", "love", "NLP", "deeply" |
| 2 | **Bigram** | 1 preceding word | "I love", "love NLP", "NLP deeply" |
| 3 | **Trigram** | 2 preceding words | "I love NLP", "love NLP deeply" |
| 4 | **4-Gram** | 3 preceding words | "I love NLP deeply" |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Sparsity Trade-off:** Smaller N (e.g., unigrams) → less data needed but weaker context. Larger N (e.g., 5-grams) → richer context but extreme data sparsity. In KTU-style problems, **unigrams, bigrams, and trigrams** are the focus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chain Rule of Probability — The Starting Point

To derive any N-gram model, we begin with the **Chain Rule of Probability** (a fundamental identity from probability theory):

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_n \mid w_1, \dots, w_{n-1})$$

In compact notation:

$$P(w_1^n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$$

where $w_1^{i-1}$ denotes the history $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the Chain Rule insufficient?** The terms $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ have **exponentially growing conditioning contexts**. The Markov Assumption *truncates* this history to a fixed window of size $N-1$, making estimation feasible.

### 2.2 Applying the Markov Assumption: N-Gram Model Formulation

By applying the $k$-th order Markov assumption (with $k = N-1$), the chain rule reduces to:

$$P(w_1^n) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-(N-1)}^{i-1})$$

For specific N values:

**Unigram Model (N=1):**
$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i)$$

**Bigram Model (N=2):**
$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$$

**Trigram Model (N=3):**
$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})$$

### 2.3 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for N-Grams

The probabilities in N-gram models are estimated using **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** — i.e., relative frequency counting over a training corpus.

**Unigram Probability:**
$$P(w_i) = \frac{C(w_i)}{\sum_{w \in \mathcal{V}} C(w)} = \frac{C(w_i)}{N_{\text{total}}}$$

**Bigram Probability:**
$$P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$$

**General N-Gram Probability:**
$$P(w_i \mid w_{i-(N-1)}^{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-(N-1)}^{i})}{C(w_{i-(N-1)}^{i-1})}$$

where:
- $C(\cdot)$ denotes the count of the given sequence in the training corpus.
- The denominator is the count of the $(N-1)$-gram prefix (the context).
- The numerator is the count of the full $N$-gram.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of N-Gram LM |
|---|---|
| **Speech Recognition** | Disambiguates "recognize speech" vs. "wreck a nice beach" |
| **Machine Translation** | Reranks candidate translations by fluency |
| **Spell Checking & Autocorrect** | Ranks "I went to" higher than "I went too" |
| **Text Generation & Predictive Typing** | Suggests next word on mobile keyboards |
| **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)** | Post-processing to fix recognition errors |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Key Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chain Rule | $P(w_1^n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ | Exact decomposition |
| 2 | Markov Assumption (k-th order) | $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-k}^{i-1})$ | Truncates history |
| 3 | Unigram Probability | $P(w_i) = \dfrac{C(w_i)}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | MLE over individual words |
| 4 | Bigram Probability | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$ | MLE over pairs |
| 5 | Trigram Probability | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-2}, w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{i-2}, w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})}$ | MLE over triples |
| 6 | Sentence Probability | $P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})$ | Product over all positions |
| 7 | Log-Probability (numerical stability) | $\log P(W) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})$ | Avoid underflow |
| 8 | Perplexity (evaluation metric) | $PP(W) = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})\right)$ | Lower is better |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL — Sparsity Problem (Recurring KTU Question):** N-gram models suffer from the zero-probability problem. If a particular $(N-1)$-gram prefix never appears in training, $P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1}) = 0/0$ (undefined). Even if it does appear, unseen $N$-grams yield zero, making $P(W) = 0$. The **fix** is *smoothing* techniques such as **Laplace (Add-1)**, **Add-k**, **Good-Turing**, or **Kneser-Ney**. While not the core of this topic, the problem must always be mentioned in KTU answers.

### 2.6 How an N-Gram Model Operates — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Tokenization:** Split the training corpus into words (and sentence boundaries marked with $\langle/s\rangle$).
2. **Counting Phase:** Slide a window of size $N$ across the corpus and tally each unique N-gram.
3. **Probability Estimation:** For each $(N-1)$-gram prefix, compute conditional probability via MLE.
4. **Inference Phase:** For a new sentence, apply the chain rule with the Markov approximation, multiplying the conditional probabilities at each position.
5. **Padding Convention:** The first $N-1$ positions are usually conditioned on special start tokens $\langle s \rangle$, e.g., $P(w_1 \mid \langle s \rangle)$ for unigrams, $P(w_1 \mid \langle s \rangle, \langle s \rangle)$ for trigrams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this useful in engineering?** N-gram LMs are computationally *cheap*, *interpretable*, and *deployable on edge devices* (mobile keyboards). They are the workhorse of classical NLP pipelines and remain a benchmark in low-resource scenarios. Modern transformer-based LMs (BERT, GPT) generalize the N-gram idea to longer contexts using attention mechanisms.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Computing Sentence Probability Using a Bigram Model

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Pattern):**
Given a tiny training corpus and a target sentence, compute its probability under a bigram language model.

**Training Corpus (5 sentences):**
1. "I love NLP"
2. "I love deep learning"
3. "deep learning is fun"
4. "NLP is fun"
5. "I love learning"

**Target Sentence:** *"I love NLP"*

#### Step 1: Tokenize and Add Sentence Boundary

We append the start symbol $\langle s \rangle$ at the beginning and the end symbol $\langle /s \rangle$ at the end:

$$\text{Corpus tokens (bigram view):} \quad \langle s \rangle \ I \quad \langle s \rangle \ I \quad \langle s \rangle \ I \quad \langle s \rangle \ I \quad \langle s \rangle \ I$$

Wait — let us be careful. We add $\langle s \rangle$ once per sentence (at the start). Each sentence has its own start-end pair.

#### Step 2: Extract All Bigrams and Count

Sliding a window of size 2 across each sentence:

**Sentence 1:** "I love NLP" → tokens: $\langle s \rangle$, I, love, NLP, $\langle /s \rangle$

| Bigram | Count |
|---|---|
| $(\langle s \rangle, I)$ | 1 |
| $(I, love)$ | 1 |
| $(love, NLP)$ | 1 |
| $(NLP, \langle /s \rangle)$ | 1 |

**Sentence 2:** "I love deep learning" → tokens: $\langle s \rangle$, I, love, deep, learning, $\langle /s \rangle$

| Bigram | Count |
|---|---|
| $(\langle s \rangle, I)$ | +1 → 2 |
| $(I, love)$ | +1 → 2 |
| $(love, deep)$ | 1 |
| $(deep, learning)$ | 1 |
| $(learning, \langle /s \rangle)$ | 1 |

**Sentence 3:** "deep learning is fun" → tokens: $\langle s \rangle$, deep, learning, is, fun, $\langle /s \rangle$

| Bigram | Count |
|---|---|
| $(\langle s \rangle, deep)$ | 1 |
| $(deep, learning)$ | +1 → 2 |
| $(learning, is)$ | 1 |
| $(is, fun)$ | 1 |
| $(fun, \langle /s \rangle)$ | 1 |

**Sentence 4:** "NLP is fun" → tokens: $\langle s \rangle$, NLP, is, fun, $\langle /s \rangle$

| Bigram | Count |
|---|---|
| $(\langle s \rangle, NLP)$ | 1 |
| $(NLP, is)$ | 1 |
| $(is, fun)$ | +1 → 2 |
| $(fun, \langle /s \rangle)$ | +1 → 2 |

**Sentence 5:** "I love learning" → tokens: $\langle s \rangle$, I, love, learning, $\langle /s \rangle$

| Bigram | Count |
|---|---|
| $(\langle s \rangle, I)$ | +1 → 3 |
| $(I, love)$ | +1 → 3 |
| $(love, learning)$ | 1 |
| $(learning, \langle /s \rangle)$ | +1 → 2 |

#### Step 3: Aggregate the Final Bigram Counts

| Bigram $(w_{i-1}, w_i)$ | Count |
|---|---|
| $(\langle s \rangle, I)$ | 3 |
| $(\langle s \rangle, deep)$ | 1 |
| $(\langle s \rangle, NLP)$ | 1 |
| $(I, love)$ | 3 |
| $(love, NLP)$ | 1 |
| $(love, deep)$ | 1 |
| $(love, learning)$ | 1 |
| $(deep, learning)$ | 2 |
| $(learning, \langle /s \rangle)$ | 2 |
| $(learning, is)$ | 1 |
| $(NLP, \langle /s \rangle)$ | 1 |
| $(NLP, is)$ | 1 |
| $(is, fun)$ | 2 |
| $(fun, \langle /s \rangle)$ | 2 |

#### Step 4: Compute Unigram Counts (Denominators)

| Word | Count |
|---|---|
| $\langle s \rangle$ | 5 |
| I | 3 |
| love | 3 |
| NLP | 2 |
| deep | 2 |
| learning | 3 |
| is | 2 |
| fun | 2 |
| $\langle /s \rangle$ | 5 |

#### Step 5: Apply the Bigram MLE Formula

$$P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$$

For our target sentence "I love NLP" with start/end tokens, the bigrams are:
- $(\langle s \rangle, I)$
- $(I, love)$
- $(love, NLP)$
- $(NLP, \langle /s \rangle)$

Computing each:

$$P(I \mid \langle s \rangle) = \frac{C(\langle s \rangle, I)}{C(\langle s \rangle)} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6$$

$$P(love \mid I) = \frac{C(I, love)}{C(I)} = \frac{3}{3} = 1.0$$

$$P(NLP \mid love) = \frac{C(love, NLP)}{C(love)} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

$$P(\langle /s \rangle \mid NLP) = \frac{C(NLP, \langle /s \rangle)}{C(NLP)} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

#### Step 6: Multiply to Get the Sentence Probability

$$P(\text{I love NLP}) = P(I \mid \langle s \rangle) \cdot P(love \mid I) \cdot P(NLP \mid love) \cdot P(\langle /s \rangle \mid NLP)$$

$$P(\text{I love NLP}) = 0.6 \times 1.0 \times \frac{1}{3} \times 0.5$$

$$P(\text{I love NLP}) = 0.6 \times 1.0 \times 0.3333 \times 0.5 = 0.1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Log-Probability Trick:** In KTU answers, always show the final numerical value *and* a remark that in real systems one would convert to log-space: $\log P(\text{I love NLP}) = \log(0.6) + \log(1.0) + \log(0.333) + \log(0.5) \approx -2.303$. This avoids underflow for long sentences and earns extra conceptual marks.

### 3.2 General Derivation: From Chain Rule to N-Gram

**Starting Point — Chain Rule:**

$$P(w_1^n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$$

**Apply the $(N-1)$-th Order Markov Assumption:**

Each $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ is replaced by $P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})$, where indices less than 1 are treated as start tokens $\langle s \rangle$:

$$P(w_1^n) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{max(1, i-N+1)}^{i-1})$$

**For unigrams (N=1):**

The history is empty, so the conditioning term is dropped:

$$P(w_1^n) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i)$$

**For bigrams (N=2):**

The history is the single preceding word. For $i=1$, we treat $w_0 = \langle s \rangle$:

$$P(w_1^n) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$$

**For trigrams (N=3):**

The history is the two preceding words:

$$P(w_1^n) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General N-Gram Probability Formula (Board Exam Must-Know):**
> $$P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-N+1}^{i})}{C(w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})}$$
> - **Numerator:** Count of the full N-gram $w_{i-N+1}, \dots, w_i$.
> - **Denominator:** Count of the $(N-1)$-gram prefix $w_{i-N+1}, \dots, w_{i-1}$.
> - **MLE Assumption:** This is the *maximum likelihood* estimate — the parameter value that maximizes the probability of the observed training data.

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Unigram vs Bigram vs Trigram

| Property | Unigram | Bigram | Trigram |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conditioning context** | None | 1 previous word | 2 previous words |
| **Probability formula** | $P(w_i) = C(w_i)/N$ | $P(w_i \vert w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-1},w_i)/C(w_{i-1})$ | $P(w_i \vert w_{i-2},w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-2},w_{i-1},w_i)/C(w_{i-2},w_{i-1})$ |
| **Memory required** | $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ | $\approx \vert \mathcal{V} \vert^2$ | $\approx \vert \mathcal{V} \vert^3$ |
| **Data sparsity** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Context sensitivity** | None | Local (adjacent) | Slightly broader |
| **Typical use** | Baseline | Most common | High-accuracy tasks |
| **Markov order** | 0-th | 1st | 2nd |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world vocabulary sizes:** Google's Web 1T 5-gram corpus used a vocabulary of $\approx 13$ million words. The number of *possible* 5-grams is astronomically larger. Even Google's massive corpus covers only a tiny fraction — this is the fundamental data-sparsity issue that drives research into neural LMs.

### 3.4 Derivation of Perplexity (Evaluation Metric — Frequently Asked in KTU)

**Perplexity** is the standard intrinsic evaluation metric for language models. For a test set $W$ of $N$ words:

$$PP(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)^{-\frac{1}{N}}$$

Taking the chain rule:

$$PP(W) = \left[ \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1}) \right]^{-\frac{1}{N}}$$

Taking the reciprocal root:

$$PP(W) = \exp\left[ -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1}) \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Perplexity is the *effective branching factor* — the weighted average number of choices the model is confused among at each step. **Lower perplexity = better language model.** If a trigram model has $PP=20$, it is "as confused" as if it had to choose uniformly among 20 words at each step.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Markov Assumption Hierarchy — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Full Chain Rule"]:::top -->|"P(w_i | w_1...w_{i-1})"| B["Markov Assumption Truncation"]:::mid
    B -->|"Order 0: No history"| C["Unigram Model"]:::leaf
    B -->|"Order 1: 1 prev word"| D["Bigram Model"]:::leaf
    B -->|"Order 2: 2 prev words"| E["Trigram Model"]:::leaf
    B -->|"Order k"| F["General N-Gram Model"]:::leaf

    C --> G["P(w_i) = C(w_i)/N"]
    D --> H["P(w_i | w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-1},w_i)/C(w_{i-1})"]
    E --> I["P(w_i | w_{i-2},w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-2},w_{i-1},w_i)/C(w_{i-2},w_{i-1})"]

    classDef top fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mid fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0e7490,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#a16207,stroke:#a16207,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The top node represents the **exact chain rule** (intractable for long histories).
- The middle node represents the **Markov Assumption** that truncates the history.
- The leaf nodes are the *concrete N-gram models* obtained by choosing $N-1$ as the order.
- Each leaf's formula box shows the corresponding MLE probability estimator.

### 4.2 Bigram Model Architecture — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["Training Corpus"]:::in --> B1["Tokenization + Add start/end tokens"]:::proc
    B1 --> C1["Count all bigrams C(w_{i-1},w_i)"]:::proc
    B1 --> C2["Count all unigrams C(w_{i-1})"]:::proc
    C1 --> D1["Compute P(w_i | w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-1},w_i) / C(w_{i-1})"]:::core
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1["Bigram Probability Table"]:::out

    F1["New Sentence"]:::in --> G1["Apply chain rule with bigram approximation"]:::proc
    E1 --> G1
    G1 --> H1["P(sentence) = product of conditional probabilities"]:::core
    H1 --> I1["Final Sentence Probability"]:::out

    classDef in fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    classDef proc fill:#475569,stroke:#475569,color:#ffffff
    classDef core fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#4d7c0f,stroke:#4d7c0f,color:#ffffff
```

**Block-by-block interpretation:**

- **Training Pipeline (top row):** Raw corpus → tokenized with start/end markers → parallel bigram and unigram counting → MLE probability computation → final probability table.
- **Inference Pipeline (bottom row):** New sentence enters → bigram approximation of chain rule applied → multiplication of conditional probabilities → final scalar sentence probability.
- The shared **Probability Table** is the trained model artifact deployed at inference time.

### 4.3 Sliding-Window Bigram Extraction — Process Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["Start Token BOS"]:::tok
    W1["w1"]:::tok
    W2["w2"]:::tok
    W3["w3"]:::tok
    W4["w4"]:::tok
    E1["End Token EOS"]:::tok

    S1 --- W1
    W1 --- W2
    W2 --- W3
    W3 --- W4
    W4 --- E1

    BG1["Bigram 1: (BOS, w1)"]:::bigram
    BG2["Bigram 2: (w1, w2)"]:::bigram
    BG3["Bigram 3: (w2, w3)"]:::bigram
    BG4["Bigram 4: (w3, w4)"]:::bigram
    BG5["Bigram 5: (w4, EOS)"]:::bigram

    S1 ==> BG1
    W1 ==> BG2
    W2 ==> BG3
    W3 ==> BG4
    W4 ==> BG5

    classDef tok fill:#0369a1,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
    classDef bigram fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation:** A bigram model uses a sliding window of size 2 over the tokenized sentence. The first token is preceded by a **BOS (Beginning of Sentence)** marker so that the very first conditional probability $P(w_1 \mid BOS)$ is well-defined. The last token is followed by an **EOS (End of Sentence)** marker to allow the model to learn sentence termination patterns. Each adjacent pair becomes one bigram, contributing one factor to the final sentence probability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why BOS and EOS tokens are mandatory in KTU answers:** Without explicit start/end tokens, the bigram model cannot assign a probability to the first word of a sentence (no preceding context) and cannot model the natural stopping behavior. Always include these tokens in your count tables during exams.

### 4.4 Decision Flow for Choosing an N-Gram Order

```mermaid
graph TD
```
