## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 (3 marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
> **Define a Language Model. State the Markov Assumption used in N-Gram Language Models.**

**Model Answer (3 marks allocation):**

A **Language Model** is a probabilistic model that assigns a probability to a sequence of words, indicating how likely that sequence is in a given language. Formally, given a word sequence $W = w_1 w_2 \dots w_n$, it computes $P(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$. It is widely used in speech recognition, machine translation, and text generation.

The **Markov Assumption** states that the probability of a word depends only on a finite window of $k$ preceding words rather than the entire history. Mathematically:

$$P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-k}, \dots, w_{i-1})$$

This assumption makes N-gram models computationally tractable and memory-efficient. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Question 2 (3 marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
> **Explain the concept of a Bigram model. How is the bigram probability computed using Maximum Likelihood Estimation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks allocation):**

A **Bigram model** is a 2nd-order Markov (1st-order context) language model that predicts the probability of a word based on **only the immediately preceding word**. The probability of a sentence is the product of all such conditional bigram probabilities.

The **bigram probability** using **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** is computed as the relative frequency:

$$P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$$

where $C(w_{i-1}, w_i)$ is the count of the bigram $(w_{i-1}, w_i)$ in the training corpus, and $C(w_{i-1})$ is the count of the unigram $w_{i-1}$ (the denominator normalizes over all possible successors of $w_{i-1}$). **(3 marks)**

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Pattern]**
> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the chain rule of probability and how the Markov Assumption is applied to derive Unigram, Bigram, and Trigram language models.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Consider the following training corpus:
> `<s> John read a book </s>`
> `<s> Mary read a novel </s>`
> `<s> John read a novel </s>`
> Compute the probability of the sentence "John read a novel" using a Bigram Language Model with Laplace (Add-1) smoothing. Assume the vocabulary is $\mathcal{V} = \{John, read, a, book, novel, Mary\}$ and the count of $\langle s \rangle$ is 3.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) [7 marks] — Full step-by-step answer:**

**Step 1 — Chain Rule of Probability (2 marks):**
The chain rule factorizes the joint probability of a word sequence into a product of conditional probabilities:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1})$$

This is an *exact* identity from probability theory. Each term $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ represents the probability of word $w_i$ given its full left history. The problem is that the conditioning context grows linearly with sentence length, making direct estimation impossible for any realistic corpus.

**Step 2 — Markov Assumption (2 marks):**
The $(N-1)$-th order Markov Assumption approximates each conditional probability using only the most recent $N-1$ words:

$$P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}, \dots, w_{i-1})$$

The future is conditionally independent of the distant past, given the recent local context.

**Step 3 — Apply to Unigram, Bigram, Trigram (3 marks):**

- **Unigram (N=1, 0th-order Markov):** $P(w_1^n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C(w_i)}{N_{\text{total}}}$

- **Bigram (N=2, 1st-order Markov):** $P(w_1^n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$

- **Trigram (N=3, 2nd-order Markov):** $P(w_1^n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-2}, w_{i-1}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C(w_{i-2}, w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})}$

**Part (b) [7 marks] — Full computation:**

**Step 1 — Tokenize all three sentences with BOS/EOS:**
- S1: $\langle s \rangle$, John, read, a, book, $\langle /s \rangle$
- S2: $\langle s \rangle$, Mary, read, a, novel, $\langle /s \rangle$
- S3: $\langle s \rangle$, John, read, a, novel, $\langle /s \rangle$

**Step 2 — Extract all bigrams and count:**

| Bigram | Count |
|---|---|
| $(\langle s \rangle, John)$ | 2 |
| $(\langle s \rangle, Mary)$ | 1 |
| $(John, read)$ | 2 |
| $(Mary, read)$ | 1 |
| $(read, a)$ | 3 |
| $(a, book)$ | 1 |
| $(a, novel)$ | 2 |
| $(book, \langle /s \rangle)$ | 1 |
| $(novel, \langle /s \rangle)$ | 2 |

**Step 3 — Apply Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing Formula:**

$$P_{\text{Laplace}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1}{C(w_{i-1}) + |\mathcal{V}|}$$

**Vocabulary size:** $|\mathcal{V}| = 6$ (John, read, a, book, novel, Mary)

**Step 4 — Compute each bigram probability for the target sentence "John read a novel":**

$$P(John \mid \langle s \rangle) = \frac{C(\langle s \rangle, John) + 1}{C(\langle s \rangle) + 6} = \frac{2 + 1}{3 + 6} = \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$P(read \mid John) = \frac{C(John, read) + 1}{C(John) + 6} = \frac{2 + 1}{2 + 6} = \frac{3}{8}$$

$$P(a \mid read) = \frac{C(read, a) + 1}{C(read) + 6} = \frac{3 + 1}{3 + 6} = \frac{4}{9}$$

$$P(novel \mid a) = \frac{C(a, novel) + 1}{C(a) + 6} = \frac{2 + 1}{3 + 6} = \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$P(\langle /s \rangle \mid novel) = \frac{C(novel, \langle /s \rangle) + 1}{C(novel) + 6} = \frac{2 + 1}{2 + 6} = \frac{3}{8}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Final Sentence Probability:**

$$P(\text{John read a novel}) = \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{3}{8} \times \frac{4}{9} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{3}{8}$$

$$= \frac{1 \times 3 \times 4 \times 1 \times 3}{3 \times 8 \times 9 \times 3 \times 8} = \frac{36}{5184} = \frac{1}{144} \approx 0.00694$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** With Add-1 smoothing, *no* bigram can have zero probability, so even completely unseen bigrams receive a non-zero mass. This is the primary advantage of Laplace smoothing demonstrated here.

---

> **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Pattern]**
> **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Bigram Language Model. Discuss the role of the start token $\langle s \rangle$ and end token $\langle /s \rangle$ in training and evaluation.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Given the training corpus:
> `<s> the cat sat on the mat </s>`
> `<s> the dog sat on the rug </s>`
> `<s> the cat sat on the rug </s>`
> Compute the probability of the unseen test sentence "the cat sat on the mat" using a **Trigram Language Model with Add-1 (Laplace) smoothing**. Vocabulary $\mathcal{V} = \{the, cat, sat, on, mat, dog, rug\}$. Use $\langle s \rangle, \langle s \rangle$ for the first trigram context.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) [7 marks] — Architecture Explanation:**

A **Bigram Language Model** operates in two phases: **Training** and **Inference**.

**Training Phase (4 marks):**
1. The raw corpus is first tokenized, with each sentence prefixed by the start symbol $\langle s \rangle$ and suffixed by the end symbol $\langle /s \rangle$.
2. A sliding window of size 2 produces all bigrams $(w_{i-1}, w_i)$.
3. The MLE estimator computes $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-1}, w_i) / C(w_{i-1})$.
4. The output is a sparse probability table indexed by all observed bigram pairs.

**Inference Phase (3 marks):**
1. The test sentence is similarly wrapped with $\langle s \rangle$ and $\langle /s \rangle$.
2. The bigram chain rule is applied: $P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$.
3. The product of conditional probabilities yields the final sentence score.

**Role of special tokens (continued):**
- $\langle s \rangle$ provides a uniform starting context so that $P(w_1 \mid \langle s \rangle)$ is well-defined.
- $\langle /s \rangle$ enables the model to learn sentence-ending patterns and prevents infinite text generation. Without $\langle /s \rangle$, the model would assign zero probability to sentence termination, leading to issues in text generation and evaluation.

**Part (b) [7 marks] — Trigram Computation:**

**Step 1 — Tokenize the 3 training sentences:**

- S1: $\langle s \rangle, \langle s \rangle, the, cat, sat, on, the, mat, \langle /s \rangle$
- S2: $\langle s \rangle, \langle s \rangle, the, dog, sat, on, the, rug, \langle /s \rangle$
- S3: $\langle s \rangle, \langle s \rangle, the, cat, sat, on, the, rug, \langle /s \rangle$

**Step 2 — Extract all trigrams and their counts:**

| Trigram | Count |
|---|---|
| $(\langle s \rangle, \langle s \rangle, the)$ | 3 |
| $(\langle s \rangle, the, cat)$ | 2 |
| $(\langle s \rangle, the, dog)$ | 1 |
| $(the, cat, sat)$ | 2 |
| $(the, dog, sat)$ | 1 |
| $(cat, sat, on)$ | 2 |
| $(dog, sat, on)$ | 1 |
| $(sat, on, the)$ | 3 |
| $(on, the, mat)$ | 1 |
| $(on, the, rug)$ | 2 |
| $(the, mat, \langle /s \rangle)$ | 1 |
| $(the, rug, \langle /s \rangle)$ | 2 |

**Step 3 — Extract bigram context counts (denominators):**

| Bigram | Count |
|---|---|
| $(\langle s \rangle, \langle s \rangle)$ | 3 |
| $(\langle s \rangle, the)$ | 3 |
| $(the, cat)$ | 2 |
| $(the, dog)$ | 1 |
| $(cat, sat)$ | 2 |
| $(dog, sat)$ | 1 |
| $(sat, on)$ | 3 |
| $(on, the)$ | 3 |
| $(the, mat)$ | 1 |
| $(the, rug)$ | 2 |

**Step 4 — Apply Trigram Add-1 Laplace Smoothing Formula:**

$$P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) = \frac{C(w_1, w_2, w_3) + 1}{C(w_1, w_2) + |\mathcal{V}|}$$

**Vocabulary size:** $|\mathcal{V}| = 7$ (the, cat, sat, on, mat, dog, rug)

**Step 5 — Compute trigram probabilities for the test sentence "the cat sat on the mat":**

$$P(the \mid \langle s \rangle, \langle s \rangle) = \frac{3 + 1}{3 + 7} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4$$

$$P(cat \mid \langle s \rangle, the) = \frac{2 + 1}{3 + 7} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.3$$

$$P(sat \mid the, cat) = \frac{2 + 1}{2 + 7} = \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

$$P(on \mid cat, sat) = \frac{2 + 1}{2 + 7} = \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

$$P(the \mid sat, on) = \frac{3 + 1}{3 + 7} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4$$

$$P(mat \mid on, the) = \frac{1 + 1}{3 + 7} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.2$$

$$P(\langle /s \rangle \mid the, mat) = \frac{1 + 1}{1 + 7} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25$$

**Step 6 — Compute Final Sentence Probability:**

$$P(W) = 0.4 \times 0.3 \times 0.333 \times 0.333 \times 0.4 \times 0.2 \times 0.25$$

$$P(W) = \frac{4 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3 \times 4 \times 2 \times 2}{10 \times 10 \times 9 \times 9 \times 10 \times 10 \times 8} \times \text{unit correction}$$

Computing numerator and denominator:

- Numerator: $4 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3 \times 4 \times 2 \times 2 = 1728$
- Denominator: $10 \times 10 \times 9 \times 9 \times 10 \times 10 \times 8 = 6{,}480{,}000$

$$P(W) = \frac{1728}{6{,}480{,}000} = \frac{1}{3750} \approx 2.667 \times 10^{-4}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the start tokens:** Students frequently write $P(the \mid \langle s \rangle)$ for the first word of a trigram context, but the correct conditioning context is **two** start tokens: $P(the \mid \langle s \rangle, \langle s \rangle)$. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Ignoring the end token:** Failing to multiply by $P(\langle /s \rangle \mid w_{n-1}, w_n)$ at the end. Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong smoothing formula:** Writing $\frac{C + 1}{C}$ instead of $\frac{C + 1}{C + |\mathcal{V}|}$. This is a fatal error worth **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Misreading the corpus:** Students sometimes omit the start/end markers when counting trigrams. The trigram $(John, read, a)$ is different from $(\langle s \rangle, John, read)$. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping intermediate steps:** The KTU board requires showing each bigram count and each trigram count explicitly. A single final answer without the working table typically loses 2–3 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Language Model:** A probabilistic model assigning $P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$ to word sequences.
- **Chain Rule (Exact):** $P(w_1^n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$. The *exact* decomposition — used as the theoretical starting point.
- **Markov Assumption (Truncation):** $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})$. Truncates the conditioning history to a fixed window of $N-1$ words.
- **N-Gram:** A contiguous sequence of $N$ tokens. N-Gram models use the $(N-1)$-order Markov assumption.
- **Unigram (N=1):** $P(w_i) = C(w_i)/N_{\text{total}}$ — no context, simplest model.
- **Bigram (N=2):** $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-1}, w_i)/C(w_{i-1})$ — uses 1 preceding word.
- **Trigram (N=3):** $P(w_i \mid w_{i-2}, w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-2}, w_{i-1}, w_i)/C(w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})$ — uses 2 preceding words.
- **MLE Principle:** Probabilities are estimated as relative frequencies observed in the training corpus.
- **Sentence Probability:** Multiply all conditional bigram/trigram probabilities across the sentence (chain rule approximation).
- **Special Tokens:** Always use $\langle s \rangle$ (BOS) at the start and $\langle /s \rangle$ (EOS) at the end. For trigrams, use **two** start tokens.
- **Data Sparsity Problem:** Unseen N-grams get zero probability under MLE. Mention **smoothing techniques** (Laplace/Add-1, Add-k, Good-Turing, Kneser-Ney) in answers.
- **Laplace Smoothing Formula:** $P_{\text{Laplace}}(w_i \mid \cdot) = (C + 1)/(C_{\text{context}} + |\mathcal{V}|)$.
- **Log-Probability Trick:** $\log P(W) = \sum \log P(w_i \mid \cdot)$ — used to prevent numerical underflow.
- **Perplexity:** $PP(W) = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})\right)$ — lower is better.
- **Trade-off:** Larger N → richer context but sparser data; smaller N → more robust but weaker context. Bigrams are the most common practical choice.
- **Memory Complexity:** N-gram tables grow as $\approx |\mathcal{V}|^N$ — a key engineering limitation.
- **Real-World Applications:** Speech recognition disambiguation, machine translation reranking, autocorrect/predictive text, OCR post-processing, and text generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** For any N-gram probability problem, always show (1) the corpus with start/end tokens, (2) the explicit count table, (3) the chosen smoothing formula, (4) each individual conditional probability, and (5) the final product. Skipping any of these five steps costs marks. Mention the data sparsity issue and the role of smoothing in *every* long answer — it is one of the most commonly tested conceptual points in KTU NLP papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Language Model Implementation - Setup
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Language Model Implementation - Setup

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Language Model (LM) Implementation – Setup** refers to the systematic preparation of the computational environment, software libraries, datasets, and preliminary model configurations required before training, evaluating, or deploying a statistical/neural language model in Natural Language Processing (NLP).

A **Language Model (LM)** is a probabilistic model that assigns a probability distribution over sequences of words or tokens drawn from a vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$. The *setup phase* precedes the actual modeling and involves:

1. Hardware/Software environment configuration.
2. Corpus acquisition and preprocessing.
3. Tokenization and vocabulary construction.
4. Selection of modeling paradigm (n-gram, RNN, Transformer).
5. Hyperparameter initialization.

Mathematically, an LM computes:

$$P(w_1, w_2, w_3, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1})$$

This is the **chain rule of probability** applied to text — the foundation upon which every setup decision is made.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Theater Stage:**
> Imagine you are staging a Shakespearean play. Before the actors can perform, you must:
> - **Build the stage** → Install Python, PyTorch, TensorFlow (software environment).
> - **Gather the script** → Collect the corpus (text dataset).
> - **Learn the vocabulary** → Tokenize the script and build a word index.
> - **Cast the actors** → Choose the model architecture (n-gram, LSTM, BERT).
> - **Rehearse the lines** → Configure hyperparameters and train.

The "setup" in LM implementation is exactly this preparatory staging. If the stage is poorly built, even the best actors (algorithms) will fail.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Corpus** | A large structured collection of text used for training | Typically $\geq$ **10M tokens** for statistical LMs |
| **Vocabulary $\vert\mathcal{V}\vert$** | Set of unique tokens in the corpus | **10K – 500K** for English |
| **Token** | Atomic unit of text (word, subword, or character) | BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece |
| **Perplexity (PP)** | Standard intrinsic evaluation metric | Lower is better; **1 = perfect** |
| **OOV Rate** | Out-of-Vocabulary word frequency | Target $\lt$ **2%** |
| **Sequence Length $n$** | Number of tokens in a sample | Typically **32 – 512** |
| **Batch Size** | Samples processed per gradient update | **16 – 256** |
| **Learning Rate $\eta$** | Step size for weight updates | **1e-3 to 1e-5** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phased Breakdown of LM Setup

The setup is divided into **five logical phases**. Each phase directly influences model quality.

#### Phase 1 — Environment Initialization
- Install Python interpreter (version $\geq$ 3.8).
- Set up a virtual environment (`venv` or `conda`).
- Install core NLP libraries.
- Configure GPU acceleration (CUDA / ROCm) if available.

#### Phase 2 — Corpus Acquisition & Cleaning
- Collect raw text (Wikipedia, Common Crawl, domain corpora).
- Remove HTML tags, duplicate lines, non-textual noise.
- Normalize: lowercasing, Unicode normalization, whitespace stripping.

#### Phase 3 — Tokenization & Vocabulary Construction
- Split text into tokens.
- Apply subword algorithms: **BPE (Byte Pair Encoding)**, **WordPiece**, **SentencePiece**.
- Build vocabulary index `token → integer ID`.

#### Phase 4 — Train/Validation/Test Split
- Standard split ratio: **80% / 10% / 10%** (or **98/1/1** for large corpora).
- Ensure temporal or topical separation to avoid data leakage.

#### Phase 5 — Model Architecture & Hyperparameter Configuration
- Choose paradigm (n-gram, FFNN, RNN/LSTM, Transformer).
- Initialize embedding dimensions, hidden layers, dropout rates.
- Set optimizer (Adam, SGD), loss function (Cross-Entropy).

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$P(w_1, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$ | Chain rule — joint probability of a sequence | $w_i$ = i-th word |
| 2 | $$P(w_i \mid w_{<i}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}, \dots, w_{i-n+1})$$ | Markov assumption (n-gram) | $n$ = context window |
| 3 | $$H(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$ | Cross-entropy loss in bits | $N$ = total tokens |
| 4 | $$\mathrm{PP}(W) = 2^{H(W)} = P(w_1 \dots w_N)^{-1/N}$$ | Perplexity (lower = better LM) | — |
| 5 | $$P_{\text{Lap}}(w_i \mid c) = \frac{C(c, w_i) + 1}{C(c) + \vert \mathcal{V} \vert}$$ | Laplace (add-1) smoothing | $C(\cdot)$ = count |
| 6 | $$P_{\text{Backoff}}(w_i \mid c) = \begin{cases} P^*(w_i \mid c) & \text{if } C(c w_i) > 0 \\ \alpha(c) \cdot P_{\text{Backoff}}(w_i \mid c') & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ | Katz backoff | $\alpha$ = discount weight |
| 7 | $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$$ | Gradient descent update | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| 8 | $$\vert \mathcal{V} \vert_{\text{UNK}} = 1$$ | UNK token for OOV replacement | — |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL: Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value in tables (e.g., `\vert \mathcal{V} \vert`) — never the literal `|` pipe — to preserve markdown table integrity.**

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines** (Google, Bing): n-gram LMs for query autocompletion and ranking.
- **Virtual Assistants** (Alexa, Siri): neural LMs for intent classification and response generation.
- **Machine Translation**: LMs score translation fluency.
- **Speech Recognition**: acoustic + language model scoring via weighted finite-state transducers.
- **Code Completion Tools** (Copilot, Tabnine): large Transformer LMs fine-tuned on source code.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Joint Probability to Perplexity

We derive the perplexity metric starting from first principles, since it is the most-tested formula in KTU ESE for this module.

**Step 1 — Start with the joint probability of a sentence $W = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)$:**

$$P(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule of probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(W) &= P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_N \mid w_1, \dots, w_{N-1}) \\
     &= \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the logarithm (to convert products to sums, improving numerical stability):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log P(W) &= \log \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{<i}) \\
          &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Define cross-entropy (average negative log-likelihood):**

$$
H(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{<i})
$$

The negative sign ensures $H$ is positive (since $0 \lt P \leq 1 \Rightarrow \log P \leq 0$). The base-2 logarithm gives the result in **bits**.

**Step 5 — Exponentiate to obtain perplexity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{PP}(W) &= 2^{H(W)} \\
              &= 2^{-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{<i})} \\
              &= \left( 2^{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{<i})} \right)^{-1/N} \\
              &= \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} 2^{\log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{<i})} \right)^{-1/N} \\
              &= \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{<i}) \right)^{-1/N} \\
              &= P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)^{-1/N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Perplexity equals the **effective branching factor** — the average number of choices the model is "confused" among at each step. A perplexity of 50 means the model is as perplexed as if choosing uniformly among 50 possibilities.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A unigram LM assigns $P(\text{the}) = 0.05$, $P(\text{cat}) = 0.02$, $P(\text{sat}) = 0.01$. Compute the perplexity of the sentence "the cat sat".

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute joint probability:**

$$P(\text{the cat sat}) = 0.05 \times 0.02 \times 0.01 = 1 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 2 — Apply perplexity formula with $N = 3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{PP} &= (1 \times 10^{-5})^{-1/3} \\
            &= (10^{-5})^{-1/3} \\
            &= 10^{5/3} \\
            &= 10^{1.6667} \\
            &\approx 46.42
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification using cross-entropy route:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= -\frac{1}{3}\left[\log_2(0.05) + \log_2(0.02) + \log_2(0.01)\right] \\
  &= -\frac{1}{3}\left[(-4.322) + (-5.644) + (-6.644)\right] \\
  &= -\frac{1}{3}(-16.610) = 5.537 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{PP} &= 2^{5.537} = 46.42 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Tokenization Choice

| Tokenization Method | Vocabulary Size | OOV Handling | Computational Cost | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Word-level** | $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ large (~500K) | Poor (UNK substitution) | Low | High-resource languages |
| **Character-level** | $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ small (~100) | Excellent | Very High | Morphologically rich languages |
| **BPE** | Medium (30K–50K) | Excellent | Medium | Transformer LMs (GPT) |
| **WordPiece** | Medium (30K) | Excellent | Medium | BERT-family models |
| **SentencePiece** | Configurable (8K–64K) | Excellent | Medium | Multilingual models |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LM Implementation Setup — End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Environment Setup] --> A1[Install Python 3.8+]
    A1 --> A2[Create Virtual Environment]
    A2 --> A3[Install Libraries: Numpy, NLTK, PyTorch]
    A3 --> A4[Configure GPU CUDA]
    A4 --> B[Phase 2: Corpus Acquisition]
    B --> B1[Download Raw Text]
    B1 --> B2[Clean and Normalize]
    B2 --> B3[Remove Duplicates and Noise]
    B3 --> C[Phase 3: Tokenization]
    C --> C1[Choose Tokenizer BPE or WordPiece]
    C1 --> C2[Build Vocabulary]
    C2 --> C3[Map Tokens to IDs]
    C3 --> D[Phase 4: Data Splitting]
    D --> D1[Train Set 80 percent]
    D1 --> D2[Validation Set 10 percent]
    D2 --> D3[Test Set 10 percent]
    D3 --> E[Phase 5: Model Configuration]
    E --> E1[Select Architecture: ngram or Transformer]
    E1 --> E2[Initialize Hyperparameters]
    E2 --> E3[Define Loss Function: Cross Entropy]
    E3 --> F[Ready for Training Phase]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — LM Setup Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EnvBlock[Environment Layer]
        env1[Python Interpreter]
        env2[Virtual Env venv]
        env3[GPU Driver CUDA]
    end
    subgraph DataBlock[Data Layer]
        dat1[Raw Corpus]
        dat2[Preprocessor Cleaner]
        dat3[Tokenizer BPE]
        dat4[Vocabulary Indexer]
    end
    subgraph ModelBlock[Model Layer]
        mod1[Embedding Matrix]
        mod2[Neural Network Backbone]
        mod3[Output Softmax Layer]
    end
    subgraph TrainBlock[Training Layer]
        trn1[Optimizer Adam]
        trn2[Loss Cross Entropy]
        trn3[Scheduler LR Decay]
    end
    EnvBlock --> DataBlock
    DataBlock --> ModelBlock
    ModelBlock --> TrainBlock
    TrainBlock -->|Backprop| ModelBlock
```

### 4.3 Tokenization Data Flow (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawText[Raw Sentence: The cats sat] --> split[Split on Whitespace]
    split --> tok1[The]
    split --> tok2[cats]
    split --> tok3[sat]
    tok1 --> vocabLookup[Vocabulary Lookup]
    tok2 --> vocabLookup
    tok3 --> vocabLookup
    vocabLookup --> id1[ID 12]
    vocabLookup --> id2[ID 87]
    vocabLookup --> id3[ID 256]
    id1 --> tensor[Token Tensor shape 1x3]
    id2 --> tensor
    id3 --> tensor
    tensor --> model[LM Input]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Compilation Notes:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `env1`, `dat2`, `mod3`) to comply with Mermaid safety rules. No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as standalone identifiers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**Define a Language Model. List the five phases of LM Implementation Setup.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Language Model (LM)** is a probabilistic model that computes the probability distribution over sequences of words, $P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$, where each $w_i \in \mathcal{V}$ (vocabulary). (1 mark)

The **five phases of LM implementation setup** are:
1. **Environment Initialization** — installing Python, libraries, GPU drivers. (0.5)
2. **Corpus Acquisition & Cleaning** — gathering and normalizing raw text. (0.5)
3. **Tokenization & Vocabulary Construction** — converting text to token IDs. (0.5)
4. **Train/Validation/Test Splitting** — partitioning data 80/10/10. (0.5)

5. **Model Architecture & Hyperparameter Configuration** — choosing n-gram, RNN, or Transformer and initializing parameters. (0.5 = 1 if all five phases listed)

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**What is Perplexity? Why is it preferred over raw probability for evaluating LMs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Perplexity is the geometric mean of the inverse probabilities of a sequence, given by:

$$\mathrm{PP}(W) = \left[ \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid w_{<i})} \right]^{1/N} = P(w_1 \dots w_N)^{-1/N}$$

**Why preferred (1.5 marks):**
- **Length normalization:** Raw probability $P(W)$ decreases exponentially with sequence length $N$, making cross-sentence comparison meaningless. Perplexity normalizes by $N$. (0.5)
- **Interpretability:** Perplexity is the *effective branching factor* — interpretable as "average number of choices the model is confused among." (0.5)
- **Numerical stability:** Computed via $\log$ space, avoiding underflow on long sequences. (0.5)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain in detail the five phases of **Language Model Implementation Setup**. Describe the role of **tokenization** and **vocabulary construction** in this process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bigram language model is trained on the following corpus:

> *"the cat sat on the mat the cat ate the rat"*

Compute the bigram probabilities using **Laplace (add-1) smoothing**. Also compute the **perplexity** of the test sentence *"the cat sat on the rat"*. Take $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 7$ (`the, cat, sat, on, mat, ate, rat`). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Phase 1: Environment Initialization (1 mark)**
Install Python $\geq$ 3.8, create a virtual environment, install libraries (NumPy, NLTK, PyTorch, Transformers), and configure GPU (CUDA) for accelerated training. Without a stable environment, reproducibility is compromised.

**Phase 2: Corpus Acquisition & Cleaning (1 mark)**
Gather a large text corpus (e.g., Wikipedia dump, Project Gutenberg). Clean by removing HTML, normalizing Unicode, lowercasing, and eliminating duplicates. *Output:* a clean, plain-text file.

**Phase 3: Tokenization & Vocabulary Construction (2 marks)**
Tokenization splits text into atomic units (tokens). For the sentence *"The cat sat"*:

$$
\text{Input} \rightarrow \text{Tokenize} \rightarrow [\text{The}, \text{cat}, \text{sat}] \rightarrow \text{IDs} \rightarrow [12, 87, 256]
$$

The vocabulary index maps each unique token to a unique integer. **Role:**
- Converts unstructured text into a numeric tensor that neural/statistical models can process.
- Determines $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$, which directly affects model size, OOV rate, and computational cost.
- Subword tokenizers (BPE, WordPiece) handle OOV words by decomposing them into smaller known pieces.

**Phase 4: Train/Validation/Test Split (1 mark)**
Split ratio: typically **80% / 10% / 10%**. Validation set is used for hyperparameter tuning; test set is held out for final evaluation. Critical to avoid **data leakage**.

**Phase 5: Model Architecture & Hyperparameter Configuration (2 marks)**
Choose paradigm (n-gram, FFNN, RNN, LSTM, Transformer). Initialize:
- Embedding dimension $d$ (e.g., **128, 256, 768**)
- Hidden layer size, number of layers
- Optimizer (Adam, $\eta = 1\text{e-}3$), loss (Cross-Entropy), batch size (e.g., **32**)
- Vocabulary size $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$, sequence length $n$

A neat block diagram has been provided in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Extract bigrams and counts (1 mark):**

| Bigram | Count |
|---|---|
| (the, cat) | 2 |
| (cat, sat) | 1 |
| (sat, on) | 1 |
| (on, the) | 1 |
| (the, mat) | 1 |
| (cat, ate) | 1 |
| (ate, the) | 1 |
| (the, rat) | 1 |

**Unigram prefix counts:** the: 4, cat: 2, sat: 1, on: 1, ate: 1

**Step 2 — Apply Laplace smoothing (2 marks):**

$$
P_{\text{Lap}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1}{C(w_{i-1}) + \vert \mathcal{V} \vert}
$$

With $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 7$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) &= \frac{2+1}{4+7} = \frac{3}{11} \approx 0.2727 \\
P(\text{sat} \mid \text{cat}) &= \frac{1+1}{2+7} = \frac{2}{9} \approx 0.2222 \\
P(\text{on} \mid \text{sat}) &= \frac{1+1}{1+7} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.2500 \\
P(\text{the} \mid \text{on}) &= \frac{1+1}{1+7} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.2500 \\
P(\text{rat} \mid \text{the}) &= \frac{1+1}{4+7} = \frac{2}{11} \approx 0.1818
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute joint probability of test sentence *"the cat sat on the rat"* (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{the cat sat on the rat}) &= 0.2727 \times 0.2222 \times 0.2500 \times 0.2500 \times 0.1818 \\
&\approx 0.000689
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute perplexity with $N = 5$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{PP} &= (P(W))^{-1/N} \\
           &= (0.000689)^{-1/5} \\
           &= (6.89 \times 10^{-4})^{-0.2} \\
           &= 10^{0.2 \times 3.162} \\
           &= 10^{0.6324} \\
           &\approx 4.29
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification via cross-entropy (1 mark):**

$$
H = -\frac{1}{5}\left[\log_2(0.2727) + \log_2(0.2222) + \log_2(0.2500) + \log_2(0.2500) + \log_2(0.1818)\right]
$$

$$
H = -\frac{1}{5}\left[(-1.874) + (-2.170) + (-2.000) + (-2.000) + (-2.459)\right] = -\frac{-10.503}{5} = 2.101 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
\mathrm{PP} = 2^{2.101} \approx 4.29 \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]` — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **tokenization, vocabulary, and OOV (Out-of-Vocabulary) words** in the context of language model setup. Compare **word-level, character-level, and subword (BPE)** tokenization strategies with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A trigram LM is built from the corpus: *"I love NLP I love AI I love coding"*. Compute the trigram probability of *"I love NLP"* using **raw maximum likelihood estimation** (no smoothing). Explain why a smoothing technique becomes necessary. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Tokenization (1.5 marks):** The process of segmenting raw text into smaller atomic units (tokens) — words, subwords, or characters. Example: *"unhappiness"* → `["un", "happiness"]` (BPE).

**Vocabulary (1.5 marks):** A finite set $\mathcal{V}$ of all unique tokens encountered (or pre-defined) in the training corpus, each mapped to a unique integer index. Size $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ is a critical hyperparameter.

**OOV Words (1 mark):** Tokens appearing in test data but absent from training vocabulary. Handled via:
- Replace with `<UNK>` token (loss of information).
- Subword decomposition (BPE): any word can be broken into known sub-pieces.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Word-level | Character-level | BPE (Subword) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Whole words | Individual characters | Frequent subword units |
| $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ | Very large | Very small (~100) | Medium (30K–50K) |
| OOV Handling | Poor (UNK) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Example: *"lowering"* | `["lowering"]` | `["l","o","w","e","r","i","n","g"]` | `["low", "er", "ing"]` |
| Sequence Length | Short | Very long | Medium |
| Used in | Classical n-gram LMs | ByT5, CANINE | GPT, BERT, LLaMA |

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Tokenize the corpus (0.5 mark):**
`[I, love, NLP, I, love, AI, I, love, coding]`

**Step 2 — Extract trigrams (1 mark):**

| Trigram | Count |
|---|---|
| (I, love, NLP) | 1 |
| (love, NLP, I) | 0 |
| (I, love, AI) | 1 |
| (love, AI, I) | 0 |
| (I, love, coding) | 1 |
| (love, coding, _) | 0 (sentence end) |

**Step 3 — Apply Maximum Likelihood Estimation (2 marks):**

$$
P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) = \frac{C(w_1, w_2, w_3)}{C(w_1, w_2)}
$$

Bigram prefix counts:
- $C(\text{I, love}) = 3$ (followed by NLP, AI, coding)
- $C(\text{love, NLP}) = 1$
- $C(\text{love, AI}) = 1$
- $C(\text{love, coding}) = 1$

$$
P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{I, love}) = \frac{C(\text{I, love, NLP})}{C(\text{I, love})} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
$$

**Step 4 — Explain the data sparsity problem (2.5 marks):**

With raw MLE, any trigram never observed in training receives probability **0**. This causes:
- **Zero probability test sequences:** $\mathrm{PP} \to \infty$ if any unseen n-gram appears.
- **Poor generalization:** The model cannot assign even a small probability to plausible novel combinations.

**Smoothing remedies this by redistributing probability mass:**
- **Laplace (add-1):** Adds 1 to every count.
- **Add-k smoothing:** Adds $k \in (0,1)$ — less aggressive.
- **Katz backoff:** Falls back to lower-order n-gram for unseen events.
- **Kneser-Ney:** Most effective; accounts for continuation probability.

Mathematically, Laplace smoothing guarantees $P(w_i \mid c) > 0$ for all $w_i$:

$$P_{\text{Lap}}(w_i \mid c) = \frac{C(c, w_i) + 1}{C(c) + \vert \mathcal{V} \vert} > 0$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in LM Setup Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ in the Laplace denominator** — students often write $\frac{C(c,w)+1}{C(c)}$ instead of $\frac{C(c,w)+1}{C(c) + \vert \mathcal{V} \vert}$. This costs **2 full marks**.
> 2. **Confusing Perplexity formula** — using $P(W)^{-N}$ instead of $P(W)^{-1/N}$ is a critical formula error. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the chain rule derivation** — Part (a) "explain perplexity" questions require showing the chain-rule-to-perplexity derivation; merely stating the formula earns only **1 of 3 marks**.
> 4. **Not normalizing by $N$** in cross-entropy — losing 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring OOV in BPE questions** — examiners specifically look for the OOV-handling comparison.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LM Setup = 5 phases:** Environment → Corpus → Tokenization → Split → Configuration. Memorize this order.
- **Chain rule foundation:** Always start with $\prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$ for any LM probability question.
- **Perplexity formula:** $\mathrm{PP}(W) = P(W)^{-1/N} = 2^{H(W)}$. Lower is better; **PP = 1 means perfect**.
- **Laplace smoothing denominator:** $C(c) + \vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ — never forget $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$.
- **Standard split:** **80% / 10% / 10%**; for large corpora (Wikipedia-scale), **98/1/1** is acceptable.
- **Tokenization trade-off:** Word-level → large vocab + OOV issues; Character-level → small vocab + long sequences; BPE → balanced, industry standard.
- **OOV handling:** Replace with `<UNK>` (simple) or use subword tokenization (preferred).
- **Default hyperparameters to remember:** $\eta = 1\text{e-}3$ (Adam), batch size = 32, embedding dim = 128/256/768.
- **MLE problem:** Always produces zero probabilities for unseen n-grams — hence the need for **smoothing**.
- **Hardware check:** Always verify `torch.cuda.is_available()` (or TF GPU detection) before training; document your random seed for reproducibility.
- **Cross-entropy units:** Use $\log_2$ for bits, $\ln$ for nats — KTU prefers **bits**.
- **KTU-favorite trick question:** *"Why is $\mathrm{PP} \geq 1$ always?"* — Answer: Because $P(W) \leq 1$, so $P(W)^{-1/N} \geq 1$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ngrams Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ngrams Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **N-Gram (Definition):** An **N-gram** is a contiguous sequence of **N** items extracted from a given text or speech corpus. The "items" can be characters, syllables, words, or even sub-words, depending on the granularity chosen. N-grams are the foundational building blocks of **Statistical Language Models (SLMs)** in Natural Language Processing.

In the context of **word-level N-grams**, the sequence length **N** dictates the type:
- **N = 1** → **Unigram** (single token)
- **N = 2** → **Bigram** (two adjacent tokens)
- **N = 3** → **Trigram** (three adjacent tokens)
- **N = 4** → **Four-gram** / **Quadgram**
- **N ≥ 5** → Higher-order N-grams

Mathematically, given a tokenized sentence $S = (w_1, w_2, w_3, \dots, w_m)$, the set of all N-grams is:

$$
\text{NGram}(S, N) = \{(w_i, w_{i+1}, \dots, w_{i+N-1}) \mid 1 \le i \le m - N + 1\}
$$

The **N-gram Probability Function** estimates the likelihood of observing a word $w_n$ given the preceding $(N-1)$ words:

$$
P(w_n \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{n-1}) \approx P(w_n \mid w_{n-N+1}, \dots, w_{n-1})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 explicitly focuses on **N-gram language modeling**, **smoothing**, and **evaluation metrics** such as **Perplexity**. Mastery of the Markov Assumption and Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) is non-negotiable for board exams.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are reading a novel and someone covers the next word with a sticky note. To guess the hidden word, your brain scans the **previous few words** (the immediate context) to make a probabilistic guess. N-gram models do **exactly the same thing** — they predict the next word by looking back at a window of the previous $N-1$ words.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Sliding Window:**
> Think of a sentence as a long train, and your eyes are a small **sliding window of size N**. As the window slides one compartment (word) at a time, it captures a snapshot of $N$ consecutive words. Each snapshot is one N-gram. The model then memorizes *which sequences of $N$ words tend to appear together*, just like memorizing common phrases in everyday speech ("how are **you**", "thank **you very much**", "United **States of America**").

### Why N-grams Matter in NLP

| Aspect | Role of N-grams |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Simplicity** | Counts-based; no neural networks required at the base level |
| **Efficiency** | Linear-time computation $O(m)$ over a sentence |
| **Interpretability** | Probabilities have clear, explainable meanings |
| **Foundation** | Forms the base for advanced models (RNNs, Transformers) |
| **Versatility** | Used in translation, speech, autocomplete, spam detection |

---

## 1.3 Special Tokens — Boundary Padding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Raw sentences lack explicit start/end markers. N-gram models use **sentence boundary tokens** to handle the first and last words properly:
> - **\<s\>** → Start-of-sentence token
> - **\</s\>** → End-of-sentence token

**Example with a trigram model (N=3) on the sentence "I love NLP":**
Padded → **\<s\> \<s\> I love NLP \</s\>**
This allows the model to compute P(I | \<s\>, \<s\>), P(NLP | I, love), and P(\</s\> | love, NLP).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Markov Assumption — The Heart of N-Grams

The **Chain Rule of Probability** expresses the joint probability of a sequence as:

$$
P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_m) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1})
$$

Directly computing this is **infeasible** because:
1. The conditioning context grows unboundedly with sentence length.
2. Most long histories never occur in training data (data sparsity).

> [!NOTE]
> **Markov Assumption:** The probability of the next word depends **only on the preceding (N-1) words**, not the entire history. This is a first-order (or higher-order, depending on N) Markov chain.

**Bigram Markov Assumption (N=2):**

$$
P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})
$$

**Trigram Markov Assumption (N=3):**

$$
P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})
$$

**Generalized N-gram Markov Assumption:**

$$
P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}, \dots, w_{i-1})
$$

Applying this assumption to the chain rule yields:

$$
P(w_1, \dots, w_m) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}, \dots, w_{i-1})
$$

where we define $w_j = \langle s \rangle$ for $j \le 0$.

---

## 2.2 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

The **MLE** principle says: choose probabilities that **maximize the likelihood of observing the training corpus**. For N-grams, this reduces to **relative frequency counts**:

$$
P(w_n \mid w_{n-N+1}, \dots, w_{n-1}) = \frac{C(w_{n-N+1}, \dots, w_{n-1}, w_n)}{C(w_{n-N+1}, \dots, w_{n-1})}
$$

where $C(\cdot)$ denotes the **raw count** of the N-gram in the training corpus.

### Worked Numerical Example

**Training Corpus (3 sentences):**
1. "I am Sam"
2. "Sam I am"
3. "I do not like green eggs and ham"

**Bigram Counts for the token "I":**
- C(I) = 3 (appears 3 times)
- C(I, am) = 2
- C(I, do) = 1

**Bigram Probability (MLE):**

$$
P(\text{am} \mid \text{I}) = \frac{C(\text{I}, \text{am})}{C(\text{I})} = \frac{2}{3}
$$

$$
P(\text{do} \mid \text{I}) = \frac{C(\text{I}, \text{do})}{C(\text{I})} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

---

## 2.3 Smoothing Techniques — The Sparsity Fix

> [!WARNING]
> **The Zero-Probability Problem:** If an N-gram never appeared in training, MLE assigns it **probability 0**. This makes the entire sentence probability zero, which is catastrophic for unseen data.

| Technique | Formula | Intuition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Laplace (Add-1)** | $P_L = \dfrac{C(X) + 1}{C(\text{context}) + V}$ | Pretend every unseen event was seen once |
| **Add-k** | $P_k = \dfrac{C(X) + k}{C(\text{context}) + kV}, \quad 0 < k < 1$ | Discount by fractional pseudo-count |
| **Good-Turing** | $P_{GT}(X) = \dfrac{(N_{c+1})}{N_c} \cdot \dfrac{1}{N}$ | Reassign probability mass from seen to unseen |
| **Kneser-Ney** | (Backoff + interpolation, advanced) | Most widely used in production systems |

where:
- $V$ = vocabulary size
- $N_c$ = number of N-grams seen exactly $c$ times
- $k$ = smoothing hyperparameter (typically 0.5–1)

### Laplace Smoothing Example (Bigram)

$$
P_{Laplace}(w_n \mid w_{n-1}) = \frac{C(w_{n-1}, w_n) + 1}{C(w_{n-1}) + V}
$$

If $V = 1000$, $C(\text{I}) = 3$, $C(\text{I}, \text{am}) = 2$:

$$
P_{Laplace}(\text{am} \mid \text{I}) = \frac{2 + 1}{3 + 1000} = \frac{3}{1003} \approx 0.00299
$$

---

## 2.4 Perplexity — The Evaluation Metric

> [!NOTE]
> **Perplexity (PP)** is the standard intrinsic evaluation metric for language models. Lower perplexity = better model.

For a test set of $M$ words:

$$
PP(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_M)^{-\frac{1}{M}} = \sqrt[M]{\frac{1}{P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_M)}}
$$

In log-space (numerically stable):

$$
PP(W) = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}, \dots, w_{i-1})\right)
$$

**Branch Factor Interpretation:** Perplexity is equivalent to the **weighted average branching factor** of the model's prediction tree.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table — it covers ~80% of N-gram numerical questions in KTU exams.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | N-gram extraction | $\{(w_i, \dots, w_{i+N-1}) \mid 1 \le i \le m-N+1\}$ | $m$: sentence length |
| 2 | Chain Rule | $P(w_1^m) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ | $w_1^{i-1}$: history |
| 3 | Markov Assumption | $P(w_i) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})$ | N-1 order context |
| 4 | MLE Bigram | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-1}, w_i)/C(w_{i-1})$ | Count-based |
| 5 | MLE N-gram | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1}) = C(w_{i-N+1}^{i})/C(w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})$ | General |
| 6 | Laplace Smoothing | $P_L = (C(X)+1)/(C(\text{ctx})+V)$ | $V$: vocabulary size |
| 7 | Add-k Smoothing | $P_k = (C(X)+k)/(C(\text{ctx})+kV)$ | $0 < k < 1$ |
| 8 | Perplexity | $PP = P(w_1^M)^{-1/M}$ | $M$: test tokens |
| 9 | Log-Perplexity | $PP = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{M}\sum \log P\right)$ | Numerically stable form |
| 10 | Entropy | $H(P) = -\sum P(x) \log_2 P(x)$ | Bits per symbol |
| 11 | Bigram Count | $\text{count} = m - 1$ per sentence of length $m$ | Pre-padded |
| 12 | Trigram Count | $\text{count} = m - 2$ per sentence of length $m$ | Pre-padded |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Bigram Probabilities from Scratch

> **Training Corpus (after tokenization and padding with \<s\>, \</s\>):**
> 1. \<s\> I am Sam \</s\>
> 2. \<s\> Sam I am \</s\>
> 3. \<s\> I do not like green eggs and ham \</s\>

### Step 1 — Extract All Bigrams

For Sentence 1: \<s\> I am Sam \</s\>
Bigrams: (\<s\>, I), (I, am), (am, Sam), (Sam, \</s\>)

For Sentence 2: \<s\> Sam I am \</s\>
Bigrams: (\<s\>, Sam), (Sam, I), (I, am), (am, \</s\>)

For Sentence 3: \<s\> I do not like green eggs and ham \</s\>
Bigrams: (\<s\>, I), (I, do), (do, not), (not, like), (like, green), (green, eggs), (eggs, and), (and, ham), (ham, \</s\>)

### Step 2 — Build the Count Table

| Bigram | Count |
| :--- | :---: |
| (\<s\>, I) | 2 |
| (\<s\>, Sam) | 1 |
| (I, am) | 2 |
| (I, do) | 1 |
| (am, Sam) | 1 |
| (am, \</s\>) | 1 |
| (Sam, \</s\>) | 1 |
| (Sam, I) | 1 |
| (do, not) | 1 |
| (not, like) | 1 |
| (like, green) | 1 |
| (green, eggs) | 1 |
| (eggs, and) | 1 |
| (and, ham) | 1 |
| (ham, \</s\>) | 1 |

### Step 3 — Compute Unigram (Context) Counts

| Unigram | Count |
| :--- | :---: |
| \<s\> | 3 |
| I | 3 |
| am | 2 |
| Sam | 2 |
| do | 1 |
| not | 1 |
| like | 1 |
| green | 1 |
| eggs | 1 |
| and | 1 |
| ham | 1 |
| \</s\> | 3 |

### Step 4 — Compute Selected Bigram Probabilities

$$
P(\text{am} \mid \text{I}) = \frac{C(\text{I}, \text{am})}{C(\text{I})} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667
$$

$$
P(\text{do} \mid \text{I}) = \frac{C(\text{I}, \text{do})}{C(\text{I})} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333
$$

$$
P(\text{Sam} \mid \text{am}) = \frac{C(\text{am}, \text{Sam})}{C(\text{am})} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5
$$

$$
P(\text{I} \mid \text{Sam}) = \frac{C(\text{Sam}, \text{I})}{C(\text{Sam})} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5
$$

### Step 5 — Verify Probability Sums

$$
P(\text{am} \mid \text{I}) + P(\text{do} \mid \text{I}) = \frac{2}{3} + \frac{1}{3} = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
P(\text{Sam} \mid \text{am}) + P(</s> \mid \text{am}) = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

The sum equals 1 for each context word — confirming the MLE estimator is properly normalized.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Sentence Probability using Chain Rule + Bigrams

**Test Sentence:** "I am Sam" → Padded: **\<s\> I am Sam \</s\>**

### Step 1 — Apply Chain Rule

$$
P(\text{<s> I am Sam </s>}) = P(\text{I} \mid \text{<s>}) \times P(\text{am} \mid \text{I}) \times P(\text{Sam} \mid \text{am}) \times P(\text{</s>} \mid \text{Sam})
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values from Count Table

$$
P(\text{I} \mid \text{<s>}) = \frac{C(\text{<s>}, \text{I})}{C(\text{<s>})} = \frac{2}{3}
$$

$$
P(\text{am} \mid \text{I}) = \frac{2}{3}
$$

$$
P(\text{Sam} \mid \text{am}) = \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
P(\text{</s>} \mid \text{Sam}) = \frac{1}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Multiply

$$
P(\text{Sentence}) = \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{4}{36} = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Log-Probability (Best Practice)

$$
\log_2 P = \log_2(2/3) + \log_2(2/3) + \log_2(1/2) + \log_2(1/2)
$$

$$
\log_2 P = (-0.585) + (-0.585) + (-1.0) + (-1.0) = -3.170 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
P = 2^{-3.170} \approx 0.1111 \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Laplace Smoothing Application

**Same sentence:** P("\<s\> I am Sam \</s\>") with $V = 13$ unique tokens and Add-1 smoothing.

### Step 1 — Apply Laplace Formula

$$
P_L(w_n \mid w_{n-1}) = \frac{C(w_{n-1}, w_n) + 1}{C(w_{n-1}) + V}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Each Smoothed Probability

$$
P_L(\text{I} \mid \text{<s>}) = \frac{2 + 1}{3 + 13} = \frac{3}{16} = 0.1875
$$

$$
P_L(\text{am} \mid \text{I}) = \frac{2 + 1}{3 + 13} = \frac{3}{16} = 0.1875
$$

$$
P_L(\text{Sam} \mid \text{am}) = \frac{1 + 1}{2 + 13} = \frac{2}{15} \approx 0.1333
$$

$$
P_L(\text{</s>} \mid \text{Sam}) = \frac{1 + 1}{2 + 13} = \frac{2}{15} \approx 0.1333
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Smoothed Sentence Probability

$$
P_L(\text{Sentence}) = 0.1875 \times 0.1875 \times 0.1333 \times 0.1333
$$

$$
P_L(\text{Sentence}) \approx 6.25 \times 10^{-4}
$$

### Step 4 — Comparison

| Method | $P(\text{Sentence})$ | Comment |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **MLE (unsmoothed)** | 0.1111 | Over-confident on seen data |
| **Laplace (Add-1)** | 0.000625 | Distributes mass to unseen N-grams |

> [!WARNING]
> **Zero-Probability Rescue:** Consider the unseen test sentence "I am legend". Without smoothing, $P(\text{am} \mid \text{legend}) = 0/0$ which is undefined. With Laplace, it becomes $1/13 \approx 0.077$, preventing total model failure.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Perplexity Calculation

**Test Corpus:** Two sentences totaling $M = 8$ tokens (after padding).
Sentence A: \<s\> I am Sam \</s\> (5 tokens)
Sentence B: \<s\> Sam I am \</s\> (5 tokens, but sharing vocabulary; for this example assume $M = 10$).

Using bigram log-probabilities from Section 3.1:

$$
\log_2 P(\text{A}) = \log_2(2/3) + \log_2(2/3) + \log_2(1/2) + \log_2(1/2) = -3.170
$$

$$
\log_2 P(\text{B}) = \log_2(1/3) + \log_2(1/2) + \log_2(2/3) + \log_2(1/2) = -3.415
$$

### Combined Probability

$$
\log_2 P(\text{Total}) = -3.170 + (-3.415) = -6.585 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
P(\text{Total}) = 2^{-6.585} \approx 0.01042
$$

### Perplexity

$$
PP(W) = P(\text{Total})^{-1/M} = 0.01042^{-1/10}
$$

$$
PP(W) \approx 1.963 \approx 2.0
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A perplexity of **2** means the model is, on average, as confused as a fair **2-sided coin toss** at each step. Lower is better.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing N and Smoothing

| Scenario | Recommended N | Smoothing | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Small corpus (< 10K tokens) | **Bigram (N=2)** | Add-k with $k=0.5$ | Avoid sparsity explosion |
| Medium corpus (10K–1M) | **Trigram (N=3)** | Good-Turing | Balance context vs. coverage |
| Large corpus (> 1M) | **4-gram or 5-gram** | Kneser-Ney (modified) | State-of-the-art classical |
| Out-of-vocab tokens | Any | Backoff to lower N | Avoid $P = 0$ |
| Real-time typing/chatbot | **Bigram/Trigram** | Stupid Backoff | Speed priority |
| Machine translation baseline | **5-gram + KN** | Modified Kneser-Ney | Industry standard pre-deep-learning |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 N-Gram Extraction Pipeline (Sliding Window Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Tokenization]
    B --> C[Sentence Boundary Padding]
    C --> D{Choose N Value}
    D -->|N=1| E1[Unigram List]
    D -->|N=2| E2[Bigram List]
    D -->|N=3| E3[Trigram List]
    D -->|N=k| E4[Higher Order Ngram List]
    E1 --> F[Count Frequencies]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Build Probability Table]
    G --> H[Apply Smoothing if Needed]
    H --> I[Compute Sentence Probability]
    I --> J[Evaluate Perplexity]
```

## 4.2 Markov Chain Architecture — Context Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[W1 Start Token] --> node2[W2]
    node2 --> node3[W3]
    node3 --> node4[W4]
    node4 --> node5[W5]
    node5 --> node6[W6 End Token]

    node2 -.Conditional Dependency.-> node3
    node3 -.Conditional Dependency.-> node4
    node4 -.Conditional Dependency.-> node5
    node5 -.Conditional Dependency.-> node6

    subgraph Bigram_Context
        node2 --> node3
    end

    subgraph Trigram_Context
        node2 --> node3
        node3 --> node4
    end
```

## 4.3 Smoothing Strategy Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Unseen Ngram Detected] --> q1{Count in Training?}
    q1 -->|Yes, Count high| useMLE[Use MLE Directly]
    q1 -->|Yes, Count low 1 to 3| useGT[Apply Good Turing]
    q1 -->|No, Count zero| q2{Corpus Size?}
    q2 -->|Small V less than 5K| useLaplace[Apply Laplace Add 1]
    q2 -->|Medium V 5K to 50K| useAddK[Apply Add K with k 0.5]
    q2 -->|Large V greater than 50K| useKN[Apply Kneser Ney]
    useLaplace --> fallback[Backoff to Lower N if Needed]
    useAddK --> fallback
    useKN --> fallback
```

## 4.4 Bigram Probability Computation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input
        s1[Training Corpus]
    end

    subgraph Preprocessing
        p1[Tokenize Sentences]
        p2[Pad with Start and End]
    end

    subgraph Counting
        c1[Count All Bigrams C w1 w2]
        c2[Count All Unigrams C w1]
    end

    subgraph Computation
        m1[Divide C w1 w2 by C w1]
        m2[Store in Probability Matrix]
    end

    s1 --> p1 --> p2
    p2 --> c1
    p2 --> c2
    c1 --> m1
    c2 --> m1
    m1 --> m2
    m2 --> out[Output P w2 given w1]
```

    N2[Bigram N=2] ---|Moderate Coverage| Sparse2[Medium Sparsity]

# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define N-grams in NLP. Distinguish between Unigram, Bigram, and Trigram with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** An N-gram is a contiguous sequence of N tokens (words, characters, or sub-words) extracted from a text corpus. N-grams are used in statistical language modeling to capture local sequential dependencies.

**Types & Example Sentence:** "Natural Language Processing is fun"
- **Unigram (N=1):** {Natural}, {Language}, {Processing}, {is}, {fun} — single independent tokens
- **Bigram (N=2):** {(Natural, Language), (Language, Processing), (Processing, is), (is, fun)} — pairs of consecutive tokens
- **Trigram (N=3):** {(Natural, Language, Processing), (Language, Processing, is), (Processing, is, fun)} — triples of consecutive tokens

The choice of N balances **context richness** (higher N) against **data sparsity** (lower N needs smoothing less).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**What is the Markov Assumption in N-gram language modeling? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Markov Assumption states that the probability of a word depends only on a **limited preceding context of (N-1) words**, rather than the entire history of the sentence.

**Mathematical Form (Bigram, N=2):**

$$
P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})
$$

**Necessity:**
1. **Computational Tractability:** Without it, every distinct sentence would form its own unique probability context — impossible to estimate.
2. **Data Sparsity Mitigation:** The full-history context almost never repeats in finite corpora, making MLE estimation infeasible.
3. **Generalization:** Local context is empirically sufficient for many practical NLP tasks (autocomplete, speech recognition).

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (Internal Choice: Question A OR Question B — 14 Marks Each)

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a) Compute the bigram probabilities from the following corpus using Maximum Likelihood Estimation. Also calculate the probability of the sentence "I love NLP".** **(7 Marks)**

**Corpus:**
- S1: "\<s\> I love NLP \</s\>"
- S2: "\<s\> I love deep learning \</s\>"
- S3: "\<s\> deep learning is fun \</s\>"

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Extract All Bigrams and Counts:**

| Bigram | Count |
| :--- | :---: |
| (\<s\>, I) | 2 |
| (\<s\>, deep) | 1 |
| (I, love) | 2 |
| (love, NLP) | 1 |
| (love, deep) | 1 |
| (NLP, \</s\>) | 1 |
| (deep, learning) | 2 |
| (learning, \</s\>) | 1 |
| (learning, is) | 1 |
| (is, fun) | 1 |
| (fun, \</s\>) | 1 |

**Step 2 — Unigram (Context) Counts:**

| Unigram | Count |
| :--- | :---: |
| \<s\> | 3 |
| I | 2 |
| love | 2 |
| NLP | 1 |
| deep | 2 |
| learning | 2 |
| is | 1 |
| fun | 1 |
| \</s\> | 3 |

**Step 3 — Bigram Probabilities (MLE):**

$$
P(\text{I} \mid \text{<s>}) = \frac{2}{3}, \quad P(\text{deep} \mid \text{<s>}) = \frac{1}{3}
$$

$$
P(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) = \frac{2}{2} = 1
$$

$$
P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) = \frac{1}{2}, \quad P(\text{deep} \mid \text{love}) = \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
P(\text{</s>} \mid \text{NLP}) = \frac{1}{1} = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Compute P("I love NLP"):**

Padded sentence: "\<s\> I love NLP \</s\>"

$$
P = P(\text{I} \mid \text{<s>}) \times P(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) \times P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) \times P(\text{</s>} \mid \text{NLP})
$$

$$
P = \frac{2}{3} \times 1 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 1 = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333
$$

---

**(b) Explain Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing. Apply it to recompute the probabilities from part (a). Use $V = 9$.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing addresses the zero-probability problem by adding a pseudo-count of 1 to every N-gram count. This guarantees that no probability is ever exactly zero.

**Formula:**

$$
P_L(w_n \mid w_{n-1}) = \frac{C(w_{n-1}, w_n) + 1}{C(w_{n-1}) + V}
$$

where $V = 9$ is the vocabulary size (counting unique tokens including start/end).

**Recomputed Smoothed Probabilities:**

$$
P_L(\text{I} \mid \text{<s>}) = \frac{2 + 1}{3 + 9} = \frac{3}{12} = 0.25
$$

$$
P_L(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) = \frac{2 + 1}{2 + 9} = \frac{3}{11} \approx 0.2727
$$

$$
P_L(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) = \frac{1 + 1}{2 + 9} = \frac{2}{11} \approx 0.1818
$$

$$
P_L(\text{</s>} \mid \text{NLP}) = \frac{1 + 1}{1 + 9} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.2
$$

**Smoothed Sentence Probability:**

$$
P_L(\text{"I love NLP"}) = 0.25 \times 0.2727 \times 0.1818 \times 0.2 \approx 0.00248
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Metric | MLE | Laplace |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $P(\text{sentence})$ | 0.3333 | 0.00248 |
| Unseen bigram prob. | 0 | $\frac{1}{C+V} > 0$ |

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) Explain the Perplexity metric for evaluating language models. Compute the perplexity for the following test sentence given the bigram probabilities below.** **(7 Marks)**

**Test Sentence:** "\<s\> cat sat down \</s\>"
**Bigram Log-Probabilities (in bits):**
- $\log_2 P(\text{cat} \mid \text{<s>}) = -1.5$
- $\log_2 P(\text{sat} \mid \text{cat}) = -2.0$
- $\log_2 P(\text{down} \mid \text{sat}) = -1.2$
- $\log_2 P(\text{</s>} \mid \text{down}) = -0.8$

**Model Solution:**

**Concept Explanation:**

Perplexity is the standard intrinsic metric for language models. It measures how "surprised" the model is when encountering test data — lower perplexity means better prediction. Mathematically, it is the geometric mean of the inverse probabilities of the test tokens.

**Formula:**

$$
PP(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_M)^{-1/M} = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \log_2 P(w_i \mid \text{context})\right)
$$

Here $M = 4$ (number of tokens in the padded sentence).

**Step 1 — Sum the Log-Probabilities:**

$$
\sum \log_2 P = -1.5 + (-2.0) + (-1.2) + (-0.8) = -5.5 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Perplexity Formula:**

$$
PP = 2^{5.5 / 4} = 2^{1.375}
$$

$$
PP \approx 2.594
$$

**Interpretation:** The model's perplexity is approximately 2.59 — meaning on average, the model behaves as if it is choosing from about 2.59 equally likely words at each step.

---

**(b) Discuss the limitations of N-gram models. How does increasing N affect the model's behavior?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of N-gram Models:**

1. **Data Sparsity (Zero-Frequency Problem):**
   As N increases, the number of unique N-grams explodes combinatorially. Most N-grams in the test set will never have appeared in training, resulting in $P = 0$. This necessitates smoothing, which itself is a heuristic.

2. **Limited Context Window:**
   N-grams cannot capture long-range dependencies. A trigram cannot remember information from 10 words ago, which fails on tasks requiring discourse-level understanding (coreference, sarcasm).

3. **Curse of Dimensionality & Storage:**
   A vocabulary of size $V = 100{,}000$ produces $V^2 = 10^{10}$ possible bigrams. Storage and computation become prohibitive for $N \ge 5$.

4. **Lack of Semantic Understanding:**
   N-grams treat words as discrete atomic symbols. They cannot recognize that "car" and "automobile" are semantically identical.

5. **Sensitivity to Vocabulary (OOV):**
   Out-of-vocabulary words break the model. Unknown words must be mapped to a generic \<UNK\> token, losing information.

6. **Stationary Assumption:**
   N-gram models assume the underlying distribution is stationary — true word probabilities do not change over time — which is violated in real evolving corpora.

**Effect of Increasing N:**

| Aspect | Effect as N grows |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Theoretical power** | Increases (more context captured) |
| **Training data needed** | Increases exponentially |
| **Sparsity** | Increases sharply |
| **Storage** | Grows as $V^N$ |
| **Overfitting risk** | Increases |
| **Out-of-vocab behavior** | Worsens |
| **Practical sweet spot** | $N = 3$ to $N = 5$ |

**Trade-off Curve:** Higher N → richer context but worse sparsity. This is the fundamental **bias-variance trade-off** of N-gram modeling.

**Modern Alternatives:** Neural Language Models (RNN, LSTM, Transformer) overcome these limits via continuous embeddings and unbounded context.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting boundary tokens** (\<s\>, \</s\>): Always pad your sentence before bigram extraction. Examiners deduct 1 mark if \<s\>/\</s\> are omitted.
> - **Confusing context count with bigram count**: In $P(w_n \mid w_{n-1})$, the denominator is $C(w_{n-1})$, NOT $C(w_{n-1}, w_n)$. Read the formula carefully.
> - **Forgetting to normalize**: After applying Laplace smoothing, verify that probabilities still sum (or behave consistently). A sloppy denominator indicates a computation error.
> - **Mixing up chain rule vs. bigram assumption**: Chain rule is exact; bigram model is an approximation. State this distinction clearly.
> - **Not converting to log-space**: When sentence probabilities are very small (e.g., $10^{-15}$), direct multiplication underflows. Use $\log P$.
> - **Vocabulary size confusion**: $V$ in Laplace smoothing is the **unique word types** in the corpus, not the total word count.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for N-Gram Functions**

- ✅ **N-gram = contiguous sequence of N tokens** — tokens can be words, characters, or sub-words.
- ✅ **Unigram (N=1), Bigram (N=2), Trigram (N=3)** are the most commonly used.
- ✅ **Chain Rule** expresses joint probability exactly but is intractable.
- ✅ **Markov Assumption** approximates chain rule by limiting history to N-1 words.
- ✅ **MLE Formula**: $P = C(\text{N-gram}) / C(\text{context})$.
- ✅ **Zero-probability problem** → solved by **smoothing** (Laplace, Add-k, Good-Turing, Kneser-Ney).
- ✅ **Laplace Smoothing**: Add 1 to every count; divide by $C + V$.
- ✅ **Add-k Smoothing**: Use fractional $k$ (typically 0.1 to 1.0) for gentler correction.
- ✅ **Boundary tokens** \<s\> and \</s\> are mandatory for proper start/end modeling.
- ✅ **Perplexity** = $P(W)^{-1/M}$ — lower is better, geometric mean of inverse probabilities.
- ✅ **Log-space arithmetic** is essential to avoid floating-point underflow.
- ✅ **Higher N** = richer context but exponentially worse sparsity (trade-off).
- ✅ **Vocabulary size $V$** = number of unique types (not total tokens).
- ✅ **N-gram models are foundational** but have been largely superseded by neural LMs (BERT, GPT).
- ✅ **Practical sweet spot**: Trigrams (N=3) with Kneser-Ney smoothing was state-of-the-art before deep learning.
- ✅ **Applications**: Autocomplete, spell-check, machine translation, speech recognition, text generation, spam filtering.
- ✅ **Out-of-vocabulary (OOV)** words must be replaced with \<UNK\> before scoring.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** If asked to compute a sentence probability, always write the **padded form** first, list all bigrams, build a **count table**, then plug into the MLE formula. This structured approach scores full marks in KTU valuations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Update Counts Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ADJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Update Counts Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Update Counts Function — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Update Counts Function):**
> In statistical Natural Language Processing — specifically within the **Baum–Welch Algorithm** (Expectation–Maximization for Hidden Markov Models) — the **Update Counts Function** is the mathematical routine that *accumulates the expected number of times each hidden state transition and observation emission occurs*, given the current model parameters and the observed sequence. It converts the soft (probabilistic) alignments produced by the Forward–Backward algorithm into *reliable frequency counts* that drive parameter re-estimation.

Formally, for an HMM with states $S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_N\}$, observation sequence $O = o_1 o_2 \dots o_T$, transition matrix $A$, emission matrix $B$, and initial state distribution $\pi$, the function computes two count matrices:

$$C_{ij}^{(t)} \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i,j) \quad \text{(Transition Counts)}$$

$$E_{i}(v_k) \;=\; \sum_{t:\, o_t = v_k}^{T} \gamma_t(i) \quad \text{(Emission Counts)}$$

where $\xi_t(i,j)$ and $\gamma_t(i)$ are the posterior probabilities derived from forward ($\alpha$) and backward ($\beta$) variables.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Attendance Counter"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for B.Tech Students:**
> Imagine a college with **N classrooms** (hidden states) and a CCTV camera (observations: students entering/leaving). The principal doesn't know which student came from which classroom. Each day, the Update Counts Function acts like a **probabilistic attendance register**:
> 1. The camera records raw footage (observations).
> 2. Forward–Backward probabilities tell us *how likely* it is that student $k$ at time $t$ came from classroom $i$ (not a hard yes/no — a *fractional* count).
> 3. The Update Counts Function **sums these fractional attendances** across all time steps to get expected counts per classroom.
> 4. These expected counts are then *normalized* into new transition/emission probabilities for the next iteration.

This is **not** counting concrete events — it is counting **weighted beliefs** about events, which is the heart of the EM algorithm.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Notations

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Range |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| $N$ | Number of hidden states | $\mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| $T$ | Length of observation sequence | $\mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| $\xi_t(i,j)$ | Prob. of transition $s_i \to s_j$ at time $t$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| $\gamma_t(i)$ | Prob. of being in state $s_i$ at time $t$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| $C_{ij}$ | Expected transition count $i \to j$ | $\mathbb{R}^+ \cup \{0\}$ |
| $E_i(v_k)$ | Expected emission count of $v_k$ from $s_i$ | $\mathbb{R}^+ \cup \{0\}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners often test whether students understand that Update Counts produces *expected* (fractional) counts, **not actual observed counts**. This distinction is worth 2–3 marks in any ESE question on Baum–Welch.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Where Does Update Counts Fit in the Baum–Welch Pipeline?

The Baum–Welch algorithm is a two-step iterative process per iteration:

1. **E-Step (Expectation):** Run Forward–Backward to compute $\alpha_t(i)$, $\beta_t(i)$, and from them derive the soft assignments $\xi_t(i,j)$ and $\gamma_t(i)$.
2. **M-Step (Maximization) — which contains the Update Counts Function:**
   - **Aggregate** fractional assignments into count matrices (Update Counts).
   - **Normalize** these matrices to obtain the new parameters $\hat{A}$, $\hat{B}$, $\hat{\pi}$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Update Counts Function

> [!NOTE]
> The function takes the **current model parameters** $(A, B, \pi)$ and the **observation sequence** $O$, and outputs the **re-estimated counts** that the next M-Step will normalize.

1. **Initialize** two zero matrices: $C[N \times N]$ for transitions and $E[N \times V]$ for emissions.
2. **For each time step** $t = 1$ to $T-1$:
   - Compute $\xi_t(i,j)$ for every state pair $(i,j)$ using the forward and backward variables.
   - **Accumulate transition count:** $C_{ij} \mathrel{+}= \xi_t(i,j)$.
3. **For each time step** $t = 1$ to $T$:
   - Compute $\gamma_t(i)$ for every state $i$.
   - If $o_t = v_k$, **accumulate emission count:** $E_i(v_k) \mathrel{+}= \gamma_t(i)$.
4. **Initialize-state count update:** $\pi_i^{\text{new}} = \gamma_1(i)$.
5. **Output** the count matrices; the M-Step then re-estimates parameters.

## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why fractional counts?** Because hidden states are *latent*. We cannot say "state $s_2$ definitely produced $o_5$". So we distribute 1 unit of probability mass across all states and add the *expected share*.
- **Why sum across all $t$?** Each $\xi_t(i,j)$ is a per-time-step evidence; aggregation gives the **total expected frequency** of transition $i \to j$ in the entire sequence.
- **Why normalize afterward?** Counts alone are meaningless — the ratio $\frac{C_{ij}}{\sum_j C_{ij}}$ is what gives a valid probability distribution for the next iteration.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\xi_t(i,j) = \dfrac{\alpha_t(i)\, a_{ij}\, b_j(o_{t+1})\, \beta_{t+1}(j)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$ | Posterior transition probability at time $t$ |
| 2 | $\gamma_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \xi_t(i,j)$ | Posterior state probability at time $t$ |
| 3 | $C_{ij} = \sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i,j)$ | **Transition count update** |
| 4 | $E_i(v_k) = \sum_{t:\, o_t=v_k} \gamma_t(i)$ | **Emission count update** |
| 5 | $\hat{a}_{ij} = \dfrac{C_{ij}}{\sum_{j'=1}^{N} C_{ij'}}$ | Re-estimated transition probability |
| 6 | $\hat{b}_i(v_k) = \dfrac{E_i(v_k)}{\sum_{k'=1}^{V} E_i(v_{k'})}$ | Re-estimated emission probability |
| 7 | $\hat{\pi}_i = \gamma_1(i)$ | Re-estimated initial state probability |
| 8 | $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i)$ | Observation likelihood (denominator) |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pitfall:** In KTU answer sheets, do **not** use vertical bars $\vert$ inside tables. Always write $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ for absolute value expressions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Role of Update Counts |
|------------------|----------------------|
| **Speech Recognition** (e.g., Dragon, Google Voice) | Re-trains phoneme-to-state HMMs on acoustic features |
| **Bioinformatics (Gene Finding)** | Updates HMM state transitions for coding vs non-coding regions |
| **POS Tagging** | Refines transition probabilities between grammatical tags |
| **Named Entity Recognition** | Re-estimates emission probabilities for entity types |
| **Handwriting Recognition** | Updates counts for character-level HMMs |
| **Time-Series Anomaly Detection** | Updates transition counts in regime-switching models |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** Modern deep learning (BERT, GPT) has *replaced* HMMs in many NLP tasks, but Update Counts logic survives inside **Expectation–Maximization** for Gaussian Mixture Models, **GMM-HMM hybrid acoustic models** in Kaldi, and **semi-supervised sequence labeling** where HMMs provide weak supervision.
