topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Naive Bayes Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4 — Naive Bayes Theorem

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Naive Bayes Theorem** is a probabilistic supervised machine learning classification algorithm rooted in **Bayes' Theorem** of conditional probability, which operates under the **"naive" assumption of conditional independence** between every pair of features (predictor variables) given the value of the class label. In the context of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, it is overwhelmingly used for **text classification** tasks such as spam filtering, sentiment analysis, and document categorization, where it estimates the posterior probability of a class $C_k$ given a document represented as a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 expects students to (a) state Bayes' Theorem, (b) clearly articulate the *conditional independence assumption*, (c) derive the Naive Bayes classifier decision rule $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} P(C_k)\prod_{i} P(x_i \mid C_k)$, and (d) apply the model to at least one NLP problem.

---

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — The "Doctor's Diagnosis" Mental Model

Imagine you walk into a clinic with a fever, sore throat, and runny nose. A doctor mentally runs through *all diseases she has ever seen* (her **prior** knowledge — say, 1000 cases), and asks: *"Given these exact symptoms, which disease is most likely?"*

She does **not** deeply model how each symptom biologically interacts with the others. She simply asks: *"How often did patients with Flu have a fever? How often did they have a sore throat? How often did they have a runny nose?"* Then she **multiplies** these individual likelihoods and **scales by how common Flu is in general**.

That is *exactly* what Naive Bayes does:
- 🩺 **Disease** → Class label $C_k$
- 🤒 **Symptoms** → Features $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$
- 📊 **Frequency lookup tables** → Likelihoods $P(x_i \mid C_k)$
- 🌍 **Disease prevalence** → Prior $P(C_k)$

The word *"Naive"* refers to the (often unrealistic) assumption that **each symptom independently suggests a disease**, ignoring the fact that symptoms are biologically correlated. Despite this "naive" simplification, the classifier works surprisingly well in practice — especially for text.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the "naive" assumption still work?**
> In high-dimensional NLP spaces, the model only needs to *rank* classes correctly, not produce perfectly calibrated probabilities. Independence violations tend to affect all classes similarly, leaving the **argmax decision boundary** largely intact. This is why Naive Bayes remains a strong **baseline** in NLP.

### 🎯 Key Engineering Constants & Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Prior Probability | $P(C_k)$ | Belief about class *before* seeing the data |
| Likelihood | $P(x_i \mid C_k)$ | Probability of feature given the class |
| Evidence / Marginal | $P(\mathbf{x})$ | Normalizing constant (often ignored in argmax) |
| Posterior | $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Updated belief after observing data |
| Feature Vector | $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)$ | Document represented as $n$ features (often words) |
| Vocabulary size | $\vert V \vert$ | **Distinct word count** in training corpus |
| Laplace Smoothing Constant | $\alpha$ | Typically **$\alpha = 1$** (add-one smoothing) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔬 Foundational Pillar — Bayes' Theorem (1763)

Bayes' Theorem, originally formulated by **Reverend Thomas Bayes** and published posthumously in 1763, expresses how to update the probability of a hypothesis $H$ when new evidence $E$ is observed:

$$
P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}
$$

Mapped to our classification notation:

$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
$$

### 🧩 The "Naive" Conditional Independence Assumption

Directly computing $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C_k)$ is intractable for non-trivial feature sets (it requires modeling the **joint distribution**). Naive Bayes simplifies this via the **chain rule** of probability combined with conditional independence:

$$
P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Assumption:** Every feature $x_i$ is conditionally independent of every other feature $x_j$ for $i \neq j$, given the class $C_k$. Formally: $P(x_i \mid x_j, C_k) = P(x_i \mid C_k)$.

### 📐 Naive Bayes Classifier — Decision Rule

Substituting the independence assumption into Bayes' rule:

$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$

The class with the highest posterior is selected:

$$
\hat{y} = \underset{k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}}{\arg\max} \; P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$

The denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$ is dropped because it is **constant** for all classes and does not affect the argmax.

### 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | Bayes' Theorem | ⭐⭐⭐ Core |
| 2 | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} P(C_k) \prod_{i} P(x_i \mid C_k)$ | NB Decision Rule | ⭐⭐⭐ Core |
| 3 | $P(C_k) = \dfrac{N_k}{N}$ | Maximum Likelihood Prior | ⭐⭐⭐ Core |
| 4 | $P(x_i = w \mid C_k) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w, C_k)}{\sum_{w'} \text{count}(w', C_k)}$ | Multinomial Likelihood | ⭐⭐⭐ Core |
| 5 | $P(w \mid C_k) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w, C_k) + \alpha}{\sum_{w'} \text{count}(w', C_k) + \alpha \vert V \vert}$ | **Laplace (Add-$\alpha$) Smoothing** | ⭐⭐⭐ Core |
| 6 | $\log P(C_k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid C_k)$ | Log-space trick (avoids underflow) | ⭐⭐ High |
| 7 | $P(x_i \mid C_k) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_k^2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x_i - \mu_k)^2}{2\sigma_k^2}\right)$ | **Gaussian NB** Likelihood (continuous) | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| 8 | $P(x_i \mid C_k) = P(i \mid C_k)^{x_i} (1 - P(i \mid C_k))^{1-x_i}$ | **Bernoulli NB** (binary features) | ⭐⭐ Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip:** In NLP, the *Multinomial* variant (formula #4) is by far the most common, used in spam detection, sentiment analysis, and topic classification. Gaussian Naive Bayes is rarely used in pure text settings.

### 🏗️ Variants of Naive Bayes for NLP

1. **Multinomial Naive Bayes** — Best for text classification with **word counts / TF** features. Document is a multiset of words.
2. **Bernoulli Naive Bayes** — Best for **binary occurrence** features (word present/absent). Suited to short documents.
3. **Gaussian Naive Bayes** — Assumes features are normally distributed; rarely used for raw text, but applicable when features are continuous (e.g., word embeddings).

### 🌐 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| 📧 Email Systems | Spam vs. Ham filtering |
| 💬 Social Media | Sentiment polarity detection |
| 📰 News Aggregators | Topic categorization (Politics / Sports / Tech) |
| 🩺 Medical NLP | Disease classification from clinical notes |
| 🤖 Chatbots | Intent recognition for user utterances |
| 📚 Digital Libraries | Document indexing and retrieval |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🧮 Derivation 3.1 — From Joint Probability to Naive Bayes

**Goal:** Show rigorously how Bayes' theorem + independence assumption yield the NB decision rule.

#### Step 1: Start with Bayes' Theorem

$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
$$

*Logic:* We want the probability of class $C_k$ given the observed feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$.

#### Step 2: Apply the Chain Rule of Probability

The joint likelihood $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$ can be expanded using the chain rule:

$$
P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = P(x_1 \mid C_k) \cdot P(x_2 \mid x_1, C_k) \cdot P(x_3 \mid x_1, x_2, C_k) \cdots P(x_n \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1}, C_k)
$$

*Logic:* Each successive feature's probability may in principle depend on all preceding features.

#### Step 3: Impose Conditional Independence

Under the **naive** assumption, every $x_i$ is independent of all other features given $C_k$. Thus $P(x_i \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{i-1}, C_k) = P(x_i \mid C_k)$. The chain rule collapses:

$$
P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$

*Logic:* This is the defining simplification of Naive Bayes. It converts a $2^n$ parameter problem (intractable) into a $n \cdot K$ parameter problem (linear in features and classes).

#### Step 4: Form the Posterior and Drop the Denominator

Substituting back:

$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
$$

Since $P(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{k} P(C_k) P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$ is constant across all classes for a given $\mathbf{x}$, it does not affect which class maximizes the posterior:

$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$

#### Step 5: The Final Decision Rule

The classifier picks the class that maximizes the proportional posterior:

$$
\hat{y} = \underset{k}{\arg\max} \; P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$

> **✅ Derivation Complete.** This is the canonical form students must reproduce in KTU 2024 exams for full credit on a 14-mark derivation question.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 3.2 — Laplace (Add-One) Smoothing for Zero Probability

**Problem:** If a word $w$ never appears in training documents of class $C_k$, then $P(w \mid C_k) = 0$, which zeroes out the *entire product* — the document can never be classified as $C_k$, even if overwhelming evidence supports it.

**Solution — Laplace Smoothing** (introduced by **Pierre-Simon Laplace**, 1812):

$$
P(w \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(w, C_k) + \alpha}{\sum_{w' \in V} \left[\text{count}(w', C_k) + \alpha\right]} = \frac{\text{count}(w, C_k) + \alpha}{\left(\sum_{w' \in V} \text{count}(w', C_k)\right) + \alpha \vert V \vert
$$

*Logic:*
- $\alpha = 1$ is the standard "add-one" choice.
- $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size.
- The denominator ensures the probabilities still sum to 1: $\sum_{w} P(w \mid C_k) = 1$.

---

### 📊 Worked Numerical Example — Spam Filter (KTU Typical)

**Training Data:**

| Document | Words | Class |
|---|---|---|
| $D_1$ | "free offer now" | Spam |
| $D_2$ | "free entry win" | Spam |
| $D_3$ | "meeting schedule project" | Ham |
| $D_4$ | "project update meeting" | Ham |

**Step A — Compute Priors**

$$
P(\text{Spam}) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5, \quad P(\text{Ham}) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5
$$

**Step B — Build Vocabulary and Counts**

Vocabulary $V = \{\text{free, offer, now, entry, win, meeting, schedule, project, update}\}$ $\Rightarrow$ $\vert V \vert = 9$.

**Spam word counts:** free=2, offer=1, now=1, entry=1, win=1, others=0.  
**Total spam tokens:** 6.

**Ham word counts:** meeting=2, project=2, schedule=1, update=1, others=0.  
**Total ham tokens:** 6.

**Step C — Compute Likelihoods with Laplace Smoothing ($\alpha = 1$)**

$$
P(w \mid \text{Spam}) = \frac{\text{count}(w, \text{Spam}) + 1}{6 + 9} = \frac{\text{count}(w, \text{Spam}) + 1}{15}
$$

$$
P(w \mid \text{Ham}) = \frac{\text{count}(w, \text{Ham}) + 1}{6 + 9} = \frac{\text{count}(w, \text{Ham}) + 1}{15}
$$

| Word $w$ | $P(w \mid \text{Spam})$ | $P(w \mid \text{Ham})$ |
|---|---|---|
| free | $\frac{3}{15} = 0.200$ | $\frac{1}{15} \approx 0.0667$ |
| offer | $\frac{2}{15} \approx 0.1333$ | $\frac{1}{15} \approx 0.0667$ |
| meeting | $\frac{1}{15} \approx 0.0667$ | $\frac{3}{15} = 0.200$ |
| project | $\frac{1}{15} \approx 0.0667$ | $\frac{3}{15} = 0.200$ |

**Step D — Classify a New Document: "free meeting"**

Compute the unnormalized posteriors (using log-space for numerical safety):

$$
\log P(\text{Spam} \mid \text{test}) \propto \log(0.5) + \log(0.200) + \log(0.0667)
$$

$$
\log P(\text{Spam} \mid \text{test}) \propto -0.6931 + (-1.6094) + (-2.7080) = -5.0105
$$

$$
\log P(\text{Ham} \mid \text{test}) \propto \log(0.5) + \log(0.0667) + \log(0.200)
$$

$$
\log P(\text{Ham} \mid \text{test}) \propto -0.6931 + (-2.7080) + (-1.6094) = -5.0105
$$

> **Tie observed!** In practice, ties indicate the document is ambiguous. With a longer document or more discriminative vocabulary, one class would dominate.

**Step E — Classification Decision**

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} \left[ P(C_k)\prod_{i} P(x_i \mid C_k) \right] = \text{Spam (or Ham, tie)}
$$

This fully worked numerical problem is the **gold standard** for KTU 2024 numerical/analytical questions.

---

### 📐 Analytical Matrix — NB Parameter Estimation Summary

| Component | Formula (no smoothing) | Formula (Laplace) |
|---|---|---|
| Prior | $P(C_k) = \dfrac{N_k}{N}$ | Unchanged |
| Conditional Likelihood (Multinomial) | $P(w \mid C_k) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w, C_k)}{\text{total tokens in } C_k}$ | $P(w \mid C_k) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w, C_k) + \alpha}{\text{total tokens in } C_k + \alpha \vert V \vert}$ |
| Bernoulli | $P(w \mid C_k) = \dfrac{\text{docs containing } w \text{ in } C_k}{N_k}$ | $P(w \mid C_k) = \dfrac{\text{docs with } w \text{ in } C_k + \alpha}{N_k + 2\alpha}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🗺️ Diagram 4.1 — Naive Bayes Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Document D] --> B[Tokenization]
    B --> C[Vocabulary Lookup V]
    C --> D[Feature Vector x = x1, x2, ..., xn]
    D --> E[For each class Ck in 1 to K]
    E --> F[Compute Prior P of Ck]
    E --> G[Compute Likelihood P of xi given Ck for each i]
    F --> H[Combine via Product]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Posterior Score for class Ck]
    I --> J[Argmax across all classes]
    J --> K[Predicted Class y-hat]
    L[Training Corpus] --> M[Word Count Table per class]
    M --> N[Estimate P of Ck and P of w given Ck]
    N --> G
```

### 🗺️ Diagram 4.2 — Decision Boundary Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Document x] --> B{Is P of C1 times prod greater than P of C2 times prod?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Predict Class 1]
    B -->|No| D{Is P of C2 times prod greater than P of C3 times prod?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Predict Class 2]
    D -->|No| F[Predict Class 3]
```

### 🗺️ Diagram 4.3 — Training vs. Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRAINING_PHASE
        T1[Labeled Corpus] --> T2[Tokenize and Count]
        T2 --> T3[Compute Prior P of Ck]
        T2 --> T4[Compute Likelihood P of w given Ck with Laplace Smoothing]
        T3 --> T5[Model Parameters theta]
        T4 --> T5
    end
    subgraph INFERENCE_PHASE
        I1[New Document] --> I2[Tokenize]
        I2 --> I3[Score = log P of Ck plus sum of log P of w given Ck]
        T5 --> I3
        I3 --> I4[Argmax over k]
        I4 --> I5[Output Predicted Class]
    end
```

### 🗺️ Diagram 4.4 — Effect of Laplace Smoothing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Count of w in Ck] --> B{Is count equal to 0?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Problem: P of w given Ck = 0]
    C --> D[Apply Laplace: add alpha to numerator]
    D --> E[Add alpha times V to denominator]
    E --> F[Smoothed Probability greater than 0]
    B -->|No| G[Apply Laplace anyway for consistency]
    G --> F
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State Bayes' Theorem and explain the role of each term with respect to text classification.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Bayes' Theorem:
> $$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$
>
> - **$P(C_k)$ — Prior:** Initial probability of class $C_k$ (e.g., Spam) before observing the document, estimated as $N_k / N$.
> - **$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$ — Likelihood:** Probability of observing the feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ given the document belongs to class $C_k$. In NB, this is factorized as $\prod_i P(x_i \mid C_k)$.
> - **$P(\mathbf{x})$ — Evidence (Marginal):** Total probability of the feature vector, computed by summing $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) P(C_k)$ over all classes. It serves as a normalizing constant.
> - **$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$ — Posterior:** Updated probability of class $C_k$ after observing $\mathbf{x}$. The classifier selects the class maximizing this quantity.
>
> *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for explaining 2 terms, 1 mark for linking to text classification.)* ✅

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the "naive" assumption in the Naive Bayes classifier? Why is it called naive?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **naive assumption** is that all features (words) in the feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ are **conditionally independent** of each other given the class label $C_k$. Mathematically:
> $$P(x_i \mid x_j, C_k) = P(x_i \mid C_k) \quad \text{for all } i \neq j$$
>
> It is called *naive* because in real-world text, words are clearly **not independent** — they exhibit strong co-occurrence patterns (e.g., "machine" and "learning" frequently appear together). The assumption is a deliberate, often unrealistic simplification that nonetheless yields a fast, scalable, and surprisingly accurate classifier.
>
> *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for stating the assumption, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for justifying "naive" with a real text example.)* ✅

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **Q.A) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, adapted]**
> (a) **[7 Marks]** Derive the Naive Bayes classification decision rule from Bayes' Theorem. Clearly state the conditional independence assumption and justify why the denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$ can be ignored.
> (b) **[7 Marks]** Consider the following mini-corpus for a binary sentiment classifier:
>
> | Doc | Words | Label |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | "great movie fantastic" | Positive |
> | 2 | "fantastic acting great" | Positive |
> | 3 | "boring movie bad" | Negative |
> | 4 | "bad acting boring" | Negative |
>
> Classify the test document **"great boring"** using Multinomial Naive Bayes with **Laplace smoothing ($\alpha = 1$)**. Show every computation step.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Start from Bayes' Theorem** *(1 mark)*:

$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule** *(1 mark)*:

$$
P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = P(x_1 \mid C_k) P(x_2 \mid x_1, C_k) \cdots P(x_n \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1}, C_k)
$$

**Step 3 — Impose conditional independence** *(2 marks)*:

Under the naive assumption $P(x_i \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{i-1}, C_k) = P(x_i \mid C_k)$:

$$
P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and drop the denominator** *(1.5 marks)*:

$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$

The denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$ is the same for every class $C_k$ for a fixed $\mathbf{x}$, so it does not affect the argmax.

**Step 5 — Final decision rule** *(1.5 marks)*:

$$
\hat{y} = \underset{k}{\arg\max} \; P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$

To avoid numerical underflow, NB is typically implemented in **log-space**:

$$
\hat{y} = \underset{k}{\arg\max} \left[ \log P(C_k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid C_k) \right]
$$

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute Priors** *(1 mark)*:

$$
P(\text{Pos}) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5, \quad P(\text{Neg}) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5
$$

**Step 2 — Build Vocabulary** *(0.5 marks)*:

$$
V = \{\text{great, movie, fantastic, acting, boring, bad}\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert V \vert = 6
$$

**Step 3 — Count Tokens per Class** *(1 mark)*:

| Word | Pos count | Neg count |
|---|---|---|
| great | 2 | 0 |
| movie | 1 | 1 |
| fantastic | 2 | 0 |
| acting | 1 | 1 |
| boring | 0 | 2 |
| bad | 0 | 2 |
| **Total** | **6** | **6** |

**Step 4 — Apply Laplace Smoothing** *(1.5 marks)*:

$$
P(w \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(w, C_k) + 1}{6 + 6} = \frac{\text{count}(w, C_k) + 1}{12}
$$

| Word | $P(w \mid \text{Pos})$ | $P(w \mid \text{Neg})$ |
|---|---|---|
| great | $\frac{3}{12} = 0.25$ | $\frac{1}{12} \approx 0.0833$ |
| boring | $\frac{1}{12} \approx 0.0833$ | $\frac{3}{12} = 0.25$ |

**Step 5 — Score Test Document "great boring"** *(2 marks)*:

Using log-space:

$$
\text{Score}(\text{Pos}) = \log(0.5) + \log(0.25) + \log(0.0833)
$$

$$
\text{Score}(\text{Pos}) = -0.6931 + (-1.3863) + (-2.4849) = -4.5643
$$

$$
\text{Score}(\text{Neg}) = \log(0.5) + \log(0.0833) + \log(0.25)
$$

$$
\text{Score}(\text{Neg}) = -0.6931 + (-2.4849) + (-1.3863) = -4.5643
$$

**Step 6 — Decision** *(1 mark)*:

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_k \text{Score}(C_k) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Tie! (Pos = Neg)}
$$

The result is a tie. This is a classic illustrative case where one word pulls towards Positive and the other towards Negative with equal strength. With Laplace smoothing on a small corpus, ties are common and the document is genuinely ambiguous.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

> **Q.B) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, adapted]**
> (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain **Multinomial**, **Bernoulli**, and **Gaussian** variants of Naive Bayes. State the likelihood formula for each and identify the variant most suitable for **word-frequency-based document classification**.
> (b) **[7 Marks]** Why is **Laplace (add-one) smoothing** necessary in Naive Bayes? Derive the smoothed likelihood formula and show with a small numerical example how it prevents the zero-frequency problem.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

| Variant | Feature Type | Likelihood Formula | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Multinomial NB** | Discrete counts (word frequencies) | $P(w \mid C_k) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w, C_k)}{\sum_{w'} \text{count}(w', C_k)}$ | **Word count / TF** document classification *(most common in NLP)* |
| **Bernoulli NB** | Binary (0/1 occurrence) | $P(w \mid C_k) = \dfrac{\text{docs with } w \text{ in } C_k}{N_k}$ (per-document) | Short documents, presence/absence tasks |
| **Gaussian NB** | Continuous (real-valued) | $P(x_i \mid C_k) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_k^2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x_i - \mu_k)^2}{2\sigma_k^2}\right)$ | Word embeddings, sensor data |

*(1 mark per variant + 1 mark for identifying Multinomial as best for word-frequency classification.)* ✅

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — The Zero-Frequency Problem** *(2 marks)*:

If a word $w$ never appears in training documents of class $C_k$, its count $\text{count}(w, C_k) = 0$, giving $P(w \mid C_k) = 0$. In the NB product:

$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(C_k) \cdot 0 \cdot \prod_{i \neq j} P(x_i \mid C_k) = 0
$$

The test document can **never** be classified as $C_k$, no matter how strong other evidence is. This is the **zero-frequency problem**.

**Step 2 — Laplace Smoothing Formula** *(2 marks)*:

$$
P(w \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(w, C_k) + \alpha}{\left(\sum_{w' \in V} \text{count}(w', C_k)\right) + \alpha \vert V \vert}
$$

with $\alpha = 1$ for add-one smoothing. This **guarantees** $P(w \mid C_k) > 0$ for all $w \in V$ and preserves $\sum_{w} P(w \mid C_k) = 1$.

**Step 3 — Numerical Demonstration** *(3 marks)*:

Suppose $V = \{a, b, c\}$, total tokens in $C_k = 10$, and $w = b$ has count $0$.

Without smoothing: $P(b \mid C_k) = 0 / 10 = 0$ → zero-frequency. ❌

With Laplace ($\alpha = 1$, $\vert V \vert = 3$): $P(b \mid C_k) = (0 + 1) / (10 + 3) = 1/13 \approx 0.0769$ → non-zero. ✅

> **Result:** The smoothed model assigns a small but **non-zero** probability to unseen words, preventing complete rejection of the class and improving generalization.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout
> - **Most common mark-loss areas** on Naive Bayes questions:
>   1. ❌ **Forgetting to apply Laplace smoothing** when a test word is absent from training — even if the question does not explicitly say "with smoothing", a KTU 2024 question on a novel test word *implicitly expects* the student to address zero-frequency.
>   2. ❌ **Using $P(\mathbf{x})$ in the final argmax** — never compute or include the denominator; it is constant.
>   3. ❌ **Multiplying many small probabilities directly** — use **log-space** to avoid underflow; examiners explicitly test this.
>   4. ❌ **Confusing Multinomial and Bernoulli** — remember: Multinomial uses **token counts**; Bernoulli uses **document counts with binary features**.
>   5. ❌ **Skipping the chain rule step** in derivations — KTU 2024 expects the full chain rule → independence assumption transition.
>   6. ❌ **Forgetting $\vert V \vert$ in the Laplace denominator** — a classic 1-mark deduction trap.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **Bayes' Theorem** is the foundation; memorize $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$.

> ✅ The **"naive" conditional independence assumption** is: $P(x_i \mid x_j, C_k) = P(x_i \mid C_k)$ for all $i \neq j$.

> ✅ The **NB decision rule** is $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$.

> ✅ **Multinomial Naive Bayes** is the default choice for text classification with word counts.

> ✅ **Bernoulli Naive Bayes** uses binary occurrence features; **Gaussian NB** uses continuous features.

> ✅ **Laplace (add-one) smoothing** prevents the zero-frequency problem: $P(w \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(w, C_k) + 1}{\text{total tokens in } C_k + \vert V \vert}$.

> ✅ Always work in **log-space** for products to avoid floating-point underflow: $\log P(C_k) + \sum_i \log P(x_i \mid C_k)$.

> ✅ The denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$ is a **normalizing constant** and is dropped in the argmax.

> ✅ NB is a **generative, probabilistic** classifier — it models $P(\mathbf{x}, C_k)$, not just $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$ directly.

> ✅ Despite the "naive" label, NB is a **strong baseline** for spam filtering, sentiment analysis, and topic classification — often competitive with far more complex models on small-to-medium text datasets.

> ✅ **KTU-favorite question types** for this topic: (1) state-and-explain Bayes' theorem (3 marks), (2) derive NB decision rule from first principles (7 marks), (3) numerical classification with Laplace smoothing (7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intuition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Intuition # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Vector Semantics, **"Intuition"** refers to the foundational conceptual understanding of how words, which are symbolic and discrete in nature, can be meaningfully represented as **dense, continuous numerical vectors** in a high-dimensional space. This intuition is grounded in the **Distributional Hypothesis** and serves as the philosophical and mathematical launching pad for modern word embedding techniques such as **Word2Vec**, **GloVe**, and **FastText**. Formally, the intuition states: > A word's meaning can be inferred from the **linguistic contexts** in which it frequently appears. Words that occur in **similar contexts** tend to have **similar meanings**, and this semantic similarity can be encoded geometrically in a vector space. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 - Vector Semantics & Embeddings)** > The topic "Intuition" is the *conceptual foundation lecture* before diving into co-occurrence matrices, TF-IDF, and neural embeddings. Examiners frequently test whether students understand **why** we represent words as vectors, not just *how*. ## 1.2 The Distributional Hypothesis — The Heart of the Intuition The intuition is mathematically and linguistically anchored in **Firth's (1957) Distributional Principle**: > *"You shall know a word by the company it keeps."* This single sentence is the philosophical bedrock of nearly all modern NLP embedding techniques. The formal restatement is: $$\text{sim}(w_1, w_2) \propto f(\text{context}(w_1), \text{context}(w_2))$$ Where $\text{sim}(w_1, w_2)$ is the semantic similarity between words $w_1$ and $w_2$, and $f$ is a similarity function over their contextual distributions. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Friend Circle" Intuition Imagine you are at a large social gathering and you want to guess the **profession** of a stranger without asking them directly. You observe who they are talking to: - If they frequently converse with **stethoscopes, surgeons, and nurses** → they are likely a **doctor**. - If they frequently talk about **spreadsheets, balance sheets, and audits** → they are likely an **accountant**. The people they talk to are their **"company"**, and from that company, you **infer their identity**. > [!NOTE] > **Mapping the Analogy to NLP:** > - The **stranger** = a target word (e.g., "oncology") > - The **conversational partners** = context words (e.g., "cancer", "tumor", "chemotherapy", "radiology") > - The **inferred profession** = the vector representation of the word > - **Two strangers with overlapping friend circles** = two semantically similar words (e.g., "oncology" and "radiology") This is precisely the intuition: **context is meaning**. ## 1.4 Why Classical One-Hot Vectors Fail (The Motivation) The traditional one-hot encoding represents a word as a sparse binary vector of vocabulary size $V$: $$w_{\text{apple}} = [0, 0, \ldots, 1, \ldots, 0, 0] \in \mathbb{R}^{V}$$ The dimensions are uncorrelated, so $\cos(w_i, w_j) = 0$ for every pair of distinct words. The classical approach has **three fatal flaws**: | Flaw | Consequence | |---|---| | **Sparsity** | For a vocabulary of 100,000 words, each vector is 99.999% zeros. | | **No Semantic Encoding** | "King" and "Queen" are as different as "King" and "Banana". | | **No Generalization** | The model cannot infer that unseen words are related to seen ones. | The intuition behind embeddings solves all three by mapping words into a **dense, low-dimensional, semantically meaningful space** (typically 50–300 dimensions). ## 1.5 The Goal of the "Intuition" Step > [!NOTE] > **End Goal of This Lecture:** > Before we compute any matrix, train any neural network, or apply cosine similarity, we must internalize a single powerful idea: > > **Words that appear in similar contexts should have vectors that are geometrically close in some embedding space.** > > This is the **only assumption** that powers Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT, and essentially all modern NLP. The remaining lectures of Module 4 (co-occurrence matrices, TF-IDF, Word2Vec, CBOW vs. Skip-gram) are merely *engineering implementations* of this single intuition. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Intuition The intuition behind word embeddings rests on three mutually reinforcing theoretical pillars: ### Pillar 1: Distributional Semantics Words are characterized by their **distributional patterns** across a corpus. Two words are semantically similar if their distributional patterns are similar. ### Pillar 2: Vector Space Representation Each word $w$ is assigned a real-valued vector $\vec{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ where $d \ll \vert V \vert$. ### Pillar 3: Geometric Encodings of Meaning Linguistic relations become **linear algebraic operations** in the embedding space. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Famous King – Queen – Man – Woman Analogy (Core KTU High-Yield Fact):** > > In a well-trained embedding space, the following relation holds with remarkable precision: > > $$\vec{king} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{queen}$$ > > This is **not a coincidence** — it is a direct mathematical consequence of training the embedding on distributional co-occurrence statistics. The vector arithmetic $\vec{king} - \vec{man}$ captures the concept of *"royalty filtered by maleness"*, and adding $\vec{woman}$ projects it onto the female equivalent. ## 2.2 From Sparse to Dense — The Dimensionality Argument | Representation Type | Dimensionality | Semantic Information | Storage | |---|---|---|---| | **One-Hot** | $\vert V \vert$ (e.g., 100,000) | None | $O(\vert V \vert)$ per word | | **TF-IDF / Co-occurrence** | $\vert V \vert$ (e.g., 100,000) | Yes, but sparse | $O(\vert V \vert)$ per word | | **Dense Embedding (Word2Vec)** | $d$ (e.g., 50–300) | Yes, dense | $O(d)$ per word | The intuition dictates a **compression** of the sparse distributional information into a dense semantic fingerprint. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **exam-critical mathematical backbone** of the Intuition topic. Memorize it. | # | Concept | Formula | Explanation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Distributional Hypothesis** | $\text{sim}(w_1, w_2) \propto f(C(w_1), C(w_2))$ | Similarity is a function of contextual overlap. | | 2 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos(\vec{u}, \vec{v}) = \dfrac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\vert\vert\vec{u}\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert\vec{v}\vert\vert}$ | Standard metric in embedding space, range $[-1, 1]$. | | 3 | **Euclidean Distance** | $d(\vec{u}, \vec{v}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(u_i - v_i)^2}$ | Geometric distance; inversely related to similarity. | | 4 | **Semantic Offset (Analogy)** | $\vec{w}_4 \approx \vec{w}_1 - \vec{w}_2 + \vec{w}_3$ | E.g., $\vec{queen} \approx \vec{king} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman}$. | | 5 | **Embedding Matrix** | $E \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times d}$ | Each row is the dense vector of a vocabulary word. | | 6 | **One-Hot Lookup** | $\vec{w} = E^T \vec{e}_w$ | $\vec{e}_w$ is the one-hot vector of $w$. | | 7 | **Cosine for One-Hot** | $\cos(\vec{e}_i, \vec{e}_j) = 0, \ \forall i \neq j$ | Demonstrates the failure of one-hot encoding. | | 8 | **Skip-gram Softmax** | $P(c \mid w) = \dfrac{\exp(\vec{v}_c \cdot \vec{v}_w)}{\sum_{c' \in V} \exp(\vec{v}_{c'} \cdot \vec{v}_w)}$ | Context probability given center word. | | 9 | **CBOW Softmax** | $P(w \mid c) = \dfrac{\exp(\vec{v}_w \cdot \bar{\vec{v}}_c)}{\sum_{w' \in V} \exp(\vec{v}_{w'} \cdot \bar{\vec{v}}_c)}$ | Center word probability given context window. | | 10 | **Co-occurrence Vector (raw)** | $\vec{w} = [\text{count}(w, c_1), \text{count}(w, c_2), \ldots, \text{count}(w, c_n)]$ | Naive distributional representation. | > [!WARNING] > **KTU LaTeX Pitfall:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in formulas inside markdown tables (e.g., $\vert V \vert$, $\vert\vert\vec{u}\vert\vert$) — never the raw pipe `|`. The raw pipe breaks the table renderer in PDF generation. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The intuition behind embeddings is **not academic** — it powers the following production systems: - **Search Engines** (Google, Bing) — query-document matching via embedding cosine similarity. - **Recommendation Systems** (Netflix, Spotify) — user/item embeddings for collaborative filtering. - **Machine Translation** — cross-lingual word alignment. - **Sentiment Analysis** — feed embeddings into classifiers. - **Named Entity Recognition & POS Tagging** — embedding-augmented CRFs and BiLSTMs. - **Large Language Models (LLMs)** — every modern LLM begins with an embedding layer trained on this very intuition. ## 2.5 The Intuition Flowchart (Conceptual Logic Chain) The reasoning chain that transforms raw text into semantic vectors: 1. **Observe** large text corpora (e.g., Wikipedia, Common Crawl). 2. **Record** co-occurrence statistics of every word with its neighbors. 3. **Assume** distributional hypothesis (similar context = similar meaning). 4. **Encode** these statistics into a dense matrix $E \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times d}$. 5. **Measure** semantic similarity using cosine similarity. 6. **Verify** that linguistic analogies become vector arithmetic. 7. **Apply** embeddings to downstream NLP tasks. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Co-occurrence to Embedding Intuition We will rigorously derive **why** the distributional hypothesis mathematically forces semantically similar words to cluster in vector space. ### Step 1 — Define the Co-occurrence Matrix Let $C \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times \vert V \vert}$ be a symmetric word–word co-occurrence matrix, where $C_{ij}$ counts the number of times word $j$ appears in the context window of word $i$ within a corpus. For a corpus of $N$ tokens, the simplest definition is: $$C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[w_k = i] \cdot \mathbb{1}[\exists \, t \in (k - L, k + L), \, t \neq k : w_t = j]$$ Where $L$ is the half-window size and $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function. ### Step 2 — Row Vectors as Distributional Fingerprints The $i$-th row of $C$, denoted $\vec{r}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$, is the raw distributional signature of word $i$: $$\vec{r}_i = [C_{i1}, C_{i2}, \ldots, C_{i \vert V \vert}]$$ > **Intuition:** If two words $i$ and $j$ share many of the same context words, their row vectors will have **overlapping non-zero entries** at the same indices. This is the **first formal manifestation** of the distributional hypothesis. ### Step 3 — Apply a Reweighting Scheme (e.g., PPMI) The raw counts are dominated by high-frequency words ("the", "of", "is"). We apply **Positive Pointwise Mutual Information (PPMI)**: $$\text{PMI}(w_i, w_j) = \log_2 \left( \frac{P(w_i, w_j)}{P(w_i) \cdot P(w_j)} \right)$$ Where: $$P(w_i, w_j) = \frac{C_{ij}}{\sum_{i,j} C_{ij}}, \quad P(w_i) = \frac{\sum_j C_{ij}}{\sum_{i,j} C_{ij}}$$ The PPMI transformation is: $$\text{PPMI}(w_i, w_j) = \max\left(0, \text{PMI}(w_i, w_j)\right)$$ ### Step 4 — Dimensionality Reduction via SVD The PPMI matrix $M$ is huge ($\vert V \vert \times \vert V \vert$) and noisy. We factor it via **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)**: $$M = U \Sigma V^T$$ Where $U, V \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times \vert V \vert}$ are orthogonal and $\Sigma = \text{diag}(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \ldots, \sigma_{\vert V \vert})$ with $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \ldots \geq \sigma_{\vert V \vert} \geq 0$. We retain only the top $d$ singular values: $$M_d = U_d \Sigma_d V_d^T$$ The embedding of word $i$ is the $i$-th row of $U_d \Sigma_d$: $$\vec{e}_i = (\vec{u}_i)^T \Sigma_d \in \mathbb{R}^d$$ > **Intuition in Plain English:** SVD finds the **principal directions of co-occurrence variation**. Words that vary together (i.e., co-occur with similar partners) end up projected onto the **same principal axes**, which is why they cluster in the reduced space. ### Step 5 — Justification of Cosine Similarity in Embedding Space Why cosine and not Euclidean distance? We want to measure the **angular closeness** of two word vectors, not their magnitudes. Two words with identical meaning but different corpus frequencies should be **close** — frequency inflates vector magnitude but not direction. $$\cos(\vec{e}_i, \vec{e}_j) = \frac{\vec{e}_i \cdot \vec{e}_j}{\vert\vert\vec{e}_i\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert\vec{e}_j\vert\vert}$$ A cosine value close to **+1** means the words are semantically synonymous; close to **0** means unrelated; close to **−1** means antonymous. ## 3.2 Worked Example — The King–Queen Analogy Consider a tiny corpus-derived embedding in 2D (after training). Suppose the trained vectors are: $$\vec{king} = [0.5, \ 0.7], \quad \vec{queen} = [0.6, \ 0.8]$$ $$\vec{man} = [0.3, \ 0.2], \quad \vec{woman} = [0.4, \ 0.3]$$ **Step 1:** Compute the gender direction: $$\vec{king} - \vec{man} = [0.5 - 0.3, \ 0.7 - 0.2] = [0.2, \ 0.5]$$ **Step 2:** Add the female offset: $$[0.2, \ 0.5] + \vec{woman} = [0.2 + 0.4, \ 0.5 + 0.3] = [0.6, \ 0.8]$$ **Step 3:** Compare with $\vec{queen}$: $$[0.6, \ 0.8] \approx \vec{queen} = [0.6, \ 0.8] \quad \checkmark$$ The reconstruction is exact. This is **not magic** — it is the deterministic consequence of the distributional hypothesis being correctly encoded into the geometry of the embedding space. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Representation Schemes | Property | One-Hot | Co-occurrence (Raw) | PPMI + SVD | Word2Vec (NN) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Dimensions** | $\vert V \vert$ | $\vert V \vert$ | $d \ll \vert V \vert$ | $d \ll \vert V \vert$ | | **Sparsity** | Extreme | High | Moderate | None (dense) | | **Captures Semantics** | ❌ No | ⚠️ Partial | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **Captures Analogy** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **Scalability** | ✅ Trivial | ❌ $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | ⚠️ Moderate | ✅ Efficient | | **Training Time** | N/A | N/A | Slow (SVD) | Fast (SGD) | | **Out-of-vocab handling** | ✅ Easy | ✅ Easy | ✅ Easy | ❌ Poor (mitigated by FastText) | ## 3.4 The Mathematical "Why" Behind the Intuition The most rigorous way to justify the distributional intuition is via the **Johnson–Lindenstrauss Lemma**: > For any set of $n$ points in a high-dimensional space, there exists a Lipschitz mapping into a $d$-dimensional space (with $d = O(\log n / \epsilon^2)$) that approximately preserves all pairwise distances within a factor of $(1 \pm \epsilon)$. In other words, the **semantic distances** between words (measured by contextual overlap) can be **preserved** under dimensionality reduction. This is the formal guarantee that SVD-style embeddings are mathematically sound. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Pipeline from Raw Text to Word Embeddings ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Corpus
e.g., Wikipedia] --> B[Tokenization &
Vocabulary Building] B --> C[Context Window
Definition L=2 to L=5] C --> D[Co-occurrence
Matrix Construction] D --> E[Reweighting
TF-IDF or PPMI] E --> F[Dimensionality
Reduction via SVD] F --> G[Dense Embedding
Matrix E in R^V x d] G --> H[Cosine Similarity
Computation] H --> I[Semantic
Applications] I --> I1[Word Analogy
king - man + woman approx queen] I --> I2[Document
Classification] I --> I3[Information
Retrieval] I --> I4[NER and POS
Tagging] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Distributional Hypothesis Visualization ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Context_1["Context around 'doctor'"] C1A[patient] --> Doc[doctor] Doc --> C1B[hospital] Doc --> C1C[stethoscope] end subgraph Context_2["Context around 'nurse'"] C2A[patient] --> Nur[nurse] Nur --> C2B[hospital] Nur --> C2C[medicine] end Doc -.similar.-> Nur Doc -.similar.-> Sur[surgeon] Sur -.shares context.-> Doc Sur -.shares context.-> Nur ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** Words whose context windows overlap heavily (e.g., `doctor`, `nurse`, `surgeon`) end up as **close points** in the embedding space. This overlap is the geometric manifestation of the distributional hypothesis. ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Embedding Space Geometry (2D Projection) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph EmbedSpace["2D Projection of Embedding Space"] K[king] Q[queen] M[man] W[woman] P[prince] PR[princess] end K --- Q M --- W K --- M Q --- W P --- PR K --- P Q --- PR ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** The geometric relationships mirror linguistic ones. The vector $\overrightarrow{king \to queen}$ is parallel to $\overrightarrow{man \to woman}$ and to $\overrightarrow{prince \to princess}$, encoding the universal *"gender"* axis. ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: CBOW vs. Skip-gram Architecture (High-Level Block View) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CBOW["CBOW - Continuous Bag of Words"] CBOW_In[Context Words
w_t-2, w_t-1, w_t+1, w_t+2] --> CBOW_Embed[Embedding Layer
Average or Sum] CBOW_Embed --> CBOW_Hid[Hidden Layer
d dimensions] CBOW_Hid --> CBOW_Out[Output Softmax
Predict center w_t] end subgraph SkipGram["Skip-gram"] SG_In[Center Word
w_t] --> SG_Embed[Embedding Layer] SG_Embed --> SG_Hid[Hidden Layer
d dimensions] SG_Hid --> SG_Out[Output Softmax
Predict context words] end ``` > **Intuition for the Block Diagram:** Both architectures are **engineered implementations** of the same distributional hypothesis. CBOW predicts a word from its context; Skip-gram predicts context from a word. They differ in input/output roles but are mathematically duals. ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: SVD Pipeline Block View ```mermaid flowchart TD M[Co-occurrence Matrix M
size V x V] --> P[Apply PPMI Weighting] P --> SVD[Singular Value
Decomposition
M = U Sigma V^T] SVD --> Truncate[Keep top d
singular values] Truncate --> E[Embedding Matrix
U_d times Sigma_d
size V x d] E --> Use[Use rows as
word vectors] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** State and explain Firth's Distributional Hypothesis. Why is it considered the foundation of modern word embedding techniques? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Firth's Distributional Hypothesis, proposed by the linguist J. R. Firth in 1957, states: > *"You shall know a word by the company it keeps."* Formally, it asserts that two words are semantically similar if they appear in similar linguistic contexts. The hypothesis is the philosophical and mathematical foundation of all modern word embedding techniques (Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText, BERT) because it provides a **supervisory signal** derived from raw text alone — no human-labeled semantic databases are required. A neural network or matrix factorization method can automatically learn that *"king"* and *"queen"* are related by observing that they both frequently co-occur with words like *"royal", "palace", "crown", and "throne"*. Without this hypothesis, the entire field of distributional semantics would be theoretically unjustified. *(1 mark: stating the hypothesis. 1 mark: formal explanation. 1 mark: connecting to modern embeddings.)* ### Question 2 > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Distinguish between sparse and dense word representations. Why are dense embeddings preferred for downstream NLP tasks? **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Sparse (One-Hot / Co-occurrence) | Dense (Word2Vec / GloVe) | |---|---|---| | **Dimensionality** | $\vert V \vert$ (very high) | $d$ (e.g., 50–300) | | **Non-zero entries** | Few (mostly zeros) | All (every dimension carries meaning) | | **Semantic capture** | Limited / None for one-hot | High | | **Storage cost** | $O(\vert V \vert)$ per word | $O(d)$ per word | Dense embeddings are preferred because: 1. They capture **semantic similarity** (cosine of related words is high). 2. They are **computationally efficient** for downstream models (RNNs, transformers). 3. They **generalize** to unseen word pairs by virtue of smooth geometric proximity. *(1 mark: definitions. 1 mark: comparison table. 1 mark: reasons for preference.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) > **`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`** (a) Explain the intuition behind representing words as dense vectors. How does this intuition differ from the classical one-hot encoding approach? Discuss with a suitable example. > **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the Skip-gram and CBOW architectures of Word2Vec. Show how both models embody the distributional hypothesis. **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — 7 marks **The Intuition (3 marks):** The intuition behind word embeddings is rooted in the **Distributional Hypothesis**. Words that occur in similar contexts tend to have similar meanings. This single observation justifies mapping every word to a **dense, low-dimensional, real-valued vector** such that: $$\text{contextual similarity} \iff \text{geometric proximity in } \mathbb{R}^d$$ The formal goal is to learn a function $f: V \to \mathbb{R}^d$ such that for any two words $w_1, w_2$: $$\cos(f(w_1), f(w_2)) \approx \text{Sim}(w_1, w_2)$$ **Comparison with One-Hot (2 marks):** In one-hot encoding, each word $w_i$ is assigned a vector $\vec{e}_i \in \{0,1\}^{\vert V \vert}$ with a single 1 at position $i$. The dot product of any two distinct one-hot vectors is 0, so cosine similarity is **identically zero** for all distinct words. This means the model has **no information** about the semantic relationship between "apple" and "orange" vs. "apple" and "airplane" — they are all equally "different". In contrast, dense embeddings place semantically related words in **the same region of space**: $$\cos(\vec{e}_{\text{apple}}, \vec{e}_{\text{orange}}) = 0.87, \quad \cos(\vec{e}_{\text{apple}}, \vec{e}_{\text{airplane}}) = 0.12$$ **Example (2 marks):** Consider the sentence *"The kitten purred softly as it drank milk."* and *"The puppy barked loudly as it drank water."* The words *kitten* and *puppy* both appear in similar syntactic and semantic slots (subject of a sentence, with a verb describing a sound, drinking something). A well-trained embedding would place $\vec{\text{kitten}}$ and $\vec{\text{puppy}}$ close together, while pushing $\vec{\text{airplane}}$ far away — **without any explicit supervision**. #### Part (b) — 7 marks **Skip-gram (3.5 marks):** Skip-gram uses a **center word** to predict the surrounding context words within a window of size $L$. - **Input:** The one-hot vector $\vec{e}_{w_t}$ of the center word at position $t$. - **Hidden Layer:** A dense projection $\vec{h} = W \vec{e}_{w_t}$, where $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times \vert V \vert}$ is the embedding matrix. Effectively, $\vec{h}$ is the row of $W$ corresponding to $w_t$. - **Output:** A softmax over the entire vocabulary for each context position: $$P(c \mid w_t) = \frac{\exp(\vec{v}_c \cdot \vec{h})}{\sum_{c' \in V} \exp(\vec{v}_{c'} \cdot \vec{h})}$$ The training objective maximizes the sum of log-probabilities of actual context words. **CBOW — Continuous Bag of Words (3.5 marks):** CBOW is the **mirror image** of Skip-gram. It uses the surrounding context words to predict the center word. - **Input:** The averaged (or summed) one-hot vectors of context words $\{w_{t-L}, \ldots, w_{t-1}, w_{t+1}, \ldots, w_{t+L}\}$. - **Hidden Layer:** $\vec{h} = \frac{1}{2L} \sum_{j \neq t, \vert t-j \vert \leq L} W \vec{e}_{w_j}$. - **Output:** Softmax over the vocabulary, predicting $w_t$. **How Both Embody the Distributional Hypothesis:** Both architectures **implicitly assume** that words with similar co-occurrence distributions should produce similar predictions. The shared hidden representations, when trained via backpropagation, are forced to **encode contextual equivalence** into the geometry of the embedding space. This is precisely the distributional hypothesis, operationalized as a maximum-likelihood objective. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) > **`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`** (a) Derive the PPMI (Positive Pointwise Mutual Information) reweighting of a co-occurrence matrix. Why is PPMI preferred over raw counts? > **(b)** With reference to SVD-based word embeddings, show how dimensionality reduction transforms sparse co-occurrence vectors into dense semantic embeddings. Why is the Johnson–Lindenstrauss Lemma relevant here? **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — 7 marks **Derivation (5 marks):** Let $C \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times \vert V \vert}$ be the word–word co-occurrence matrix. We define the joint probability: $$P(w_i, w_j) = \frac{C_{ij}}{N}, \quad \text{where } N = \sum_{i,j} C_{ij}$$ The marginal probabilities are: $$P(w_i) = \frac{\sum_j C_{ij}}{N}, \quad P(w_j) = \frac{\sum_i C_{ij}}{N}$$ **Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI)** measures how much more often two words co-occur than expected under independence: $$\text{PMI}(w_i, w_j) = \log_2 \left( \frac{P(w_i, w_j)}{P(w_i) \cdot P(w_j)} \right)$$ Plugging in the definitions: $$\text{PMI}(w_i, w_j) = \log_2 \left( \frac{C_{ij} \cdot N}{(\sum_k C_{ik}) \cdot (\sum_k C_{kj})} \right)$$ **Negative PMI values** occur when two words co-occur *less* than chance — these are usually noise. We clip them to zero: $$\text{PPMI}(w_i, w_j) = \max\left(0, \text{PMI}(w_i, w_j)\right)$$ **Why PPMI over raw counts? (2 marks):** 1. **Frequency bias removal:** Raw counts over-emphasize high-frequency words like *"the"*, *"of"*, *"is"*. PPMI normalizes by expected frequency, surfacing genuine semantic associations. 2. **Information-theoretic justification:** PMI is a well-defined measure from information theory with clear probabilistic meaning. 3. **Reduced noise:** Negative values (which are typically uninformative) are clipped, improving the signal-to-noise ratio. #### Part (b) — 7 marks **SVD Pipeline (5 marks):** Given the PPMI matrix $M \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times \vert V \vert}$, we compute its **Singular Value Decomposition**: $$M = U \Sigma V^T$$ Where $U, V \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times \vert V \vert}$ are orthogonal matrices (i.e., $U^T U = I$, $V^T V = I$) and $\Sigma = \text{diag}(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \ldots, \sigma_{\vert V \vert})$ with singular values in descending order. We truncate to the top $d$ components: $$M_d = U_d \Sigma_d V_d^T$$ The **word embedding** for word $i$ is the $i$-th row of $U_d \Sigma_d$: $$\vec{e}_i = (U_d \Sigma_d)_{i,:} \in \mathbb{R}^d$$ **Why this works (the intuition):** The top $d$ singular vectors correspond to the directions of **maximum variance** in the co-occurrence distribution. Words that share similar co-occurrence profiles project onto the same top singular vectors, so they end up with similar embeddings. The discarded dimensions correspond to low-variance noise that would otherwise dilute the semantic signal. **Johnson–Lindenstrauss Lemma (2 marks):** The Johnson–Lindenstrauss (JL) Lemma states: > For any set of $n$ points in a high-dimensional Euclidean space, there exists a linear mapping into a $d$-dimensional space (with $d = O(\epsilon^{-2} \log n)$) such that all pairwise distances are preserved within a multiplicative factor of $(1 \pm \epsilon)$. **Relevance:** The JL Lemma provides a **mathematical guarantee** that the dimensionality reduction performed by SVD is *not* lossy in a meaningful sense. The semantic distances between words (as measured by cosine similarity in the original PPMI space) are approximately preserved in the lower-dimensional embedding space. This is what makes the entire SVD-based pipeline theoretically sound, not merely empirically effective. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in the "Intuition" Topic:** > > 1. **Confusing intuition with implementation** (–2 marks): Students often launch into Word2Vec equations without first explaining *why* we need embeddings. The intuition lecture is precisely about the *why*. KTU examiners specifically allocate 3–4 marks for the conceptual "why" in any 14-mark question. > > 2. **Misquoting Firth** (–1 mark): Writing *"a word is known by the words it keeps"* is acceptable; writing *"a word is defined by its dictionary entry"* is a fatal conceptual error. > > 3. **Confusing cosine similarity with Euclidean distance** (–2 marks): Always explicitly state that **cosine measures angle (direction)**, not magnitude. In one-hot encoding, the angle is undefined (all vectors are orthogonal) — this is the precise technical reason one-hot fails. > > 4. **Omitting the JL Lemma** (–1 mark): For derivation questions on SVD-based embeddings, students who skip the JL Lemma are flagged as lacking mathematical maturity — KTU examiners deduct marks for this. > > 5. **Skipping the arithmetic verification of the analogy** (–1 mark): When explaining $\vec{king} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{queen}$, always show the **numerical computation**, not just the abstract equation. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Distributional Hypothesis** is the philosophical and mathematical foundation — Firth, 1957. - **Words = vectors** in a dense, low-dimensional space; similar context → similar vector. - **One-hot encoding fails** because cosine similarity between any two distinct words is 0. - **Cosine similarity** (not Euclidean) is the standard similarity metric in embedding space, range $[-1, +1]$. - **King–Queen–Man–Woman analogy**: $\vec{king} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{queen}$ is the canonical example of semantic arithmetic. - **PPMI** reweights raw co-occurrence counts to remove frequency bias and highlight genuine associations. - **SVD** reduces the PPMI matrix from $\vert V \vert$-dimensional to $d$-dimensional embeddings while preserving semantic structure. - **Johnson–Lindenstrauss Lemma** guarantees that such dimensionality reduction approximately preserves pairwise distances. - **Word2Vec** has two architectures: **Skip-gram** (predict context from center) and **CBOW** (predict center from context) — both are operationalizations of the distributional hypothesis. - **Dense embeddings** are preferred for downstream NLP tasks due to computational efficiency and semantic richness. - **Vector space models** underpin all modern NLP, including LLMs like BERT, GPT, and T5. - **KTU exam tip:** Always begin any 14-mark answer on this topic with the distributional hypothesis — it signals conceptual clarity and immediately earns 2–3 marks.

topics.title: Spam Detection Using Naïve Bayes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Spam Detection Using Naïve Bayes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Spam Detection Using Naïve Bayes** is a supervised text classification technique in Natural Language Processing (NLP) that probabilistically classifies an incoming email (or message/document) as either **SPAM** (unsolicited, junk) or **HAM** (legitimate) by applying **Bayes' Theorem** under the **conditional independence assumption** over the words (tokens) appearing in that document.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Naïve Bayes is a *probabilistic generative classifier* based on applying Bayes' theorem with the "naïve" assumption that every feature (word) is conditionally independent of every other feature, given the class label. For a document $D$ composed of $n$ features $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$ and class $c \in \{spam, ham\}$, the classifier selects:
>
> $$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in \{spam, ham\}} \; P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \vert c)$$

**Spam** refers to unsolicited, bulk, or fraudulent messages (phishing, advertisements, scams), while **Ham** refers to legitimate, wanted emails.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bayes' Theorem (Foundational Identity)** — For two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:
>
> $$P(A \vert B) = \dfrac{P(B \vert A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$
>
> In the NLP context: $A$ = class label (Spam/Ham), $B$ = observed word/document.

## 1.2 Intuition — Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **"The Judge Who Reads Only One Clue at a Time"**
> Imagine a judge deciding whether a letter is *real fan mail* or *promotional junk*. Instead of reading the whole letter as one giant interconnected story, the judge scans it as **independent hints**:
> - Does it contain the word *"free"*? +1 vote for spam.
> - Does it contain *"offer"*? +1 vote for spam.
> - Does it contain *"meeting"*? +1 vote for ham.
> - Does it contain *"project"*? +1 vote for ham.
>
> The judge **multiplies** the historical tendencies of each word (learned from past letters) and **picks whichever side scores higher**. This is exactly what Naïve Bayes does — it ignores word-order/grammar dependencies and treats every word as an *independent witness* for or against spam.

The *naïveté* lies in the assumption that words like "free" and "money" appearing together in a spam email are **statistically independent** events given the spam class — an assumption that is *mathematically false* in real text but works remarkably well in practice and is computationally cheap.

## 1.3 Why Naïve Bayes for Spam Filtering?

| Property | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Fast training** | Only counts of words per class are required (one pass). |
| **Fast inference** | Just multiplication of per-word probabilities. |
| **Handles high-dimensional sparse data** | Email vocabulary can be 50,000+ words; NB scales linearly. |
| **Works well with little data** | Needs only a few hundred labeled emails to give reasonable accuracy. |
| **Probabilistic output** | Gives a *confidence score*, not just a label — useful for threshold tuning. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Use-Case Highlights (KTU Context):**
> - **Gmail / Yahoo Mail spam filters** historically use Naïve Bayes variants.
> - **SMS spam detection** in mobile OSes (e.g., Android Messages).
> - **YouTube / social media comment moderation** for spam/abuse detection.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Pipeline — How Naïve Bayes Classifies an Email

The classification of a document $D = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$ into class $c$ proceeds in **six logical stages**:

1. **Tokenization & Preprocessing:** Split the email into individual words (tokens). Normalize case, remove punctuation, optionally remove stop-words and apply stemming.
2. **Feature Extraction:** Represent $D$ as a *Bag-of-Words* vector — either binary presence, raw counts, or TF-IDF weights.
3. **Compute Class Priors $P(c)$:** The fraction of training documents belonging to class $c$.

   $$P(c) = \frac{N_c}{N_{total}}$$

   where $N_c$ = number of training docs in class $c$.

4. **Compute Likelihoods $P(w_i \vert c)$:** The probability that word $w_i$ appears given the class. Two common estimators:

   - **Multinomial Naïve Bayes** (uses word *frequency*):
     $$P(w_i \vert c) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c)}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c)}$$
   - **Bernoulli Naïve Bayes** (uses word *presence/absence*):
     $$P(w_i \vert c) = \frac{\text{docs in } c \text{ containing } w_i + \text{smoothing}}{N_c + 2}$$
   - **Gaussian NB** — assumes continuous features follow a normal distribution (rare for text).

5. **Apply Naïve (Conditional Independence) Assumption:**
   $$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n \vert c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \vert c)$$
   This is the heart of the algorithm — it converts an *intractable* joint probability into a *simple product*.

6. **Posterior Decision Rule (MAP — Maximum A Posteriori):**
   $$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} \; P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \vert c)$$

   Since $P(D)$ is constant across classes, it is dropped from the argmax.

## 2.2 The Zero-Probability Problem & Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** If a test email contains a word never seen in training (e.g., "cryptocurrency" if not in spam training data), then $P(w_i \vert c) = 0$, which makes the **entire product equal to zero**, even if every other word strongly indicates spam. This is the **zero-frequency / zero-probability problem**.

**Laplace Smoothing** (also called *add-one* or *add-k* smoothing) fixes this by adding a small constant $\alpha$ (typically 1) to every count:

$$P(w_i \vert c) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c) + \alpha}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

where $\vert V \vert$ is the size of the vocabulary. This guarantees that no probability is exactly zero.

## 2.3 Log-Space Computation (Underflow Prevention)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Underflow Warning:** When a document contains many words (say 200), multiplying 200 small probabilities (each < 1) results in a number smaller than the floating-point precision limit ($\approx 10^{-308}$), causing **arithmetic underflow** to zero.

**Solution — Logarithmic Transformation:** Since $\log$ is a *monotonically increasing* function, maximizing $P(c \vert D)$ is equivalent to maximizing $\log P(c \vert D)$:

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} \; \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \vert c)$$

This converts products into *sums* (numerically stable) and is the **standard implementation practice**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below summarizes **every formula** you must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic. **No pipes are used inside numeric notation; absolute value uses the word form.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(c \vert D) = \dfrac{P(D \vert c)\,P(c)}{P(D)}$ | Probabilities are dimensionless in $[0,1]$ |
| 2 | Class Prior | $P(c) = \dfrac{N_c}{N_{total}}$ | $N_c$ = docs in class $c$ |
| 3 | Multinomial Likelihood | $P(w_i \vert c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i, c)}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c)}$ | Uses raw word frequencies |
| 4 | Bernoulli Likelihood | $P(w_i \vert c) = \dfrac{\text{docs containing } w_i \text{ in } c}{N_c}$ | Binary presence/absence |
| 5 | Naïve Independence Assumption | $P(w_1,\dots,w_n \vert c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \vert c)$ | The "naïve" part |
| 6 | MAP Decision Rule | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c \; P(c)\prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \vert c)$ | Pick class with highest posterior |
| 7 | Laplace Smoothing | $P(w_i \vert c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i, c) + \alpha}{\sum \text{count}(w, c) + \alpha \cdot \lvert V \rvert}$ | $\alpha = 1$ is standard |
| 8 | Log-Probability Form | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c \; \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n}\log P(w_i \vert c)$ | Avoids underflow |
| 9 | Accuracy | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Performance metric |
| 10 | Precision | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of mails flagged spam, how many are truly spam |
| 11 | Recall | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of all true spam, how many were caught |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Email gateways** (e.g., SpamAssassin, Apache James) deploy Multinomial NB as a first-pass filter, followed by deeper rule engines.
- **Phishing detection** in browsers and corporate security stacks.
- **SMS fraud filtering** on telecom networks (especially in regions where SMS is a payment channel).
- **Scalability:** Training scales **O(N × V)** (linear in training data); inference is **O(V)** per document.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — End-to-End Spam Classification

This is the **canonical KTU-style numerical problem** you must master. Pay attention to every algebraic transition.

### 3.1.1 Training Corpus Setup

We have **10 training emails** with the vocabulary $V = \{\, \text{free},\ \text{money},\ \text{buy},\ \text{now},\ \text{project},\ \text{meeting} \,\}$ and $|V| = 6$.

**HAM emails (legitimate) — 6 documents:**

| Doc ID | Text | Class |
|---|---|---|
| $D_1$ | project meeting | HAM |
| $D_2$ | meeting project | HAM |
| $D_3$ | buy project now | HAM |
| $D_4$ | meeting now project | HAM |
| $D_5$ | buy meeting | HAM |
| $D_6$ | project meeting buy | HAM |

**SPAM emails — 4 documents:**

| Doc ID | Text | Class |
|---|---|---|
| $D_7$ | free money | SPAM |
| $D_8$ | free money buy | SPAM |
| $D_9$ | free money buy | SPAM |
| $D_{10}$ | free buy now | SPAM |

> **Test Email $D_{test}$:** "free money buy" — Classify this email.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute Class Priors

$$P(\text{HAM}) = \frac{N_{\text{HAM}}}{N_{total}} = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6$$

$$P(\text{SPAM}) = \frac{N_{\text{SPAM}}}{N_{total}} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Build Word-Count Vectors Per Class

**Counts in HAM class** (total words in HAM = 13):

| Word | Count in HAM |
|---|---|
| project | 4 |
| meeting | 4 |
| buy | 3 |
| now | 2 |
| free | 0 |
| money | 0 |
| **Total** | **13** |

**Counts in SPAM class** (total words in SPAM = 11):

| Word | Count in SPAM |
|---|---|
| free | 4 |
| money | 3 |
| buy | 3 |
| now | 1 |
| project | 0 |
| meeting | 0 |
| **Total** | **11** |

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Without Laplace Smoothing (Naïve Computation)

For the test document $D_{test} = \{\text{free}, \text{money}, \text{buy}\}$:

$$P(\text{HAM} \vert D_{test}) \propto P(\text{HAM}) \cdot P(\text{free}\vert \text{HAM}) \cdot P(\text{money}\vert \text{HAM}) \cdot P(\text{buy}\vert \text{HAM})$$

$$\propto 0.6 \cdot \frac{0}{13} \cdot \frac{0}{13} \cdot \frac{3}{13}$$

$$= 0.6 \cdot 0 \cdot 0 \cdot \frac{3}{13} = 0$$

$$P(\text{SPAM} \vert D_{test}) \propto P(\text{SPAM}) \cdot P(\text{free}\vert \text{SPAM}) \cdot P(\text{money}\vert \text{SPAM}) \cdot P(\text{buy}\vert \text{SPAM})$$

$$\propto 0.4 \cdot \frac{4}{11} \cdot \frac{3}{11} \cdot \frac{3}{11}$$

$$= 0.4 \cdot \frac{36}{1331} = 0.4 \cdot 0.02705 \approx 0.01082$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Observation:** Without smoothing, the HAM posterior becomes exactly **zero** because the word *"free"* never appeared in HAM training documents. This is the famous **zero-frequency problem**. We **cannot conclude** that the email is spam from this result alone — smoothing is mandatory.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Apply Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing, $\alpha = 1$, $|V| = 6$

**Smoothed likelihoods for HAM:**

$$P(w_i \vert \text{HAM}) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, \text{HAM}) + 1}{13 + 6} = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, \text{HAM}) + 1}{19}$$

| Word | count | count + 1 | $P(w_i \vert \text{HAM})$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| free | 0 | 1 | $\dfrac{1}{19} \approx 0.05263$ |
| money | 0 | 1 | $\dfrac{1}{19} \approx 0.05263$ |
| buy | 3 | 4 | $\dfrac{4}{19} \approx 0.21053$ |

**Smoothed likelihoods for SPAM:**

$$P(w_i \vert \text{SPAM}) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, \text{SPAM}) + 1}{11 + 6} = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, \text{SPAM}) + 1}{17}$$

| Word | count | count + 1 | $P(w_i \vert \text{SPAM})$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| free | 4 | 5 | $\dfrac{5}{17} \approx 0.29412$ |
| money | 3 | 4 | $\dfrac{4}{17} \approx 0.23529$ |
| buy | 3 | 4 | $\dfrac{4}{17} \approx 0.23529$ |

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Compute Smoothed Posteriors (Unnormalized)

$$P(\text{HAM} \vert D_{test}) \propto 0.6 \cdot \frac{1}{19} \cdot \frac{1}{19} \cdot \frac{4}{19}$$

$$= \frac{0.6 \cdot 4}{19^3} = \frac{2.4}{6859} \approx 0.0003499$$

$$P(\text{SPAM} \vert D_{test}) \propto 0.4 \cdot \frac{5}{17} \cdot \frac{4}{17} \cdot \frac{4}{17}$$

$$= \frac{0.4 \cdot 80}{17^3} = \frac{32}{4913} \approx 0.006514$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Normalize to True Probabilities

$$\text{Total} = 0.0003499 + 0.006514 = 0.006864$$

$$P(\text{HAM} \vert D_{test}) = \frac{0.0003499}{0.006864} \approx 0.0510 \; (5.10\%)$$

$$P(\text{SPAM} \vert D_{test}) = \frac{0.006514}{0.006864} \approx 0.9490 \; (94.90\%)$$

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Final Classification (Argmax Decision Rule)

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_c \{P(\text{HAM} \vert D_{test}),\; P(\text{SPAM} \vert D_{test})\}$$

$$= \arg\max_c \{0.0510,\; 0.9490\} = \textbf{SPAM}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The email "free money buy" is classified as **SPAM** with **94.90% confidence**. This matches intuition: words "free" and "money" are strong spam indicators in the training data.

### 3.1.9 Verification Using Log-Space (Numerical Stability Check)

$$\log P(\text{HAM} \vert D_{test}) = \log(0.6) + \log(\tfrac{1}{19}) + \log(\tfrac{1}{19}) + \log(\tfrac{4}{19})$$

$$= -0.5108 + (-2.9444) + (-2.9444) + (-1.5581) = -7.9577$$

$$\log P(\text{SPAM} \vert D_{test}) = \log(0.4) + \log(\tfrac{5}{17}) + \log(\tfrac{4}{17}) + \log(\tfrac{4}{17})$$

$$= -0.9163 + (-1.2238) + (-1.4436) + (-1.4436) = -5.0273$$

Since $-5.0273 > -7.9577$, the SPAM class wins — same conclusion as before. **Log-space yields the same decision but never underflows.**

## 3.2 Confusion Matrix for Spam Filters (KTU High-Yield)

|  | **Predicted SPAM** | **Predicted HAM** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual SPAM** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) — *missed spam, lands in inbox!* |
| **Actual HAM** | False Positive (FP) — *legitimate email wrongly blocked!* | True Negative (TN) |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** A spam filter must balance **Recall** (catch all spam) against **Precision** (don't block important emails). Increasing the spam-classification threshold (e.g., from 50% to 80%) raises Precision but lowers Recall.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Spam Detection Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Email Text Input] --> B0[Tokenization]
    B0 --> C0[Lowercasing and Stopword Removal]
    C0 --> D0[Stemming and Lemmatization]
    D0 --> E0[Vocabulary Lookup Bag of Words Vector]
    E0 --> F0{Multinomial or Bernoulli NB Model}
    F0 --> G0[Compute Priors P Ham and P Spam]
    G0 --> H0[Compute Likelihoods P word class]
    H0 --> I0[Apply Laplace Smoothing alpha equals 1]
    I0 --> J0[Apply Naive Independence Assumption Multiply]
    J0 --> K0[Compare Posteriors via Argmax]
    K0 --> L0{Decision}
    L0 --> L1[Classify as SPAM]
    L0 --> L2[Classify as HAM]
    L1 --> M0[Route to Junk Folder]
    L2 --> N0[Deliver to Inbox]
```

## 4.2 Training Phase vs. Inference Phase Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAINING_PHASE
        T0[Labeled Email Corpus N documents] --> T1[Tokenize Each Document]
        T1 --> T2[Build Word Count Matrix]
        T2 --> T3[Compute P class per class]
        T2 --> T4[Compute P word class with Laplace]
        T3 --> T5[Store as Model Parameters]
        T4 --> T5
    end
    subgraph INFERENCE_PHASE
        I0[New Unseen Email] --> I1[Tokenize]
        I1 --> I2[Apply Stored Model]
        T5 -.lookup.-> I2
        I2 --> I3[Compute Log Posteriors]
        I3 --> I4[Argmax Decision]
        I4 --> I5[Output Label Spam or Ham]
    end
```

## 4.3 Laplace Smoothing Mechanism (Detail View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Raw count of word in class] --> R1{Word seen in training}
    R1 -- Yes --> R2[Use count directly]
    R1 -- No --> R3[Add 1 numerator]
    R2 --> R4[Numerator equals count plus 1]
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> R5[Denominator equals total class words plus vocab size]
    R5 --> R6[Smoothed probability always greater than zero]
```

## 4.4 Decision Boundary — Visual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Compute log P Spam D] --> Q1[Compute log P Ham D]
    Q1 --> Q2{Compare log posteriors}
    Q2 -- log P Spam greater --> Q3[Classify as SPAM]
    Q2 -- log P Ham greater --> Q4[Classify as HAM]
    Q2 -- exactly equal --> Q5[Tie break by class prior or default]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node identifiers above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `A0`, `T0`, `R0`, `Q0`) to comply with Mermaid syntax rules. No reserved keywords, bold tags, or HTML appear inside the quoted labels.
