```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Raw Corpus D] --> nodeB[Text Preprocessing]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Tokenization]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Stop-word Removal]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Lemmatization]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Vocabulary V]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Term-Document Matrix A]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Choose Model}
    nodeH --> nodeI[LSA via SVD]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[PLSA via EM]
    nodeH --> nodeK[LDA via Gibbs Sampling]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Topic Word Distributions phi]
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM[Topic-Tagged Documents]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Evaluation Perplexity and Coherence]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Topic Interpretation]
```

### 4.2 LDA Generative Process Plate Diagram (Adapted as Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Hyperparameter alpha Dirichlet prior over theta] --> nodeB[Per Document: Sample theta_d from Dir alpha]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Per Word: Sample topic z_dn from Multinomial theta_d]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Hyperparameter beta Dirichlet prior over phi]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Per Topic: Sample phi_k from Dir beta]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Per Word: Sample w_dn from Multinomial phi_z]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Observed Word w_dn]
```

### 4.3 LSA Pipeline via SVD Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Term Document Matrix A N x M] --> nodeB[Compute SVD: A = U Sigma V transpose]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Select top K singular values]
    nodeC --> nodeD[U_K Term Topic Matrix N x K]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Sigma_K Diagonal K x K]
    nodeC --> nodeF[V_K Document Topic Matrix M x K]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Topic Space Projection]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Low Rank Approximation A_K]
```

### 4.4 LDA Inference Cycle (Gibbs Sampling)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Initialize z_dn randomly for all words] --> nodeB[Loop until convergence]
    nodeB --> nodeC[For each word position d n]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute conditional p of z_dn given all others]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Sample new topic assignment z_dn]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Update count matrices n_dk and n_kw]
    nodeF --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeG[After burn-in: Estimate theta_dk and phi_kw]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Output Topic Distributions]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
*Define Topic Modeling. List any two techniques used for topic modeling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Topic Modeling is an unsupervised NLP technique that automatically discovers abstract latent themes (topics) from a large collection of text documents. Each document is represented as a probabilistic mixture of topics, and each topic is represented as a probability distribution over words. Two widely used techniques are:
1. **Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA)** — Uses Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of the Term-Document matrix.
2. **Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA)** — A generative Bayesian model using Dirichlet priors for topic distributions. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for two techniques.)*

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
*What is the role of the Dirichlet distribution in LDA?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Dirichlet distribution in LDA serves as a conjugate prior over the multinomial topic distributions:
- It provides a prior over the document-topic distribution $\theta_d \sim \text{Dir}(\alpha)$.
- It provides a prior over the topic-word distribution $\phi_k \sim \text{Dir}(\beta)$.
- It enables tractable Bayesian inference due to Dirichlet-Multinomial conjugacy.
- The concentration hyperparameter $\alpha$ controls sparsity — smaller $\alpha$ yields sparser topic mixtures. *(3 marks for full explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA)** model in detail with its generative process and plate diagram. Discuss the role of hyperparameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** LDA is a generative probabilistic model that assumes each document is a mixture of topics, and each topic is a distribution over words. It uses Dirichlet priors for Bayesian regularization.

**Generative Process (5 marks for explanation):**
1. Choose $K$ topics for the corpus.
2. For each topic $k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$: Draw $\phi_k \sim \text{Dir}(\beta)$ — distribution over vocabulary.
3. For each document $d \in \{1, \ldots, M\}$:
   - Draw topic proportions $\theta_d \sim \text{Dir}(\alpha)$.
   - For each of the $N_d$ word positions:
     - Draw a topic $z_{d,n} \sim \text{Multinomial}(\theta_d)$.
     - Draw a word $w_{d,n} \sim \text{Multinomial}(\phi_{z_{d,n}})$.

**Plate Diagram Description (1 mark):**

The outer plate spans documents $M$, the inner plate spans words $N_d$. Observed variable is $w$; latent variables are $\theta$, $\phi$, and $z$. Priors $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are corpus-level.

**Role of Hyperparameters (1 mark):**
- $\alpha$ controls document-topic sparsity: smaller $\alpha$ means documents belong to fewer topics.
- $\beta$ controls topic-word sparsity: smaller $\beta$ means topics use fewer distinguishing words.

---

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **Term-Document Matrix** and its decomposition using **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** for Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Term-Document Matrix Construction (2 marks):**

Consider a small corpus of 3 documents with vocabulary {cat, dog, fish, bone, milk, bowl}:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Each row represents a term; each column represents a document; entries are word counts.

**SVD Decomposition (3 marks):**

LSA computes $A = U \Sigma V^T$. For rank-2 approximation:

$$U = \begin{bmatrix} -0.55 & -0.18 \\ -0.40 & -0.20 \\ -0.45 & 0.45 \\ -0.40 & -0.20 \\ -0.30 & 0.55 \\ -0.25 & 0.60 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 3.85 & 0 \\ 0 & 2.78 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V^T = \begin{bmatrix} -0.55 & -0.55 & -0.62 \\ -0.45 & 0.78 & -0.43 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Interpretation (2 marks):**
- The two latent dimensions can be labeled "Pets" (cat, dog) and "Food" (milk, bowl, bone).
- Documents $d_1, d_2$ cluster near "Pets" axis; $d_3$ clusters near "Food" axis.
- Truncation to rank $K$ captures dominant semantic patterns while filtering noise.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **LSA, PLSA, and LDA** in terms of mathematical foundation, generative capability, parameter estimation, and handling of unseen documents. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | LSA | PLSA | LDA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Math Foundation** | Linear Algebra (SVD) | Probabilistic (EM) | Bayesian (Dirichlet priors) |
| **Generative** | No (deterministic) | Yes | Yes |
| **Inference** | Closed-form SVD | Expectation-Maximization | Variational Inference / Gibbs Sampling |
| **Parameters** | $N \cdot K + K \cdot M$ | $K \cdot (N + M)$ | $K \cdot (N + M) + 2$ (priors) |
| **New Documents** | Easy via fold-in | Cannot generalize (overfits) | Generalizes via prior |
| **Sparsity Control** | None | None | Tuned via $\alpha$ and $\beta$ |

*(6 marks for table + 1 mark for concluding remark: LDA is preferred due to Bayesian generalization and better topic coherence.)*

---

**(b)** Describe the **Gibbs Sampling algorithm for LDA inference** with the conditional distribution equation used for sampling each topic assignment. Explain why exact inference is intractable. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Why Exact Inference is Intractable (3 marks):**

The exact posterior $p(z \mid w, \alpha, \beta)$ requires evaluating:

$$p(z \mid w, \alpha, \beta) = \frac{p(w, z \mid \alpha, \beta)}{\sum_{z'} p(w, z' \mid \alpha, \beta)}$$

The denominator sums over all $K^{N_d}$ possible topic assignments for each document. For a corpus with $N_d = 100$ words and $K = 20$ topics, this gives $20^{100}$ combinations — computationally impossible. Hence, approximate inference is necessary.

**Gibbs Sampling Algorithm (2 marks):**

1. Initialize topic assignments $z_{d,n}$ randomly for all words.
2. For each iteration until convergence:
   - For each word position $(d, n)$:
     - Decrement count matrices $n_{d,k}$ and $n_{k, w_{d,n}}$.
     - Sample new $z_{d,n}$ from the conditional distribution.
     - Increment count matrices.
3. After burn-in, estimate $\hat{\theta}_{d,k}$ and $\hat{\phi}_{k,w}$ from final counts.

**Conditional Distribution Equation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(z_{d,n} = k \mid z_{-(d,n)}, w, \alpha, \beta) \propto \frac{n_{d,k}^{-(d,n)} + \alpha_k}{\sum_{k'} n_{d,k'}^{-(d,n)} + \alpha_{k'}} \cdot \frac{n_{k, w_{d,n}}^{-(d,n)} + \beta_{w_{d,n}}}{\sum_{v} n_{k,v}^{-(d,n)} + \beta_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

The first ratio represents the probability of topic $k$ in document $d$, and the second ratio represents the probability of generating word $w_{d,n}$ from topic $k$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Confusing LSA with PLSA:** LSA is deterministic (SVD-based) and NOT generative. PLSA is generative. LDA extends PLSA with Bayesian priors.
> - **Forgetting Dirichlet Priors:** Writing LDA's generative story without explicitly mentioning $\text{Dir}(\alpha)$ and $\text{Dir}(\beta)$ costs full marks.
> - **Missing Normalization in Gibbs Sampling:** Forgetting to normalize the conditional probability across topics $k = 1, \ldots, K$.
> - **Misinterpreting Plate Diagrams:** The outer plate spans documents; inner plate spans word positions. Observed variable $w$ is shaded; $\theta, \phi, z$ are unshaded (latent).
> - **Wrong Notation:** Writing $\theta_k$ instead of $\theta_d$ (topics-per-document vs. per-word).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Topic Modeling = Unsupervised Discovery of Latent Themes** in a document corpus.
- **LSA** uses **SVD on Term-Document Matrix**; linear algebraic, deterministic, no priors.
- **PLSA** introduces **latent topic variable $z$**; trained with **EM algorithm**; overfits training data.
- **LDA** adds **Dirichlet priors** $\alpha$ (doc-topic) and $\beta$ (topic-word); Bayesian generalization.
- **LDA Generative Story:** For each topic → draw $\phi_k \sim \text{Dir}(\beta)$; for each document → draw $\theta_d \sim \text{Dir}(\alpha)$; for each word → draw $z \sim \text{Mult}(\theta_d)$, then draw $w \sim \text{Mult}(\phi_z)$.
- **Exact posterior inference is intractable** because of $K^{N_d}$ combinatorial explosion.
- **Approximate inference methods:** Variational Inference and Collapsed Gibbs Sampling.
- **Gibbs Sampling update rule** is proportional to product of *document-topic count* and *topic-word count*.
- **Evaluation Metrics:** Perplexity (lower = better) and Topic Coherence $C_v$ (higher = better).
- **Engineering Applications:** Search engines, recommender systems, biomedical mining, social media analytics, legal discovery.
- **For 14-mark answers**, always include: definition, generative process, plate diagram description, role of hyperparameters, mathematical equations, and real-world example.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Latent Semantic Modeling (Indexing). Deep Learning: Word Embeddings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Latent Semantic Modeling (Indexing). Deep Learning: Word Embeddings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Latent Semantic Indexing (LSI)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Latent Semantic Indexing (LSI), also called Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA), is a mathematical-statistical technique used in Natural Language Processing and Information Retrieval. It applies **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** to a large term-document matrix in order to uncover the hidden (latent) semantic associations between words and documents. The goal is to transform the high-dimensional, sparse word-count representation into a lower-dimensional, dense semantic space where related terms cluster together.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Idea:** Documents that share many co-occurring words are projected into the same low-dimensional "concept space," even when they don't use the exact same vocabulary. This addresses two classic IR problems — **synonymy** (different words, same meaning) and **polysemy** (same word, different meanings).

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Library Dewey Decimal" Approach:**
Imagine a massive library where books are cataloged by exact words on the cover. A student searching for *"car"* will miss books titled *"automobile"*, *"vehicle"*, or *"sedan"* — even though they are conceptually identical. LSI acts like a **smart librarian** who reads every book, identifies the *underlying topics* (e.g., "transportation," "engineering," "history"), and re-shelves the books by topic rather than by literal word. Now, a query for *"car"* automatically pulls in books on *"automobile"* because they share the same hidden topic coordinates. The "latent" topics are never explicitly written; they are *discovered mathematically* through SVD.

### 1.2 Word Embeddings (Deep Learning Era)

**Formal Definition:**
A **word embedding** is a dense, low-dimensional, learned vector representation of a word, where each dimension encodes a latent semantic or syntactic feature. Unlike sparse one-hot encodings, embeddings place semantically similar words *close together* in a continuous vector space (typically 50–300 dimensions). They are *learned* from large corpora using shallow neural network architectures such as **Word2Vec (CBOW / Skip-gram)**, **GloVe**, and **FastText**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Embedding Evaluation:**
> - **Cosine Similarity** — measures angular closeness of two vectors: range $\in [-1, 1]$
> - **Euclidean Distance** — measures straight-line proximity in vector space
> - **Embedding Dimension (d)** — typically 50, 100, 200, or 300
> - **Context Window Size (c)** — typically 2 to 10 surrounding words

**Intuitive Analogy — "Countries on a Meaning Map":**
Picture a giant 2D world map where every word is plotted as a country. *"King"* sits near *"queen"*, *"monarch"*, *"royalty"*. *"Paris"* sits near *"France"*, *"Berlin"*, *"capital"*. The map wasn't drawn by a human — it was *learned* automatically by reading billions of sentences. The remarkable property is that **directions on the map carry meaning**: the vector from *"man"* to *"woman"* is almost identical to the vector from *"king"* to *"queen"*. This is the famous analogy equation: $\vec{king} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{queen}$. Embeddings are the "GPS coordinates" of meaning.

### 1.3 Syllabus Highlights & Key Distinctions

| Aspect | LSI / LSA | Word Embeddings |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin Era** | Pre-deep-learning (1990s) | Deep learning era (2013 onwards) |
| **Math Tool** | Linear Algebra (SVD) | Shallow Neural Networks |
| **Representation** | Global, document-level | Local, word-level |
| **Training Data** | Term-document co-occurrence | Context window prediction |
| **Solves** | Synonymy, polysemy | Semantic similarity, analogy |
| **Key Algorithms** | Truncated SVD | Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Be prepared to contrast *count-based* methods (LSI) with *prediction-based* methods (Word2Vec). Examiners frequently ask: "Why are embeddings better than one-hot encodings?" or "Explain how SVD reduces dimensionality in LSI."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Latent Semantic Indexing — Operational Pipeline

**Step 1 — Construct the Term-Document Matrix (A):**
Each entry $A_{ij}$ represents the frequency of term $i$ in document $j$. The matrix is large, sparse, and high-dimensional (vocabulary size $\times$ number of documents).

**Step 2 — Apply TF-IDF Weighting (optional but standard):**
Raw counts are biased toward frequent terms. TF-IDF normalizes for term importance.

**Step 3 — Singular Value Decomposition (SVD):**
The matrix $A$ is factorized into three matrices:
$$A = U \Sigma V^{T}$$

where:
- $U$ is an $m \times m$ orthogonal matrix of left singular vectors (term vectors)
- $\Sigma$ is an $m \times n$ diagonal matrix of singular values (sorted descending)
- $V^{T}$ is an $n \times n$ orthogonal matrix of right singular vectors (document vectors)

**Step 4 — Dimensionality Reduction (Truncation):**
Keep only the top $k$ singular values (where $k \ll \min(m, n)$), producing the best rank-$k$ approximation $A_k$:
$$A_{k} = U_{k} \Sigma_{k} V_{k}^{T}$$

**Why this works — The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
- **Why SVD?** It finds the *principal axes of variation* in the data — the directions of maximum semantic spread.
- **Why truncate?** Removing low-magnitude singular values filters out noise, polysemy noise, and rare-term artifacts.
- **Why does it solve synonymy?** Synonyms co-occur in similar documents, so their row vectors in $U_k$ become nearly identical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Ecker–Young–Mirsky Theorem:** Truncated SVD gives the *optimal* low-rank approximation in the Frobenius norm sense. This is why LSI produces semantically meaningful spaces.

### 2.2 Word Embeddings — Theoretical Foundations

**Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013):** A shallow two-layer neural network trained on one of two architectures:

**A) Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBOW):**
Predicts the *target word* from surrounding context words. Faster to train, better for frequent words.
- Input layer: context word vectors (one-hot or embeddings)
- Output: probability distribution over vocabulary for the target word

**B) Skip-gram:**
Predicts surrounding *context words* from a single target word. Slower but produces better embeddings for rare words.
- Input: target word vector
- Output: probability distribution for each context word

**GloVe (Global Vectors, Pennington et al., 2014):**
Combines count-based (global co-occurrence statistics) with prediction-based (local context window) methods. Uses a weighted least-squares objective on the global co-occurrence matrix.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Purpose / Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SVD Decomposition | $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$ | Core LSI factorization |
| 2 | Truncated SVD Reconstruction | $A_{k} = U_{k} \Sigma_{k} V_{k}^{T}$ | Best rank-k approximation |
| 3 | Frobenius Reconstruction Error | $\vert\vert A - A_{k} \vert\vert_{F}^{2} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_{i}^{2}$ | Quality of dimensionality reduction |
| 4 | TF-IDF Weight | $w_{ij} = tf_{ij} \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{df_{i}}\right)$ | Term importance scoring |
| 5 | Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(u, v) = \dfrac{u \cdot v}{\vert\vert u \vert\vert \, \vert\vert v \vert\vert}$ | Embedding similarity metric |
| 6 | Euclidean Distance | $d(u, v) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} (u_{i} - v_{i})^{2}}$ | Vector space distance |
| 7 | Word Analogy Equation | $\vec{b} - \vec{a} + \vec{c} \approx \vec{d}$ (e.g., king - man + woman ≈ queen) | Semantic relationship test |
| 8 | Skip-gram Softmax Objective | $P(w_{O} \mid w_{I}) = \dfrac{\exp(v_{w_{O}}^{\prime \, T} v_{w_{I}})}{\sum_{w=1}^{W} \exp(v_{w}^{T} v_{w_{I}})}$ | Output probability for context word |
| 9 | CBOW Objective | Maximize $\prod_{t=1}^{T} P(w_{t} \mid w_{t-c} \ldots w_{t+c})$ | Predict center from context |
| 10 | Negative Sampling Loss | $\log \sigma(v_{w_{O}}^{\prime \, T} v_{w_{I}}) + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{E}_{w_{k} \sim P_{n}}[\log \sigma(-v_{w_{k}}^{\prime \, T} v_{w_{I}})]$ | Efficient training (replaces full softmax) |
| 11 | GloVe Loss Function | $J = \sum_{i,j=1}^{W} f(X_{ij}) \left(v_{i}^{T} v_{j} + b_{i} + b_{j} - \log X_{ij}\right)^{2}$ | Global co-occurrence objective |
| 12 | SVD Rank Constraint | $k \ll \min(m, n)$ typically $k = 100$–$300$ | Latent dimensions retained |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Symbol Conventions:** $U, V$ = orthogonal matrices; $\Sigma$ = diagonal singular values; $v_{w}$ = input vector of word $w$; $v_{w}^{\prime}$ = output vector of word $w$; $X_{ij}$ = co-occurrence count between words $i$ and $j$; $W$ = vocabulary size; $c$ = context window; $d$ = embedding dimension.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LSI Applications:** Search engines (legacy IR systems), document clustering, cross-lingual retrieval, automated essay grading, plagiarism detection.
- **Word Embeddings Applications:** Machine translation, sentiment analysis, chatbots, recommendation systems, biomedical NLP (BioBERT, PubMedBERT), code search engines, legal document analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: SVD-Based LSI on a Toy Corpus

**Corpus (3 documents, 6 unique terms):**
- D1: *"ship boat ocean"*
- D2: *"boat vessel ship"*
- D3: *"car truck vehicle"*

**Step 1 — Build the Term-Document Matrix A (raw counts):**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix}
& D1 & D2 & D3 \\
ship   & 1 & 1 & 0 \\
boat   & 1 & 1 & 0 \\
ocean  & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
vessel & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
car    & 0 & 0 & 1 \\
truck  & 0 & 0 & 1 \\
vehicle& 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Here $m = 6$ (terms) and $n = 3$ (documents). The matrix is sparse.

**Step 2 — Compute SVD $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$:**
For pedagogical clarity, we work through the eigenvalue decomposition of $A^{T} A$ to obtain $V$ and $\Sigma$, then derive $U$.

$$
A^{T} A = \begin{bmatrix}
3 & 2 & 0 \\
2 & 3 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 3
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Find eigenvalues of $A^{T} A$:**

$$
\det(A^{T}A - \lambda I) = 0 \Rightarrow (3 - \lambda)^{3} - 4(3 - \lambda) = 0
$$

Factoring:

$$
(3 - \lambda)\left[(3 - \lambda)^{2} - 4\right] = 0
$$

$$
(3 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda - 2)(3 - \lambda + 2) = 0
$$

$$
(3 - \lambda)(1 - \lambda)(5 - \lambda) = 0
$$

Eigenvalues: $\lambda_{1} = 5, \quad \lambda_{2} = 3, \quad \lambda_{3} = 1$

**Step 4 — Compute singular values $\sigma_{i} = \sqrt{\lambda_{i}}$:**

$$
\sigma_{1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236, \quad \sigma_{2} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732, \quad \sigma_{3} = 1
$$

**Step 5 — Find right singular vectors (columns of V) by solving $(A^{T}A - \lambda_i I) v_i = 0$:**

For $\lambda_1 = 5$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} -2 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} v_1 = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

For $\lambda_2 = 3$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} v_2 = 0 \Rightarrow v_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

For $\lambda_3 = 1$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} v_3 = 0 \Rightarrow v_3 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Construct V:**

$$
V = \begin{bmatrix}
1/\sqrt{2} & 0 & 1/\sqrt{2} \\
1/\sqrt{2} & 0 & -1/\sqrt{2} \\
0 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute $U = A V \Sigma^{-1}$ (column by column):**
For column 1 of $U$:

$$
u_1 = \frac{1}{\sigma_1} A v_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10}} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 8 — Truncate to $k = 2$ dimensions:**
Retain top 2 singular values and corresponding vectors. This projects the 6-term vocabulary into a 2D "concept space" where:
- *ship, boat, ocean, vessel* cluster together (the "maritime" axis)
- *car, truck, vehicle* cluster together (the "vehicle" axis)

### 3.2 Skip-gram Model — Loss Function Derivation

**Setup:** Given a center word $w_I$ (input) and a context word $w_O$, the model computes the dot product $v_{w_O}^{\prime T} v_{w_I}$, passes it through softmax, and trains to maximize the probability of observing $w_O$ given $w_I$.

**Step 1 — Softmax probability:**

$$
P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp(v_{w_O}^{\prime T} v_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{W} \exp(v_{w}^{T} v_{w_I})}
$$

**Step 2 — Log-likelihood objective over the corpus:**

$$
L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{-c \leq j \leq c, \, j \neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_{t})
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the softmax expression:**

$$
L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{j} \left[ v_{w_{t+j}}^{\prime T} v_{w_{t}} - \log \sum_{w=1}^{W} \exp(v_{w}^{T} v_{w_{t}}) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Take the negative to convert to a minimization loss:**

$$
J = -L = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{j} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_{t})
$$

**Step 5 — Gradient update rule via backpropagation:**

$$
v_{w_{I}} \leftarrow v_{w_{I}} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial v_{w_{I}}}
$$

**Why the denominator is a problem:** Summing over vocabulary $W$ (often 100,000+) at every step is computationally infeasible. This motivates **Negative Sampling**, which approximates the softmax by sampling only $K$ negative examples (typically $K = 5$ to $20$).

**Negative Sampling Approximation:**

$$
J_{NG} = -\log \sigma(v_{w_O}^{\prime T} v_{w_I}) - \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{E}_{w_k \sim P_n(w)} \left[ \log \sigma(-v_{w_k}^{\prime T} v_{w_I}) \right]
$$

where $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ is the sigmoid function and $P_n(w) \propto U(w)^{3/4}$ is the unigram distribution raised to the 3/4 power (empirically chosen to down-weight very frequent words).

### 3.3 Cosine Similarity Derivation for Embedding Comparison

Given two word vectors $u, v \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$:

**Step 1 — Dot product:**

$$
u \cdot v = \sum_{i=1}^{d} u_i v_i
$$

**Step 2 — L2 norms:**

$$
\vert\vert u \vert\vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} u_i^{2}}, \qquad \vert\vert v \vert\vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} v_i^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Cosine similarity:**

$$
\cos \theta = \frac{u \cdot v}{\vert\vert u \vert\vert \, \vert\vert v \vert\vert}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\cos \theta = 1$: identical direction (highly similar)
- $\cos \theta = 0$: orthogonal (unrelated)
- $\cos \theta = -1$: opposite (antonyms often)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cosine over Euclidean?** Embeddings are *direction-sensitive*, not magnitude-sensitive. A longer vector doesn't mean a "more important" word; it reflects training frequency. Cosine normalizes this out.

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Concept | Output | Dimensionality |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Build term-document matrix $A$ | $m \times n$ sparse matrix | $m \times n$ |
| 2 | SVD: $A = U\Sigma V^{T}$ | Three matrices | $m \times m$, $m \times n$, $n \times n$ |
| 3 | Truncate to rank $k$ | $U_k$, $\Sigma_k$, $V_k$ | $m \times k$, $k \times k$, $k \times n$ |
| 4 | Reconstruct $A_k$ | Low-rank approximation | $m \times n$ |
| 5 | Word2Vec training | Embedding matrix $E$ | $W \times d$ |
| 6 | Cosine similarity lookup | Scalar $\in [-1, 1]$ | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Latent Semantic Indexing (LSI) — End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    corpusA[Raw Text Corpus] --> preprocessA[Tokenization and Stopword Removal]
    preprocessA --> matrixA[Term Document Matrix A]
    matrixA --> tfidfA[TF-IDF Weighting]
    tfidfA --> svdA[Singular Value Decomposition]
    svdA --> factorU[Matrix U - Term Concepts]
    svdA --> factorSigma[Matrix Sigma - Singular Values]
    svdA --> factorV[Matrix V Transpose - Document Concepts]
    factorSigma --> truncA[Truncation to top k values]
    truncA --> lowDimA[Low Dimensional Semantic Space]
    lowDimA --> queryA[Query and Document Matching]
    queryA --> rankA[Ranked Retrieval Results]
```

### 4.2 Word2Vec Architecture Comparison — CBOW vs Skip-gram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CBOW[CBOW Architecture]
        c1[Context Word 1] --> emb1[Embedding Layer]
        c2[Context Word 2] --> emb1
        c3[Context Word 3] --> emb1
        c4[Context Word 4] --> emb1
        emb1 --> avg1[Average or Sum]
        avg1 --> hid1[Hidden Layer]
        hid1 --> out1[Output Softmax]
        out1 --> tgt1[Predicted Target Word]
    end

    subgraph SG[Skip-gram Architecture]
        t1[Target Word] --> emb2[Embedding Layer]
        emb2 --> hid2[Hidden Layer]
        hid2 --> out2[Output Softmax with Negative Sampling]
        out2 --> ctx1[Predicted Context Word 1]
        out2 --> ctx2[Predicted Context Word 2]
        out2 --> ctx3[Predicted Context Word 3]
        out2 --> ctx4[Predicted Context Word 4]
    end
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Embedding Learning System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputLayer[Input Text Stream] --> tokenize[Tokenizer Module]
    tokenize --> vocab[Vocab Builder]
    vocab --> onehot[One Hot Encoding Layer]
    onehot --> embedTable[Embedding Lookup Table E of size W x d]
    embedTable --> ctxWin[Context Window Extractor of size c]
    ctxWin --> arch[Architecture Selector - CBOW or Skip-gram]
    arch --> fwdProp[Forward Propagation]
    fwdProp --> loss[Loss Computation - Softmax or Negative Sampling]
    loss --> bwdProp[Backpropagation]
    bwdProp --> update[Gradient Update of E and Output Weights]
    update --> embedTable
    update --> eval[Evaluation Module - Cosine Similarity and Analogies]
    eval --> output[Final Word Vectors]
```

### 4.4 Dimensionality Reduction Visualization Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HD[High Dimensional Sparse Space]
        h1[Word A as 50000 dim vector]
        h2[Word B as 50000 dim vector]
        h3[Word C as 50000 dim vector]
    end
    HD --> svdStep[SVD Projection]
    svdStep --> subgraph LD[Low Dimensional Dense Space]
        l1[Word A as 300 dim dense vector]
        l2[Word B as 300 dim dense vector near A]
        l3[Word C as 300 dim dense vector far from A]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In CBOW, multiple context words feed *into* the network to predict a single center word. In Skip-gram, a single center word is used to predict *multiple* context words. The training objective adjusts the shared embedding table $E$ such that words appearing in similar contexts end up geometrically close in the output dense space.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Latent Semantic Indexing. Mention any two advantages of LSI over traditional keyword-based retrieval.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Latent Semantic Indexing (LSI) is a vector-space retrieval technique that uses Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) to project a high-dimensional term-document matrix into a lower-dimensional "concept space," thereby capturing hidden semantic relationships between terms and documents.

**Advantages (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Solves synonymy problem:** Documents using different but synonymous terms (e.g., *"car"* and *"automobile"*) are matched because they share similar latent dimensions.
2. **Solves polysemy problem:** Truncation of low singular values discards noise arising from words with multiple meanings, refining the semantic focus.
3. **Reduces noise and dimensionality:** Filters out less informative dimensions, producing more accurate retrieval.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is a word embedding? How is it different from a one-hot vector representation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **word embedding** is a dense, low-dimensional, real-valued vector representation of a word (typically 50–300 dimensions) that is *learned* from a large text corpus using neural network models such as Word2Vec, GloVe, or FastText. The vector encodes semantic and syntactic properties — semantically similar words are placed close together in the embedding space.

**Differences from one-hot encoding (1.5 marks):**

| Feature | One-Hot Vector | Word Embedding |
|---|---|---|
| Dimensionality | Equal to vocabulary size (e.g., 50,000) | Small (e.g., 100–300) |
| Density | Sparse (mostly zeros) | Dense (all values non-zero) |
| Semantic info | No relationship captured | Captures similarity and analogy |
| Construction | Rule-based | Learned from data |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the steps involved in Latent Semantic Indexing. How does SVD help in dimensionality reduction? **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Consider the following term-document matrix. Compute its SVD and reconstruct a rank-2 approximation. Discuss the semantic structure revealed. **\[7 Marks\]**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix}
& D1 & D2 & D3 \\
human & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
interface & 1 & 0 & 1 \\
computer & 1 & 1 & 0 \\
system & 0 & 1 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

The steps of LSI are: **\[1 mark each\]**

1. **Build Term-Document Matrix (A):** Construct an $m \times n$ matrix where entry $A_{ij}$ = frequency of term $i$ in document $j$. **(1 mark)**

2. **Apply TF-IDF Weighting:** Replace raw counts with TF-IDF weights to reduce the influence of common words. **(1 mark)**

3. **Apply Singular Value Decomposition:** Decompose $A = U\Sigma V^{T}$, where $U$ contains term vectors, $\Sigma$ contains singular values, and $V$ contains document vectors. **(1 mark)**

4. **Dimensionality Reduction (Truncation):** Keep only top $k$ singular values and corresponding vectors, producing $A_{k} = U_{k}\Sigma_{k}V_{k}^{T}$. **(1 mark)**

5. **Query Matching in Reduced Space:** Project both queries and documents into the reduced $k$-dimensional space and rank by cosine similarity. **(1 mark)**

**How SVD helps in dimensionality reduction (2 marks):**
SVD identifies the principal axes of variation in the term-document matrix. The largest singular values in $\Sigma$ capture the most significant semantic relationships, while the smallest ones correspond to noise. By retaining only the top $k$ values, we approximate the original matrix using a smaller, denser representation that preserves dominant semantic patterns while discarding redundant information. By the Ecker–Young–Mirsky theorem, this gives the best rank-$k$ approximation in the Frobenius norm.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** Compute $A A^{T}$ (size $4 \times 4$):

$$
AA^{T} = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\
1 & 2 & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 2 & 1 \\
0 & 1 & 1 & 2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Find eigenvalues of $AA^{T}$ using characteristic polynomial $\det(AA^{T} - \lambda I) = 0$. Solving yields approximate eigenvalues: $\lambda_{1} \approx 4.45, \lambda_{2} \approx 1.88, \lambda_{3} \approx 0.66, \lambda_{4} \approx 0.01$.

**Step 3:** Singular values: $\sigma_{1} \approx 2.11, \sigma_{2} \approx 1.37, \sigma_{3} \approx 0.81, \sigma_{4} \approx 0.10$.

**Step 4:** Keep top 2 singular values to get rank-2 approximation $A_{2} = U_{2}\Sigma_{2}V_{2}^{T}$. The truncation drops $\sigma_{3}$ and $\sigma_{4}$, which carry minimal semantic content. **(3 marks for computation)**

**Step 5 — Semantic Interpretation (2 marks):**
In the reduced 2D space:
- The terms *"human"* and *"computer"* lie along one axis (representing a "user–machine" latent dimension).
- *"interface"* and *"system"* lie along a different axis (representing a "technology" dimension).
- Documents D2 and D3, which share the *"system"* concept, are projected close to each other in the 2D space, even though their raw term overlap is small.

This illustrates how LSI discovers hidden semantic groupings not visible in the original matrix.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Word2Vec model with the Skip-gram architecture. Derive the softmax-based loss function and state the problem it poses. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** How does Negative Sampling solve this problem? Write the modified loss function and show how it is computed for a sample training instance. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Word2Vec Overview (1 mark):**
Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) is a shallow two-layer neural network that learns distributed word embeddings by predicting neighboring words.

**Skip-gram Architecture (2 marks):**
- Input: a single target (center) word $w_I$
- Output: probability distribution over vocabulary for each context word in a window of size $c$ surrounding $w_I$
- Hidden layer: a single dense layer of size $d$ (the embedding dimension)
- No activation in hidden layer; softmax at output

**Derivation of Softmax Loss (3 marks):**

The probability that word $w_O$ is a context word given center word $w_I$:

$$
P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp(v_{w_O}^{\prime T} v_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{W} \exp(v_{w}^{T} v_{w_I})}
$$

The objective is to maximize the log-likelihood over all center-context pairs:

$$
L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{\substack{-c \leq j \leq c \\ j \neq 0}} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_{t})
$$

The corresponding loss (to minimize):

$$
J = -L = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{j} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_{t})
$$

**Problem (1 mark):**
Computing the denominator requires summing over the entire vocabulary $W$ at every training step. For $W \approx 10^5$ and corpus size $\approx 10^9$, this becomes computationally infeasible.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Concept of Negative Sampling (2 marks):**
Instead of updating weights for all $W$ words, Negative Sampling (NEG) reformulates the problem as a binary classification:
- Treat the *true* (center, context) pair as a **positive example** ($y = 1$).
- Sample $K$ random *negative* words from a noise distribution $P_n(w)$ and treat them as **negative examples** ($y = 0$).
- Update weights only for the positive pair and the $K$ negatives.

**Modified Loss Function (3 marks):**

$$
J_{NG} = -\log \sigma(v_{w_O}^{\prime T} v_{w_I}) - \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{E}_{w_k \sim P_n(w)} \left[ \log \sigma(-v_{w_k}^{\prime T} v_{w_I}) \right]
$$

where $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ is the logistic sigmoid.

**Numerical Example (2 marks):**
For a corpus with $W = 10{,}000$, full softmax requires $10{,}000$ updates per step. With Negative Sampling and $K = 5$, only $1 + 5 = 6$ updates are needed — a $\sim 1666\times$ speedup. The noise distribution is typically $P_n(w) \propto U(w)^{3/4}$, where $U(w)$ is the unigram frequency; the $3/4$ exponent empirically down-weights extremely common words (e.g., *"the"*, *"of"*).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SVD dimensions:** Students often mix up the shapes of $U$, $\Sigma$, and $V^{T}$. Remember: if $A$ is $m \times n$, then $U$ is $m \times m$, $\Sigma$ is $m \times n$ (diagonal), and $V^{T}$ is $n \times n$. After truncation to rank $k$: $U_k$ is $m \times k$, $\Sigma_k$ is $k \times k$, $V_k^{T}$ is $k \times n$.
> 2. **Cosine vs. Dot product:** Embedding similarity is computed using **cosine similarity**, not raw dot product. Dot product is affected by vector magnitude, cosine is not.
> 3. **Confusing CBOW and Skip-gram:** CBOW predicts the *center* from *context*; Skip-gram predicts *context* from *center*. Examiners frequently swap them to test attention.
> 4. **Skipping the "Why":** Merely writing $A = U\Sigma V^{T}$ earns only partial credit. You must explain *why* truncation helps and *what* the matrices represent semantically.
> 5. **Forgetting the bias terms in GloVe:** The GloVe loss function has bias terms $b_i$ and $b_j$ — omitting them costs 1 mark.
> 6. **One-hot vs. Embedding dimensions:** Students often state embedding dimension as 50,000 (the vocabulary size). The whole point of embeddings is to make $d \ll W$.

---

### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **LSI = Latent Semantic Indexing** — uses SVD on term-document matrix to project into low-dimensional concept space.
- **SVD Formula:** $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$ — where $U$ = term vectors, $\Sigma$ = singular values, $V$ = document vectors.
- **Truncation rank $k$** is chosen so that $k \ll \min(m, n)$; typical $k = 100$ to $300$.
- **Reconstruction error:** $\vert\vert A - A_{k} \vert\vert_{F}^{2} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_{i}^{2}$.
- **LSI strengths:** Solves synonymy, polysemy, noise reduction; produces dense representations.
- **LSI limitations:** Computationally expensive for huge corpora; static (cannot update with new words without re-computing).
- **Word embedding** = dense, low-dim, learned vector (typical $d = 50$–$300$).
- **Word2Vec architectures:** CBOW (predict center from context) and Skip-gram (predict context from center).
- **Skip-gram Softmax:** $P(w_O \mid w_I) = \dfrac{\exp(v_{w_O}^{\prime T} v_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{W} \exp(v_{w}^{T} v_{w_I})}$.
- **Negative Sampling** replaces full softmax with $K$ sampled negatives (typically $K = 5$–$20$); noise distribution $P_n(w) \propto U(w)^{3/4}$.
- **GloVe** combines global co-occurrence matrix with local context; objective: weighted least-squares on log-co-occurrence.
- **Cosine similarity** is the standard similarity metric: $\cos \theta = \dfrac{u \cdot v}{\vert\vert u \vert\vert \, \vert\vert v \vert\vert}$, range $\in [-1, 1]$.
- **Analogy equation:** $\vec{king} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{queen}$ — famous Word2Vec property.
- **One-hot vs Embedding:** One-hot is sparse, high-dim, semantically empty; embedding is dense, low-dim, semantically rich.
- **Count-based vs Prediction-based:** LSI/LSA = count-based (uses term-document frequencies); Word2Vec = prediction-based (uses neural network training on context windows).
- **Ecker–Young–Mirsky Theorem:** Truncated SVD provides the optimal low-rank approximation in Frobenius norm.
- **Practical takeaway:** For modern NLP, **pre-trained embeddings** (Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText) and **contextual embeddings** (BERT, GPT) have largely replaced LSI for word-level tasks, but LSI remains foundational for document-level retrieval and topic modeling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nonlinear Neural Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET864 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nonlinear Neural Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nonlinear Neural Networks (NNNs)** are computational models composed of interconnected artificial neurons arranged in multiple layers, where the input–output mapping of at least one layer involves a **nonlinear activation function**. In the context of Natural Language Processing (NLP), these networks transform discrete symbolic representations of text (tokens, words, sentences) into continuous, high-dimensional vector spaces capable of capturing complex linguistic phenomena such as **syntax, semantics, sentiment, and discourse**.

Mathematically, a nonlinear neural network implements a function:

$$f : \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{m}$$

composed of an alternating cascade of **affine transformations** (linear maps + bias) and **element-wise nonlinear activation functions**. Formally, for an $L$-layer feedforward network:

$$\mathbf{h}^{(l)} = \sigma^{(l)}\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{(l)}\mathbf{h}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}\right), \quad l = 1, 2, \dots, L$$

where $\sigma^{(l)}$ is a **nonlinear** scalar function, $\mathbf{W}^{(l)}$ is the weight matrix at layer $l$, $\mathbf{b}^{(l)}$ is the bias vector, and $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$ is the input representation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 KTU scheme stresses that *linear models (e.g., logistic regression, BoW classifiers) are fundamentally incapable of separating nonlinearly distributed linguistic features*. Nonlinear neural networks overcome this by composing piecewise-linear and curved decision boundaries.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are a **postmaster** sorting thousands of letters into two categories — *complaints* and *compliments*. If you draw a straight line on a table and place letters on either side, you will fail miserably because the words expressing frustration and appreciation are scattered in clusters that **cannot be separated by a single straight line**.

A **nonlinear neural network** is like giving the postmaster a *flexible, curvy rubber sheet* stretched over the table. The sheet bends around the letter-clusters, naturally separating complaints from compliments even when they are intricately interwoven in the feature space.

| Concept | Linear Model Analogy | Nonlinear Neural Network Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Decision Boundary | A straight ruler on the table | A flexible, curved rubber sheet |
| Feature Combination | Only "AND/OR" style rules | Complex, hierarchical phrase patterns |
| Capacity | Shallow sorting by word presence | Deep, context-aware understanding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Language is inherently **nonlinear** — negation ("not good"), sarcasm ("yeah, right"), and compositional semantics ("I love it" vs. "I don't love it") cannot be expressed as linear combinations of bag-of-words features.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following bold metrics are universally used to characterize nonlinear neural networks in NLP:

- **Activation Saturation Range:** Sigmoid $\sigma(z) \in (0, 1)$, Tanh $\tanh(z) \in (-1, +1)$
- **ReLU Dead-Zone Threshold:** **0** (neurons with $z \leq 0$ produce zero gradient)
- **Universal Approximation Bound:** A single hidden layer with **sufficiently many neurons** can approximate any Borel-measurable function on a compact domain to arbitrary precision (Hornik, 1989).
- **Standard Embedding Dimension:** **d = 50, 100, 200, 300** (typical for word2vec / GloVe)
- **He Initialization Variance Bound:** $\text{Var}(W) = \frac{2}{n_{\text{in}}}$ (for ReLU units)
- **Xavier Initialization Variance Bound:** $\text{Var}(W) = \frac{1}{n_{\text{in}}}$ (for Tanh/Sigmoid units)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Motivation: Why Nonlinearity is Mandatory in NLP

A purely linear classifier (e.g., Softmax over linear logits) computes:

$$y = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b})$$

This is **incapable** of representing interactions between features. Consider the toy sentiment problem:

| Sentence | "good" (x₁) | "not" (x₂) | True Sentiment |
|---|---|---|---|
| "movie was good" | 1 | 0 | Positive |
| "movie was not good" | 1 | 1 | Negative |
| "movie was bad" | 0 | 1 | Negative |

The class label is $y = x_1 \oplus \overline{x_2}$ (XOR-like), which is **not linearly separable**. A single linear boundary always fails. A nonlinear hidden unit computes $h = \sigma(x_1 - x_2)$, which *can* carve the correct region.

---

## 2.2 Structural Components of a Nonlinear Neural Network

### 2.2.1 The Artificial Neuron

A single neuron performs two operations:

$$\begin{aligned}
z &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b \\
a &= \sigma(z)
\end{aligned}$$

- **Step 1 (Pre-activation):** Linear combination $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b$ — geometrically, computes how aligned input $\mathbf{x}$ is with weight vector $\mathbf{w}$.
- **Step 2 (Activation):** Nonlinear squashing $\sigma(z)$ — introduces curvature, enabling hierarchical feature composition.

### 2.2.2 The Layer Abstraction

A *layer* is a vectorized parallel application of $m$ neurons to the same input:

$$\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{(l)}\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}\right)$$

### 2.2.3 Depth and Width

- **Width** = number of neurons per layer (e.g., hidden size 128, 256).
- **Depth** = number of layers (e.g., shallow: 1–2 hidden layers; deep: 12+ in Transformers).
- *Universal Approximation Theorem* guarantees representational power with one wide hidden layer, but in practice **deep + narrow** networks generalize better.

---

## 2.3 Core Activation Functions

| Function | Formula | Range | Derivative | NLP Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sigmoid** | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | Output layer for binary sentiment |
| **Tanh** | $\tanh(z) = \dfrac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $1 - \tanh^{2}(z)$ | Hidden layer in classical RNNs/LSTMs |
| **ReLU** | $\max(0, z)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $\mathbf{1}_{z>0}$ | Default hidden activation in modern NLP |
| **Leaky ReLU** | $\max(\alpha z, z), \; \alpha \approx 0.01$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | $\mathbf{1}_{z>0} + \alpha \cdot \mathbf{1}_{z \leq 0}$ | Mitigates dying-ReLU problem |
| **Softmax** | $\dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | $(0, 1)$, sums to 1 | — | Multi-class classification (NER, POS) |
| **GELU** | $z \cdot \Phi(z)$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | $\Phi(z) + z\phi(z)$ | Transformer FFN blocks (BERT, GPT) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In modern NLP (BERT, RoBERTa, GPT), the **GELU** activation dominates because it provides a smooth, probabilistic gating that empirically outperforms ReLU in transformer feed-forward blocks.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single Neuron Output | $a = \sigma\!\left(\sum_i w_i x_i + b\right)$ | Linear + nonlinear |
| 2 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Saturates for $\vert z \vert > 4$ |
| 3 | Sigmoid Derivative | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | Max value **0.25** |
| 4 | Tanh Derivative | $\tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(z)$ | Max value **1.0** |
| 5 | ReLU Derivative | $\text{ReLU}'(z) = \mathbf{1}_{z>0}$ | Zero for $z \leq 0$ |
| 6 | Softmax | $\text{softmax}(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Outputs probability simplex |
| 7 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $L = -\sum_{i} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | Standard NLP loss |
| 8 | Chain Rule (Backprop) | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)})^{\top}$ | $\delta^{(l)}$ = error signal |
| 9 | Gradient Descent Update | $\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(l)} - \eta \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}$ | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| 10 | He Init. (ReLU) | $W_{ij} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \dfrac{2}{n_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | Keeps variance stable |
| 11 | Xavier Init. (Tanh) | $W_{ij} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \dfrac{1}{n_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | For symmetric activations |
| 12 | Universal Approximation | $\lim_{m \to \infty} \sup_{x} \vert f(x) - f_m(x) \vert = 0$ | $m$ = hidden width |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When writing the derivative of Sigmoid, students frequently forget to express it **in terms of the output** $a$, i.e., $\sigma'(z) = a(1-a)$. Board examiners explicitly look for this simplification.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the industry, nonlinear neural networks power:

- **Sentiment Analysis (E-commerce Reviews):** Classifying product reviews as positive/negative — uses a 2-layer MLP on top of averaged word embeddings.
- **Named Entity Recognition (NER):** BIO tagging of tokens — uses BiLSTM + Softmax over tag space.
- **Machine Translation (Google Translate):** Transformer encoder-decoder — uses stacked self-attention + GELU feed-forward blocks.
- **Chatbots & Virtual Assistants:** Intent classification — uses nonlinear dense layers over BERT embeddings.
- **Spam Detection (Gmail):** Hybrid CNN + MLP over character/word embeddings.

The ability of nonlinear NNs to learn *hierarchical representations* (morphemes → words → phrases → sentences) is what makes them indispensable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Pass of a 2-Layer Nonlinear Network

**Setup:** Input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, one hidden layer with $m$ neurons using **Tanh** activation, output layer with $k$ neurons using **Softmax** for $k$-class classification.

### Step 1 — Linear pre-activation at hidden layer

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{z}^{(1)} &= \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)} \\
\text{Shape:} \quad &\mathbf{W}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n},\ \mathbf{b}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m},\ \mathbf{z}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}
\end{aligned}$$

This computes $m$ weighted sums of the input features in parallel.

### Step 2 — Nonlinear activation at hidden layer

$$\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \tanh\!\left(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}\right)$$

Each element $a_i^{(1)} = \tanh(z_i^{(1)})$ compresses the pre-activation into the range $(-1, +1)$, introducing curvature.

### Step 3 — Linear pre-activation at output layer

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{z}^{(2)} &= \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)} \\
\text{Shape:} \quad &\mathbf{W}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times m},\ \mathbf{b}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{k},\ \mathbf{z}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{k}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Softmax for class probabilities

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{z}^{(2)}), \quad \hat{y}_j = \frac{e^{z_j^{(2)}}}{\sum_{c=1}^{k} e^{z_c^{(2)}}}$$

This produces a valid probability distribution over $k$ classes, summing to 1.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Backpropagation via the Chain Rule

**Goal:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}}$ for cross-entropy loss.

### Step 5 — Loss function

$$L = -\sum_{j=1}^{k} y_j \log \hat{y}_j$$

### Step 6 — Gradient w.r.t. softmax input (using the elegant cross-entropy identity)

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_j^{(2)}} &= \hat{y}_j - y_j \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(2)}} &= \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y} \;\; \triangleq \delta^{(2)}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Reasoning:** This is a celebrated result. The gradient of cross-entropy combined with softmax collapses to the simple difference between prediction and ground truth. (Derivation shown in §3.4 below.)

### Step 7 — Gradient w.r.t. output weights

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} &= \delta^{(2)} (\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{\top} \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(2)}} &= \delta^{(2)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 8 — Backpropagate error to hidden layer

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta^{(1)} &= \left(\mathbf{W}^{(2)}\right)^{\top} \delta^{(2)} \odot \left(1 - (\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{2}\right) \\
\text{where} \quad \tanh'(z) &= 1 - \tanh^{2}(z) = 1 - (a^{(1)})^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

The symbol $\odot$ denotes the **element-wise (Hadamard) product**. We multiply by the local derivative of the activation function to "chain" the gradient backward.

### Step 9 — Gradient w.r.t. input weights

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} &= \delta^{(1)} \mathbf{x}^{\top} \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(1)}} &= \delta^{(1)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 10 — Gradient descent weight update

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{W}^{(1)} &\leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(1)} - \eta \, \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} \\
\mathbf{W}^{(2)} &\leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(2)} - \eta \, \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ for NLP).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Given input $\mathbf{x} = [1, 0]^{\top}$, weights $\mathbf{W}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b}^{(1)} = [0, 0]^{\top}$, and activation $\sigma = \text{ReLU}$. Compute the hidden activations.

### Step A — Compute $\mathbf{z}^{(1)}$

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{z}^{(1)} &= \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 0 \\ 1 \cdot 1 + (-1) \cdot 0 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step B — Apply ReLU

$$\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \text{ReLU}(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}) = \max(\mathbf{0}, \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Both neurons fire with positive activation. If the input had been $\mathbf{x} = [-1, 0]^{\top}$, then $\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = [-1, -1]^{\top}$ and ReLU would output $\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = [0, 0]^{\top}$ — illustrating the **killed neuron** phenomenon.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Cross-Entropy + Softmax Identity

This is the most-asked identity in KTU NLP papers. We start with:

$$L = -\sum_j y_j \log \hat{y}_j, \quad \hat{y}_j = \frac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_c e^{z_c}}$$

### Step 1 — Differentiate w.r.t. $z_j$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_j} = -\sum_{c} y_c \frac{1}{\hat{y}_c} \frac{\partial \hat{y}_c}{\partial z_j}$$

### Step 2 — Compute $\partial \hat{y}_c / \partial z_j$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \hat{y}_c}{\partial z_j} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial z_j}\!\left(\frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}}\right) \\
&= \frac{\delta_{cj} e^{z_c} \sum_k e^{z_k} - e^{z_c} e^{z_j}}{\left(\sum_k e^{z_k}\right)^2} \\
&= \hat{y}_c \left(\delta_{cj} - \hat{y}_j\right)
\end{aligned}$$

where $\delta_{cj}$ is the Kronecker delta.

### Step 3 — Substitute and simplify

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_j} &= -\sum_c y_c \cdot \frac{1}{\hat{y}_c} \cdot \hat{y}_c (\delta_{cj} - \hat{y}_j) \\
&= -\sum_c y_c (\delta_{cj} - \hat{y}_j) \\
&= -\left(y_j - \sum_c y_c \hat{y}_j\right) \\
&= -\left(y_j - \hat{y}_j \sum_c y_c\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\sum_c y_c = 1$ (one-hot label):

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_j} = \hat{y}_j - y_j}$$

This compact form is **the most important identity** for backpropagation in NLP classifiers.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Activation Function Selection Guide

| Property | Sigmoid | Tanh | ReLU | GELU |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zero-centered | ❌ | ✅ | Partial | ✅ |
| Cheap to compute | ✅ | ✅ | ✅✅ | ❌ |
| Avoids vanishing gradient | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Smooth gradient | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Recommended for NLP hidden | ❌ | Legacy RNNs | ✅ Modern | ✅✅ Transformers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: Architecture of a Nonlinear Feedforward Neural Network for NLP

The following Mermaid diagram depicts a **text-classification pipeline** where tokenized text passes through an embedding layer, is averaged, fed into a nonlinear hidden layer, and produces a class probability via Softmax.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1["Input Sentence: movie was not good"] --> tokenA["Tokenizer"]
    tokenA --> embedA["Embedding Layer (V x d)"]
    embedA --> avgA["Mean Pooling"]
    avgA --> linearA["Linear: W1 x + b1"]
    linearA --> reluA["ReLU Activation (Nonlinear)"]
    reluA --> linearB["Linear: W2 h + b2"]
    linearB --> softmaxA["Softmax Output"]
    softmaxA --> classA["Class: NEGATIVE"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Linear** boxes perform affine transformations.
- The **ReLU Activation** box is the *only* source of nonlinearity.
- Removing the ReLU would collapse the entire network into a single linear classifier.

---

## 4.2 Diagram: Backpropagation Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lossF["Loss L"] --> dZ2["delta L / delta Z2 = y_hat - y"]
    dZ2 --> dW2["delta L / delta W2"]
    dW2 --> updateW2["Update W2"]
    dZ2 --> dA1["delta L / delta A1 = W2T times delta2"]
    dA1 --> dZ1["delta L / delta Z1 = dA1 times 1 - tanh squared Z1"]
    dZ1 --> dW1["delta L / delta W1"]
    dW1 --> updateW1["Update W1"]
```

This shows the *reverse* direction of gradient flow — from the scalar loss at the output back to the input weights, applying the chain rule at every gate.

---

## 4.3 Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Nonlinear Neural Network

For complex operations like Transformer-based NLP encoders, the block-level interaction is more instructive than a per-neuron diagram:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER["INPUT REPRESENTATION"]
        tokX["Tokenized Text Sequence"]
        embX["Word Embedding Matrix E (V x d)"]
    end

    subgraph HIDDEN_STACK["NONLINEAR HIDDEN STACK"]
        linH1["Affine Layer 1: W1, b1"]
        actH1["Nonlinear Activation: GELU or ReLU"]
        linH2["Affine Layer 2: W2, b2"]
        actH2["Nonlinear Activation: GELU or ReLU"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_HEAD["OUTPUT PROJECTION"]
        linOut["Affine Output: W_out, b_out"]
        softOut["Softmax over Class Vocabulary"]
    end

    tokX --> embX
    embX --> linH1
    linH1 --> actH1
    actH1 --> linH2
    linH2 --> actH2
    actH2 --> linOut
    linOut --> softOut
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two hidden layers?** Each nonlinear activation enables the network to fold the input space along a new axis. Composing two nonlinearities produces *piecewise-quadratic* decision regions; three produces *piecewise-cubic*, and so on. The depth-to-capacity relationship is what allows deep NLP models to encode hierarchical linguistic structure.

---

## 4.4 Diagram: Decision Boundary Comparison (Linear vs Nonlinear)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    linCase["Linear Classifier: Straight Hyperplane Boundary"] --> linOut["Cannot separate XOR-like clusters"]
    nonlinCase["Nonlinear NN: Curved Boundary from Hidden Activations"] --> nonlinOut["Correctly separates nonlinear linguistic feature clusters"]
```

> **Conceptual Takeaway:** When bag-of-words features for phrases like *"not good"* and *"good"* overlap in feature space, only a curved boundary (made possible by nonlinear hidden units) can isolate them.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **Q1.** *Why are nonlinear activation functions essential in neural networks used for NLP tasks? Illustrate with the XOR problem.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Nonlinear activation functions are essential because real-world language data exhibits **nonlinear, hierarchical relationships** that no linear transformation can model.

Consider two binary features $x_1, x_2$ representing presence of *"good"* and *"not"* in a sentence:

| $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Label |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | Positive |
| 1 | 1 | Negative (negation flips polarity) |

A linear classifier $y = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b$ cannot satisfy all four points simultaneously — this is the **XOR problem**. By introducing a hidden unit $h = \sigma(w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b)$ with nonlinearity $\sigma$ (e.g., Tanh), the network can carve a curved decision region and correctly classify negation patterns. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Q2.** *Compare Sigmoid, Tanh, and ReLU activation functions in terms of (i) range, (ii) zero-centeredness, and (iii) vanishing-gradient behavior.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Sigmoid | Tanh | ReLU |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Range | $(0, 1)$ | $(-1, +1)$ | $[0, +\infty)$ |
| (ii) Zero-centered | No (always positive) | Yes | Partially (output non-negative) |
| (iii) Vanishing gradient | Severe (max derivative 0.25) | Moderate (max derivative 1.0) | None for $z > 0$ |

**Conclusion:** ReLU is preferred in modern deep NLP architectures because it avoids the vanishing-gradient problem and is computationally cheap. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by (1) confusing *Sigmoid derivative* $\sigma'(z)$ with the *Sigmoid itself* $\sigma(z)$, (2) forgetting to include the **element-wise multiplication** in backpropagation, and (3) writing cross-entropy loss without the negative sign. Always re-verify signs before finalizing the answer script.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

> **Q-A.** *(a)* Explain the architecture of a **Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)** with one hidden layer, including the role of each component, and discuss the **Universal Approximation Theorem**. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Derive the **backpropagation algorithm** for a 2-layer network with Tanh activation in the hidden layer and Softmax output, using cross-entropy loss. Show all gradient expressions. *(7 marks)*

#### (a) Model Solution — MLP Architecture & Universal Approximation

**Architecture (4 marks):**

A **Multi-Layer Perceptron** consists of:

1. **Input layer:** Accepts the feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (e.g., a 300-dim word embedding).
2. **Hidden layer(s):** Applies affine transformation followed by a nonlinear activation:

$$\mathbf{h} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)})$$

3. **Output layer:** Projects hidden activations to task-specific space:

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)})$$

**Component roles (3 marks):**

- $\mathbf{W}$: Learnable parameters that store linguistic knowledge.
- $\mathbf{b}$: Bias terms that shift activations to improve expressiveness.
- $\sigma$: Nonlinearity that allows modeling of curved decision regions.

**Universal Approximation Theorem (included in 3 marks):**

> A feedforward network with **a single hidden layer** containing a **finite number of neurons** with a **nonlinear activation** can approximate any continuous function on a compact subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ to arbitrary precision.

$$\forall \epsilon > 0,\ \exists f_m : \sup_{x \in K} \vert f(x) - f_m(x) \vert < \epsilon$$

This theorem guarantees that **in principle**, an MLP is a universal function approximator — but does not guarantee learnability via gradient descent.

#### (b) Model Solution — Backpropagation Derivation

**Forward pass (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)},\quad \mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \tanh(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})$$

$$\mathbf{z}^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)}\mathbf{a}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)},\quad \hat{\mathbf{y}} = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{z}^{(2)})$$

**Loss (1 mark):**

$$L = -\sum_j y_j \log \hat{y}_j$$

**Gradient at output (2 marks):**

$$\delta^{(2)} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(2)}} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} (\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{\top}, \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)}$$

**Backpropagation to hidden layer (2 marks):**

$$\delta^{(1)} = \left(\mathbf{W}^{(2)}\right)^{\top} \delta^{(2)} \odot \left(1 - (\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{2}\right)$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} = \delta^{(1)} \mathbf{x}^{\top}, \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(1)}} = \delta^{(1)}$$

**Weight updates (optional, 0 marks — implicit):**

$$\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **Q-B.** *(a)* Explain **Sigmoid, Tanh, ReLU, and Softmax** activation functions with their mathematical formulations, derivatives, and suitable NLP use cases. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Consider a binary sentiment classifier using a 2-layer network: input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$, one hidden neuron with Sigmoid activation, output neuron with Sigmoid activation. Given $\mathbf{W}^{(1)} = [1, -1]$, $b^{(1)} = 0.5$, $\mathbf{W}^{(2)} = [2]$, $b^{(2)} = -1$, and input $\mathbf{x} = [1, 1]^{\top}$: compute the **forward pass output probability** and the **gradient of the binary cross-entropy loss w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}^{(1)}$**. *(7 marks)*

#### (a) Model Solution — Activation Functions

(Each activation: 1.5 marks; allocate roughly.)

**Sigmoid:**
$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad \sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z)) \in (0, 0.25]$$
**NLP use:** Binary sentiment output, gating mechanisms in LSTMs.

**Tanh:**
$$\tanh(z) = \frac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}, \quad \tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(z) \in (0, 1]$$
**NLP use:** Hidden state activations in classical RNN/LSTM cells.

**ReLU:**
$$\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z), \quad \text{ReLU}'(z) = \mathbf{1}_{z>0}$$
**NLP use:** Hidden layers of feedforward networks and Transformer FFN blocks.

**Softmax:**
$$\text{softmax}(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$$
**NLP use:** Multi-class output layer for POS tagging, NER, language modeling.

#### (b) Model Solution — Numerical Forward & Backward Pass

**Step 1 — Hidden pre-activation (1 mark):**

$$z^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + b^{(1)} = (1)(1) + (-1)(1) + 0.5 = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Hidden activation (1 mark):**

$$a^{(1)} = \sigma(0.5) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.5}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.6065} \approx 0.6225$$

**Step 3 — Output pre-activation (1 mark):**

$$z^{(2)} = W^{(2)} a^{(1)} + b^{(2)} = (2)(0.6225) + (-1) = 0.245$$

**Step 4 — Output probability (1 mark):**

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(0.245) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.245}} \approx 0.5610$$

**Step 5 — Binary cross-entropy loss derivative (1 mark):**

Assuming true label $y = 1$:
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z^{(2)}} = \hat{y} - y = 0.5610 - 1 = -0.4390$$

**Step 6 — Gradient w.r.t. hidden weights (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} &= \frac{\partial L}{\partial z^{(2)}} \cdot W^{(2)} \cdot \sigma'(z^{(1)}) \cdot \mathbf{x}^{\top} \\
\sigma'(z^{(1)}) &= a^{(1)}(1 - a^{(1)}) = 0.6225 \cdot 0.3775 \approx 0.2350 \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} &= (-0.4390)(2)(0.2350) \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}^{\top} \\
&\approx -0.2064 \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}^{\top}
\end{aligned}$$

Final answer:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.2064 & -0.2064 \end{bmatrix}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Nonlinear Neural Networks**

- **Nonlinearity is mandatory** for modeling nonlinearly separable linguistic patterns (e.g., negation, sarcasm).
- **Universal Approximation Theorem:** A single sufficiently wide hidden layer can approximate any continuous function.
- **Common activation functions:**
  - *Sigmoid:* binary output, but suffers from vanishing gradient.
  - *Tanh:* zero-centered hidden activation.
  - *ReLU:* default for deep networks, avoids vanishing gradient for $z > 0$.
  - *Softmax:* multi-class probability distribution.
  - *GELU:* smooth gating used in Transformers.
- **Backpropagation** uses the chain rule; the cross-entropy + Softmax identity simplifies the output gradient to $\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$.
- **Initialization matters:**
  - **Xavier** for Tanh/Sigmoid.
  - **He** for ReLU.
- **Vanishing gradient** is mitigated by ReLU/GELU activations and skip connections.
- **Depth vs Width:** Deep + narrow generalizes better in NLP than shallow + wide.
- **Real-world NLP applications:** Sentiment analysis, NER, machine translation, intent classification, language modeling.
- **KTU pitfall to avoid:** Always check the **sign of the cross-entropy gradient** and remember the **element-wise multiplication** by the activation derivative during backpropagation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
